or classical m arc n much closer to the teachers than to the artists).

Those who have acquired the bulk of their cultural capital in and for school have more classical', safer cultural investments than those who have received a large cultural inheritance. For example, whereas the members of the dominant class with rhe highest qualifications (the agrégation or a diploma from a grande école) never mention certain works or certain painters ypical of middle-brow culture, such as Buffet or Utrillo, have considerable knowledge of composers, and prefer the Well-Tempered Clavier or the Firebird Suite, the highly educated members of the working and middle classes more often make choices which indicate their respect for a more 'scholastic' culture (Goya, Leonardo, Breughel, Watteau, Raphael), and a significant proportion of them concur with the opinion that paintings are nice but difhicult'. By contrast, those who originate from the dominant class know more works and more often choose works further from ‘scholastic'culture (Braque, Concerto for the Left Hand). Similarly, those members of the established petite bourgeoisie (craftsmen, shopkeepers, clerical and commercial employees, junior executives) who have relatively low educational capital (BÉPC or below) make choices clearly marked by their trajectory. Thus, those who are rising socially show their respect for legitimate culture in various ways (e.g., they are more likely to agree that paintings are nice but difficult') and choose works typical of middle-brow (Buffet, Utrifllo) or even popular taste (Blue Danube). However, those whose fathers belonged to the upper classes manifest, at equivalent levels of educational capital, greater familiarity with musical works (although they are no more familiar with the composers' names), just as they more often say they like the Im. pressionists, visit museums more often and more often choose academically consecrated works (Raphael or Leonardo).

MANNERS AND MANNER OF ACQUISITION Cultural (or linguistic) competence, which is acquired in relation to a particular feld functionutilization-—. in a given relationship to culture or language—function other words, what are grasped through indicators such as educatio level or social origin or, more precisely, in the structure of the relati ship between them, are also different modes of production of the cu vated habitus, which engender differences not only in the competen acquired but also in the manner of applying them. These difference manner constitute a set of secondary properties, revealing different ditions of acquisition and predisposed to receive very different value the various markets.

Knowing that 'manner' is a symbolic manifestation whose mean and value depend as much on the perceivers as on the producer, one see how it is that the manner of using symbolic goods, especially th regarded as the attributes of excellence, constitutes one of the key mi ers of class' and also the ideal weapon in strategies of distinction, thar as Proust put it, 'the infinitely varied art of marking distances'. The id ogy of natural taste contrasts two modalities of cultural competence its use, and, behind them, two modes of acquisition of culture.8 T early, imperceptible learning, performed within the family from the liest days of life and extended by a scholastic learning which presupp and completes it, differs from belated, methodical learning not so m in the depth and durability of its effects—as the ideology of cultural neer' would have it—as in the modality of the relationship to langu and culture which it simultaneously tends to inculcate. It confers self-certainty which accompanies the certainty of possessing cultura gitimacy, and the ease which is the touchstone of excellence; it prod the paradoxical relationship to culture made up of self-confidence (relative) ignorance and of casualness amid familiarity, which bour families hand down to their offspring as if it were an heirloom.

The competence of the 'connoisseur', an unconscious mastery o instruments of appropriation which derives from slow familiariza and is the basis of familiarity with works, is an ‘art', a practical mas which, like an art of thinking or an art of living, cannot be transmi solely by precept or prescription. Learning it presupposes the equive of the prolonged contact between disciple and master in a traditte education, i.e., repeated contact with cultural works and cultured ped And just as the apprentice or disciple can unconsciously acquire the of the art, including those that are not consciously known to the m himself, by means of a self-abandonment, excluding analysis and selec of the elements of the exemplary conduct, so too the art-lover, in a s surrendering himself to the work, can internalize its principles of struction, without these ever being brought to his consciousness and mulated or formulable as such; and this is what makes all the differd between the theory of art and the experience of the connoisseur, wh generally incapable of stating the principles of his judgements. By trast, all institutionalized learning presupposes a degree of rationa tion, which leaves its mark on the relationship to the goods consun The sovereign pleasure of the aesthete dispenses with concepts. It is sential part of what schools communicate is again acquired incidentally, such as the system of classification which the school system inculcates through the order in which it inculcates knowledge or through the presuppositions of its own organization (the hierarchy of disciplines, sections, exercises etc.) or its operation (mode of assessment, rewards and punishments etc.). But, in order to transmit at all, it has to perform a degree of rationalization of what it transmits. Thus, for example, in place of practical schemes of classification, which are always partial and linked to practical contexts, it puts explicit, standardized taxonomies, fixed once and for all in the form of synoptic schemas or dualistic typologies (e.g., 'classical'/'romantic'), which are expressly inculcated and therefore conserved in the memory as knowledge that can be reproduced in virtually identical form by all the agents subjected to its action.

To avoid any absolutization of the culture in relation to which the autodidact's middle-brow culture is objectively defined, it has to be remembered that the higher one rises in the social hierarchy, the more one's tastes are shaped by the organization and operation of the educational system, which is responsible for inculcating the programme'(syllabus and intellectual schemes) which governs ‘cultivated minds' even in their pursuit of the personal touch' and their aspiration to 'originality'. Discrepancies between edu. cational qualifications and cultural competence (linked to social trajectory and largely attributable to the domestic transmissrion of non-scholastic cultural capital) are, however, sufciently frequent to safeguard the irreducibility, recognized even by academics, of 'authentic' culture to 'scholastic' knowledge, which as such is devalued.

By providing the means of expression which enable practical preferences to be brought to the level of quasi-systematic discourse and to be consciously organized around explicit principles, the educational system orher words, what are grasped through indicarors such as educational level or social origin or, more precisely, in rhe strucrure of the rehtion ship berween them, are also different modes of production of the culti vated habirus, which engender diffeences nor only in the competences acquired but also in rhe manner of applying them. These differences in manner constitute a ser of secondary properties, revealing different con. dicions of acquisition and predisposed ro receive very diferent values in the various markets.

Knowing that 'manner' is a symbolic manif srarion whose meaning and value depend as much on the perceivers as on the producer, one can see how it is thar the manner of using symbolic goods, especially those regarded as rhe atrributes of excellence, constiruces one of the key markers of class' and also rhe ideal weapon in serategies of distinction, that is, as Proust put it, 'the infinitely varied art of marking distances'. The ideol. ogy of natural raste contrasts two modaliries of culrural competence and its use. and, behind them, two modes of acquisition of culture." Toral. early, imperceptible learning, performed wichin the family from the earliest days of life and exrended by a scholasric learning which presuppo ses and compleres it, differs from belared, methodical learning not so much in che depth and durability of its effects--as the ideology of culrural 'veneer'would have it—as in the modality of the relationship to language and culrure which it simulraneously cends to inculcate." Ic confers the self-certainty which accompanies the cerrainty of possessing cultural legitimacy, and the ease which is the touchstone of excellence; it produices the paradoxical relationship to culture made up of self-confdence amid (relarive) ignorance and of casualness amid familiarity, which bourgeois families hand down ro their offspring as if ir were an heirloom.

By providing the means of expression which enable practical prefer.   
cal prin ciples of rasre. As grammar does forlinguistic competence, it ra-of beaury', in those who already have it, giving them   
Ple), prece ing to principles (of harmony or rheroric, for examulae, instead of relying on improvisation; it substi

rational pedagogy which tends to convey piecemeal, in a doctrinal set of explicit norms and formulae, explicitly taught, generally negative rather than positive, what tradirional learning transmits in the form of a total sryle directly grasped in practice. But above ail-and rhis is why aesthetes so abhor pedagogues and pedagogy---the rational teaching of art provides substicures for direct experience, it offers short cuts on the long path of familiarization, it makes possible practices which are the product of con. ceprs and rules instead of springing from the supposed spontancity of taste, thereby offering a solurion to those who hope to make up for lost time.

The ideology of narural raste owes its plausibility and its efhcacy ro the fact that, like all the ideological straregies generated in the everyday class srruggle, it naturalizes real differences, converting diferences in the mode of acquisition of culture into differences of nature; it onty recognizes as legitimate the relation to culture (or language) which least bears the visible marks of its genesis, which has nothing 'academic', 'scholastic', 'bookish', 'aff ected' or 'studied' about it, but manifests by irs ease and naturalness rhat true culrure is nature—a new mystery of immaculate conceprion. This is clearly seen in the remarks of an aesthere of the culinary art, who writes no diff erently from Pierre Francastel when the latrer, in a devastating confession for an art hisrorian, rejects 'intellecrualized knowl. edge', which can only 'recognize', in favour of 'visual experience', the sole means of access to true vision':

'Taste must nor be confused with gastronomy. Whereas taste is the nalural gift of recognizing and loving perfection, gastronomy is the set of rulø which govem the cultivarion and educalion of rasre. Gastronomy is to rasre as grammar and literature are to che literary sense. And this brings us to the heart of the problem: if the gourmet is a delicate connaisseur, is the gastronome a pedant? .. . The gourmet is his own gastronome, just as che man of raste is his own grammarian ... Not everyone is a gourmet; thar is why we need gastronomes We musr look upon gastronomes as we look upon pedagogues in general: they are sometimes intolerable pedants, bur they have their uses. They belong to the lourr, modest order, and it is up to them to improve this rather minor genre by means of ract, re straint and elegant lighrness . . . There is such a thing as bad raste . .. and persons of refinement know this instinctivel y. For those who do not, rules are needed.2

Knowledge by experience, which, likeAquinas's cognitio Dei experimentalis, feels and deplores che essential inadequacy of words and concepts o express the realiry 'tasred' in mystical union, rejects as unworthy the in rellectual love of art, the knowledge which identifies exp rience of the work with an intellecrual operation of deciphering.

The grear debates over taste which lterary works arouse or dramatize (such as the question of the preécieux, who by codifying and rarionalizing salon delicacy, an art of living defined as indefinable, changed its whole narure) involve nor only the virtues with which the different fractions of the dominant class identify, but, 2s the Chevalier de Méré so well purs it, the manners of practising thcm, which are themselves kinds of virtues', and through which senior. ity in their class, and their way of gecting thete, are expressed or be. trayed.

Innumera:ble illustrations could be cited from the vast literarure designed to codify, inseparably, ordinary behaviour and the creation andperception of works of art, in short everything which falls under the absolute jurisdiction of taste, one of che key words of che age." but one example will suffice, because it explicitly links manner, mode of acquisition and rhe group it desig. nates: 'The authot [Furerière, the bourgeois author of Le Roman bourgeois who had criticized La Fontaine and Benserade} shows clearly thar he is neicher of sociery nor of the court and that his rasre is of a pedantry one cannot even hope to rectify. Ccrtain things are never understood if they are not understood ar once: some hard and rough minds will never be led into the charm and grace of Benserade's ballets and La Fontzine's fables. That door is closed to them, and so is mine . .. One can only pray to God for such a man and hope never to have dealings wirh him' (Mme. de Sévigné, lerter to Bussy-Rabutin, 14 May 1686).

Paradoxically, precocity is an effect of seniority: aristocracy is the form par excellence of precocity since it is nothing other than the seniority which is the birthright of the offspring of ancient families (at leasr in so cieties in which age and aristocracy—virtually equivalent notionsare recognized as values). And this initial starus-derived capital is enhanced by the advantages which precocious acquisition of legitimare culture gives in learning cultural skills, whether table manners or the art of conversation, musical culture or the sense of propriety, playing tennis or p nunciation. The embodied cultural capital of the previous generations functions as a sort of advance (both a head-start and a credit) which, by providing from the outser the example of culture incarnated in familiar modeis, enables the newcomer to start acquiring the basic ciements

Ease or Cultivated Natural

'This civility is perceived in the fearutes, the manner, in the slighcest actions of the body and mind,: and the more one considers it, the more one is charmed by it, without realizing where it comes from. .. . ## 72 / A Social Critique of the Jud gement of Taste

Every group rends to set up the means of petperuaring itself beyond the f. nite individuals in whom it is incarnared. (This was one of Durkheim's fundamental insights.) In order to do so, it establishes a whole ser of mech. anisms, such as delegarion, representation and symbolization, which confer ubiquiry and eternicy. The represenrative (e.g, the king) is erernal. As Kantarovirch has shown, the king has two bodies, a biological, morral body, subject to biological infrmities, passion or imbeciliry, and a political body, immorra:l, immaceriał and freed from infirmities or weaknesses.He can secure ubiquiry by delegating to orhers the authoricywith which he is invested. His raxes are levied by fiscus ubique presens, and, as Post observes. thie delegate who holds plena potestar agendi 'can do everyching thar the mandaror himself can do', thanks to his procuratio ad omnia facienda. Again, universitas non moritur. Dearh, from the point of view of groups, is only an accident, and personihed collectives organize themselves in such a way thar rhe demise of the mortal bodies which once embodied the group—representa.tives, delegates, agents, spokesmen—does not affect the existence of the group or the function in which it is realized: dignitas non morilur.

If this is accepred (and it would need to be esrablished more systemati. cally), then capital makes it possible to appropriare the collectively produced and accumulared means of really overcoming anthropological limies. The mcans of escaping from generic alienations include represenracion, the portrait or statue which immorralizes the person represented (sometimes, by a sorr of plconasm, in his own liferime); and memorials, the combstone, the written word, aere perennius, which celebrates and 'hands on to posrerity', and, in particular, historical writing, which gives a place in legitimate hisiory-hence the particular status which the public, especially the bourgeois public, gives to historians, the masters of scientifc eternization-and the commemorative ceremonies in which the group offers tributes of homage and gratitude to the dead, who are thereby shown to be sill living and active. Thus it can be seen that erernal life is one of the most sought-after social privileges; the qualicy of the erernity depends, of course, on the qualiry and exrent of the group providing it, and can range from a requiem mass organized by che family to an annual national holiday.

If the foregoing argument suggests an ‘analysis of essence' (rhough far removed, it would seem, from Heidegger and his ‘old chest'). that is be cause most groups have sought to lay down absolute, final differences by means of the irreversibiliry of time, which gives inflexible rigour to every form of social order based on the order of successions. The holders claimants to succession—farher and son, owner and heir, master and ciple, predecessor and successor—are separated by nothing, except time; but there is every sorr of social mechanism to make this gap unbridge able. Thus, in the struggle between the diferent 'manners', i.e., the differ ent manners of acquiring, the dominant groups are always on the side of of 'pedants.

A practical mstery of social signifcance, based on funcrional and sttucural homology,underlies an facilitates everyday reading of che cassics', and, even more, since it is a pr ctical use,literary quoration, a quite special use of discourse which is a sore of summons to appear as advocate and witness, addressed to a past auchor on the basis of a social solidarity disguised as intellectual solidarity. The prctical sense of meaning, which stops shott of objccifying the social affinity which makes it possible--since chat would nullify the desired eftect, by relativizing both the reading and the text-provides simultaneously a social use and a denial of che social basis of that use.

Identifying the invariants must nor, however, lead us to creat a particu. lar scate of che struggle as eternal, and a true comparative srudy would have to take account of the specific forms that the struggle and the themes in which it is expressed take on when the objective relarions be rween the class fractions change. It seems, forexample, thar in the second half of che seventeenth century the growing authority of the mondains and of the court, combined with the tendency of high society to become more cultivared, reduced the distance between doctes and mondains; this led to the rise of a new species of man of letters, rypified by the Jesuits Rapin and Bouhours,® masters of rhetoric who were themselves both doctes and mondains, who frequented artisrs and aristocrars and helped to produce a synthesis of the demands of the court and the academy (and did so by shifring the centre of the debare from the question of worthy subjects ro chat of che style in which they might be rreated).

Similarly, nowadays, the fact thar an increasingly large proportion of the espcally in Frnce, the grande cles) is tending to modify the form of che

versitiesa The case of the relations berween the nineteenth-century German uniincely courts represents another srare of the power redifferent conhguration of rhe images of the scholarly urtly viccues. As Norbert Elias very clearly shows,   
regrated i intellectuals were much earlier and much more complerely in- 2 to the world of the court in France chan in Germany. The

conventions of style and forms of civilicy which dominate che educa tional system and all its products, in particular the attention given to language and to intellectual propricty, derived, in the case of France from court society, whereas in Germany the intelligentsia, especially in the universities, set itself up in opposition co the court and the French models it was importing, summing up its vision of 'high society' in the antithesis between Civilization', characterized by frivolity and superfici alicy, and 'Culture', defined by seriousness, profundiry and authenticity." In other words, there is the same basic opposition berween doctes and mondains, with identical content, but with the values reversed: here the doctes could not assert their autonomy except by asserting their own vir rues and their own manner of practising them', thereby devaluing high. society virtues.

The fact remains that the pedant's' situation is never entirely comfortable. Against the 'populace' and with the mondain aristocracy-who have every reason also to accept it, since they have an interest in birthrights—he is inclined to accept the ideology of innate tastes, since it is the only absolure guarantee of his election; but against the mondain he is forced to assert the value of his acquirements, and, indeed, the value of the work of acqjuisition, the slow effort to improve the mind', as Kant put it, which is a blemish in the cyes of the mondain, but in his own eyes his supreme merit.

The embarrassment of academic minds, indebted and commitred to acquisition, surfaces whenever ir is a question of the adeguare approach to a work of art and the right way to acquire it; and rhe contradiccion is at the heart of all cheir aesrhetic theories, nor to mention their atremprs to establish a pedagogy of art. "The ideology of narural gifrs is too porent, even wirhin the educational system, for an expression of faith in che powers of a rational pedagogy aimed at ceducing the practical schemes of familiarity to codifed rules, despite the facr chat this practical affirmation of the nacural righc to art is the narural weapon of those who appeal to knowledge and ideas and aim to discredit the divine right of rhe advocares of immediace experience and pleasure. For example, rhere are all rhe debares over the teaching of art (more specihcally, the reaching of drawing)—a concradiction in terms for some, who hold chat beauty is neither raught nor learnt, bur is a grace rransmirred from invested masters to predestined disciples; for orhers, a held of pedagogy like any ncher. (One chinks, for example, of the polemics berween the advocares of rational pedagogy, such as Guillaume, and the champions of cthe charismatic view, such as Ravaisson, over the in troduction of drawing lessons inco general education in rhe earlly years of the Third Republic.)

EXPERtENCE AND KNOWLEDGE Ideology is an illusion consistent with interest, but a well-grounded illusion. Those who invoke exp against knowledge have a basis for their prejudice in the real opposition between the domestic learning and the scholastic learning of culture much he same way as the relation to painting of chose who have discovered it belatedly, in the quasi-scholastic atmosphere of the museum, dif. fers from the relation developed by chose born into a world filled with art objects, familiar family property, amassed by successive generations, testifying to cheir wealth and good taste, and sometimes 'home-made' (like jam or embroidered linen).

Differençes linked to social origin are no doubt most marked in personal production of visual art or the playing of a musical instrument, apritudes which, boh in their acquisirion and in their performance, presuppose not only dipositions associared with long establishmenc in the world of art and culeure but also economic means (especially in the case of piano.playing) and spare time. At equal educational levels, they vary strongly by social origin. Thus, among holders of the baccalduréas, 11.5 percent of the respondents who originate from the dominanc.class ay they often play a musical instrument, compared with 5 percent of those of middle-class or workingclass origin. Among graduares, the corresponding proportions are 22.5 per. cent and s percent. Painring and sculpture, relarively neglected by those with che highest qualifcations, ace also, ac equal educarional levels, much more common among respondents of dominanc-class origin.

Status-linked familiarity is manifested in, for example, knowledge of the opportunities and conditions for acquiring works of art, which depends nor only on the material and culural capacity ro appropriate but also on longstanding membership in a social world in which art, being an object of appropnation, is present in the form of familiar, personal objects. Thus, in the survey commissioned by the Ministy of Culture (C.S. VIIl), the percentage

g chose who play an instnement-peciaily the pianoamong others) or L'Enfant et les sortilèges is much more strongly linked to social origin than to educarional capital. By contrast, with works like the WellTempered Clavier or the Art of Fugue, thete is a stronger correlation with educational capital chan with social origin. Through these indicators, despite their imperfections, one can distinguish different relations to the hierarchical, hieratchizing world of cul. tural works, which are closely linked to a set of interrelated differences and which stem from different modes of acquisition—domestic and scholastic, or exclusively scholastic—of cultural capital. Thus, when Ro land Barthes makes an aesthetic out of a particular relation to music, produced by early, domestic, practical' acquaintance, and describes aes. thetic enjoyment as a sort of immediare communication berween the lis. rener's body and the performer's inner body', present in 'the grain of the singer's voice' or 'the pad of the pianist's ingers', he is in fact referring to the opposition berween two modes of acquisition.

On the one hand, there is music for record collectors (linked to a demand arising from the growth of the number of listeners and the disap pearance of practitioners'), an expressive, dramatic, sentimentally clear art of communication, of understanding: 'This culture ... wants art, wants music, provided they be clear, that they "translate" an emotion and represent a signified (the "meaning" of a poem): an art that inoculates pleasure (by reducing it to a known, coded emotion) and reconciles the subject to what in music can be said: what is said about it by Institucion, Criticism, Opinion. On the other hand, there is an art which prefers the sensible to sense, which hates eloquence, grandiloquence, pathos and the pathetic, the expressive and che dramatic. This is French mélodie, Duparc, the later Faurć, Debussy, everything thar in another age would have been called pure music, the intimism of the piano, the maternal instrument, and the intimacy of the bourgeois salon. In this antithesis between two relations to music which are aways defined, more unconsciously than consciously, in relation to each other-the taste for the artists of the past, Panzera or Cortot, loved even for their imperfections which evoke the freedom of the amateur, implies a distasre for modern performers and their impeccable recordings for mass production-one again finds the old opposition berween the docte, who is bound to the code (in every sense), the rules, and therefore the Institution and Criticism, and the hedonistic mondain, who, being on che side of natute, the 'natural', is content to feel and enjoy, and who expels all crace of intellectualism, didacticism, pedantry from his artistic experience.

OBJECT LEssONS Every material inheritance is, strictly speaking, also cultural inheritance. Family heirtooms not only bear material witr the age and continuity of the lineage and so consecrate its social identity. which is inseparable from permanence over time; they also contributei a practical way to irs spiritual reproduction, that is, to transmitti

hed in existing by its perfection, its harmony and beauty, a world which pable of playing and appreciating them. And finally it is an immediate adherence, at the deepest level of the habitus, to the tastes and distastes, Symparhies and aversions, fantasies and phobias which, more than declared opinions, forge the unconscious unity of a class.

If a group's whole life-style can be read of from the style it adopts in furnishing or clothing, this is not only because these properties are the objectifcation of the economic and culrural necessity which determined their selection, but also because the social relations objectified in familiar objecrs, in their luxury or poverty, their 'distinction' or 'vulgarity', their 'beaury' or 'ugliness', impress rhemselves through bodily experiences which may be as profoundly unconscious as the quiet caress of beige carpets or the thin clamminess of tattered, garish linoleum, the harsh smell of bleach or perfumes as imperceptible as a negative scent.' Every inte. rior expresses, in its own language, the present and even the past state of its occupants, bespeaking the elegant self-assurance of inherited wealth, the flashy arrogance of the nouveaux riches, the discrcet shabbiness of the poor and the gilded shabbiness of poor relations' striving to live beyond their means; one thinks of the child in D. H. Lawrence's story The Rocking.Horse Winner' who hears throughout the house and even in his bedroom, full of expensive toys, an incessant whispering: There must be more money.' Experiences of chis sort would be the material of a so cial psychoanalysis which set out to grasp the logic whereby the social relations objectified in thmgs and also, of course, in people are insensibly intrnalized taking theit place in a lasting relation to the world and.to others which manifests itself, for example, in thresholds of tolerance of the natural and social word, of noise, overcrowding, physical or verbal one dimension 74

choicesof The effect of mode of acquisition is most marked in the ordinary are existence, such as furnitute, clothing or cooking, larly revealing of deep-rooted and long-standing dispo  
have ro ying ourside the scope of the educational system, they ed, as it were, by naked taste, without any explicit   
ing agcn iption, other than from semi-legitimate legitimizs women's weeklies or 'ideał home' magazines.

This means that, however imperfect it may be, given the poesent state of functioning of the educational system, the minimal rationalization implied by every institutionalized pedagogy, in particular the transformation of clas 'sense', functioning in practical form, into partially codified knowledge (e.g.. lirerat histor , with its clssications by periods, genres and styles), has the effect of reducing, at least among the most over-selected survivors the weight of what is abandoned to inherited 'senses' and, consequently, the diferences linked to economic and cultutal inheritance. It is also true that these differences continue to function in other areas, and that they recover their fuil force as soon as the logic ofthe struggle for distinction moves its real stakes into these areas—which it of course always tends to do.

The adjectives the respondenes have chosen to describe an interior, and the source of their furnirure, are more closely linked to their social origin than to their educational qualifications (unlike their judgement on pho. tographs or their knowledge of composers), because nothing, perhaps, more directly depends on early leaming. especiaily the leaming which takes place without any express intention to teach, than the dispositions and knowledge that are invested in clothing, furnishing and cooking or, more precisely, in the way clothes, furniture and food are bought. Thus, the mode of acquisition of furniture (department store, antique-dealer, shop or Flea Market) depends at least as much on social origin as on schooling. At equal educarional levels, those members of the dominant class who were also born into that classwho, more often than the others, inheri ted some of their furitureacquire their furniture (espc cially those living in Paris) from an antique-dealer more often than those born into other classes, who tended to buy from a department store, a specialized shop or the Flea Market. dAnd it is probably in castes in sod that one would fnd the strongest world, the world of primordial astes and basic foods of the archetypal relation to the archetypal culrural good. in which pieasure-giving is an integral part of pleasure and of the selective disposition towards pleasure which is acquired through pleasure.

While the aim was to identify preferences in food, the search for the most economical and 'synthetic' questions led me to question the respondents on the mcals they served on special occasions, an interesting indicator of the mode of self-presentation adopte in 'showing ofl a life-style (in which furniture also plays a part). For a complere understanding of choices in this area, a particularly complex set of factors has to be borne in mind: rhe style of meal that people like to offer is no doubr a very good indicator of che image chey wish to givt or avoid giving to others and, as such. it is the sysrematic expression of a system of facrors including, in a dition to the indicarors of the position occupied in the economic and cultural hierarchies, economic trajectory, social trajectory and culrural trajectory.

This bemg so, it is not surprising thar the effects are most visible in the petite bourgeoisie. The members of the esrablished petite bourgeoisie more ofren serve cheir friends 'plenaful and good', 'simple but well-presented meals chan the new petite bourgeoisie, who prefer to serve original\* ineals or por luck' But one also finds strong diff erences linked to trajectory. Thus new petit bourgeois of middle or working-class origin more often offer 'plentiful and good' meals, which is never the case with rhose of upper class origin, who, by conrtrast, are very inclined to the 'original and exotic' 1n the established petite hourgeoisie, the propensity to offer plentiful and god meais is as strong among those in declinc as among chose who are upwardly mobile and originate from the working classes. The former never say rhey offer por luck' or original and exotic' meals, whereas the larter sometimes do (chough not, of course, as often as the new petit bourgeois).

u pl  u o orporcality (such as che 'unique, pure note' of the Philebus,   
which alrca dy reserved them for the few'), contain an clement which, as   
in the cru refers dest' pleasures of the rastes of food. the archetype of all ascc   
term o the oldest and deepest experiences, those which de.   
vour over-determine the primitive oppositions-biter/sweer. Aahot/cold, coarse/deticate, ausrere,/bright—which are as

essential to gastronomic commentary as to the refined appreciations of This product of an 'art' in Durkheim's sense, .e., 'a pure practice without rheory', and sometimes of a simple mimesis, a sort of symbolic gymnas. rics, always contains also something inefable, not through excess, as the celebrants would have it, but by default, someching which communicates,as it were, from body to body, like the rhythm of music or the fla. vour of colours, that is, falling shorr of words and concepts. Art is also 'bodily ching', and music, the most pure' and 'spiritual' of the arts. is perhaps simply the most corporeal. Linked to états d'âme which are also stares of che body or, as they were once called, humouis, it ravishes, car. ries away, moves. It is pitched not so much beyond words as below them, in gestures and movements of che body, rhythms—which Piaget somewhere says characterize the functions located, like everything which gov. erns raste, at the articulation of the organic and the mental quickening and slowing, crescendo and decrescendo, tension and relaxation." This is no doubr why, once it leaves the realm of pure technique, musical criticism scarcely speaks other than in adjectives and exclamations. As mystics speak of divine love in the language of human love, so the least inadequate evocations of musical pleasure are those which can replicate the peculiar forms of an experience as deeply rooted in the body and in primitive bodily expcriences as the tastes of food.

1NHERITED CAPITAL AND ACQUIRED CAPITAL ThUS, the differenceS which the relarionship to educarional capital leaves unexplained, and which mainly appear in the relarionship with social origin, may be due to differences in rhe mode of acquisition of the cultural capital now possessed. Bur rhey may also be due to differences in rhe degree to which rhis capital is recognized and guaranteed by academic qualifcations: a cerrain proportion of the capital actually owned may nor have received academic sanction, when it has been directly inherited and even when it has been acquired in school. Because of rhe long hysteresis of the mode of acquisition, the same educational qualifications may guarantee quite different relarions to culrure--but decreasingly so, as one rises in the educational hierarchy and as more value comes to be set on ways of using knowledge and less on merely knowing. If the same volume of educational capital (guaranteed culrural capiral) may cortespond to different volumes of socially proficabie cultural capital, chis is firsr because alrhough the educational system, by its monopoly of certihcation, governs the conversion of inherited culrural capital into educational capital, it does nor have a monopoly on rhe production of culrural capital. $1 + g i v c s$ irs sanction to inherired capital to a greater or less exterıt (i.e., there is an unequal conversion of inherited culural capital) because, at diferent stitution itself).

8in águre 3), are opposed, fr t, to those who lack both educational capital and inherired culrural capital (A) (and to al rhose who ate siruated lower down the axis representing perfect ecoaversion of cultural capital inro educational capiral). But rhey are also opposed, on the one hand, to those who. with equivalent inherited culcural capital, have brained lower educa. ronalcapital (C or C) (or who have an inherired culrutal capital greater than their educational capitalc.g., C' relarive ro B', or D' relative ro D) and who are closer to them, especially as regards general culrure, than the holders of idenrical qualifications; and, on the ocher hand, to chose who have similar educationalcapital bur who started off with less culrural capiral (Dot D') aud whose relarion to culture, which rhey owe more to rhe school and less to the family, is less familiar and more scholastic. (These secondary oppositions occur at every level of the axis.)

Figure 3 Thc relationship betsween inherited culrural capiral and educational capital  
![](images/282bd61a601cd85710f6f7e3e64aaed9562cde8109d34e99b70cac0011aaf1ef.jpg)

One could construct a similar diagram for each rype of capital (eco. nomic, cultural and social) possessed initially and ar the time of observa. rion, and then define the set of possible cases for rhe relationship between inirial capital (defined as regards volume and composition) and eventual capital, characterized in the same way. (There would be, for example, individuals declining in all rypes of capital, or declining in only one and rising in others—reconversion-erc.) If one sufficiently refined the analysis of the species of capital (dividing cultural capital, for example, into sub-species such as lirerary, scientific and legal-economic capital) or the analysis of the level, it would be possible to find all rhe cases empirically observed, in all their complexity but also in their quasi-infinire multipliciry.

To be entirely rigorous, one would have to allow for structural changes, such as the devaluation of nominal qualifications which occurs in periods (as in recent years) when the educational system is used more intensively. (This devaluation has been symbolized by placing the line indicating the real equivalents of qjualificarions below the bisector which marks the equiva. lents of the nominal value of qualifications.) One would also have to make allowance for the discrepancy berween the number of years of study and the qualification obtained (which becomes more probable as initial capital rises and schooling becomes more widespread—so that it now affects even the working classes whose childten often leave secondary school without any qualificarion). It would then be seen that, to explain cerrain practices ade. quarely (in particular, aurodidacticism) one has to consider nor only the qualification and the number of years of schooling but also the relationship between the two (which may generare self-assurance or embarrassment, arrogance or resentment etc). One might also consider the relationship between age ar the end of schooling and the legitimate age for a qualification, such as the bac (baccalauréat) ar 17 or the age limits fot the concours (entrance examinations for the grandes écoles). One of the mediations chrough which cultural capital is transformed into educational capiral is speed of progress through the system.

The discrepancy berween educational capital and the culrural capital actually possessed, which is the source of differences between holders of identical educacional capiral, can also result from rhe fact that the same educational ualification may correspond to schooling of very unequal duration (i.e., there is unequal conversion of scholastically acquired cultural capital). The direct or indirect effects of one or several years of study may in fact not be sanctioned .by the award of a diploma—as is the case with all those who drop out in the two years leading to the bacca. lauréat or, at a higher level, those who have spent one or two years at university without obtaining a qualification. But in addition, because the frequency of this discrepancy has risen with the chances of access of the different classes to secondary and higher education, agents belonging to different generations (as identified by age-groups) are likely to have de voted a very differenr number of years of study (with all the related effects, including greater non-certified competence, of course, but also the diterent mod of generation correspond different relations to the educarional system which are expretssed in dinerent straregies of cultural investment nor guaranteed by the educational institution (i.e., autodidacticism).

In the absence of more precise indicarors of the overall scyle of culrural consumption (e.g., the opposition berween the sarirical weeklies Le Canard Enchainé and Charlie Hebdo, or, in the area of popular science, berween Science et Vie and Psychologie), one can study the information the survey provides on favourire singers. Ir might be thought that the fact that, at all levels of educational capiral, the youngest respondents choose che singers of the younger generation (Françoise Hardy or Johnny Hallyday) more often than the older respondents, who more often choose older sirigers (Guétary or Mariano), is adequately explained by the dates of the singers' frsr appearance in the feld of cultural producrion. In facr, among baccalauréar-holders, the youngest more often choose Jacques Douai (who was born in 1920and performed at the Vieux Colombier in 1963), Jaccques Brel (who was born in 1929, made his Paris debut in 1953 at the Théâtre des Trois Baudets and perfotmedat the Paris Olympia in 1958 and 1961) or even Léo Ferré (born 1916, degrees in Arts and Political Science, debur in Paris cabarers 1946), whereas the older ones more often choose Edith Piaf (born 1915, died 1963, debut at the AC in 1937), Luis Mariano (born 1920, lirst success at Casino Montparnasse, 1945), Gilbert Bécaud (born 1927, first became known in righr.bank cabarets and then at Olympia; consecrated in 1954, the Sécaud year') or even Petula Clark (born 1933, top of the bill at Olympia 1960, voted 'most likeable and popular star' in 1963)."º It can be seen chat o make sense of chese telarionships one has to rake int account nt only the singers' ages or che dares when they made their breakthroughs school system.

r dif erences bera'een educational generations within the of the class. The younger differ from the older not so vestments. like their elders they read scientific and technical works, but they are slightly more interested in philosophical essays or poetry. They go no more frequently to museums, but when they do, they go more often to the Modern Art Museum. These rendencies are particularly pronounced amongst those of them (relatively more numerous than among the older ones) who originare from the middle or upper classes and who know a (telarively) very high number of musical works and composers, are in. terested in modern art and philosophy and often go to the cinema. But what perhaps most distinguish the two generations of rechnicians are the external signs-dress and hairstyle, in particular—and also their declared preferences; the younget ones, who seek to draw close to the srudent style, say they follow fashion and like clothes which 'suir their personality', whereas the older ones more often choose sober and correct' or classically cut' clothes (choices characteristic of established petit bourgeois).

The old-style autodidact was fundamentally defined by a reverence for culrure which was induced by abrupt and early exclusion, and which led to an exalted, misplaced piery, inevirably perceived by the possessors of legitimate culture as a sort of grotesque homage.

The recognition of incompetence and cultural unworrhiness which charac. terizes old-sryle aurodidacticism is especially seen among members of the established petite bourgeoisie originating from the working or middle classes, who say very frequently (7 percent of them, compared with 31 percent of the new petite bourgeoisie originating from the same classes) thar 'paintings are nice bur difhicult'. The clearest manifestation of the cultural alienation of old-style autodidacts is their readiness ro offer proof of rheir culture even when it is not asked for, betraying cheir exclusion by their eagerness to prove their membership (in contrast to the well-born, who mask their ignorance by ignoring questions or situations which might expose it).

In these outsiders, who seek to use a deeply orthodox self-teaching as a way of continuin ga brutally foreshortened ttajecrory by their own initiative, the whole relation ro culture and cultural authorities bears the stamp of exclusion by a system that can get the excluded to recognize their exclusion. By contrast, new-style autodidacts have often kept a place in the educational system up to a relatively high level and in the course of this long, ill-rewarded association have acquired a relation to legitimate culture that is at once 'liberated' and disabused, familiar and disenchanted. It has nothing in common wich the distant reverence of the old-style autodidact, although it leads to equally intense and passionate investments, but in quite different areas, disclaimed or abandoned by the educational system--strip cartoons or jazz rarher than history or astronomy, psychology (even parapsychology) or ecology rather than archaeology or geology." These are the categories which provide the audience for all the productions of the counter-culture' (Charlie Hebdo, L'Echo des Sa

valeur). The holders of the monopoly of manipulation of the sacred, the literati stades frighten the mystic and bring him back to the old accustomed ventive censorship by the institution can take place withour anyone having t apply controlsor constraints. Whereas traditional autodidacts sril expect che academic institution to indicate and open the short cuts of popularization and the vulgate, which are always, directly or indirectly, dominared by the institution," the most liberated of the new autodidacts seek their gurus among the heresiarchs who still perform the function traditionally fulflled by the aurhorities, namely, as Scholem also says, that of 'showing exactly what the novice has to expect ar every step' and 'providing the symbols with which this experience can be described or interpreted.'

THE wO MARKETs The family and the school function as sites in which the competences deemed necessary at a given time are constituted by usage itself, and, simultancously, as sites in which the price of those competences is determined, i.e., as markers which, by their positive or negative sancrions, evaluate performance, reinforcing what is acceprable, discouraging whar is not, condemning valueless dispositions to extinction (jokes which 'fall fat' or, though acceptable in another context, in another market, here seem out of place' and only provoke embarrass. mert or disapproval. quotations—in Larin, for example—which sound 'pedantic' or 'laboured'). In other words, the acquisition of culrural competence is inseparable from insensible acquisition of a sense' for sound culcural investment.

This investment sense, being the product of adjustment to the objecrive chances of turning comperence to good account, facilitates forward adjustment to these chances, and is irself a dimension of a relation to -1

way suggested char the corresponding behaviour is guided by rational calculation of maximum proht, as the ordinary usage of rhese concepts, no doubr mistakenly, implies. Culture is the site, par excellence, of misrecognition, because, in generating strategies objectively adapted to the objective chances of profit of which it is the product, the sense of in. vestment secures profits which do not need to be pursued as profts; and so it brings to chose who have legitimare culcure as a second nature the supplementary profit of being seen (and secing chemselves) as perfecrly disinterested, unblemished by any cynical or mercenary use of culrure. I'his means thar the term 'investment', for example, must be undersrood in the dual sense of economic investment—which it objectively al ways is, though misrecognised—and che sense of affecrive inves tment which it has in psychoanalysis, or, more exactly, in the sense of illusio, belief, an involvement in the game which produces the game. The art-lover knows no other guide than his love of art, and when he moves, as if by instinct, towards whar is, ar each moment, the thing to be loved,likesome businessmen who make money even when they are nor trying to, he is not pursuing a cynical calculation, but his own pleasure, the sincere enthusiasm which, in such matters, is one of the preconditions of successful investment.

So, for example, it is true that the effect of the hierarchies of legitimacy (rhe hierarchyof the arts, of genses etc.) can bedescribed asa particular case of the 'labelling’ effect well known to social psychologists. Just as people see a face differently depending on the ethnic label it is given,8ºso the value of the arcs, genres, works and authors depends on the social marks artached to them at any given moment (e-g., place of publicarion). But the fact remains rhat the arc-lover's sense ofcultural investment which leads him always to love what is lovable, and only that, and always sincerely, can be supported by unconscious deciphering of the countiess signs which at every moment say whar is to be loved and what is not, what is or is not to be seen, without ever being explicitly oriented by puusuit of the associated symbolic profrs. The specific competence (in classical music or jazz, theatre or 6lm erc.) depends on the chances which the different markets, domesric, scholastic or occupational, together offer for accumulating, applying and exploiring it, i.e., the degree to which they encourage acquisition of this competence by promising or guaranreeing it profits which will reinforce it and induce new investments. The chances of using cultural comperence prohcably in the different markets play a part, in particular, in defning the propensity to make 'scholastic' investments and also the investments in extra-curricular general culture' which seem to owe nothing to the constraints or incentives ofthe institution.

The more legitimare a given area, the more necessary and profitable'it is to be competent in it, and the more damaging and 'costly' to competent." But this does not suffice to explain why it is that, as one differences linked to social trajectoty (and capital composition), with the tography or jazz, occupying an intermediate position. Here too, ir is in the relarionship between the properties of the held (in particulat, the chances of negarive or positive sancrions it offers 'on average', for any agent) and che properties of the agent, that the 'eficacy' of these properries is defined. Thus both the propensity towards non-academic' investments and che area to which chey are directed depend, stricrly speaking. not on the average' rare of profit offered by the area in question but on the rate of proft it offers each agent or particular category of agents in terms of the volume and composition of their capital.

The hierarchy of 'average' rares of profit broadly corresponds to the hierarchy of degrees of legitimacy, so that knowledge of classicai or even avant-garde literature yields higher ‘average' profics, in rhe scholastic market and elsewhere, than knowledge of cinema, or, a fortiori, srrip cartoons, detective stories or sport. But the speifc profits, and the conse. quent propensitics to invest, are only defined in che relationship berween a held and a particular agent with particular characteristics. For example, those who owe most of their culrural capiral to the educational system, such as primary and secondary teachers originating from the working and middle classes, are particularly subject ro the academic definition of legitimacy, and cend to proportion their investments very srictly to the value the educational system sets on the different areas.

By contrast, 'middle-ground' arts such as cinema, jazz, and, even more, strip cattoons, science fiction or detective stories are predisposed to attract the investments either of those who have entirely succeeded in converting their culrural capiral into educational capital or those who, not having acquired legitimate culruse in the legirimate manner (i.e., chrough early familiatization), mairitain an uneasy relarionship wich it, subjectively or objectively, or both. These arts, not yer fully legitimate, which are disdained or neglected by the big holders of educational capital ofer a efuge and a revenge to those who, by appropriaring them, sepossessed consists solely of he caital acquired in nd recognized by the educational system. (Thus, members of the new petite bourgeoisie hav generally inherited more cultural capital than the primary teachers tors but fewer composers.)

In fact, one can never entirely escape from the hierarchy of legitima cies. Because the very meaning and value of a cultural object varies ac. cording to the sysrem of objects in which it is placed, derective stories. science fiction or strip cartoons may be entirely prestigious cultural assets or be reduced to their ordinary value, depending on whether they are as sociated with avant-garde literature or music-in which case they appear as manifesrations of daring and freedom---r combine to form a constel. lation typical of middle-brow raste—when they appear as what they are, simple substitures for legitimate assets.

Given that each social spacefamily or school, for example-functions both as one of the sites where competence is produced and as one of the sites where it is given its price, one might expect each held to set the highest price on the products created within it. Thus one might expect the scholastic field to give the highest value to scholasticallycertified cul. tural capital and the scholastic modality, wherea:s themarkets dominated by extra-scholastic valuessociety'salons and dinners, or all the occasions of professional life (appointment interviews, board meetings, conferences etc.) or even academic life (oral examinations at ENA or Sciences Po, for example), in which the whole person is evaluatedwould set the highest value on the familiar relacion to culrure, devaluing all the dispositions and competences which bear the markof scholastic acquisirion. But this would be to ignore the effects of domination whereby the products of the scholastic mode of production may be devalued as 'scholastic' in the scholastic market itself.® Indeed, the clearest sign of the heteronomy of the scholastic marker is seen in its ambivalent treatment of the products of the 'scholastic' habits, which varies inversely with the auitonomy of the educational system as a whole (variable at diff erent times and in diff erent countries) and of its constiruent institutions, with respect to the demands of the dominant fraction of the dominant class.83

What is certain is that there exists an immediate afhnity between the dispositions that are acquired by farniliarization with legitimate culture and the 'high-society' market (or the most 'high-society' secrors of the educational market). The ordinary occasions of social life exclude tests as brutal as a closed questionnaire, the limiting case of the scholastic exan nation which the scholastic institution itself refuses whenever, implicitly accepting the high-sociery depreciation of the 'scholastic', it turns an amination intended to verify and measure competence into a variant of high-society conversation. In contrast to the most 'scholastic' of scholasric situations, which aim to disarm and discourage strategies of bl high-society occasions give unlimited scope to an art of playing wit taled by most social situations, even in the quasi-scholastic siruation of a survey.

In asking questions abour painters in such a way that the knowledge claimed could not be verified im any way, the aim was not o much to measure the specihc comperence (which, one may assume, depends on the same factorsas knowledge of composers) as to grasp indirectly the relationship to legitimate culture and the differential effects of the survey situation. Thus, respondents who e knowledge was not equal to their familiarity may have feli entitled to use strategies of bluff which are highly successful in the ordinary u es of culrure (this is particularly the case with the new petite bourgeoisie). But bluft irself is only proftable if it is guided by the vague knowledge given by familiarity. Thus,while the room for manoeuvre in rhis guestion allowed the least comperent to fasten on proper names which corre pond neirher co knowledge nor preference, such as Picasso (mentioned by 21 percent of the unskilled and emi-skilled workers) or Braque (10 percent), who was being celebrated in various ways ar the time of the survey, it also functioned a a trap with Rousseau (10 percent), who was practically never mentioned by the other classe and was probably confused with che writer. (Breughel, by contrast, was never menrioned by the un. skilled and semi-skilled, no doubt because they would not risk pronouncing a name rhey were not likely to have heard.)

To bring to light rhis society sense', generally associated with strong in  
herited cultural capital bur irreducible to a sum of strictly verihable knowl  
edge, one only has to compare the variations in two dimensions of cultural

knowledge and confused intuitions of familiarity. For examproximate tvely low educational capital. The Parisian or even provincial primary reacher, who can beat the small employer, the provincial doctor or the Parisian antique-dealer in the tests of pure knowledge, is likely to appear incomparably inferior to them in all the situations which demand selfassurance or flair, or even the bluff which can cover lacunae, rather than the prudence, discretion and awareness of limirs that are associated with scholastic acquisition. One can confuse Bernard Buffet with Jean Dubuf. fet and yet be quite capable of hiding one's ignorance under the commonplaces of celebration or the knowing silence of a pout, a nod or an inspired pose; one can identify philosophy with Saint-Exupéry, Teilhatd de Chardin or even Ieprince-Ringuet, and still hold one's own in today's most prestigious market-placesreceprions, conferences, interviews, de bates, seminars, committees, commissions so long as one possesses the serof distinctive features, bearing, posture, presence, diction and pronunciation, manners and usages, without which, in these markets at least, all scholastic knowledge is worth litcle or nothing and which, partly because schools never, or never fully, teach them, define the essence of bourgeois distinction.

Educationally equivalent individuals (e.g., the students of the grandes écoles) may dif er radically as regards bodily hexis, pronunciation, dress or familiarity with legitimate culture, not to mention the whole set of specific compe. tences and capacities which function as admission tickets to the bourgeois world, such as dancing, the rare sports, or pariour games (especially bridge).

fACTORS AND POWERS It is nOwclear that the difficulty of the analysis was due to the fact that what the very tools of analysis—educational level and social origin—designate is bcing fought out in struggles which have rhe object of analysis—art and the relarion to the work of art—as their prize in reality itself. These struggles are fought berween chose who are identified with the scholastic definition of culture and the scholastic mode of acquisition, and those who defend a 'non-institutional' culture and relation to culture. Batties over authors and schools, which hold the limelight of the literary or artistic srage, conc al more important struggles, such as chose which op pose teachers (from whose ranks, throughout rhe nineteenth centur. critics were often recruited) and wtiters, who tend to be more closcly linked, by origin and connections', to the dominant fractions of the dominant class, or the endess truggles erween the dominaed fractions plished man and che educarion designed to produce him.

To put 'education' before insttuction', 'character' before 'intel. ligehce', sport before culture is to assert, withm the scholastic world icself, the existence of a hierarchy irreducible to the specifically academic hierarchy which privileges che second term in each of these oppositions.8

These struggles are not confned to the past, as is shown by the exisrence of cwo roures ro the senior positions in large firms, one leading from the Ecote des Roches or the major Jesuit colleges and grear bourgeois lycées (in the 16th arrondissement) to the Law Faculty or, increas ingly. to Sciences Po or HEC, the other running from the ordinary provincial or Parisian lycée to the Ecole Polytechnique.® It is still more clearly s en in the opposition, at the level of the grandes écoles, between two academic markets differing profoundly in the content of che culrural competence demanded, in the value set on manners and the criteria used to evaluate them, with ar one extreme rhe Ecole Normale Supérieure (ENS} and Polytechnique and at the ocher Sciences Po and the Ecole Narionale d'Administrarion (ENA). These struggles over the legitimare dehnition of culture and the legitimare way of evaluating it are only one dimension of the endless struggles which divide every dominant class. Behind the virtues of the accomplished man the legirimate titles to che exercise of domination are ar stake. Thus the glorification of character. building' sPort and the valorization of economic and political culture, at Position berween the mal and the female, the serious and rile, the realistic and the unrealistic.

therefore be cancelled our or inverted in another feld or another state of the same field. The dispositions constiruting the cultivated habitus are only formed, only function and are only valid in a field, in the relationship with a field which, as Gaston Bachelard says of the physical field, is irself a 'feld of possible forces', a 'dynamic situation', in which forces are only manifested in their relationship with cerrain dispositions. This is why rhe same pracrices may receive opposite meanings and values in different felds, in diferent configurations or in opposing sectors of the same field.

So reflective analysis of the tools of analysis is not an epistemological scruple but an indispensable pre-condition of scientificknowledge of the object. Positivisr laziness leads the whole, purely defettsive, effort of verifcarion to be focussed on the intensity of the relationships found, instead of bringing questioning to bear on the very conditions of measurement of the relationships, which may even explain the relarive intensity of the diff erent relationships. In order to believe in rhe independence of the 'independent variables'of positivist merhodology, one has to be unaware that 'explanatory factors' are also powerswhich are only valid and operative in a certain feld, and that they rherefore depend on the struggles which are fought, within each field, to transform the price-forming mechanisms which defne it. If it is easy to imagine helds in which the weight of the two dominant 'facrors' would be inverted (and cests which would be the experimental expression of this, giving greater prominence, for example, to less 'scholastic' objects and forms of questioning), this is because what is ultimarely ar srake in everyday struggles over culture is the rransformation of the price-forming mechanisms defining the relative values of the culural production associated with educational apital and social trajectory (and rhe primary variables through which they are grasped).

market, with its own consecraring agencies, that is, like the high-society or intellectual markers, capable of challenging the pretension of the edu. carional system to impose the principles of evaluation of competences and manners which reign in the scholastic marker, or ar least its most 'scholastic' sectors, on a perfecrly unified market in cultural goods.

But on things whose rules and principles had been instilled into her by her mother, on the way to make certain dishes, to play Beethoven's sonatas, to 'receive' with cordiality, she was quite sure that she had a right idea of perfection and of discerning how far others approximated to it. For rhese three things, moreover, perfecrion was almost the same, a kind of simpliciry in the means, a sobriety and a charm. She repudiated with horror the introduction of spices in dishes that did not absolutely require them, affecration and abuse of the pedals in piano. playing, departure from perfect naturalness, and exag. gerated ralking of oneself in 'receiving.' From the first mouthful, from the hrst notes, from a simple letter she preened herself on knowing if she had ro deal with a good cook, a real musician, a woman properlly brought up. She may have many more fingers than I, but she lacks taste, playing that very simple Ana'anse with so much emphasis.' No doubr a most brilliant woman ful of parts, but it is a want of tact to speak of oneself in such a case.' 'Possibly a very knowing cook, but she does not know how to do steak and fried potatoes' Steak and fried potatoes, an ideal competition-piece, a kind of culinary Patheric Sonasa, a gastronomic equivalent to what is in social life the visit of a lady who comes for a servant's 'characrer' and who, in an acr as simple as that, can suffciently display the presence or absence of tact and education.

The Social Space and Its Transformations

Ifrhe rcsearch had sropped ar this point it would probably not raise grear objections, so self-evident is the idea of the irreducibility of artistic tasre. However, as has already been shown by the analysis of the social conditions of the aestheric disposition, the dispositions which govern choices beiwcen the goods of legitimare culture cannot be fully understood unless they are reintegrated into the system of dispositions, unless 'culture', in the restricted, normative sense of ordinary usage, is reinserted into culture' in the broad, anthropological sense and the elaborated taste for the most refned objects is brought back into relation with the elementary casre for the favours of food.' The duał meaning of the word 'raste', which usually serves to justify the illusion of sponraneous generation which this cultivated disposition tends ro produce by presenting irself in the guise of an innate disposition, must serve, for once, to remind us that taste in the sense of the 'faculry of immediately and intuitively judging aesdictic values' is inseparable from taste in the sense of the capacity to discern the flavours of foods which implies a preference for some of them The abstraction which isolates dispositions towards legitimate cul.

consun tion of the most legitimate culrural goods is a particular case of comp for rare goods and practices, whose particularity no move rhe magical barrier which makes legitimare culrure into a sepa consumprion into the world of ordinary consumption (against which it endlessly defines irself) has, inter alia, rhe virtue of reminding us that the consumption of goods no doubr always presupposes a labour of appr priation, to diff erent degrees depending on the goods and the consum or, more precisely, that rhe consumer helps to produce the product he consumes, by a labour of identifcation and decoding which, in the case of a work of art, may constiture the whole of the consumprion andgratifcation, and which requires time and dispositions acquired over

Economists, who never jib at an absrracrion, can ignore what happens to products in the relationship with the consumers, that is, with the dis positions which define their useful properties and real uses. To hypothe size, as one of them does, thar consumers perceive the same decisive attribures, which amounts to as uming thar products possessobjective or, as they are known, technical' characteristics which can impress themselves as such on all perceiving subjects, is to proceed as if perception only seized on the characreristics de ignated by the manufacturers' brochures (and so-called informarive' publicity) and as if social uses could be derived from the operating instructions. Objects, even industrial prod ucts, are not objective in the ordinary sense of the word, i.e., independent of the incerest and tastes of those who perceive them, and they do not impose the self-evidence of a universal, unanimously approved meaning. The sociologist's rask would be much easier if, when faced with each relarionship berween an independent variable' and a 'dependent variable', he did not have to derermine how the perceprion and appreciarion of what is designared by the dependent variable' vary according to the classes determined by the 'independent variable', or, in other words, identify the system of perrinent features on the basis of which each of the classes of agents was really determined.² Whar science has to establish is the objectivity of the object which is esrablished in the relationship berween an object defined by the possibilities and impossibilities it offers, which are only revealed in the world of social use (inctuding, in the case of a technical object, the use or function for which ir was designed) and the dispositions of an agent or clas of agents, that is, the schemes of perception, appreciation and acrion which constitute its objective utility in a practical usage. T'he aim is not, of course, to reintroduce any form of what is called lived experience', which is most often merely a thinly disguised projection of the researcher's 'lived experience';4 but to move beyond che abstract relarionship berween consumers with interchangeable tastes and products with unif ormly perceived and appreciated properties to the rela tionship berween rastes which vaty in a necessaty way according to cheir between practices and a situation, the meaning of which is produced by the habirus through categories of perception and appreciation that are themselves produced by an observable social condirion.

## Class Condition and Social Conditioning

Because it can only account for practices by bringing to light successively the series of effects which underlie chem, analysis initially conceals the structure of the life-sryle characteristic of an agent or class of agents, char is, the unicy hidden under che diversicy and multipliciry of the ser of practices performed in fields governed by different logics and therefore inducing different forms of realizarion, in accordance with the formula: [(habitus) (capital) ] + field = practice. It also conccals the structure of the symbolic space marked out by the whole set of these strucrured practices, all the distinct and distinctive life-sryles which are always defined objectively and sometimes subjectively in and chrough theit murual relationships. So it is necessary to reconstruct whar ha been raken apart, frst by way of verification but also in order to rediscover the kerne] of truth in the approach characreristic of common-sense knowledge, namely, the intuition of the systematic nature of life-styles and of the whole set which they constitute. To do this, one must return to the pra.ctice-unifyimng and praclice-generating principle, i.e., class habitus, the internalized form of class condirion and of the conditionings it enrails. One must therefore consttuct che objedtive clas, the set of agents who are placed in homogeneous conditions of existence imposing homogeneous conditionings and producing homogencous systems of dispositions capable of generating tems of classificatory schemes).

s of condirions of existence) by the name of an occupation, one is structed in a particular respect (rhat is, in a particularly determinane re specr) always bring with them,in addition to the pertinent propertiesby which rhey are classifed, sccondary properries which are thus smuggled into the explanatory model.? This means that a class or class fraction is defined not only by its position in the relations of production, as identi. hed chrough indices such as occuparion, income or even educational level, but also by a certain sex-ratio, a certain distribution in geographical space (which is never socially neurral) and by a whole ser of subsidiary chara.cterisrics which may funcrion, in che form of racit requirements, as real principles of selection or exclusion withour ever being formally stated (this is the case with ethnic origin and sex). A number of official criteria in facr seive as a mask for hidden criceria: for example, the requir ing of a given diploma can be a way of demanding a parricular social origin.

One needs to examine whar the list of the critcria used by the analyst derives from the srare of che struggle between the groups separated by these criteria, or more precisely from thecapacity of groups defined by these criteria, to ger chemselves recognized as such. There would be less likelihood offorgettingthat unskilled workers are to a large extent women and immigrants if groups based on sex or nationality of origin had constitured themselves as such within the working class. Furthermore, the fallacy of the apparent facror would nor be so frequent if it were not the simple retransla rion onto the terrain of science of the legitimating strategies whereby groups tend to pur forward this or that legirimate property, the overt principle of their constitutiou, to camouflage che real basis of their existence. Thus the most selective groups (a concerr audience or che students of a grande école) may doubty conceal the real principle of their selection: by declining to announce the real principles of cheir existence and their repro ducrion, they are obliged to rely on mechanisms which lack the specific, sys tematic rigouc of an explicit condition of entry and cherefore allow exceptions (unlike clubs and all 'elires' based on co-option, they cannot vet che whole set of properties of rhe 'elect', i.e., che roral person).

The members of groups based on co-option, as are most of the corps pro cected by an overt or covert numeras clausus (docrors, archite:ces, professors, engin eers etc.) always have something cise in common beyond the characreristics explicitly demanded. The common image of che professions, which is no doubt one of the real dererminants of 'vocations', is less absrract and unreal than that presented by staristicians; it takes into account not only che narure of the job and che income, bur those secondaiy characteristics which are often the basis of their social value (prestige or discredit) and

Simlarly. when on is tring t assess the cvolution of a social caregory idntfid by ccuparion), crude erors a inevirable if, by considering only one of che pernnt properties, one ignoes all the substitutio efects in which the evolurion is also expressed. The collecrive rajectory of a social class may be manifested in che fact that it is becoming feminized' or 'masculinı zed' growing older or young, getting poorer or richer. (The decline ofa position may be manifested eirher in femmization'—which may be accompanied by a rise in social origm-or in 'democrarization' or in agemg'.) The same would be true of any group defned by reference to a position in 2 field-e.g., a university discipline in the hierarchy of disciplines, a title of nobility in the aristocratic hierarchy, an educational qualification in the academic hierarchy.

The parricular relations between a dependent variable (such as polirical opinion) and so-called independent vatiables such as sex, age and religion, or even educational level, income and occupation cend to mask the complete system of relationships which constirutes the true principlc of the specific strength and form of the effects registered in any particular correlation. The most independent of independent' variables conceals a whole network of sratistical relarions which are present, implicitly, in its rclationship with any given opinion or practice. Here roo, instead of asking statistical technology to solve a problem which it can only displace, it is nccessary to analyse rhe divisions and variations which the diff erent secondary variables (sex, age etc.} bring inro the class defned by the main variable, and consider everyching which, though present in the real def. nition of rhe class, is nor consciously caken into account in che nominal definition, the one summed up in the name used to designare it, or 'herefore in interprering the relationship in which it is placed.
