Thus one would have to examine in cach case to what extent occupational conditions of ex istence assist or hinder this efect, which would mean raking into account the characterisrics of thie work (unpleasantness etc.), the conditions in which it is performed—noise, or silence permirting conversarion etc-the remporal rhythms it imposes, the spare rime it ailows, and especially the form of the horizonral or vertical relations it encourages at the workplace during work or in rest periods—or outside.

This erect no doubr explains a number of dif erences between ofice workers (ledger clerks, bank clerks, agency clerks. typists) and commercial employees (mainly shop assistants), which are not entirely accounted for either by differences hnked to class fraction of origin (ofce workers are cather more ofcen the children of farmers; commercial employees the children of small employers) or by difterences in educational capiral (rhe first more ofren have the BEPC, the second a CAP).

The commercial employees and the ofhice workers, who are distributed in much the same way as regards sex, age and income, are separated by important dif erences in dispositions and practices. Ofhce workers are more as cericthey more often expect their friends to be conscientious or well brought up, more often prefer a neat, clean and tidy interior and like Brel, Guétary, Mariano, the Hungarian Rhapsody, L'Arlésienne, Raphael, Watteau and leonardo. Urrillo or Van Gogh.

Among the efecrs which the relationslip berween class fracrion and prac tices simulraneously reveals and conceals, there is also the effecr of che position in the distribution of the secondary properties attached to a class. Thus, members of the class who do not possess al the modal properties-- e.g., men in a strongly feminized occuparion or a worker's son ar ENA have their social identity deeply marked by thls membership and the social image which it imposes and which they have to situare themselves in relaton ro, wherher by acceprance or rejection.

Sirnilarly, relarionships such as chose berween ducarional capital, or age, and income mask the relationship linking the rwo apparently independent variables. Age determines income ro an exrent which varies according co educational capital and occupation, which is itself pardly determined by edu cational capital and also by other, more hidden factors sucl as sex and inherited culrural or social capital. ducational capital, thar is, the choice' of the type of educational capital giceto age may conceal a relationship to educational capital when age is in fact the key to different modes of access o the position-by qualification or internal promotionand different school generations and diffeent chances of acess to the educarional system (the oldest agents have lower educational capital than the youngest), or to social class, by virtue of the different social defnitions of precociousness or backwardness in the various areas, parcicularly in schooling.

In fact, the change in chances of access is only one aspect of a more sysrematic change which also involves che very definition of competence, and tends to make compar isons between the generations increasingly difhculs. The conflicts berween holders of comperences of differentages and different educational levels—old school-cercihcate holder versus new bachelier (baccalaurear-holder)centre precisely on the definition of comperence, with the old gencration complaining thar the new generation does not possess the competences formerly defined as elemenrary and basic: 'They can't spell nowadays', 'They can't even add up'.

When, as often happens, the analysis is conducted variable by variable, r of attriburing to one of the variables (such as sex ot age,

The naivety of the inclination to attribure the differences recorded in re lation to age to a generic effect of biological ageing becomes self-evident when one sees, for example, that the ageing which, in the privileged classes, s associated with a move to the tight, is accompanied, among manual workers, by a move to the left. Similarly, in the relative precocity of executives, measured for example by the age at which they reach a given position, one sces in fact the expresseion of everything which di. vides them, despite the apparent identity of condition at a given mo ment, namely their whole previous and subsequent ttrajectoty, and the capital volume and structure which govern it.

CONSTRUCTE CLASS Social class is not dehned by a property (noteven the most determinant one, such as the volume and composition of capital) nor by a collection of properties (of sex, age, social origin, ethnic originproportion of blacks and whites, for example, or natives and immigrants-income, educational level etc.), nor even by a chain of properties strung out from a fundamental property (position in the relations of production) in a relation of cause and effect, conditioner and conditioned; but by the structure of relations between all the pertinent properties which gives its specific value to each of them and to the effects they exert on practices.\* Constructing, as we have here, classes as homogeneous as possible with respect to the fundamentał determinants of the material condirions of exisrence and the conditionings they impose, therefore means that even in constructing the classes and in interpreting the variarions of the distribution of properties and practices in relation to these classes, one consciously takes into account the network of secondary characteristics which are more or less unconsciously manipulated whenever the classes are defined in terms ofa single criterion, even one as pertinent as occupation. It also means grasping the principle of the ob jective divisions, i.e., divisions internalized or objectifed in distinctive properties, on the basis of which the agents are mosr likely to divide and come together in realiry in their ordinary practices, and also to mobilize themselves or be mobilized (in accordance with the specific logic, linked to a specific history, of the mobilizing organizations) by and for individual or collective political action.

tualy mobilized groups) vary greatly in their functional weights and chere. fore in cheir structuring force, these principles of division are themselves set in a hierarchy; groups mobilized on the basis of a secondary criterion (such as sex or age) are likely to be bound togerher less permanently and less deeply than those mobilized on the basis of the fundamental determinants of their condition.

To account for the infinite diversity of practices in a way that is both unitary and specific, one has to break with linear thinking, which only recognizes the simple ordinal structures of direct determination, and endea. vour to reconstruct the networks of interrelated relationships which are presenr in each of the facrors.’ The srructutal causality of a network of factors is quite irreducible to the cumulated effects of the set of linear re larions, of diff erent explanatory force, which the necessities of analysis oblige one to isolate, those which are established berween the different factors, taken one by one, and the practice in question; through each of the factors is exerted the effcacy of all the others, and the mulriplicity of determinations leads not to indererminacy but to over-determination. Thus the superimposition of biological, psychological and social determinations in the formation of socially defined sexual identity (a basic dimension of social personality) is only a particular, bur very important, case of a logic that is also at work in other biological determinations, such as ageing

It goes without saying that the factors constituting the constructed   
class do not all depend on one another to the same extent, and that the   
strucrure of the system they constitute is determined by those which   
other factors (age, sex, place of residence erc.) impose on practices. Sexpropert as inseparable from class properties as the yellowness of t gives to the two sexes and to their socially constitured This is why there are as many ways of realizing femininity as

clude a largeand growing—proportion of immigrants or women skilled and semi-skilled workers) or immigrant women (charwomen). Similarly, it is no accident thar the occupations in personal services-the medical and social services, the personal-care trades, old ones like hair. dressing, new ones like beauty care, and especially domestic service which combine the two aspects of the traditional definition of female tasks, service and the home—are practically reserved for women.

Nor is it accidental that the oldest classes or class fractions are also the classes in decline, such as farmers and industrial and commercial propriecors; most of the young people originating from these classes can only escape collective decline by reconverting into the expanding occupations. Similarly, an increase in the proportion of women indicates the whole trend of an occupation, in particular the absolute or relarive devaluation which may result from changes in the nature and organization of the work itself (this is the case with office jobs, for example, with the mulriplication of repetitive, mechanical casks that are commonly left to women) or from changes in relative position in social space (as in teaching, whose position has been affected by the overall displacement of the profession resulting from the overall increase in the niulr ber of positions offered).

One would have to analyse in the same way the relationship between marical status and class or class fracrion. it has been clearly shown, for example, that male celibacy is not asecondary property of the small peasantry but an essential element of the crisis affecting this fraction of the peasant class. The breakdown of the mechanisms of biological and social reproduction brought about by the specific logic of symbolic domination is one of the mediations of the process of concentration which leadsto a deep transformation of the class. But here too, one would have to subject the commonsense norion to close analysis, as has been done for educational level. Being married is nor opposed to being unmarried simply as the fact of having a legitimare spouse to the fact of not having one. One only has to think of a few limiting cases (some much more frequent than others), the 'housewife', the artist supported by his wife, the employer or executive who owes his position to his father-in-law, to see that it is dif hcult to characterize an individual without including all the propertics (and property) which are brought to each of the spouses, and not only the wife, through the other-a name (sometimes a distinguished 'de' as well), goods, an income, connections', a social status (each member of the couple being characterized by the spouse's social position, to different like the choice of a spouse for son or daughter in other societies the outcome of these (denied) power relarions which defne the domestic unit. For example, there is every reason to suppose that, given the logic of the division of labour berween the sexes, which gives precedence to women in matrers of raste (and to men in politics), the weight of the man's own taste in choosing his clothes (and therefore the degree to which his clothes express his rasre) depends nor only on his own in. hertted cultural capital and educarional capital (the traditional division of roles tends to weaken, here and elsewhere, as educational capital grows) but also on his wife's educational and cultural capital and on the gap between them. (The same is true of the weight of the wife's own preferences in politics: the effect of assignment by status which makes politics a man's business is less likely to occur, the greater the wife's educarional capital, or when the gap berween her capital and her husband's is small or in her favour.)

SOCIAL CLASS AND CLASS OF TRAJECTORIES But this is nOr alL. On the one hand, agents are not completely defned by the properties they pos sess ar a given time, whose conditions of acquisition persist in the hab. tus (che hysteresis effect); and on the other hand, the relationship between initial capital and present capital, or, to put it another way, be ween the initial and present positions in social space, is a staristical relahonship of very variable intensity. Alchough they are always perpetuared in the dispositions constiruring che habitus, che conditions of acquisition ditions of use,1 i.e., when the pracrices generated by the habitus appear staristical analysi is what might be called the Don Quixote effect). The which compares the practices of agents possessing the naty perceprion which, wirhin a group, idenrifies the parvenus and the berray the effect of condirions of existence diff erent from the present o the modal crajectory for che group in quesrion.

partly because the resist rhe forces of che field with rhir specific netia, chat is, their properties, which may exist in embodied form, as dispos tions, or in objectihed form, in goods, qualificarions ete. To a given vol. ume of inherired capital there corresponds a band of more or less equally probable trajectories leading to more or less equivalent positions (this is the feld of the possibles objectively offered to a given agent), and the shift from one rajectory to another often depends on collecrive events-wars, crises erc.--or individual events—encounters, affairs, benefactors etc.- which are usually described as (fortunate or unforrunare) accidents, al. though they chemselves depend sraristically on the posirion and disposicion of chose whom they befall (e.g., the skill in operating 'connections' which enables the holders of high social capital to preserve or increase rhis capital), when, thar is, they are not deliberarely contrived by institutions (clubs, family reunions, old-boys' or alumni associations erc.) or by the 'spontaneous' intervention of individuals or groups. 1t follows from this that position and individual trajectory are not statistically independent; all positions of arrival are not equally probable for all starting points. This implies that there is a strong correlation between social posirions and the dispositions of the agents who occupy them, or, which amounts to the same thing, the trajecrories which have led them to occupy them, and consequently that the modal trajectory is an integral part of the system of factors consrituting the class. (The more dispersed the trajectories are—as in the petite bourgeoisie—the less are practices reducible to the effect of synchronically defned position.)

The homogeneity of the dispositions associared with a position and their seemingly miraculous adjustment to the demands inscribed in it re sult partly from the mechanisms which channel towards positions individuals who are already adjusted to chem, eicher because they feel made for jobs thar are 'made'for them—this is 'vocation', the proleptic as sumprion of an objective destiny thar is imposed by practical reference to the modal rajectoty in the class of origin-or because they are seen in this light by the occupants of che poststhis is co-option based on the immediate harmony of dispositions—and partly from che dialectic which is established, throughout a liferime, berween dispositions and positions aspirations and achievements. Social ageing is nothing other chan slow renunciation or disinvestment (socially assisted and encourage which leads agents to adjust rheir aspirations to their objective chances to espouse their condition, become what chey are and make do with what

even if this entails deceiving themselves as to whar they are they have, etermined a priori within che limits of this explanatory system) will de viare from the trajecrory most common for the class as a whole and fol. tow the (higher or lower) trajectory which was most probable for members of another class." The trajectory effect which then manif ests itself, as it does whenever individuals occupying similar positions at a given time are separared by differences associated with the evolurion over ume of the volume and strucrure of their capital, i.e., by their individual trajectories, is very likely to be wrongly interprered. The correlation between a practice and social origin (measured by the father's position, the real value of which may have suffered a decline conccaled by constant nominal value) is the resulrant of two effects (which may either reinforce or offser each orher): on the one hand, the inculcation effect directly exerted by the family or the original conditions of existence; on the other hand, the specific effect of social trajectory," that is, the effecs of social rise or decline on dispositions and opinions, posirion of origin being, in chis logic, merely the starting point of a trajectory, the reference whereby the slope of the social career is defined. The need to make this distinction is self-evident in alì cases in which individuals from the same class fraclion or the same family, and therefore presumably subject to identical moral, religiou.s or political inculcations, are inclined towards divergent stances in teligion or politics by the different relarions to the social world which they owe to divergent individual trajectories. having, for example, succeeded or failed in the reconversion strategies necessary to escape the coltecrive decline of cheir class.

This crajectory effect no doubt plays a large part in blurring the rela  
aonship between social class and religious or polirical opinions, owing to   
the far that it governs che representarion of che position occupied in the   
sentialist faith in   
past, the cult of the erernity of narures, celebration of cradirion and the tory and its rituals, because the best they can expect he rerurn of the old order, from which they expect of their social being.

blurring is particularly visible in the middle classes and especially carional level. (This has the eftc, amongother things, of foregrounding what the new vulgare calls 'the problems of the couple', i.e. essetially, the problems of the sexual division of labour and the division of sexual jabour.)

In contrast to the effect of individual trajectory, which, being a devia. tion from che collective trajectory (that may have a zero slope), is imme. diarely visible, the effect of collective trajectory may not be noticed as such. When the trajectoiy effect concerns a whole class or class fraction, that is, a set of individuals who occupy an identical position and are engaged in the same collective trajectory, the one which defines a rising or declining ciass, there is a danger of attributing to the proprties synchronically atta.ched to the class, effects (e.g., politicat or religious opinions) which are in reality the product of collective transformations. The analysis is complicated by the fact that some members of a class fraction may have embarked on individual trajectories running in the opposite direction to that of the fraction as a whole. This does not mean that their practices are not marked by the collective destiny. (It is questionable, for example, whether craftsmen or farmers whose individual success seems to run counter to the collecrive decline cease to be affected by that decline.)'But here too one must avoid substanrialism. Thus, some of the properties associated with social class which may remain without efficacy or value in a given feld, such as ease and familiarity with culture in an area strictly controlled by the educational system, can take on their full force in another feld, such as high society, or in another state of the same field, like the aptitudes which, after the French Revolution, enabled the French aristocracy to become, in Marx's phrase,'thedancing-masters of Europe'.

CAPITAL AND THE MARKET But everything would still be too simple if it were sufficient to replace a factor, even a particularly powerful one such as socio-occupational category, which derives a major part of its effects from the secondary variables it governs, by a system of factors fundamentally defined by its strucrure." In fact, what is dererminant in a given area is a particular configuration of the system of properties constituting constructed class, defined in an entirely theoretical way by the whole set of actors operating in all areas of practice-volume and structure of capi tal, defined synchronically and diachronically (trajectory), sex, age, mari al sratus, place of residen e eec. It is the specific logic of the field, what is at stake and of the type of capital needed to play for it, which

and this or that practi e (eg, the rte of response to political questions, did not irself have to be explained.

To undeistand why the same system of properties (which determines and is determined by the position occupied in the field of class struggles) always has the greatest explanatory power, whatever the area in ques tioneating habits, use of credit, fertility, political opmion, religion etc.—and why, simulraneously, the relative weight of the factors which constitute it varies from one field to anocher—educational capital being most im portant in one area, economic capital in another, and so on—one only has to see that, because capital is a social relation, i.e., an energy which only exists and only produces its effects in the field in which it is produced and reproduced, each of the properties attached to class is given its value and efficacy by the specific laws of each field. In pracrice, that is, in a particular field, the properties, internalized in dispositions ot objectified in economic or cultural goods, which are attached to agents are not all simultaneously operative; the specific logic of the field determines those which are valid in chis marker, which are pertinent and active in the game in question, and which, in the relationship with this field, functon as specific capital and, consequently, as a factor explaining practices. This mcans. concretely, that the sociat rark and specific power which agenrs are assigned in a patticular held depend firstly on the specihc c pital they can mobilize, whatever their additional wealth in other types of capital (though this may also exert an effect of contami- nation).

ation   
rding gto r particular combination of factors which varies ac. This appearance itself leads to the mistake of   
g venri In explanatory systems as theie are fields, instead of seea transformed form of all the others; or worse, the field of sec articular combination of factors active in a particus a universal explanatory principle. The singutar guratic e system of explanatory factors which has to be con-

std           icular moment. of e lass struggle over tha paicular class f goods or rac enlightened opinion or a chic sport), is the form taken, in thar field, by the objectifed and internalized capital (properties and habitus) which defines social class and constitutes the principle of the production of classified and classifying practices. It represents a state of the system of properties which make class a universal principle of explanation and clas. sifica.tion, dehning the rank occupied in all possible felds.

## A Three-Dimensional Space

Endeavouring to reconsritute the unirs most homogencous from the point of view of the conditions of production of habitus, i.e., with re. spect to the elementary conditions of existence and the resultant conditionings, one can construct a space whose three fundamental dimensions are deined by volume of capiral, composition of capital, and change in these two propertis over time (manifested by past and potential trajectoıy in social space).

The primary diff erences, those which distinguish the major classes of conditions of existence, derive from the overall volume of capital, understood as the set of actually usable resources and powerseconomic capital, cultural capital and also social capital. The distribution of the different classes (and class fractions) thus runs from those who are best provided with both economic and cultural capital to those who are most deprived in both respects (see figure S, later in this section). The members of the professions. who have high incomes and high qualificarions, who very often (S2.9 percenr) originate from the dominant class (profes. sions or senior execurives), who receive and consume a large quantity of both material and cultural goods, are opposed in almost all respects to the ofice workers, who have low qualifications, often originate from the working or middle classes, who receive little and consume little, devoting a high proporrion of their time to car maintenance and home improve ment; and they are even more opposed to the skilled or semi-skilled work ers, and still more to unskilled workers or farm labourers, who have the lowest incomes, no qualifcations, and originare almost exclusively (90.5 percent of farm labourers, 84.5 percent of unskilled workers) from the working classes.

The diff erences stemming from the total volume of capital almost a ways conceal, both from common awareness and also from 'scienti knowledge, the secondary differences which, within each of the classes defined by overall volume of capital, separate class fractions, defined by diff erent asset structures, i.e., diff erent distributions of their total capi among the dif erent kinds of capiral.

group's capital.

Once one takes account of the scructure of cotal assets--and not only, has always been done implicitly, of the dominant kind in a given structure, 'birt', fortune' or 'talents', as the nineteenth century put it one has the means of making more precise divisions and also of observing the specific effects of the strucrtire of distribution berween the different kinds of capital. This may, for example, be symmetrical (as in the case of the professions, which combine very high income with very high cultural pital) or asymmerrical (in the case of higher-education and secondary reachers or employers, with culrural capital dominanr in one case, economic capiral in the other). One thus discovers two sers of homologous posirions. The fractions whose reproduction depends on economic capital, usually inherited—industrial and commercial employers at the higher level, craftsmen and shopkeepers at the intermediate level—are opposed to che fractions which are least endowed (relatively, of course) with eco. nomic capical, and whose reproduction mainly depends on cultural capiralhigher-education and secondary teachers at the higher level, primary teachers at the intermediate level.

between public-sector and private-sector execurives (C.S. V) to exam successively, the distribution of economic capital and the distributio must then be correlated.

Although it is self-evident when one considers indicarors of wealth (as will be done larer), the hierarchy of che class fractions as regards possesslon of economic capital, running from industrial and commercial employers to reachers, is already less visible when, as here, one is only dealing with in. dices of consumption (cars, boats, hotels) which are neither entirely adequate nor entirely unambiguous (see table 6). The l'irst (cars) also depends on the rype of professional activity, and the orher two depend on spare time, which, as one learns in other ways, varies inversely with economic capital Home ownership aiso depends on srability in the same place of residence (losrer among executives, engineers and teachers). Incomes are very unevenly underestimared (che rate of non-deciaration may be considered an indicator of the tendency to under-declare) and very unequally accompanied by fringe benefts such as expense-account meals and business trips (which are known to rise as one moves from teachcrs to private-sector executives and cmployers).

As regards cultural capital, except for a few invcrsions, which reflect secondary variables such as place of residence, with the corresponding supply of culrure. and income, with che means it provides, the different fractions are organized in an opposire hierarchy (see table 7). (Differentiarion accord ing to the type of capital posscssed, literary, scientific or economic and political, is mainly scen in the fact rhat engineers showmore interest in music and intellectual' games such as bridge or chess than in literary activities theatre-going or rtading Le Figaro Littéraire.)

These indicators no doubt tend to minimize the gaps berween che dififer ent fractions. Most culrural consumption also entails an economic cost: the atre-going, for example, depends on income as well as education. Moreover, equipment such as FM radios or hi-fi systems can be used in very different ways (e.g., classical music or dance music), whose values, in rerms of the dominant hierarchy of possible uses, may vary as much as the different typ of reading-matter or theatre. In fact, the position of the different fractions ranked according to their interest in the different types of reading-marter archy of cultural legitimacy (see (able 8)

Having established that the strucrure of che distribution of cconomic capiral is symmetrical and opposire to that of culturał capital, we car turn to the question of the hierarchy of the two principles of hierarchiza tion (without forgetting that this hierarchy is at alì rimes a stake ir struggles and that, in certain conjunctures, as in present-day France, tural capital may be one of rhe conditions for access to control of eco nomic capital). We may take as an indicator of the state of the por telarion berween these rwo principles of domination the frequency of in. tergenerational movements between the fractions.

If we use as indices of the rarity of a position (or, which amounts to the same thing, irs degree of closure) the proportion of its occupants who originate from the dominant class as a whole and from the fraction in question, we hnd that the resulting hierarchy corresponds fairly exactly, for both indices. to the hierarchy by volume of economic capital (see table 9). The proportion of members of each fraction who originated from the dominant class, and the proportion of individuals who originated from the fraction to which they now belong, decline in parallel as one moves from the industrial employers to che teachers, with a clear break between the three higher-ranking fractions (industrial and commercial employers and the professions) and rhe three lower-ranking fractions (engineers, public-sector executives and reachers).

The use of these indicators may be contested on the grounds that the dif erent fractions have very unequal control over the condirions of their social reproduction, so that the high proportion of endogenous employers may express nothing other than the capacity of these fractions (or at least of a proportion of cheir members) ro transmit their powers and privileges withour mediation or control. Indeed, this capacity is itself one of the rarest privileges, which, by giving greater freedom vis-à-vis aca demic verdicts, reduces the necessity or urgency of making the culrural investments which cannot be avoided by chose who depend entirely on the education system for their reproduction. The fractions richest in cul wural capiral do in fact tend to invest in their children's education as wel as in rhe cultural practices likely tomaintain and increase their specific rarity; the fractions richest in economic capital set aside cultural and educarional investments in favour of economic investments-industrial an commercial employers more so, however, rhan the new bourgeoisic private-sector executives, who manifest the same concern for rarionali userheir capital in it actively, invest in their children's education bur and especially in cultural practices which symbolize possession of terial and cultural means of mainraining a bourgeois life-style and provide a social capital, a capiral of social connections, honourabilit respecrability thar is often essential in winning and keeping the example, in making a polirical carcer.

Given thar scholastic sucess mainly depends on inherited culrural capital and on che propensity to invest in the educarional system (and chat the latrer varies with the degtee to which maintained or improved social position depends on such success), it is clear why the proportion of pupils in a given school or college who come from the culturally richest fractions rises wich che position of thar school in the specificaily academic hierarchy (mez sured, for example, by previous academic success), reaching its peak in the msritution responsible for reproducing the professorial corps (the Ecole Normale Supérieure). ln fact, like the dominant class which they help to reproduce, highereducarion instirutions are organized in accordance with two opposing principles of hierarchy. The hierarchy dominant within the educarional system, i.e., the one which ranks institutions by specifically academic critetia, and, correlatively, by the proportion of their students drawn from the culrurally richest fractions, is diametrically opposed to rhe hierarchy dominant ourside the educarional system, i.e., the one which ranks institurions by the proportion of their srudents drawn from the fractions richest in economic capiral or in power and by the position in rhe economic or power hierarchy of the occupations they lead to. 1f the offspring of the dominated fractions are tess represenred in the economically highest institurions (such as ENA or HEC) than might be expected from their pre vious academic success and the position of these schools in the specifically scholastic hierarchy, this is, of course, because these schools refuse to apply purely scholastic criteria, bur it is also because the scholastic hierarchy is most fairhfully respected (so thar the science section of the ENS is pre ferred to Polyiechnique, or the Arts faculry to Sciences Po), by thase who are mosr dependent on the educarional system. (Blindness to alternative ranking principles is most nearly complete in the case of teachers' children. whose whole upbringing inclines them to dentify al success with academic success.)

the children of industrial or agriculrural employeis, and non-owners, chiefly endowed wich educational capical and spare rime, originaring from the wageearning fracrions of the middle and upper classes or from the working class. The occupants of homologous positions, primary teachers and professors, for example, or small shopkeepers and commercial entrepreneurs, are mainly separated by che volume of the kind of capital char is dominant in the strucrure of theit assers, i.e., by differences of degrce which separare individuals unequally endowed with the same scarceresources. This is clearly seen in the case of the wage-carning petite bourgeoisie, whose asceric virtues and culrural good intenrions—which it manifests in all sorts of ways, taking evening classes, enrolling in libraries, collecting stamps erc.—very clearly express the aspiration to rise to rhe higher position, the objective destiny of rhe occupants of the lower position who manifest such dispo. ferent fractions-especilly as regards culrure-one would have to take group's chances of appropriating any given class of rare assets (as mea account of cheir distribution in a sociatlly ranked geographical space. A sured by rhe mathematical probability of access) depend partly on its ca. pacity for the specific appropriation, deined by the economic, cultural and social capital it can deploy in order to appropriate materially or sym bolically the assets in question, thar is, irs posizion in social space, and partly on the relationship beween its distribution in geographical space and the distribution of the scarce assets in that space.2º (This relationship can be measured in average distances from goods or facilities, or in travel. ling time—which involves access to private or public transport.) In other words, a group's real social distance from cerrain assets must integrate the geographical distance, which itself depends on the group's spatial distribution and, more precisely, its distribution with respect to the 'focal point' of economic and culrural values, i.e., Paris or rhe major regional centres (in some careers e.g., in the postal banking system-employ. ment or promotion entails a period of exile).21 Thus, the distance of farm workers from legirimare culrure would not be so vast if the specifically cultural disrance implied by their low culrural capiral were not compounded by cheir spatial dispeision. Similarly, many of the differences observed in rhe (cultural and other) practices of the different fractions of the dominant class are no doubr attriburable to the size of thetown they live in. Consequently. the opposition between engineers and privatesector executives on the one hand, and industrial and commercial employers on the orher, partly stems from the fact that the formermostly live in Paris and work for relatively large hrms (only 7 percenr of private-sector executives work in firms employing from 1 ro 5 people. as against 34 percent in medium-sized firms and 40 percent in firms employing more than S0 people), whercas the latter mainly run small firms (in the 1966 survey by SOFRES [Société française d'enquêres par son dages]--C.S. V6 percent of the industrialists had from 1 to 5 employ ees; 70 percent, 6 to 49; 24 percent, more than s0; in commerce, the corresponding igures are 30 percent, 42 percent and 12 percent) and mostly live in the provinces and even in the country (according to th 1968 census, 22.3 percent of the industrialists and 15.5 percent of the percent in communes of less than 10,000 inhabirants).

The model which emerges would not be so difhcult to arrive artf it di

23 Projection onto a single axis, in order to construct the continuous, liner, homogeneous, one-dimensional series with which the social hierar. chy is normally identified, implies an extremely diffcult (and, if it is unwitting, extremely dangcrous) operation, whereby the difterent types of capital are reduced to a single standard. This abstract operation has an objecrive basis in the possibility, which is always available, of convertimg one type of capital into another; however, the exchange rates vary in accordance with the power relation between the holders of the different forms of capital. By obliging one to formulate the principle of the convertibjlity of the different kinds of capital, which is the precondition for reducing the space to one dimension, the construction of a two-dimensional space makes it clear that the exchange rate of the different kinds of pitał is one of the fundamental stakes in thestruggles between class fractions whose power and privileges are linked to one or the other of these types. In parricular, this exchange rate is a stake in the struggle o vcr the dominant principle of domination (economic capital, cultural capital or social capital), which goes on ar all cimes berween the different fractions of the dominant class.

## Reconversion Strategies

Reproduction straregies, the set of ourwardly very different pracrices whereby individuals or families tend, unconsciously and consciously, to maintain or increase their assets and consequently to maintain or improve cheir position in the class structure, constitute a system which, being the product of a single unifying, generative principle, tends to 2

One of the difficulties of sociological discourse lies in the fact thac, like all language, it unfolds in sttictly lincar fashion, whercas, to escape oversimplificarion and onesidedness, one ought to be able to recall ar every point the whole nerwork of relacionships found chere. T'har is why ic has seemed useful to present a diagram which has the property, as Saussure says, of being able to 'present simulraneous complications in several dimensions', as a means of grasping the correspondence berween che srructure of social spacc—whose cwo fundamenral dimensions correspond to rhe vol. ume and composition of rhe capital of rhe groups distributed within jt--and the structure of rhe space of the symbolic properties attached to those groups. But this diagram does not aím to be the crystal ball in which the alchemists claimed to see at a glance everything happening in the world; and like marhemaricians who also treat whar they call 'imagety" as a necessaiy evil, I am tempred ro withdraw ir in rhe very act of presenring it. For t ere is reason ro fear rhat ir will eucourage readings which will reduce the homologies berween sysrems of differ. ences to direct, mechanical relarionships berween gioups and properties; or that ir will encourage the form of voyeurjism which is inherent in rhe objectivisr inrenrion, purring the sociologist in the role of the lame devil who takes off' the roofs and reveals the secrets of domestic life to his fascinared readers.

To have as exacr an idea as possible of che cheoretical model char is proposed, it has to be imagined thar three diagrams are superimposed (as could be done with transparent

sheers). The first (here, hgure s) presents the space of socialcondirions, as organized by the synchronic and diachronic distriby of the various kinds of capital: the position of each group (class fraction) in this space is determined by the setof properties characteristic in the respecrs thus defined as perti. nent. The second (hgure 6) presents rhe space of life-styles, i.c., the distribution of the practices and properties whichconstitute the life-style in which each of these conditions man ifests itself. Finaly, between the two previous diagrains one ought to insett a third, presenting the theoretical space of habitus, that is, of the generative formulae (e.g., for teach. ers, aristocratic ascericism) which underllic cach of the classes of pracrices and properties, thar is, the transformarion into a distinct and distincrive life-sryle of the necessities and facilities characteristic of a condition and a position.The figures presentedhere are not plane diagrams ofcorrespondence analyses, although various such analyses were drawn on in ordet ro construct chem, and although a number of these are organized in accordance with a similar structure (including rhe analyses of the survey dara which arepresented below).

Amongthe limitations of such a construct, che most important are due to the lacunae in the statistics, which are much better at measuring consumption or, at best, income (setting aside secondary and hidden profits) and property than capital in the strict sense (especially capital in vesred inthe economy); others are due to the inadequacies of the analyrical categories. These are very unble, for example, to identify che holders of a capiral chat can exert (For hck of rigorous indicarors of the dispersion of the different categoties, che cconomic and cultural dispersion of che most hererogeneous categories—farmers, industrial and commerciat employers, crafts. men and shopkeepers--has been indicated by wriring the corresponding names verrically berween che ex-ceme limirs defining che group.) Ic has to be remembered that che posiwon marked by the names always repiesents the central point in a space of variable extent which may in some cases be organized as a field of competition.

In the absence of a survey (perhaps impossible to cariy our in practice) rhat would provide, with respect to the same representative sample, all the indicators of eco nomic, cultural and social wealch and its cvolution which are needed in order ro coastluct an adequate rpresenrarion of social space, a sim. plified model of thar space has been construcred, based on informarion acquired rhrough earlier research, and on a set of dara taken from varrous surveys, al done by INSEE and cherefore homogeneous ar least as regards rhe construction of rhe categories (sce appendix 3). From the INSEE survey of 1967 on leisure ac-(vities (tables relating to men) I have raken indicators of spare rime such as length of the working week (C.S IV), trom the 1970 survey on vocarional training (tables relating cional level (inherited cultural capiral) and the subject's educacional level (scholastic capical) (C.S. II); from che 1970 suivey on incomes, I have taken informarion on toral incomes, rural and urban property, shares, induserial and commcrcial prohts, wages and salaries (economic capital) (C.S. I); from the 1972 survey on household consump rion, data on the coral amount spent, possession of a washingmachine and relephone, forms of tenancy of main and second residence (C.S. III); and from the 1968 census, dara on rhe size of the cown of residence.

For each of the groups represenced, I have also indicated, firstly, the distribution of che occupants of each group according to the social trajecrory which has brought them there, wich hisrograms showing the pro rtion of cach group having come from cach of the difterent classes. For the sake of legibility, these hiscograms are reproduced only for a few illustrative caregories. They suffice to show chat the proportion of individuals from the dominant class (black) rises strongly, while r e proportion from the working classes (whire) declines, as one moves up the social hierarchy. (The histogram for the 'semi-skilled' workers, not reproduced here, is inrermediare berween those of the unskilled and skilled workeis.) For the upper and middic classes ar least, one really needs to be able to give the disrribution by fraction of origin.

Secondly, I have indicared the hisrory of rhe group as a whole. This is shown by the arrows, pointing up, down or horizonrally. which indicate thar berween 1962 and 1968 the group in quesrion expanded (by at least 25 percent), contracted or remained stable. They thus make visible the opposition berween the new, strongly growing fracrions and t'e srablished, stable or declining fractions. I have chus endeavoured ro show borh the state of the power relarion between the classes which constitutes che strucrure of che social space ar a given moment and also something which is simulraneously an effect of and a factor in the cransformarion of chat strucrure, namely the reconversion serategie's whereby individuals (and groups) strive to maintain or improve their position in social space.

The synoptic schema, by bringing rogecher informarion from areas which the usual classificatory systems separaceso much so that they make mere juxtaposition appear unrhinkable or scandalous—-and so making manifest the relarionships among all the properries and practices characteristic of a group, which are perceived intuicively and which guide the classifications of everyday life, forces one to look for the basis of each of these sysrems of 'choices', on the one hand in the social condirions and conditionings characterisric of a given position in objective social space, which are expressed in those choices bur in a misrecogniz. able form; and on che orher hand, in their relationship to the other sysrems of 'choices', by reference to which their specihcally symbolic meaning and value are defined. Be. cause life-styles are essentially distinctive, a number of features do not rake on their full signifcance until chey are brought into relation not only with the social positions ihey express but also with fearures appearing ar an opposite pole of this space. This is the case, for example, wich the oppositions which are esrablished primordially berween the positions most remote from cach other in one or borh of the funda. mental dimensions of social space (i.e., wich respect to volume and composition of capital): Goya and Renoir, avant-garde chearre and bou. levard theatre, JacquesBreland Tino Rossi, France-Musique and France-Inter or Radio Luxembourg. cinema clubs and variety shows and so forth.

In addition to the informarion gathered directly by the survey, I have used a number of indices of culrural consumption, such as possession of a piano or records, TV. viewing, visits to museums, exhibi. rions, variery shows and the cinema, membership in a Iibrary, evening classes, collecrions, sports, all taken from the 1967 INSEE survey on leisure activities (C.S. IV); informa. rion on the consumption and lifestyles ofmembers of the dominant class (hi-fi equipment, sailing, cruises, bridge, picture collections, champagne, whisky, sports etc.) from surveys by the SOFRES and CESP (C.S. V and VI);information on thearre-going from a survey by SEMA (Société d'économie et de marhémariques appliquées) (C.S. XIV); on favourire actors, from the surveys by IFOP (Irsritut français de l'opinion publique) (C.S. XIV): on the reading of daily and weekly newspapers and magazines, from the surveys by the CSE (Centre de so iologie européenne) and CESP (C.S. XXVIIl); and on various culrur'al activities (ceramics, potcery, funfairs etc.) from the sur vey by the Ministry of Culrure (C.S. VII).

Similarly, the item 'stocks and shares'—top right--ap. plies to employers, the professions, private-secror execurives and engincrs.) It can be seen immediately thas passssion of a piano and the choice of the Concerto for the Left Hand are most gypical of members of the professions, that walking and mountaincering are particularly

characreristic of secondary teachers and public-secror executives; or thar swimming, placed half-way between the new petite bourgeoisie and the private-secror execurives or the engineers, belongs to the life-sryle of both chese sets of occuparions. Thus, grouped around the name of each class fraction are chose fearures of its life-style which are the most perrinent because they are the most disrinctive--though it may in fact share them with other groups. This is the case, for example, with the use of a library, which appears in the area of the juniot execurives, primary teachers and rechnicians, although it is at least as freguent among secondary and universiry teachers; bur the larrer are less marked by the practice since it is part of their occuparional role.

rion strategies. The reconversion of capital held in one form to anorher, more accessible, more profitable or more legitimate form tends to induce a transformation of asset structure.

These reconversions correspond to movemenrs in a social space which has nothing in common with the unreal and yet naively realisric space of -called social mobiliry' srudies. The same positivistic naivery which s upward mobility' in che morphological transformarions of different classes or fractions is also unaware that the reproducrion of che social siructure may, in cercain conditions, demand very lictle 'occupational herediry'. This is rrue whenever agents can only maintai'n their position in the soclal strucrure by means of a shift into a new condition (e.g., the shife from small iandowner to junior civil servant, or from small craftsman tO office worker or commercial employee).

The social space, being structured in two dimensions (overall capical   
volume ano ninant/dominated capital), allows two types of move.   
from s dow equiva wOwa cho irional mobility studies confuse, although chey are in no re unequally probable: verrical movemenrs, upwards e same verrical sector, chat is, in che same field (e.g., professor, or from small businessman to big busi   
Itl2y sc movements, from one feld to anorher, which horizonrally (a schoolteacher, or his son, becomes a r between diferent levels (a shopkeeper, or his son,

erature to economics) and therefore a transformarion of asser struc tical dimension.

The probability of entering a given fracrion of the dominant class from an. other class is, as we have seen. in inverse ratio to rhe position of thar frac. tion in the hierarchy of economic capital. (The only exception is the 'liberal protessions', which tend to transmit both economic and cultural capital and have the highest rate of endogenous recruirment.) Similarly major sideways movements within the class (industrialists' sons becoming secondary or higher-cducation teachers, or vice versa) are exrremely rare. Thus, in 1970, the probability of becoming an indusrrial or commercial employer was l.9 percent for a professor'sson, and the probability of becoming a reacher was 0.8 percenr for anindustrialist's son and 1.5percent for commercial enrrepreneur's son. The probability of becoming a craftsman or shopkeeper was 1.2 percent for a primary teacher's son, and the probability of becoming a primary teacher was 2.4 percent for a craftsman's son and 1.4 percent for a small shopkeeper's son (C.S. II, secondary analysis).

CLASS MOBILITY ANI) MOBILE CLASSES The tecent changes in the rela tionship berween the differenr classes and the educarional system-with the schooling boom' and the accomparying changes in the system itself--and also the changes in the social structure resulting from the new relarionship berween qualifcations and jobs, are the consequences of tensifed comperition for academic qualifications. One important factor in intensifyirig this comperition has doubtless been the facr thar thosc fractions of the dominant class and middle class who are richest in eco nomic capiral (i.e., industrial and commercial employers, craftsmen and radesmen) have had to make grearly increased use of the educariona system in order to ensure their social reproduction.

The disparity berween the scholastic capital of the adults of a class or class fra:crion (measured by the proportion who have a qualifcarion perior to the BEPC) and the schooling rate of the corresponding cents is much more pronounced among craftsmen, shopkeepers and in the usual correspondence between the children's educarional

schooling rare berween 1962 and 1975.24 among g farm workers, as is shown by the rapid rise in their children's

When clss fraccions who previously made lide use of che school sysgroups whose reproducrion was mainly or exclusively achieved through education to step up their investments so as to maintain the relative scarcty of their qualifications and, consequently, their position in the class strucrure. Academic qualificarions and che school system which awards them thus become one of the key srakes in an interclass comperition oshich generares a general and conrinuous growth in the demand for ducation and an inflarion of academic qualifications.

To the efects of the competition berween groups st:uggling for 'upclassing' and against 'downclassing' (déríassement), a competition thar is organized around che academic qualification (titre) and more generally around all the 'entielements' by which groups assert and constitute their own scarcity value vis.à-vis other groups, must be added the effect of whar might be termed a ssrucfural factor. Generally increased schooling has the effect of increasing the mass of culrural capital which, ar every moment, exisrs in an embodied' srate. Since the success of che school's educarive action and the durabilizy of trs cffects depend on how much culturat capiral has been direcly transmited by the family, it can be presumed that the effciency of school-based educarive action rends to rise consrantly, other rhings being equal. In shorr, the same scholastic investment becomes more profrable, a fact which no doubr contributes to infarion by bringing diplomas within reach ofa greater number of people.

Indeed, it presents no paradox to suggest chat che chief victims of the devaluing of academic qualifications are those who enter the labour market without such qualifications. The devaluation of diplomas is ac. companied by the gradual extension of the monopoly held by academic. qualification-holders over positions previously open to the academically unqualified, which has the effect of limiting the devaluation of qualifica tions by limiting the competition, but only ac the cost of restricting the career openings available to the unqualified and ofreinforcing the aca demic predetermination of occupational opportunity. In certain areas, particularly the civil service, this leads to a decline borh in the dispersal of the holders of the same qualifications among different jobs and in the dispersal of the qualifications of holders of equivalent jobs, or, in other words, a reinforced correlation berween academic qualification and job occupied.

The market in jobs open co formally qualified candidates has grown constantly, inevitably at the expense of the formally unqualified. Universal recognition of academic qualifications no doubt has the effect of unifyring the ofhcial set of qualifications for social positions and of eliminating local anomalies due to the existence of social spaces with their own rank-ordering principles. However, academic qualifcations never achieve total, exclusive acceprance. Ourside the specifically scholastic market, a diploma is worrh what irs holder is worth, economically and socially; che rate of recurn on educational capital is a finction of the eco nomic and social capital that can be devored to exploiting it-

(By contrast, among farmers, the proportion remains much the same: 23.8 percent in 1956, 23.5 percent in 1965 and 24.8 percent in 1975.) In 1975, the proportion of income derived from investment in tand, buildings, stocks and shares is much higher among private-sector than public-sector senior executives (5.9 percent and 2.7 percent respectively).

The reconversion of economic capital into educational capicał is one of the strategies which enable the business bourgeoisie to maintain the po sition of some or all of its heirs, by enabling them to extract some of the profits of industrial and commercial frms in the form of salaries, which arc a more discreet—and no doubt more reliable-mode of appropriation than 'unearned' investment income. Thus, berween 1954 and 1975 the Proportion of industrial and commercial entrepreneurs fell sharply, whercas chere was a very strong rise in the proportion of salary-eamers, sho owed their posirion to their academic qualifcations—cxecutives, engincers, reachers and intellecruals (although, ar least in the case of private-sector executives, a significant proporrion of rotal income may be derved from shares, as table 13 indicares). Similarly, che disappearance of many small commercial or craft frms conceals the reconversion work whiçh individual agents perform, with varying degrees of success, in accordancewith the demands of cheir particular situation, and which results in a transformarion of che relarive weight of che different fractions of the middle classes (see rable 14). Here, too, the decrease in che proportion of small shopkeepers, craftsmen, and farmers has been accompanied by an increase in the proportion of primaty-school teachers,

These changes in the nature of rerail frmswhich are related to changes over the same period, in the structure of household consump ton, rhemselves celaced to the growch in incomes and above all co the increase in culcural capital resulting from che upward shift of che struc. ture of educational opportunityare dialecticaly linked to a rise in the culruial capital of cheir owners or managers.

Everything suggests that the ‘craftsman' category has undergone changes vely similar to the 'shopkeeper' category, with the decline of the most exposed strata of traditional craftsmanship being offser by the boom in luxury and ‘aesrheric' crafts, which require economic assets but also cultural capital. This would explain why the fall in the volume of these middle-class categories is accompanied by a rise in cultural capital as measured by educarional level.

the alesman a on the narure and quality of his wares, as a way of oburn on a cultural capital in which technical compe mastery of the signs and emblems of distinction and taste. are the ser of strategies whereby the holders of devalued qualificar have sought to maintai'n cheir inherited positions or to obtain from state of the relationship berween diplomas and jobs.

property (titre de propiété) whic strictly technical denitions make oft So one can well understand thar the victims of devaluation are disin clined to perceive and acknowledge the devaluing of qualifications with which they are closely identifed, both objecively (chey constiture an important part of chese people's soclal identity) and subjectively. But the concern to preserve self esteem, which encourages attachment to the nominal value of qualifications and jobs, would nor be sufficient to maincain a misperception of chis devaluation, if there were not also some complicity from objective mechanisms. The mo  important of these are, first, the hysteresis of habitus, which causes previously appropriate cate gories of perception and appreciation to be applied to a new state of the qualification market; and, second, the existence of relarively autonomous markets in which the value of qualifications declines at a slower rate.

The hysteresis effect is proportionately greater for agents who are more remote from the educational system and who are poorly or only vagucly informed abour the market in educational qualifications. One of the most valuable sorts of information consticuting inheritedcultural capital is practical or theoretical knowledge of the Auctuations of the market in academic qualifications, the sense of investment which enables one to ger che best recurn on inherited culrural capital in the scholastic market or on scholastic capital in che labour marker, for exampie, by knowing the right moment to pull out of devalued disciplines and careers and to switch into chose with a furure, rather chan clinging to che scholastic values which secured the highest profts in an earlier state of che marketr By contast, the hysteresis eec means thart che holders of devalued d plomas become, in a sense, accomplices in their own mystification, since tion, because of their lack of social capital).

This attachment to an anachronistic idea of the value ofqualificat

ribution roward actual revaluation.

In the transactions in which the market value of academic qualificarions is defned, che strength of the vendors of labour power depends serting aside their social capital-on the value of their diplomas, especially when rhe relationship between qualifications and jobs is strictly codified (as is the case wich established positions, as opposed to new ones) So it is clear char che devaluation of academic diplomas is of direct advanrage to che suppliers of jobs, and rhar, while the intere ts of qualificaljon-holders are bound up with the nominal value of qualifications, i.e., with what they guarantceed by right in the earlier situation, che interests of job suppliers are bound up wich rhe real value of qualifications, in oiher words, the value rhar is derermined at the moment in question in the compeition among che candidates. (This is a scrucrural de-skilling [dégualifcation] which aggravates the efects of the de-skilling strategies that firms have been using for a long time.) The grearest losers in this srnuggle are those whose diplomas have least relative value in the hierar. chy of diplomas and are most devalued. In some case the qualification. holder fnds he has no ocher way to defend the value of his qualification than to refuse to sell his labour power at the price offered; the decision to eain unemployed is rhen equivalent to a one-man strike.

THE CHEATING OF A GENERATION In a period of 'diploma inflation'   
access to it, it to give rhem what it gave others at a time when   
sponded to objec ese aspirations were perfectly realistic, since rhey correive probabilities, but rhey are ofren quickly defated by cholastic market or the labour market. One of the s called rhe 'democratizarion of schooling' is char only

als and negarions of che adolescent counter-culture.

that are objectively and subjectively different in the various social lass Thus, for working-cass youngsters, the ransit through secondary school ing and chrough the ambiguous status of a 'student', temporarily freed from the demands of the world of work, produces misfirings of the dia lectic of aspirations and probabilities which led their predecessors to ac cept cheir social destiny, almost always unquestioningly, and sometimes with positive eagerness (like the miners' sons who used to identify their entry into manhood with their first descent into the mine). The disen chantment with their work that is fel and expressed particularly acutely by the most obvious victims of downclassing, such as baccalauréat. holders obliged to take jobs as factory workers or postmen, is, in a way. common to a whole generation. It fnds expression in unusual forms of struggle, protest and escapism that the organizations traditionally involved in industrial or political struggle find hard tounderstand, because something more than working conditions is ar srake. These young peo ple, whose social identity and self-image have beenundermined by a social system and an educational system that have fobbed them off with worthless paper, can find no other way of restoring their personal and social integrity than by a total refusal. It is as if they felt that what is at stake is no longer just personal failure, as the educational system encour ages them to believe, but rather the whole logic of the academic institution. The structural de-skilling of a whole generation, who are bound o get less out of their qualifications chan the previous generation would have obrained, engenders a sort of collective disillusionment: a whok generation, finding it has been taken for a ride, is inclined to extend to all institutions the mixture of revolt and resentment it feels towards the educational system. This anti-institutional cast of mind (which draws strength from ideological and scientihc critiques) points rowards a d nunciation of the tacit assumptions of the social order, a practical suspen its funccioning.

## Disenchanted

First I did marker research surveys. 1 had a friend in L. who was into char. I got a list of all the research frms in Paris. Afrer two months phoning and writing, finally I gor somerhing. Then, scveral monchs larer, chey still hadn't got in touch wich me. They weren'e doing any more surveys. I was entirled ro unemployment benefir, a thousand francs a month. We lived on char for seves monrhs, chen we did rwo months' grapepicking. Thien I went back to surveys for seven monchs, working free-lance. Then I quit; the place was fuli of lesbians and they gave out the work to their favourires, so I got our. Anyway, we each work a bit in curns. In this sort of sociery, work isn't the main thing in life. Now, if things pere run the way they are in China, 1 might want to work ten hours a day' (F., age 24, baccalauréar and a few months in an Arts faculty; father: private means).

Once you've Aunked your bac, you're already in the shit. There are no possible careers and che jobs you can find are complerely useless.

'All che jobs I did were boring, so I savcd up some money so I could stop working for a few months. Anyway, I prefer to srop once in a ahile so I don't ger into a rut.

'Afrer I fziled the bac, I spent the summer working as a moniror in a vacarion camp. Then I gor a job with a newspaper in Dreux. i was a trainee subedicor but afrer two months it was time to rake out my union card so I went free-lance. Bur I didn't seem to fic in. Everyrhing 1 wrore, they went through with a fine-rooch comb. I did photos, 0o0. Bur there was a power struggle in the paper. I couldn't be bothered to fight. After six months, they sropped giving me work, so 1 left. gor raken in by the "public service" myth and I signed on at the Post Office. I was on sorting for three weeks. I couldn't take any more. It was a work environment I'd never known before. Ir wasn't so much the people that gor up my nose as the relations between them, the talcrelling. T'here was no solidarity. Afrer three weeks I chucked it in. There were hve of usauxiliaries, one was fired on the spot for taking fif. reen minutes' extra break, so we all walked our. The worst of it is that you funk your exams, you hated school, and you end up being treared as an intellectual.

Next I gor a job through the employment agency, as a clerk in an office dealing with wholesale beef. T ere was a row about a bonus that wasn't given to everyone. There was a slanging-match and I got out. I'd been there two and a half months. In September I picked grapes and then i went back to the employment agency. I was a courier on a scoorer for six monchs. That was the craziest thing I've ever done. It's a ghasrly job, you get complerely paranoid on your scoorer, imagining they're all trying to run you down. I chucked it in, I couldn'r take any more.

'After two months on the dole, got a remporary job, just for the holiday period, on rhe railways. I was on electronic reservations, "of erator" they called it, or something like thar, and I srayed for four

More radical, less self-confident than the usual form of political conresrarion, and reminiscent of the mood of the frst Romanric generacion, this disenchanred tempcrament attacks che fundamenral dogmas of the perir-bourgeois ordercarecr', 'srarus', promorion' and 'gerring on.'

THI: STRUCiCil.E TO KEliP UP The specific contradiction of the scholastic mode of reproduction lies in the opposirion berwcen the interests of the class which the educational system scrves statistically and the intcrests of those class members whom it sacrihces, that is, the 'failures' who are thrcarcned with déclassement for lack of che qualifcations formally required of rightful members. Nor should one forger those holders of qual. llications which 'normally'—i.e., in an earlier stare of the relationship berw'ccn diplomas and jobs—gave access ro a bourgeois occupation, who, becaus: they do not originate from that class, lack the social capital to extract the full yield from their academic qualilications. The overproducton of qualifications, and the consequent devaluarion, tend to become a structuiral constant when theoretically equal chances of obtaining qualifi. cations are offered to all che offspring of the bourgeoisie (regardless of birth rank or sex) while the access of other classes to these qualilications also increases (in absolure rerms). The strategies which one group may employ ro try to escape downclassing and to rerurn to their class trajecy, and chose which another group employs ro rebuild the interrupred from those they have, by moving into relazively unof social space {where social dispositions count for gies aimed ar asserting the value of formal qualifications

Whereas in 1962 only 1.5 percent of semi-skilled workers aged 15-24 had the BEPC, and 0.2 percent the baccalauréat or a hig icr diploma, in 1975 che corresponding percentages were 8.2 and 1.0. Among whirecoliar workers, where by 1962 even in the oldest age group there was a relatively high percenrage of diploma-holders, the proportion of the very highly qualifed rose faster among the young, so that by 1975 a larger proportion of chem had higher qualifcarions than did the older workers (in 1962, 25.0 percent of off ice workers aged 15-24 had che BEPC, 2.0 percent the baccalauréat, and 0.2 percent a higher education degrce, compared with 38.0 percent, 8.0 percenr and 1.0 percent in 1975; the corresponding figures in 1975 for older sraff members were 16.1 percent. 3.3 percent and 1.4 per. cent). ln addition to all the changes inthe relations berween colleagues of dif erent generarions that are implied in these statistics, onc has to bear in mind the changed relarion to work which resulks from purting agents with higher qualihcarions into jobs thar are ofren de-skilled (by auromation and all che forms of job mechanization which have rurned whice-collar sraff into the production-line workers of che grear burcaucracies). There is every reason to think thar the opposition be tween the somewhar strict and even stuffy rigour of the older sraff and the casual style of the younger workers, which is doubrless perceived as sloppiness, especially when it includes long hair and a beard (the traditional emblems of the bohemian artist or inrellecrual), expresses rather more than a simple generarion gap.

scribed as an objective potentiality in the former position and in the tra jectoty leading to it. When this 'broken trajectory' effect occurs-for example, in the case of a man whose father and grandfather were polytech niciens and who becomes a sales engineer or a psychologist, or in the case of a law gra.duate who, for lack of social capiral, becomes a community cultural worker-the agent's aspirations, fying on above his real trajec tory like a projectile carried on by its own inertia, describe an ideal jectory that is no less real, or is at any rate in no way imaginary in the ordinary sense of the word. This impossible objective potentiality, inscribed at the deepest level of their dispositions as a sort of blighted hope or frustrated promise, is the common factor, behind all their differences, between those sons and daughrers of the bourgeoisie to whom the educational system has not given the means of pursuing the trajectory most likely for their class and rhose sons and daughters of the middle and working classes who have not obrained the rewards which their academic qualifcations would have guaranteed in an carlier stateof the marker two categories who are particularly likely to try to move inro the new positions.

Agents who seek to avoid downclassing can either produce new occupations more closely matching their pretensions (which were socially justified in an earlier state of relations betweenqualifications and jobs) or can refurbish the occupations to which their qualifications do give access, redefning and upgrading them in accordance with their pretensions. When agenrs start to arrive in a job who possess qualifications diffent from those of the usual occupants, they bring hitherro unknown aptitudes, dispositions and demands with them into cheir relation wich thar job, in terms of both its technical and social dehnition; and chis necessar ly causes changes in the job itself. Among rhe most visible changes ob division of labour, with autonomous starus being given ro some content.
