## A Sociological Test

Moving from right to left or from righr bank to left bank, we start with L'Aurore: 'Cheeky Françoise Dorin is going to be in hot warer with our offee-nosed, Marxist intelligentsia (the two things go together) The author of Un sale égoisle shows no respecr for the solemn boredom, profound emptiness and vertiginous nullity which characterize so many so-called 'a:vantgarde' cheatrical productions. She dares to profane with sacrilegious laughter the notorious "incommunicability" which is the alpha: and omega of rhe conremporary srage. And this perverse reactionary, who Batrers the lowest appetites of consumer society, far from acknowledging the error of her ways and wearing her boulevard playwright's repuration with humility, has the impudence to prefer the jolliry of Sacha Guitry, or Feydeau's bedroom farces, to the darkness visible of Marguerite Duras or Arrabal. This is a crime for which she will not easily be forgiven. Especially since she commits it with cheerfulness and gaiety, using all rhe dreadful devices which make lasring successes' (Gilbert Guilleminaud, L'Aurore, 12 Januaty 1973).

Situated at the fringe of rhe intellectual fcld, at a point whe e he al. ready has to speak ofie as an outsider ('our intelligenrsia'), the L'urore critic does not mince his words(he calls a reactionary' a re ccionary) and does nor hide his strarcgies. The rhetorical effect of putting words inro rhe opponent's mouth, in condirions in which his discourse, funcrioning ironically, objectively signihes the opposire of what tie mcans, presupposes and

brings into play the very structure tionship of immediate connivar ogy of posirion.

From L'Aurore we move to Le Fiparo. In perfect harmony wich the aurhor of Le Tournant-chehiar. mony of orchestrared habirus-\_the Figaro critic cannot but expericace absolute delight ar a play whichi so perfectly corresponds to his catego. rtes of perception and appreciation, his view of the theatre and his view of the world. However, being forced inro a higher degree of euphermiza. rion, he excludes overtly political judgemenrs and limirs himself to the language of aestherics and ethics: 'How grateful we should be to Mme.Françoise Dorin for being a courageouslylight author, which meanstosay that she is wittily dramatic,and smilingly serious, irreverentwithout fragility, pushing her comedy intooutright vaudeville, but in the subilestway imaginable; an aurhor who wields satire with elegance, who at all times demonstrates astoundingvirtuosity.... Françoise Dorin knows much more than any of us abouethe tricks of the dramatist's arl, the springs of comedy, rhe potenlial of a silualion, rhe comic or biting force of rhe mor juste... Yes, what skill in taking things apart, what irony in her deliberare sidestepping, what mastery in rhe way she lers you see her pulling the strings! Le Tournant gives every sort indulgence or vulgarity. And without ever being facile, since it is and age. quite clear that in this day which is it is entirely the avant conformist, it is gravity which is ri- which is the diculous and boredom

which she lives... In the end itis a question everyoae ash himself and only humour and incurable opti. can rid him of it! Veagjaeques Gautier, Le Figaro, 12 January 1973).

From Le Figare one moves narurally to L'Expres, which balances berween endorsementand disrance, therebyatraining a distinctly higher degtee of euphemization: 'It ought ro be a runaway success. ... A witty and amnusing play. A charactetr. An ator made for the parr: Jean Piat ... Wich an unfailing virtuosity shos is only occasionally overdone, with a sly cunning, a per fec masiery of the incks of the trade, Françoise Dorin has wtieren a play on the rurning points' in the Boulevard which is, lronically, the most tradirional of Boulevard plays. Orly worose pedants will probe too far inte the contast betueen twe types of thealre and the contrast belureen two conceplions of polilical life and the prrvate life bebind it. The brilltant dialogue, full of wit and epigrams, is often bicingly sarcastic. But Romain is not a caricarure, he is much less stupid than your run.ofthe-mill avant-gardist. Philippe has the plum rôle, because he is on his own ground What the aurhor of Come au gheáire genly wants to suggest is that the Boulevard stage where people speak and behave as classcruth'(Robert remaric use of formulations that are ambiguous even as regards the oppositions involved: "Ir ought to be a runaway success, 'asly cunning, a perfect mastery of che crichs of the tradc', 'Philippe has the plum rôle', all formulae which could equally be taken pejorarively. And we even find, surfacing through irs denial, a hint of the orher cruth (\*Only morose pedants will probe too far ...") or even of theplaintruth, bur doubly neurralized byambiguicy and denial (‘and not just because it is a class truth').

Le Monde offers a perfect exarmple of ostentatiously neurral discourse, even-handedly dismissing borh sides, both the overtly political discourse of L'Axrore and the disdainful silence of Le Nouvel Observateur: 'The simple, or simplistic, argument is complicated by a very subtle "twotier" structure, as if there wese rwo plays overlapping. One by Françoise Dorin, a convenrional aurhor, the other invented by Philippe Roussel, who tries to rake "the turning" to wards modern chearre. T'his conceir performs a circular movement, like a boomerang. Françoise Dorin deliber. arely exposes the Boulevard clichés which Philippe atracks and, through his voice, delivers a violent denunciarion of the bourgeoisie. On the second Aoor, she contrasts this language with thar of a young aurhor whom she assails with equal vigour. Finally, the trajectory brings the weapon back onro the Boulevard srage, and che fuciliries of che mechanism are unmasked by the devices of the traditional theatre, which are shown to have lost norhing of their value. Philippe can declare himself a "coutagcously light" playwright, inventing "characrers who talk like real people"; he can claim that his art is "withour frontiers" and therefore non-political. However, the

demonstration is entirely distorted by the model avant-garde author chosen by Françoise Dorin. Vankovicz is an epigone of Marguerite Duras, a vaguely militant, belared existentialist. He is parodic in the extreme, like rhe theatre that is denounced here ("A black curtain and a scaffold certainly help!" or the rirle of the play: "Do rake a little angst in your coffee, Mr. Karsov"). The audience sniggers at this derisive picture of modern drama; rhe denunciation of the bourgeoisie is an amusing provocation inasmuch as it rebounds onto an odious victim and finishes him off. ... To the extent that it reflects the state of bourgeois theatre and reveals its systems of defence, Le Toxrnant can be regarded as an important work. Few plays let slip so much anxiety abour an "external"threat and recuperate it with so much unconscious fury' (L. uis Dandrel, Le Monde, 13 January 1973).

The ambiguity which Robert Kanters was already beginning to cultivate here reaches its peak. The argument is 'simple or simplistic', rake your pick; the play is split in two, offering two works for the readet's choice, a 'violent' but 'recuperatory'critique of the bourgeoisie and a defence of non-political art. For anyone naive enough to ask whether the critic is for ot against', whether he fnds the play 'good or bad', there are two answers: frst, the observation by an 'objectrive informant'with a duty towards truth thar the avant-garde author portrayed is 'parodic in the extreme' and that 'the audience sniggers' (but without our knowing where the critic srands in relation to this audience, and therefore whar the sniggering signifies), and then, after a series of judgements that are held in ambiguity by many reservations, is ‘an important work', but be it noted, as a document llustrating Po.

This art of conciliacion and com promise achieves the virtuosity of art for art's sake with the critic of the Catholic paper La Croix, who laces his unconditional approval with such subtly articulated justif. cations, understatements through double negation, nuances, reservations and self-corrections that the final onciliatio oppositorum, so naively Jesuitical ‘in form and substrance', as he would say, almost seems to go without saying: 'Le Tournant, as I have said, seems to me an admirable work, in both form and substance. This is not to say it would not put many people's teeth on edge. 1 happened to be sitting next to an unconditional supporter of the avant-garde and throughout the evening 1 was aware of his suppressed anger. However, I by no means conclude that Françoise Dorin is unfair to certain very respectable-albeit often tedious—experiments in the contemporary theatre ... And if she concludes—her preference is delicately hintedwith the triumph of the "Boulevard"—but a boulevard that is itself avant-garde—that is precisely because for many years a master like Anouilh has placed him self as a guide at the crossroads of these two paths' (Jean Vigneron, L Croix, 21 January 1973).

Although the sile thing in itself, we can forn approximate idea of what its position might have een by reading it

Canard Enchainé: Theatre seems to me the wrong the course of which a famous and much loved actor recites the ta. boriously wirty text of an equally famous author in che middle of an elaborate stage ser, even a revolving ne decorated with Folon's mea. sured humour . No "ceremony" here. no "Catharsis" or "revelation" either. still less improvisation. Just a plateful of bourgeois cuisine for stomachs that have seen it all before. ... The audience, like all boulevard audiences in Paris, bursts out laughing, on cue, in the most conformist places, as and when this spirit of easy-going rationalism inspires them. The connivance is perfect and the acrors are all in on it. This play could have been wrirten ten, twenty. or thirty years ago' (M. Pierrer, Le Nouvel Observateur, 12 February 1964. reviewing

Félicien Marceau's La Preuve par quatre).

'Françoise Dorin really knous a thing or swo. She's a hirst-rate recu perator and terribly well-bred. Her Toxrnant is an excellent Boulevard comedy, which runs mainly on bad faith and demagogy. The lady wants to prove that avant-garde theatre is a dog's dinner. To do so, she takes a big bag of tricks and, needless to say, as soon as she pulls one out the au. dience rolls in the aisles and calls for more. Our author, who was just waiting for that, does it again. She gives us a young trendy leftist playwright called Vankowicz—get it?--and puts him in various ridiculous, uncomfortable and rather shady siruations, to show that this young gentleman is no more disinterested, no less bourgeois, than you and What common sense, Mme. Dorin, what lucidity, what honesty! You at least have the courage to stand by your opinions, and very healrhy, redwhite-and-blue ones they are too (Philippe Tesson, Le Canard Enchaîné, 17 March 1973 [italics in all foregoing «juorations are mine]).

laughable because they are not uttered in the appropriate place and before the tight audience. Instead, they become a 'mockery', a parody, establishing with their audience the immediate complicity of laughter, because they have persuaded rheir audience to reject (if it had ever accepted) the Presupposirions of the parodied discourse.

not cynical ns this exemplary case clearly shows, it is the logic of the homologies, 1 calculation, which causes works to be adjusted to the expeccribing as the conscious pursuit of success wich an audit the result of rhe pre-established harmony between erests (which may coincide in the person of the 'bourmore precisely, of the structural and functional hon a given writer's or artist's position in the feld of prothe position of his audience in the feld of the classes and limated and euphemized interests, such as interest in a form of theatre or philosophy which is logically associated with a certain position in a cerrain field and which (except in crisis periods) has every likeli. hood of concealing, even from its advocates, the political implications it conrains.

Berween pure disinterestedness and cynical servility, thete is room for the relationships established, objectively, without any conscious intention, between a producer and an audience, by virtue of which che prac. tices and artifacts produced in a specialized and relarively autonomous held of production are necessarily over-determined; the functions they fulfl in the internal struggles are inevitably coupled with external func tions, those which they receive in the symbolic struggles between the fractions of the dominant class and, in the long run, berween the classes. 'Sincerity' (which is one of the pre-condirions of symbolic efficacy) is only possibleand real—in the case of perfect, immediare harmony be rween the expectations inscribed in the position occupied (in a less consecrated area, one would say 'job description') and the dispositions of the occupant; it is the privilege of those who, guided by their 'sense of their place,' have found their natural site in the feld of production. In accordance with che law chat one only preaches to che conver d, a cricic can onty 'influence' his teaders insofar as they grant him this power because they are structurally artuned to him in their view of the social world, their tastes and their whole habitus. Jean-Jacques Gautier, for a long time literary critic of Le Figaro, gives a good description of this elecaive affin iry between the journalist, his paper and his readers: a good Figaro editor, who has chosen himself and been chosen through the same mechanisms, chooses a Figaro literary critic because 'he has the right tone for speaking reader'. 'lf tomorrow 1 started speaking the language of Les Temps be longer tead me or understand me, so they would not listen

things or Persons which 'suit' him is teptesen ted by all acts of co-option in felloweling, friendship or love which lead to lasting relations, socially sanctioned or not. The social sense is guided by the system of mu. (ually reinforcing and infinitely redundant signs of which each body is thc bearer—cloching, pronunciation, bearing, posrure, manners—and which, unconsciously registered, are the basis of antipathies' or sympathies'; the seemingly most immediare elective afinities' are always parrly based on the unconscious deciphering of expressive features, cach of which only takes on its meaning and value within the system of its class variations (one only has to think of the ways of laughing or smiling noted by ordinaiy language). Taste is what brings together things and people that go together.

diteafnities which orient social encountets, discouraging socially discordantrelacionships, encouraging well-marched relarionships, without these operations ever having to be formulated orher than in the socially innocent language of likes and dislikes." The exrreme improbability of the particular encounter berween particular people, which masks rhe probability of interchangeable chance events, induces couples to experience their murual election as a happy accident, a coincidence which mimics transcendent design ('made for each other') and intensifies the xnse of the miraculous.

Those whom we find to our taste put into their practices a taste which does not differ from the taste we put into operation in perceiving their practices. Two people can give each other no better proof of the affinity of their tastes than the taste they have for each other. Just as the art-lover finds a raison d'êrre in his discovery, which seems to have been waiting for al eternity for the discoverer's eye, so lovers feel 'justified in existing', as Sartre puts it, 'made for each other', constituted as the end and raison detre of another existence entirely dependent on their own existence, and cherefore accepted, recognized in their most contingent features, a way oflaughing or speaking, in short, legitimated in the aibitrariness of a wy of being and doing, a biological and social destiny. Love is also a way which makes the perceiving, naming subject (we know the role of giving in love relations), the cause and the end, in short, the raison

ness': making explicit what is given presupposes and produces a suspension of immediate attachment to the given so that the knowledge of probable relarionships may become dissociated from recognition of them; and amor fati can thus collapse into odium fati, hatred of one's destiny

## Symbolic Struggles

To escape from the subjectivist illusion, which reduces social space to the conjunctural space of interactions, that is, a discontinuous succession of abstract situations,2 it has been necessary to construct social space as an objective space, a structure of objective relations which determines the possible form of interactions and of the representations rhe interactors can have of them. However, one must move beyond this provisional ob. jectivism, which, in 'treating social facts as things', reifies what it describes. The social positions which present themselves to the observer as places juxtaposed in a static order of discrete compartments, raising the purely theoretical question ofthe limits between the groups who occupy them, are also strategic emplacements, fortresses to be defended and captured in a field of struggles.

Care must be raken to avoid the obiecrivist inclination (which is expresad and reinforced in a sparial diagram) ro mark out regions of chis space thar are defined once and for all in a single respect and delimited by clearly drawn frontiers. For example, as has been shown in the case of industrial employers and as will subsequently be shown in the exemplary case of the new middle-class fractions, a particularly indeterminate zone in chat sire of relarive indeterminancy represented by the perire bourgeoisie, each of the classes of positions which the ordinary classifications of starisrics require us ro construct can itself function as a relatively autonomous feld. One only has to substitute more stricrly defned occupational positions for the relatively abstract categories imposed by rhe necessities of starisrical accumula ton in order ro see rhe emergence of the nerwork of competitive relations which give rise, for example, ro conficts of comperence-conflicrs over the qualifcations for legitimate pracrice of rhe occuparion and rhe legiaimate scope of the practice-berwcen agents possessing differenr qualifcarions.

educational services (special teachers, remedial teachers, aphelpers etc.) lished order so as to secure a recognized place within it.

The model of social space that has been put forward here is not only limited by the nature of the data used (and usable), particularly by the practical impossibility of including in the analysis structural features such as the power which certain individuals or groups have over the economy, or even che innumerable assnociated hidden profirs. If most of chose who carry our empirical research are often led co accept, implicitly or explicitšy, a rheory which reduces the classes to simple ranked but non. antagonistic strata, this is above all because the very logic of their practice leads them to ignore what is objectively iriscribed in every distribution. A distribution, in the statisrical but also the political.economy sense, is the balance-sheet, ar a given moment, of what has been won in previous battles and can be invested in subsequent battles; it expresses a srate of the power relation between the classes or, more precisely, of the struggle for possession of rare goods and for the specifically political power over the distribution or redistribution of profit.

Thus, rhe opposition berween theories which describe rhe social world in the language of stratifcation and chose which speak the language of the class struggle corresponds to two ways of seering the social world which, though difficult to reconcile in practice, are in no way mutually exclusive as regards their principle. 'Empiricists' seem locked into the former, leaving the latter for 'theorists', because descriptive or explanatory surveys, which can only manifest classes or class fractions in rhe form of a punctual set of distributions of properties among individuals, always arnve afrer (or before) cthe battle and necessarily put into parentheses the struggle of which this distribution is the product. When the statistician forgets that all the properties he handles, not only those he classifes and mures ut also rhose he uses todssify and measure, re weaponsand prizes in the struggle berween the classes, he is inclined to abstract each s a moment in a struggle in which the agents put back into rhe srruggle, which may imply a power over the struggle itself and there fore over the capital held by others.

moment of che different types ofcapital, defines the structure of the field; The definition of che legitimate means and stakes of struggle isimn fact one of the stakes of rhe struggle, and rhe relative efficacy of rhe meansof controlling the game (the diffetent sorts of capical) is itself at stake, and therefore subject to variations in che course of the game. Thus, as has consrantly been emphasized hete (if only by use of quotation marks), the notion of overall volume of capital', which has to be constructed u order to account for certain aspccts of practice, nonetheless remains a theorerical artifact; as such, it could produce thoroughly dangerous effects if everything rhat has to be set aside in order to construct it were forgotten, not lea t che fact that the conversion rate berween one sort of capicaland another is fought over at al times and is therefore subject to endless Auctuations.

Dispositions are adjusted not only to a class condition, presenting itself as a set of possibilities and impossibilities, but also to a relationally deáned position, a rank in che class structure. They are cherefore always re lared, objectively at least, to the dispositions associated with ocher positions. This means that, being adapted' to a particular class of conditions of existence characterized by a particular degree of distance from ne cessity, class moralities' and 'aestherics' are also necessarily situated with respect to one anorher by the criterion of degree of banality or distinc: tion, and thar all the 'choices' they produce are auromatically associated with a distincr position and therefore endowed with a distnctive value This occurs even without any conscious intention of distinction or ex plicit pursuit of difference. The genuinely intentional strategies through which members of a group scek to distinguish rhemselves from the group immediately below (or believed ro be so), which they use as a foil, and to identify themselves with the group immediately above (or be lieved to be so), which they chus recognize as the possessor of the legiti mate liestyle, only ensur ful ecacy, b intetional reuplication,for bourgeoisie.

ir is no accident that each group tends to recogniae its specific values difference which is also, very often, the latesr conquest, in the structural and genetic deviation which specifically defines it. Whereas the workiag classes, reduced to 'essential' goods and virtues, demand cleanness and practicaliry, the middle classes, relatively freer from necessity, look for a warm, cosy", comfortable or near interior, or a fashionable and original garment." These are values which the privileged classes relegate to sec. ond rank because chey have long been theirs and seem to go without saying, having actained intentions socialy recognized as aesthetic, su ch as the pursuit of harmony and composition, chey cannot idenrify their distinction with properties, practices or 'virtues'which no longer have to be datmed or which, because they have become commonplace and lost their distincrive value, no longer ca» be claimed.

is rare and constitutes an inaccessible luxury or an absurd fanta hose at an earlier or lower level becomes banal and comelegated to the order of the caken-for-granted by the

Medisal service, cultural intermediaries

without any intentional pursuit of distinctive, distinguished rarhappens sense of good investment which dictates a withdrawal from outmoded, or simply devalued, objects, places or practices and a move is no accidert that tasres in painting or music so often follow paths which revivals and rehabilitations apart, reproduce histoty in biogra. phy) So the search for distinction has no need to see itself for what it is, and all the intolerances-f noise, crowds etc.—inculcated by a bourgeois upbringing are generally sufhcient to provoke the changes of terrain or object which, in work as in leisure, Jead rowards the objects, places or activities rarest at a given moment. Those who are held to be distinguished have the privilege of not worrying about their distinction; they can leave it to the objective mechanisms which provide theit distinctive properties and to the sense of distinction' which steers them away from everything 'common'. Where the petit bourgeois or nouveau riche overdoes it', be. traying his own insecurity, bourgeois discretion signals its presence by a sort of ostentatious discretion, sobriety and understatement, a refusal of everything which is 'showy', 'fashy' and pretencious, and which devalues irselfby the very intention of distinction.

When asked how they would dress if 'invited to dinner by their husband's boss\*, 33 percent of the wives of junior executives or office workers (32 percent of manual workers' wives, 29 percent of farm workers' wives) say they would 'wear their best clothes', as against only 19 percent of the wives of industrial and commercial employers, senior executives and professionals, of hom 81 percent say they would change their clothes 'but without putting on their Sunday best', compared with 67 percent of the middle-class wives and 68 percent of the working.class wives (C.S. XLIl).

Sttuggles over the appropriation of economic or cultural goods are, simultaneously, symbolic struggles to appropriate distinctive signs in the m fclassied, cassifying goods or practices, or to conserve or subvert the principles of dlassifcation of these distinctive properties. As a conseqence, the space of life-styles, i.e., the universe of rhe properties whereby given moment, of the symbolic struggles over the impothem. The dynamic of the field in which these goods are produced and reproduced and circulate while yielding profits of distinction lies in che strategies which give rise to their rarity and to belief in their value, and which combine—in their very opposition—to bring about these obiec. tive eects. Distinction', or bett, 'class', the transhigured, misrecogniz. able, legitimate form of social class, only exists through the struggles for the exclusive appropriation of the distinctive signs which make natural distinction'.

Culture is a srake which, like all social stakes, simultaneously presup. poses and demands that one take part in the game and be taken in by it; and interest in culture, without which thete is no race, no competition, Is produced by rhe very race and competition which it produces. The value of culture, the supreme fetish, is generated in the initial investment im. plied by the mere fact of entering the game, joining in the collective belief in the value of the game which makes the game and endlessly remakes the competirion for the stakes. The opposition between the au. thentic' and the 'imitation', 'true' culture and 'popularization', which maintains the game by maintaining belief in the absolure value of tle stake, conceals a collusion that is no less indispensable to the production and reproduction of the illusio, the fundamental recognition of the culrural game and its stakes. Distinction and pretension, high culture and middle-brow culture—like, elsewhere, high fashion and fashion, haute coiffure and coiffure, and so on—only exist through each other, and it is the relation, or rarher, the objective collaboration of their respective pro duction apparatuses and clients which produces the value of culture and the need ro possess it. It is in these struggles between objectively complicir opponents that the value of culture is genetated, or, which amounts to the same thing, belief in the value of çulture, interesr in culture and the interest of culture—which are not self-evident, although one of rhe effects of the game is to induce belief in the innateness of the de sire to play and the pleasure of playing. It is barbarism to ask what culture is for; to allow the hypothesis that culture might be devoid of intrinsic interest, and that interest in culture is not a narural properry unequally disttibuted, as if to separate rhe barbarians from the elect—but a simple social artifact, a particular form of fetishism; to raise the question of rhe interest of activities which are called disinterested becaus they offer no intrinsic interest (no palpable pleasure, fot example), and so to introduce the question of the interest of disinterestedness.

The struggle itself thus produces effects which tend to disguise the very existence of the struggle. If the relationship of the different classes with culture can be described indifferently either in the language voured by Maurice Halbwachs) of distance from the centres of cultural values or in the language of confict, this is because the symbolic strug gles berween the classes have no chance of being seen and organized a such, and are bound to take che form of competirive struggles helping ro reproduce the gaps which are the essence of the race. It is no accident hatapart from Proudhon, who is inspired by his petit-bourgeois horror of the dissolute, slovenly life-style of artists, and by what Marx calis his irae hominis probi', to dare to expose the hidden, repressed face of the petite bourgeoisie's ambivalent idea of artthere is pracrically no questioning of art and culrure which leads to a genuine objectification of the cultural game, so strongly are the dominated classes and rheir spokesmen imbued wirh a sense of theit cultural unworthiness.

Nothing is further from such objectification than the arristic denunciation of the art which some arrists go in for,"º or the activities grouped under the term counter-culture. T'he lartet merely contest one culture in the name of another, counterposing a culture dominated within the relatively auronomous field of cultural production and distribution (which does not make it the culture of the dominated) to a dominant cultute; in so doing they fulfil che traditional role of a cultural avant-garde which, by its very existence, helps to keep the cultural game functioning.

The dominated classes intervene in the symbolic struggles to appropriare the distinctive properties which give the distinctive life-styles their physiognomy and especially in the struggles to define the legitimate properties and the legitimate mode of appropriation, only as a passive ref. erence point, a foil. The nature against which culture is here constructed is nothing other than what is 'populat', 'low', 'vulgar', 'common'. This means that anyone who wanrs to 'succeed in life' must pay for his accession to everything which defnes truly humane humans by a change of nature, a 'social promotion' experienced as an ontological promotion, a process ofcivilization' (Hugo speaks somewhere of the 'civilizing power of Art'), a leap from nature to culture, from the animal to the human; but having internalized the class struggle, which is at the very heart of cultute, he is condemned to shame, horror, even harred of the old Adam, his language, his body and his tastes, and of everything he was bound to, his roots, his family, his peers, sometimes even his mother tongue, from which he is now separared by a frontier more absolure rhan any taboo.

The struggles to win everything which, in rhe social world, is of the otder of belief, credit and discredit, perception and appreciation, knowledge and recognition—name, renown, prestige, honour, glory, authotity everything which constitutes symbolic power as a recognized power--al. ways concern the 'distinguished' possessots and the 'pretentious' challengers. Pretension, the recognition of distinction that is affimed in the effott to possess it, albeit in the illusory form of bluff or imitation, insPires the acquisition, in itself vulgarizing, of the previously most distinctive properties; it rhus helps to maintain constant tension in the symbolic goods market, forcing rhe possessots of distinctive properties generated by this dialecric is by denition inexhaustible since the do them.

Nietzsche's 'enlightened elitism' comes close to the scientifc truth of the yet chat even this smasll number of truly culrured men was nor posible un. less a great mass, derermined, fundamentally, against their narure and only by a seductive illusion, engaged in the pursuit of culture; that therefore noching should be publicly divulged of the ridiculous disproportion between the number of truly cultivated men and the vast appararus of cul. ture; that the peculiar secret of culture was this: that countless people work for culture, apparently for themselves, but ultimately only to make a few people possible.

The symbolic struggles over being and seeming, over the symbolic manifestations which the sense of appropriateness, as strict as che old sumpruary laws, assigns to the different social conditions ('Who does he think he is?"'), separating, for example, natural 'grace' from usurped 'airs and graces', are both based and focussed on the degree of freedom from one's 'starion' thar is allowed by the specihc logic of symbolic manifesta. rions. Countless social arrangemenrs are designed to regulate the rela rions berween being and seeming, from the laws on the illegal weanig of uniforms and decorations and all forms of usurparion of titles, to the gentlesr forms of repression aimed at recalling to reality, to the 'senseof reality', of limits, those who, by exhibiring the external signs of a wealth associated with a condition higher rhan their own, show that they think themselves' something berrer than they are, the pretenrious pretenders, who berray by their poses, their postures, their 'presentation' that rhey have a self-image too far out of line with the image ochers have of them. to which they ought to cut down their self-image ('climb down').

The relarion to One's own body which is expressed in a cerrain manner ad bearing—the 'natural' self-confidence, ease and authority of someone who feels authorized, che awkwardness or arrogance of someone who brings sus. picion upon his legitimacy by his too parent ned to assert s one of the most visible traces of early and recurrent exposure to archetypal situations which are very unequally probable for the different social classes. It is one of the most powerful social markers, and for this reason che forced or af fected ease of che bluffer is always exposed to che demystifying irony of an incerlocuror who 'sees through' it and refuses to be 'taken in'.

This Since the surest sign of legitimacy is self-assurance, bluff—if ir does not mean that the strategies of prerension are lost in ad. derermined by the struggles berween agents over the representation of cheir position in the social world and, conscquenrly, of that wor'ld.

As is shown by the inversion of the relationship berween spendmg on food and on clothing, and more generally, on substance and on appear. ance, as one moves from the working class to the petite bourgeoisie, the middle classes are commilted to the symbolic. Their concern for appearance, which may be experienced as unhappy consciousness, sometimes disguised as arrogance,3 isalso a source of their pretension, a permanent disposicion towards the bluff or usuipation of social identity which con. sists in anticipating 'being'by 'sceming', appropriaring the appearances so as to have the realiry, the nominal so as to have the real, in trying to modify the positions in the objective classifcations by modifying the rep. resentarion of the ranks in the classification or of the principles of classifcation. Torn by all the contradictions between an objectively dominated condition and would-be participation in the dominant values, the petit bourgeois is haunted by the appearance he offers to others and the judgement chey make of it. He constancly overshoots the mark for fear of fall. ing shorr, betraying his uncercainty and anxiery about belonging in his anxiery to show or give the impression that he belongs. He is bound to be seenboth by the working classes, who do not have this concern with cheir being-for-orhers, and by the privileged classes, who, being sure of what they are, do not care what they seem-as the man of appearances, haunted by che look of ochers and endlessly occupied with being seen in a good light.

Deing so linked to appearance—the one he has to give, nor only to do   
his job, that is, play his role, to 'make betieve', to inspire confidence or   
respect and esenr his social character, his presencation', as a guarantee   
f the r services he offers (as is the case with salespeople, busi  
leuhan to a Berkeleian vision of the social world, reducing ich being is never more than perceived being, a menof a rheatrical performance (représentasion). His amche social structure, sometimes compounded by che

second lead, second hddle, éminence grise, agent, deputy or stand-in, suspect irs true foundation): eveiything predisposes himto perceive social world in terms of appearance and reality, and the more he has sonally had to 'climb down', the more inclined he is to observe manj tions and imposcures with che suspicious eyes of resentr

But the site par excellence of symbolic stuggles is the dominant cles irself. The conflicrs berween artists and intellectuals over che definition of culrure are only one aspect of the interminable stniggles among che dif. ferent fractions of the dominant class to impose the definition of che le gitimate stakes and weapons of social struggles; in orher words, to dehne the legitimate principle of domination, between economic, educational or social capital, social powers whose specific eficacy may be com. pounded by specifcally symbolic efficacy, that is, the authority conferred by being recognized, mandated by collective belief. The struggle berween the dominant fractions and the dominated fractions (themselves consti ruting felds organized in a structure homologous to that of the dominant class as a whole) cends, in its ideological retranslation--and here the dominated fra.crions have the initiative and the upper hand—to be orga nized by oppositions that are almost superimposable on those which the dominant vision sets up berween the dominant class and the dominared classes: on the one hand, freedom, disinterestedness, the 'purity' ofsub limated castes, salvation in the hereafter; on rhe other, necessiry, selfinteresr, base material satisfactions, salvation in rhis world. Ic follows that all the strategies which intellectuals and artists produce against the 'bourgeois' inevitably tend, quite apart from any explicit intention, and by virtue of the structure of rhe space in which rhey are generated, to be dual-action devices, directed indifferently against all forms of subiection to material interests, popular as much as bourgeois: 'I call bourgeois who ever chinks basely', as Flauberr put it. This essential over derermination explains how the 'bourgeois' can so esily use the art produced against chem as a means of demonstrating cheir distinction, whenever chey seck to show thar, compared to the dominated, they are on the side of disinceresredness, 'fteedom', urity' and che 'soul, hus rurning against the ocher classes weapons designed for use against themselves.

It is clearly no accidenr chat the dominant art and the dominant art of living agree on the same fundamental distinctions, which are all basc

render to immediare, easy satisfactions and economy of means, pcople, the most indisputable affirmation of capital as the capacity ne to ignore them.

Thus tinguistic case may be manifested either in rhe cours de force of going beyond wat is required bystrictsy grammatical or pragmatic rules, making optionai liaisons, for example, or using rare words and tropes in place of common words and phrases, or in che freedom from the demands of language or situation that is asserred in the liberties raken by those who are known to know berter. These opposing strategies, which place one above the tules and proprieties imposed on ordimary speakers, are in no way mutually exclusive. The two forms of conspicuous free. dom, unconventional constrai nt and deliberate trausgression, can cocxist at different moments or different levels of the same discourse, lexical 're. laxation' may, for example, be counterbalanced by increased rension in syntax or diction, or the reverse (this is clearly seen in condescension strtegies, in which the gap thus maintained berwecn the Jevels of lan. guageis rhe symbolic equivalenr of the double game of assertmg distance by appearing to negare it). Such strategieswhich may be perfecrly unconscious, and thereby even more effective---are the ultimate riposre to the hyper-correction strategies of pretentious outsiders, who are thrown into self-doubr about the rule and the right way to conform to it, paralyzed by a reflexiveness which is the opposite of ease, and left without a leg to stand on'.

The speaker who can take the liberty' of scanding outside rules fit only for pedants or grammarians—who, nor surprisingly, are disinclimed to write chese games with the rules into rheir codifcations of the lmguistic gameputs himself forwatd as a maker ofhigher rules, i.e., a taste-maker, an arbiter elegantium whose transgiessions are not mistakes but che an. nunciation of a new fashion, a new mode of expression or action which will become a model, and chen modal, normal, che norm, and will call for new transgressions by chose who refuse to be ranked in the mode, to be included, absorbed, in the class defined by the least classifying, least marked, most common, leasr distinctive, least distinguishing property. bound to appear simulraneously as distinctive and differe in the sense of 'natura.l facility' is no more than ease in the sense existence. This perfec coincidence is the very definition ofease which, In return, bears witness to this coincidence of ‘is' and 'ought' and to the self-afirming power it contains.

The value placed on casualness and on all forms of distance from self stems from the fact thar, in opposition to the anxious tension of the challengers, they manifest both the possession of a large capital (linguis tic or orher capital) and a freedom with respect to that capital which is.& second-order affrmation of power over necessity. Verbal virtuosities or the gratuitous expense of time or money that is presupposed by material or symbolic appropriation of works of art, or even, at the second power, the self-imposed constraints and restrictions which make up the 'asceticism of the privileged' (as Marx said of Seneca) and the refusal of the facile which is the basis of all 'pure' aesthetics, are so many repetitionsof thar variant of the master-slave dialectic through which the possessors affrm their possession of their possessions. In so doing, they disrance themselves still further from the dispossessed, who, not content with being slaves to necessity in all its forms, are suspected of being possessed by the desire for possession, and so potentially possessed by the posses sions they do not, or do not yet, possess.3

## A Cosy Samovar-StyleBedroom

Isabelle d'Ornano, the Minister's sister-in-taw, has made ber bedroom the centre-piece of ber apariment. A barogue master piece.

I know how I want to live. Decorarion is a way of expressing it.' Scorning fashion and irs convenrions, she has applied this principle throughout her apartment---a rhapsody of colours, imiration green marble and Venetian blinds—and especially in her bedroom An a)- most timeless and yct very up-todate room, which also serves as [sabelle's study when she is working (markering the 'Sisley' range of cos. merics her husband launched three years ago), as a TV room for her fve children and sometimes, since it communicares with the receprion rooms, as a second salon for big dinner parties. Originally it was a big dull library, sumpruous and boring: she has rurned it inro something warm and 'cosy', as she puts ir.

First, by having a circular balcony built around the walls almost halfway up. ... By organizing movement in the room around a centrepiece: the bed. And quire a bed it is! ... 1sabelle d'Ornano likes 'mus cular' furniture and wanted 'a bed which suggests a gondola.'Her up holsterer had his work cur out for a year and a half!

By fouting all the classical rules and combining different styles of furnirure—in facr, every style A Louis XVI inlaid roll-top desk. upholstered 'rub' easy-chairs and Sec ond Empire freside chairs, an enormous eighrcenthcenrury ctystal chandelier from the La Granja works, boughe from a Madrid anrique-dealer, one or two lirtle larenineteench-century English stands holding plants, books and an orchid ('the only flower that lasts'), two glass lamps bought for a song at Drouor, with modern shades, rwo riered bedside tables recently ordered from a cabinet-maker. By mingling colours and fabrics with a certain audacity. ... By sprinkling the ensemble, nor with knick-knacks ('they're pointless') but with dozens of photos . With wicker baskets full of bric-à- brac. With children's mugs bristling wich pencils. With novels, exhibi. rion catalogues, magazines on interior decorarion (she cuts out the

useful addresses and sticks them in scrapbooks), scattered ail over rhe place. And other very personat details, such as the painted faience ciles surrounding the chimney-piece ... In short, byadoptingan original, personal style of decoration. So much so that designer Henri Samuel, who acted as technical adviser, teplied, when I asked him to dehne this bedroom: 'It's pure d'Ornano, and that's a complimenr!'

D. de Saint-Sauveur, Le Figaro-Magazine (Madame Figaro), 7 October, 1978.

One's immediate intuition should be followed—for the purpose of testing it--when it sees in the reachers' taste for the austerity of pure works, Bach or Braque, Brecht or Mondrian, che same ascetic disposition chat is expressed in all cheir pracrices, and when it senses in chese seemingly innocent choices the symptom of a similar, but merely beteerhidden, relationship to sexuality or money; or when it divines the whole view of the world and of exisrence that is expressed in the taste for the delights of boulevard theatte or the lmpressionists, for Renoir's rosy women, Boudin's sunlit beaches or Dufy's stage sets.

As is clearly seen in theatre or painting (but che same is true of the ocher arts), what emerge through the discontinuous or disparate indices which have to be used to take measurements are two antagonistic relations to the work of art or, more precisely, two modes of aesthetic appropriaron expressing two opposite asset structures. Thus, how is one to explain why the median price paid for a theatre cicket rises from 4.17 francs among tcachers (less than is paid by junior execurives in the pri. vare sector, 4.61, and the public sector, 4.77) to 6.09 for public-sector senior executives, 7.00 for the professions, 7.58 for private-secror executives, 7.80 for commercial empioyers and 9.19 for industrialists which gives one theordinaryhierarchy of the fracrions distribured by volume of economic capital?® And how does one explain why the hierarchy of the fractions is inverted if their rate of represcntation in the heapest theatres is considered? If the elective afhniry between relatively inexpensive avant-garde thearre and the intellecrual fractions, or between the much more expensive boulevard theare and the dominant classres is understood superhicially-i.e., as simply a direct eftect of che relationship between economic cost and economic means-on is liable to forget that the expectred 'cultural' bencht, each fraction expresses its conception way of appropriating it.

The same logic explains why the desired price makes the strongest absolute contriburion to the first facror brought to light by analysis ofthe correspondences of a set of characteristics of a sample of Parisian theares and rheir audiences (C.S. XIV). Or again, why the propensity to judge the ad. mission charge of a museum as being clieap or very cheap rises very strongly, in relation to the ordinary hierarchy, as one moves from the fractions (relatively) rich in culrural capiral co those rich in economic capital, with the professions being distinguished only by a bi-modal distribution (reasonable—very cheap).

For certifed or apprentice intellectuals, activities such as theatre-going. visits to exhibitions or art' cinemas, performed with a frequency and reg. ularity which take away any exrra-ordinary' quality, are in a sense gov. erned by the pursuir of maximum cultural profic' for minimum eco. nomic cost, which implies renunciation of all ostentarious expense and all gratifications orher than those given by symbolic appropriarion of the work ('You go to rhe theatre to see the play, not to show off your ward. robe,' as one of them said). They expect the symbolic profit of their practice from the work itself, from its rariry and from their discourse abour it (afcer the shov', over a drink, or in their lectures, their articles or their books), through which they wil endeavour to appropriate part of its distinctive value. By concrast, for the dominant fractions a 'nighr out' at the thearre is an occasion for conspicuous spending. They 'dress up to go our' (which costs borh rime and money), rhey buy the most expen sive sears in the most expensive theatres just as in other areas they buy 'the best there is'; they go to a restauranc afrer the show.? Choosing a che atre is like choosing rhe right shop."º macked with al rhe signs of'qualiry’ and guarantccing no 'unpleasant surprises' or lapses of taste': 2 playwright who knows his job, who commands the springs of comedy the porential of a sicuarion, the comic or biting force of rhe mor juste', in shorr, a goldsmith or jeweller, a past mascer in the 'art of construction. who has the tricks of the dramatisr's att' ac his fingertips;" actors known for cheir ability to encer the 'twenty-fourcarat'role he offers them and to place the eager docility of a perfect thespian technician at the service' of the polyrechnician playwrighc;'² and a play which gives every sorr of en joyment, wichout a hint of self-indulgence or vulgarity', which is de signed to relieve a well-balanced audience by bringing ir back iato balance with healthy laughrer', because it only asks questions which everyone asks himself', ftom which rhe only escape' is humour and incur able optimism'.

In contrast to 'bourgeois' theatre, the opera or exhibitions (nor ro mention premietes and gala nights), which are the occasion or prerext for social ceremonies enabling a select audience to demonsrrare and expe rience its membership ofhigh sociery in obcdience to the inregrating and distinguishing rhythms of the 'sociery'calendar, the art museum admits anyone (who has the necessary culrural capiral), ar any momenr, without any constraints as regards diess, thus providing none of the social gratifcations associated with grcar 'society' occasions. Morcover, unlike the thearre and, espccially, music-hall and variery shows, it always offers. the purified, sublimared plcasures demanded by the pure aestheric and, racher like the library in this respect, it often calls for an austere, quasi-scholastic disposition, orienred as much towards the accumularion of experience and knowledge, or the pleasure of recognition and deciphering, as to. wards simple delight

The middle-class visitors and the reachersand secondarily rhe engineers are those most inchned to associate the museum with a library ('Whar do I like most? A library. It conrams works of value and you need to want to go there.' Engineer, Cambrai, age 44, Lille Museum). The same groups are most inclined to combine contemplation with acrs of recording (e g., raking notes) and accumulation (e.g., buying reproductions). One also finds that the reachers are those who most often refuse to dissociate direct exper ence of rhe work from erudite knowledge (chey are the ones who most ofren refuse the judgeinent, 'I don't need to know who painted rhe picrure or how; whar counts is wherher it is pleasurable to look ar').

dious. Loking at everything systematically is tiring. It was a self-imposed discipline. Ir is understandable thar as one moves from avant-garde concerts or plays, museums with a high cransmission level and low tourist appeal or avant-garde exhibitions to specracular exhibitions, major concerts or the 'classical' theatres, and finally to the boulevard theatre and variety shows, the rare of representation of the different fractions distributed in order of decreasing cultural capital and increasing economic capirat—i.e., teachers, administrarive executives, engineers, professionals, indusrrial and commcrcial employers—tends to change systemarically and continuously, so that the hierarchy of the fractions disrributed by their weight in the public tends to be inverted.' The teachers and the industrial or commercial employers occupy symmerrically opposite positions in the diagrams of corrtlation berween the rares of attendance ar rao categories of shows presenting opposite properties: on the one hand, concerts and art exhibirions, on the orher, varicty shows and trade exhibirions. In each case the members of the professions and the senior executives are in an intermediare position. The professions, under-represented in use of libraries and museums, are more represented among exhibirion visitors than museum visitors, and go to the theatre relatively frequently (to 'boulevard' plays or musicals rarher than classical or avant-garde theatre).

The museum, a consecrated building presenting objects withheld from private appropriation and predisposed by economic neutralization to undergo the 'neutralization' defining the 'pure' gaze, is opposed co the commercial art gallery which, like other luxury emporia ('bouriques', antique shops etc.) offers objects which may be contemplared but also boughr, just as the pure' aeschetic disposirions of the dominated fractions of the dominarit class, especially teachers, who are strongly overrepresented in museums, are opposed to those of the 'happy few' in the dominant fractions who have the means of materially appropriating works of art. ## A Grand Bourgeois 'Unique among His Kind'\*

S., a lawyer aged 45, is the son of a lawyer and his family belongs to the Parisian grande boxrgeoisie. ## 'For my personal enjoyment'

For his counrry house in Burgundy, a very big one (a thousand square mecres co furnish, afcer all!'), almost a 'mistress', he bought furniture from 'a rag-and-bonc man': 'l came across a chap, a junk dealer, who had solid wood furniture, real countty-sryle, and 1 bought other birs and pieces, stuftied animals', including srufied boars 'ohich outragcd every'one, except me ... because they are funny. ## 'Heiriooms? Don't make me laugh'

The inherited objects with which he has furnished the house are of lirrle interest co him. When his wife reminds him thar there are some, he replies: 'Heirlooms? Don't make me laugh, rhere have been chree bits of furnicure.' She enumerates them: 'When we werc gerting married, Aunt X. popped off. ## Cooking is a state of mind'

He is a busy man and does nor have much cime in the middle of the day; he almost wishes they'd in vent a pill so you didn't have to ear in the daytime. . . . Coking is a srate of mind.' Real cooking, the sort where it takes t wo days to make a madeira sauce, where you keep things simmering away for ages, that's whar 1 call cooking, and it's an att. There's no prepararion, it isn't an arr any more.'

## 'I prefer pleasure'

Among the books in his library, left by a grandmorher' or 'bought in a sorr of shop in the rue de Pro. vence', there are leacher-bound '17th-cenrury-ish' books, 'more for bo he now kees in Paris are Sort of philosophical or religious and a bit of potty' his novels and so on' (about two thousand books are in his country house. He docs not belong to any clubs. ('Some people love wearing unif orms, belonging to this or that ream or club; I'm my own man, an individualist at all costs.') He no longer hunts 'because you have to go a long way, it's rather tiring and it's also rather expensive' He plays rennis occasionally, on holiday, and goes sking 'for pleasure'. 'I'm not going to struggle up a mountain with skis over my shoulder when there's a ski-tow beside me. I like coming down more than going up. 1 pref'cr pleasure.'

painting, the starue, the Chinese vase or che piece of antique furniture belongs to the world of objects available for appropriation, thus taking its place in the series of the luxury goods which one possesses and enjoys without needing to prove the delight they give and rhe raste they illus rrate, and which, even when nor personally possd, belong ro the sta rus attributes of one's group, decorating che oftces one work in or the salons one frequents.

Princes de Cognac is produced in limired quanrires—only a few chousand bottles a year—and is only found in selecred stores and resraurants' (Connaissance des Arts, November 1973, p. 16).

The ostenrarious, gratuitous expense implied in the purchas of price less' object is the most indisputable way of showing the price one is pre. pared to set on thmgs that have no price, an absolure testimony of the irreducibiliry of love to money which only money can buy: 'What is true luxury? Refinement; a necessiy for those who can afford it, and a key for those who, when they see it, train their eye, cheit taste, and can find it in the simplest object, a scarf, a skirt, a pair of shoes, a gament, if it is beauti ful. But expensive? Haure couture is absolure rigoı r and che absolute has no price' (Mare Bohan, aruisric director of the Christian Dior company, in. terview).

‘You have to be Perrier-Joı et and own the finestvineyard on the slopes of Cramant to afford this foly and to offer it to others: a Champagne made from the most expensive grapes in the world. But the 78 centilitres in this 18th ccntury bortle have no price for a lover of Champagne for its own sake. Espccially when it's an exceptional vintage' (Conaissance des Arts, No vernber 1973, p. 14). ‘To highlighr your personaliry, we creare luxurious, delieare warches ... made only in limired edirions. Each of our watches brings out the personality of the discriminating purchaser ... You will become rhe owner of an exclusive, precious timepiece' (ibid., p. 81).

One might be reading Marx, who writes: 'Man is initially posited as a privare propetty owner, i.e., an exclusive owner whose exclusive ownership permirs him both to preserve his per onality and to distinguish himself from other men, as well as relate to them . .. private property is man's personal, disringuishing and hence essenrial existence The ap propriation of symbolic objects with a material existence, such as paintings, raises the distinctive force of ownership to the second power and reduces purely symbolic appropriation to the inferior status of a symbolic substitute. To appropriate a work of art is to assert oneself a the exclu sive possessor of the object and of the authentic raste for that object, which is thereby converred into the reified negarion of all hose who are un worthyof possessing it, for lack of the inaterial or symbolic means of rifice everything for ir'.

The consumption of works of art, an almost too obvious illustration gasrronomy, environment, riding, gardening, fshing, oenology' bling, the regniat ropics of the Parisian journal Connaissance

(which is to the distinguished rasting of narure as Con. Campagne the privilege of thos who have ancient roots. Owning a culture, 'There's norhing easier than pickling gherkins, my mother claims. As long as you pick them by a new moon, as long as you sweat rhem in cooking salt for twenty-four hours in a sroneware por after rubbing them with a linen clorh, the only sort that is rough enough. As long as you add dried but nor bone diy tarragon and bend chem to pack chem in sightly . . . etc.' (Connaissance de la Campagne, September 1973). A por of 'home-made gherkins', 'made to grandma's recipe' and brought to the rable with the appropriare verbal accompaniment—as when exhibiring che lirtle picrure by an eighteenth-century French masrer' spotted at the antique-dealer's, or the 'exquisire lirtle piece of furnitute' unearthed in a junk shop—symbolizes a squandering of time and a competence which can only be acquired by long frcquentation of old, cultivated people and things, thar is, membeiship of an ancient group, the sole guaranree of passession of all the properties which are endowed with the highest distinctive value because rhey can only be accumulated over time.

What is at srake is indecd 'personality', i.e., the qualiry of the person,   
which is affrmed in the capacity to appropriate an object of quality.   
The objects endowed with the grearcst distinctive power are those which   
most clearly artest the quality of the appropriation, and thercfore the   
qualiry of their owner, becaus their possession requires time and capaci  
tes which, requiring a long investment of time, like pictorial or musical   
cuhure, cannor be acquired in haste or by proxy,and which therefore ap   
Par as the surest indicarions of the quality of the person This explains like artistic consumprion, demand pure, pointless exwhose marker value gives them least of it to

rant dimension of whar is ofered at receptions-and, of course, t all lci sure accivities whose symbolic value alwas lies parrly in the capaciry to pending such valuable time to no purpose.

Of all he conversion rechniques designed to creare and accumulare symbolic capital, the purchase of works of art, objectified evidence of 'personal taste', is the one which is closest to the most irreproachable and inimitable form of accumulation, thar is, the inrernalizaion of distinc tive signs and symbols of power in rhe form of natural 'distinction',pr sonal 'authority' or 'culrure'. The exclusive appropriation of pricless works is nor withour analogyto the ostentatious desrruczion of wealth; the irreproachable exhibition of wealth which it permit is, simulra. ncously, a challenge thrown down to all those who cannor dissociate rheir 'being' from their 'having' and attain disinrere redness, thesupreme affrmation of personal excellence. And as is shown, for example, by the primacy given to lirerary and artistic culrure over scientihc or technical culrure, the exclusive possessors of a 'vast culture' behave no differently when they flmg imro the porlarch of social encounrers the time they have spenr without thought for immediate profit in exercises as prestigiousas they are useless.

The dominant fracrions do nor have a monopoly of the uses ofthe work of arr that are objecrively—and sometimes subjectively—oriened rowards the exclusive appropriation which artests the owner's uniquc 'personaliry'. ## A 'Truly Classical' University Teacher

Jean L., aged 36, an alumnus of the Ecole Normale Supéricure, has the agrégation in physics. Hc is now a maiire-afistans (senior lecturer or assisrant professor) in one of the Paris universiries and lives in the northwesrern suburbs. His farher (an agrépé in grammar) was a [ycćc reacher and his grandfacher a primary teacher. His wife, a pharmacist's daughrer. is a dentist. She reaches at the Paris Dental School and also runs her own practice.

## 'A Louis XIII convent ble from the Flea Market'

Preferring sobriety' and discrerion', Jean dislikes far cushions and heavy currains', and 'apartments done up by interior designers.' Now we have one or two old birs of furniture that we've picked up, ral anriques ... a Louis X]ll convent cable that some one sporred for us in the Flea Market, a Iouis XIll chest thar isn't bad', found in an antiqque shop in Amiens. 'Of course, we won't be getring any Louis XIll chairs—for one thing they're terribly expensive, and anyway, if rhey're genuine, they're not cven solid. So we'll ger some made for us in the same style regards pricesenjoy it, bur much time to it. I haven't gor much of a taste for ir, but my wife preciate it, al he same. I know I'll enjoy ir, I know she's much more sensirive to it than I am... There's one thing l'd really like to buy, I haven't done so yet, bur I somecimes look: it's old sciencifc instru. ments, because they used to make someremarkable things in the last century and three or four centuries back.'

## 'I prefer The Art of Fugue on the organ'

He has no hi-f ('I'd quite like to have one, bur for me it's not essenrial'), but he does have ‘a recordplayer thar isn't bad' ('mono, [ bought it for 600 francs four or hve

pessimism of people with problems-the opposition between material and menral comfort, with intimate, discreer interiors and traditional French cooking, and aestheric and intelectual invention, with che rasre for exotic dishes or (by inversion) pot-luck, 'studied' interiors, or (by in version) chose that are easy to maintain, furniture from the Flea Market and avant-garde shows.2

The oppositions between systems of purely aesthetic preferences that are symbolized by rhe antithe'sis Kandinsky/Renoir can be replaced in thesers of choices constituting life-styles simply by considering the characteristics of an audience such as that of Connaissance des Arts. This relarively expensive, luxury cultural journal, which is at the same time an advertising inedium for the luxury goods trade, especially in objets d'art, no doubc gives a fairly accurate picture of the groups who are united by 'bourgeois taste' and who are brought together by the most sclecr and also most expensive cultural events--smart exhibitions, gala performances at the Paris Opera, premieres. major concerts etc. The common features of the private-ector executives and professionals--and the many fewer, and therefore strongly over selected. reachers and industrial employerswho make up this readership are a liking for expensive, prestigious activities (golf. riding) and for cultural practices oriented ar least as much towards material appropriation as rOwards merdy symbolic appropriation, frequenting theatres and gallerics (predomina.ntly right-bank), aucrion rooms, antique shops and luxury boutiques. The bour geois taste' which characterizes them is opposed not only to inrcllecrual going) to the 'middling' raste of the great majotity of the industrial and especially the commercial employers, great readers of Aut-founal, who ap luxury cars (C.S. Wheres the intelectual' factions expet racher from the artist a symprvatc, domestic part of a fundmentally dual life, spuriously unified in and chrough a spurious division against itsełf disinterestedness against interesı, art against money, the spiritual agamst the temporal. Polite political newspapers, discreetly politicized or ostentatiously depoliticized, decorarive journals and coffee-table art books, Blue Guides and cravel stories, regional novels and biographies of great men are so many screens to hide social reality. 'Bourgeois' theatre, a scarcely 'de-realized' represenrarion of one of che forms of bourgeois existence, with irs beautiful stage stts, pretty women, facile advenrures, frivolous conversacion and rea.ssuring philosophy (any other combinarion of the nouns and adjectives is equally valid), is no doubt the form par excellence of the art the 'bourgeois' recognizes hecause he recognizes himself in it. The bourgeoisie expets from art (not to mention what it calls literature or philosophy) a reinforcement of its self-assuran e, and, as much our of suficiency as insufficiency, it can never really recognize the audacities of the avant-garde, even in the most highly neurralized arts, such as music. And for every enlighrened amateur who has understood rhar it costs norhing to be, like Proust's Mme. de Cambremer, in art, always on the lefc', there are many present-day admirers of Flaubert or Mahler who have the same impatience with disorder, even symbolic, and the same horror of movement', even arristically sublimated, as cheir counterparrs in the pa5t

In fact, a class or class fraction is defned nor so much by its overall judgecteristic of some fractions of the bourgeoisie and pecice   
larnd by che fie of production. Thus the anti-intellec tualism of che domithe dominant class may be expressed in the choice of   
in the intellect inclined to anti-intelectualism by their own position those most The furthet one moves from the 'purest' genres, completely purife:d of all reference to the social world and

ing, or behaviour, such as sexual or polirical conduct, contains a life-style. Like the old women in Australian myths, who overzthrow the structure of relarions between the generations by magically conserving a prize or frequenting young revolutionaries ar an age when orhers pursue honours and culrivate the powerful) can somerimes call into question one of che most deeply buried foundations of the social order, Spinoza'sobse quium, the disposition of those who have 'self-respect and feel encirtled to command respect.

One has to take inro account the whole logic of the field of artistic production and its relationship to the held of the dominant class to understand why avant-garde artistic production is bound to disappoint bour geois expectationsunequally, and always in the shorr term.26 It is no accidenc that the tasre for the artisric avant-garde appears in the analy. sis only at the end of a serie of oppositions. In fact, evetything takes place as if, although it embodies artisric legitimacy, the artistic producers' taste for the avant-garde defined irself in a quasi-negative way, as the sum of the refusals of all socially recognized casres: refusal of chemiddle-of-theroad taste of the big shopkeepers and parvenu industrialists, the groccrs' pilloried by Flauberr and others as one incarnation of the 'bourgeois', nd especially, perhaps, at presenr, the perite bourgeoisie, led by cheir culrurl pretension to the products of middie-brow culture or the most accessible products of legitimate culture (such as light opera or the ea iest boule vard theatre), which are imme:diately devalued by their new audience; re fusal of bourgeois taste, i.e., the typicaly right-bank luxury taste, which has some accomplices among the artists; and, fnally, refusal of rhe teachers' pedantic tasté," which though opposed o bour geois taste is,in che is defined of popular raste (kitsch, pop art). The artist' life- tyle which

tion to enounce.

which is an integral part of their specifc activity. Thus artists (and intel. leruals) exchange money, which they could ocherwise earn, for time, the ume which ha to be spent wichout counting to produce objects which ofen (in the short rerm) have no markets, and to discover' objects and places whose rarity and value they help to produce, antiques, back-street reswurants, new shows etc.; and they quasi-exclusively appropriare collecrive goods or services (museums, galleries, cultural broadcasts). Variations in spare time and in che relation to time are, together with unequal propensity rocon ume, among the factors which make parterns of expenditure very uncqual guides to the resourees of cach class.

## The Mark of Timne

In no other class is the opposition berween the young and old, the challengers and the pssessorsand also the opposition between the senior members of the class and che newcomers, which cannot always be su. petimposed upon it (since, in some sectors at least, the most senior are also the mosi precocious)—more determinane chan in the dominant das, which can ensure irs own perpetuation only if it is capable of overcoming che crises that are liable to arise from the competition beru cen the fractions to impose the dominant principle of domination and from the succession srruggles within each fraction. The differences between the generations (and che potencial for generation conflicts) increase with the magnicude of the changes that have occurred in the dehnitions of cupational positions or in the institutionalized means of access to ns in che relative importance of che diffierent routes

The opposition between the oldest, who valorize the most ascetic ethical consciencious'friend, as against 245 percent of the under4s, 39 percent che over-45s choose dynamic', while 15 percent of the under 45s and 54 percent of the over45s choose 'conscientious'. (Similar variations, always more marked among executives and engineers, are observed for 'derermined', which varies like dynamic' or 'wel-bred', which varies like conscientious'.) A sinilar evolution (no doubt linked to a gencral increase in cultural capiral) is found in castes in legitimate culture: thus, che younget executives and engincers more often choose Rhapsody in Biue (32 percent2 against 17.5 percent) or the Four Seasons (47 percent and 24 percent), less ofren L'Arlésienne (14.5 percent and 28 percent), Hungarian Rhapsody (32 percent and 58.5 percent), Blue Danube (13 percent and 30.5 percent).

These historical variations are particularly signifcant in the case of the fractions most direcrly linked to the economy, the engineers and executives, but they have, in a more insidious way, affecced the whole of the dominant class. They are likely to pass unnoticed because they always manifest chemselves in combination with age, so that chey can easily be taken for an effect of biological or even social age racher than generation, and because they are translated into trajectories, i.e., individual historics which are so many responses ro a given srare of che chances objectively offered ro a whole generation by collecrive history.

The liberal professions' (doctors, ar least) have succeeded in main. caining rhe craditional definition of cheir job and the competence il requires by defending, among other things, the most Malthusian condicions of access, thus in a sense escaping from history and the divisions berween the generations. By contrast, categories such as those of che exccutives and engincers bring cogether individuals separared boch in tra jecrory and in generation, in the sense of the set of products of a single mode of generation associated with a similar pattem of objective chances. In fact, because of the duality of the modes of access, by qualification and by promotion, and the corresponding divisions which prcvented an orga nized defence of the inodes of access and of che corresponding privileges. qualifed and non-qualifed candidares.30

Furthermore, changes in the economy have been refect merical and hierarchical relationships between the diffe nt managerial dustrial groups, their capital, their management and cheir patents, has caused a revaluation of the qualifcations and instirutions leading to these positions, Sciences Po, ENA or HEC on the one hand, Polytechnique and the other engineering schools on the other hand, and, simuitaneously, redistributed the chances available to rhe fracrions of the bourgeoisie who use these institucions. Thus, as a result of changes in the economic structures, and chiefly through its use of the Paris Institurs des sciences politiques, situated ar the bottom of the specifically academic hierarchy of the schools of power', the Parisian Brande bourgeoisie has re appropriated. perhaps more completely than ever, the commanding positions in the economy and the civil service (provoking collective and individual ripostes by Polytechnigue graduares, inore and more of whom are caking a detour tnrougn ria do,nur through Harvard, Columbia or M.l.T.).

ln addition, the emergence of a large number of new positions, which promise profits at least equivalent to those of thc established positions and strictly predictable career targets, but wichout oftering the sam e guaranrces of security, is tending to subvert the systern of differe nlial chances of profi. At least in the phase when both their risks and their profits are greatest, these new positions situated at critical points in the social structure are most attractive to those whose social otigin has provided them with an inclination cowards risky investments, the social connections needed in order co make them and the information needed in order to succccd in chem-

Thus, wichin a category such as that of the enginers, it is possible to disbers of the bourgeoisie. catenory of the
