## A Young Executive Who 'Knows How to Live'

Michel R., an adverrising execurive working in a Paris agency, rhe son of the managing direcror of the Ftench subsidiary of a leading multinational corporation, srudied in a private Catholic secondary school in the 17rh arrondissement and rhen at the Paris Polirical Science Insrirure; his wife, Isabelle, che daughrer of a provincial industrialisr, also went ro Sciences Po and works for a weekly news-magazine. He is 30, she is 28; they have rwo children. They live in Paris, in a modern five-roomed aparrmenr in the iSth arrondissement. They like things to be 'snug and cosy'. They have no inrerest in 'home-improvement' and havt kept cheir aparrment as chey found it. The decorarion is all the work of our predecessor. 1 didn't much like the green in the dining-room, it was rather gloomy, bur we got used ro it, and I get bored working on the place 1 live in.' '] hate thar beading on the doors, I'd like to ger rid of ir. The pseudo 16rh- or 18th-cenrury vencering or whatever it is all over this modern aparrment is ghastly; 1 pur up arith ir but ir gers on my nerves,' says Michel, who has removed some of ir but 'couldn'r face the rest.'

## 'The world of my grandparents'

'Iheir Aat is partly the world of my gra.ndparenrs, my grear-grandparents, who were grands bourgeais': piccures by Michel's grandfather, 'who spent his whole life painting and never did a day's work'; other pictures which they have been given—a Boudin, a Bissière, and a Folon. But ists in general and especially Bon. Pissarro', docs nor like them

Nor does he like still lifes, or 'problem pictures': Fernand Léger, and stuff like that, is horrible, it's thick and heavy ... two or three Braques can be interesting to look at, bur when you see two hundred of rhem, ail done the same way, it gers a bir reperirive, a bit night. marish. . . . 1 tend to go for land. scapes . . . My grandmorher's got a Bonnard in her apartment, the one really valuable pictiure she owns. We won't inherit ir because there are lors of relarives. But it would be wonderful ro own ir. I go for things thar are ourside fashion, sort of rimeless.'

Isabelle doesn't entirely agree with her husband: There are some rhings 1 like a lor in modern art, bur rhat's because 1 like the colours . For example, Vieira da Silva (she hesirates over the name), 8oudin, who is behind you, 1 like a lor.' They both occasionally visit galleries, and exhibirions two or thre times a year. Tbey went to the Braque exhibition and expect rosee rhe lmpressionists ar Durand-Ruel

## 'We'd seen a lot of mediocre stuf'

The dining-room rables and chairs mahogany. 18rh-cenrury English style, were bought in London 2s soon as they were married. 'I don't know if we'd do the same thing today . . . I can't remember why we bought them, bur from a bourgeois point of view they must be a good investment.' After visiting many a rique shops, they 'ánally chose

lcro. It's exempt from customs duries. You just have to pay VAT [value-added tax]: In the living. room they have some modern and some old furniture, a bookcase from Roche-Boboi s, a sofa from a shop in i village suisse. ...

Michel's car is 'only an old Prugeor 404', whereas his bosses 'have got Jaguars, the direcror of the agency has an Alfa-Romeo, a Lancia'. 'From time to rime, rhey say, "So you aren't trading it in?" They'd be relieved if 1 got a new car. They're afraid I'll visit clients in my car.'

## The right sort of clothes for people in 2dvertising'

Though at weekends, at home, he wears 'a filthy pair of trousers', for work he dresses with grear care and elegance. He buys his suirs at Barnes, the adverrising man's tailor, in the rue Victor Hugo in Paris. They're the cight sort of clothes for pcople who make it in adverrising-English cloch, Prince of Wales checks with a rouch of luxury. Nor the sort of rhing civil servants could ear, and bank managers couldn't get away with it either. In banking you need a plain shirr; banking isn't showy, whereas in advertising, peo. ple pur every penny they earn into clothes ... In my business we're colsrantty classifying people, rhere ure social classes, castes, and it's a matter of fitting a product to the tight caste. When someone new comes to the agency, we size them up at a glance. A guy with a velvel suit and big lapels is compensaring for something, he's not very sure of himsclf, he wants to make an impression.' For a while, the agency had 'a ánance manager from a veıy modest background; when he arrived he was so badly dresed rhar it was bad for business .. . he was dressed like a junior clerk.' Wearing a suit writh narrow lapels, narrow bortoms, a bir short, in a loud colour with a shirt that doesn't march and a narrow tie, for example, by our scanda.rds, that's grotty.'

## 'Not the way some secreraries do it'

'On the other hand, being roo fashionable is not much better,' adds Isabelle, who dresses their children 'in fairly classic style', paying particular artention to the colours. I like a pretty smocked dress from rime ro rime, and English overcoaes. Of course ir's done wrirh an eye to fashion, but nor in the silly way some secreraries at L'Express do it, dressing their children in the new kiddyboutiques, Mini-this and Mini-that, with things thar cost a forrune and are a miniature copy of the parenrs clorhes.' These secreraries 'are all weli dressed, by my srandards, they have perfect colour sense.... There were some girls vho arrived, who dressed with rerrible rasre, it was vulgar, cheap, racky, just awful .. and then, after four years, they finally got it right.' Isabelle has a friend who is always exquisitely dressed . .. the eflect is always srunning, i mean, it's chic, ir's got real class .. . She pays artencion ro every little derail.’ Michel's farher is also 'very well-dressed, norhing is ever over-donc, his colours are always perfect. Refinement without the slighrest ostentation. He has a railor in Íondon.' Michel's morher is 'equally restrained. Always a beauti-

## 'Provincial clerks who áll their gardeas with gnornes'

'The pelits bourgeais have no raste, it's a phrase weoftenuse, rhough we're wel aware it's racist.' (Michel and Isabelle constanrly indicate in this way their disrance'from the ways of the older gencration of the grande bourgeoisie--perhaps especially when speaking to a sociologist, albeir a friend's sister.) Isabelile's parents, provincial industrialists, are more severe or less tolerant: About the pesit-bourgeois phenomenon--pro. vincial clerks who fil their gatdens with gnomes, windmills and similar rubbish, Mummy used to say, "It's ourrageous; making things like rhar ought ro be lanned." ## 'A very light meal, a vegetable dish and some cheese

In cooktng, as in clothing and furnishing. they manifesr che same re fusal of pretension, of 'excess', rie same sense of distinction'. Without being a wine-buff who can rell one year from another"', Michel is 'something of an expert'. His farher-inlaw, who has a huge cellar, has slowly initiated them. When they visit him, they drink 'Margaux 1926, amazing things that they don't stock in restaurants any more .. With colleagues, for example, I'm the one who chooses rhe wine. They can see I know whar I'm doing. I dont go for some miser able Cahors, for example. I know ir doesn't taste the seme as a Saint-Esrèphe or a Saint-Emilion-. Hardly anyone knows how to

choose wine, so as soon as you like someone who knows how to live.' Ar home, they have a few magnums of Veuve Clicquor 1926 which they bough: good-quality things; we drink some two or the rintes a month and chen rhere are the Christmas presents ... If it's whisky, we drink Chivas, we're rather demanding.' They buy their claret direct from the producer 'at fifteen or eighteen francs a bortle, forty francs in the shops, a very good wime.' In the evening, when they are alone, they eat 'a very light meal, a vegerable dish and some cheese.' 'They like to invite friends for 'escalopes à la crime, sauté de veae, curry, sałmon thar we buy occasion ally.: Michel is parricularly partial o 'fore de canard frais aux raisins cooked in the coals, and confit d'oie. He has earen in 30 of the 10 best restaurants in Paris lisred in the Gault et Millau guide, ofren business lunches ('I only paid for ten of them'). He also likes tradirional French food ('plain home cooking, in other words') but is nor keen on lirrle local restaurants or 'forcign dishes. Italian or Chinese cooking'.

## 'Healthy exercise'

Michel and Isabelle are members of a golf club: 'it's marvellous, bur the people aren't. They're mosrly gaga. In France it's always a cerrain type of people, whereas in japan 30 per cent of the population belong ro 2 golf club.' Their initial subsctiption cost them 10,000 francs; they no longer go, because of the children, but they have kept up their mem bership. Michel no longer plays tennis: It's very stressful .. you have to keep movirig all che rime, running up to the ner. Ic gives me

backache... Golf is less hard on ond-hand racing bicycles and lst summer they went for long rides: It's healchy exercise.' When he was a student,Michel used to go to the TNP (Théârre Narional Populaire) in Aubervil. liers, to see Gombrowicz or Brechr, but he no longer goes; they have recently been to the Cartoucherie de Vincennes and the Paris Opera: they go to the cinema fairly often. They have a hi-fi system and a raperecordcr; they listen to the classical record reviews on France-Musique. Michel particularly likes Mozart (T he Marriage of Figaro), Schube:rt Quartets, Bach, and the Beerhoven Quarrets. 'l haven't learnt ro appreciate purely modern stuff, Webern and so on.’Michel does not read many novels but incends ro read Tony Duvert (he likes books thar are a bit srimulating'; he read Robbe-Grillet's Les Gommes but 'couldn'r get into ir'). He mainly reads ‘anything in socia) studies'— psychology, economics.

codres (execurives), a sort of junction where one encounters former engincers, endowed wirh a traditional cultural capital (usually scientihc), who exercise a (delegated) managerial authoriry; administrative execurives who have achieved promotion (in the public sector, by internal examinarion) by dint of a great effort ro carch up scholastically (evening classes etc.) that is rarely sanctioned by diplomas (except purely inrernal' titles); young gradu. ares of the grandes écoles (Polytechnique and ENA), trained for the public sector but destined, in many cases, ro move to high positions in the private xcror; and, finally, executives of a new type, generally in markering or management, deriving their educational capiral (when they have any) from the business schools or political science insritutes. and inclined to a life-sryle which differs from chat of the 'old bourgeoisie' from which many of them onginaie

Everythiog seems to indicare that the difterenr modes of access (from che ranks or by qualihcation) lead ro very diferent careers. The possessors of qualificarions move much further and faster, especially in rhe second half of their careers (all observers agree rhat autodidacts have their best chance in the period from enitry to mid.carcer, .e., to about age 35-40). ## Model Executives

A rapid analysis of the exccutive opportuniries' advertised in Le Monde in the course of a single wreek (in July 1973) is suffcicnt 1o identify the sel of characreristic fearures of the new breed of market. ing-oriented managers required by the new forms of busine organizarion:

Whether 'product manager'. 'sales engineer [these two terms are in English in the oriiginal text], 'dcp uty sales direcror', 'assistant financial manager' or 'general sales manager, he must above all be a 'negotiator' and a 'communicaror', and be

skilled in 'lop-level contacts': able to act with 'diplomacy'; adaptable to 'contacts at all levels'; 'accusromed to contacts with senior civil ser. vants, excellent negoriaror'; \*capaciry for high-level conracts'; 'top-level contacts and negoriation'; 'negotia. rion with banks'; take charge ofre. lalions with Government departments, represent the frm on national negotiating bodies'; 'rasre for conracts and morivarion'; 'rasite for problem-solving and human relations, highly arriculate;

and in interral negotiations, which means, for a head of sales management: 'an on-going co·ordination funcrion berween sales division and general managcment': for a chief buyer, 'this position entails full control of liaison berween a marketing [English in original] departmens and a producrion unir'; for a sales engincer, 'the negotiarions he will have to conduct wrill require an understanding atritude and the creativ. ity which his compctence justihes'; 'co-ordinator berween clients, sales sales service and agraduate of one of the nw busness schools, HEC, INSEAD, Ecole Institur Supérieur des Affaires (ISA), generally listed togetber perhaps rounded off by a 'period of study in an American university.

endowedwith the aptitudes ard assi. tudesimpliod in working for multina. tional or strongly export-oriented firms ('English absolurely indispensable; English vocabulary:'marketing', 'merchandising' [last two terms in English } clc., and Anglicisms: 'op portunité' erc.);

having a 'tasle for team-work' and a capacity for 'animating' otbers (the substirute for autbority): 'dynamic and adaprable ... he musr be prepared to join a ream'; 'to direcrand motivate a stalf of twenty';

creative and dymamic (like the firm it. self, which is 'rapidly expanding into the export field'): lead, animare, form a team'; 'dynamism, drive, eapacity for synthesis and tcam-work';

young ('young executive'); mobile: he must expect ro travel frequently, particularly to the USA.

A similar profile emerges from 2 typical report in L'Expansione 64 (June 1973), p. 139)—cnricled 'TheNew Rare Birds', on'new ecutive positions' thar 'are wel paid for lack of applicants': A dir forward planning will always stan at 70,000-80,000 francs a year. 60,000 agement controller at beeween and 90,000. There's a strong if possible from Peat Marwick

80,000, a "senior"110,000 70.000 to seaf ererelolont as come up s0,000 o 80,000 this year. in the major banks there are even some at 110.000-130,000 Hypeket [shop ping center] managers have been moving up in the same way. This year, five challengers have broken 28'ay: theservicing manager, che bolel director. themerchandising[in English] manager (within the marketing [English] plan, he endeavours to improve his product's posirion in the new distribution circuits, the basic merchandiser [Einglish] goes round the hypermat. ket shelves trying to get the maximum display "foorage" for his company's products), the business methods anal yst (he analyses the company's system and srandards; like audiróts, his starting salary depends to a large extent on the practices he has been trained in), and the plant manager (the Anglo-Ametican origin of this posirion means that the ideal candidate is one who has experienct in a chartcr-accounting [sic] llitm). Andl whar of the furure? Two new rare birds are on the horizon:

the marketing auditor and the publicrelations auditor.'

The porttair of the modern manager, as drawn in 1973, seems to have changed recently, no doubt because the recession is creating condi tions more favourable to the old style of management (rhere is again a demand for the 'leader of men' someone who, as an informant put it, 'can say no wirhout explaining'- and an increased demand for production specialisrs and sales manag. ers (rained 'on the ground') and also because the engineering schools have reacted to the rise of the management schools {for example, the creation of the Institure for the Sciences of Action at Polytechnique in 1977). According to a survey pub. lished in Le Nouvel Économiste (November 8, 1976), which questioned the personnel directors of 5,000 companies, firms still look for 'open mindedness', 'dynamism', 'capacity to adapt and relate', 'ability to synthesize' and 'self-motivarion', but they also insist on 'loyalty' (at Saint-Gobain) and 'team spirit' (BSN and Oréal). Some 49 percent said they atrached importance to candidates'views on polirics and trade unions, 33 percent said they did not (18 percent did nor reply).

firm, the prots accruing from their cutrural capital are at least partally appropriate by those who have power over this capital, i.e., rhose who possessthe mic capiral needed co ensure the concentrarion and urilzation capizaì. It follows from chis that their position in the dominantc an ambiguous one which leads them to à highly ambiv. och ro the firm and to the 'social order". When making demar ds o in protest, they are acruated as much by their concern to ma ween gitimare disrance, esrablished by academic verdicrs, be cing reat like them, as by rhe sense of a real solidarity of condition; nd ordinary workers, or by meritocratic indignation at greater or lesser extent, depending on rhe current stare of their in complerely possess nor completely ignore and refuse.

polirical or cultural power. Bringing into the lower positions of the do inant class the perir-bourgeois dispositions which have brought thiem rhose positions, they are opposed in almost all respecrs to the youngexec utives from che grandes écoics and often from bourgeois families, who occupy a large proportion of the new position created in che privates tor.31

The dispersion of this fraction, a simple category of bureaucratic statis tics, but also a movement of corporare defence which is affirmed in the representation it has and gives of itself. expresses the objective ambiguity of the posirion of the cadres', who are condemned to oscillare between collaboration and distance and therefore to be the object of annexation strategies which enable them to use their solidarity as a bargaining counter; it also stems from the fact that the term cadre is one of rhe titles which, as rewards atrached to the occupation of a position, are imponant weapons and prizes in the games which are played in and on the gap be tween the nominal and the real.

Although the opposition berween the new positions, with the correspond ing life-style, and the established positions does not exactly coincide with the opposition between the private sector and the public sector, it is mainly among the private-sector execurives that one finds the life-sryle charactensric of the new bourgeoisie'." And, although our survey only imperfectly captures the distinctive fearures of the new bour eoisie,33 it does re gisrera set of slight bur systematic oppositions berween the public-sector execu tives—more often originating from the working and lower classes, and closer to the engineersand the privare-sector executives---youn er, generaly of higher social origin, often graduates of HEC ot Sciences Po and closer to the professions. Private-sector execurives buy slighcly more ofcen from antique dealers; choose Dali and Kandinsky rarher than Vlaminck, Renoir and Van Gogh, who are preferred by the private-sector excçuttves choose the Ars of Fugue and theConcerto for the Lef: Hand rarher than and Scheberazade; Aznavour, Françoise Hardy and Brassens rather than

In short, differing little with respect to strict cultural competence discreet. of composers), private- and public-sector executives ate clearly (knowledge These differences would be even more marked did not each of rhe rwo eois origin, passing through high positions in the public sector and ofbour

But the new bourgeoisie is mainly characterized by its opposition to the old business bourgeoisie. Having achieved positions of power ar an earlier age, more often being graduates, more ofren belonging ro bigger, mort modern frms, the private-sector execurives are distinguished from the industrial and commercial employers, a traditional bourgeoisie with its spa holidays, its receptions and irs society' obligations, by a more modernist', 'younger' life-style, certainly one that is more consistent wich the new dominant dehnition of rhe dynamic manager (alrhough the same opposition is found among the owner-employers).

Thus, they much more ofren read the hinancial daily Les Ecbos (penetration index 126, industrial employers 91) and economic weeklies (224, indusrrial employers 190); they seem less inclined to invest their capital in propcrry; they much more ofren indulge in the sports thar are at once smart, active and often 'cybernetic', such as sailing, skiing, warer-skiing and rennis, followed by riding and golf, they more often play parlour gamcs that are both 'intellectual' and smart, such as bridge and especially chess. Above all, they identify more fully with the role of the modern execcutive who is oriented rowards the outside world (along with the public-sector executives and the engineers, they have the highest rate of foreign travel) and is open to modern ideas (as shown by their very frequent arrendance ar professional conferences or seminars). A final, apparently minor but very signifcant index of this opposition may be seen in the facr thar privare-sector executives far more often keep whisky in che house whereas the industrial and commercial employers emain most atrached to champagne, the drink of tradirion par excellence.34

This combination of luxurious' and intellectual'" properties, which   
seem incom because they are ordinarily associated with diamerri  
ally oppose ons in the dominant class, opposes the ne, business the teachess and to rhe craditional industrialists, cars, horel holidays, yachrs and golf evoke ethical dis.

## Business Tourism

'Reward seminars' and 'presrige seminars', as they are calied in the native language, are part of the range of hidden profts which modern firms offer their executives. 'Residential seminass' (those which last longer than one day and take place away from company premises) provide business for one of the most Aourishing industries. (It is estimated that 25,000 such 'scminars' were organizd in 1973.) They in. volve the hotel chains which specialize in 'business rourism' (such as Novotel, Frantel, Sofirel, P.L.M., Méridien, Mercure and Motelleric); the agencies (such as Seminorel) which promote a ser of horels spe cializing in seminars and conferences in exchange for 4 percent of curnover, consultancy frms (such as CEGOS or SEMA) and their social psychologists, who offer à la carte (sce the CEGOS 'caralogue' with its 294 'formars' ac rates ranging from 200 to 600 francs per day) creativity seminars'together with 'facilira.

## A Seminarist Confesses

The angelic smiles of the Club hostexes, a smooth check-in, punctual take-off (did I tell you we were going co Tunisia?)—ic has to be said, the journey was most agrceable, and so was our welcome at the village. 'Djerba la Douce' is a real little paradise. The groups from Lyons and Brussels arrived soon after, on special charter fights, like us. There was just time to slip into something more suited to the weather, and then we were intro. duced to the programme arid rhe

ae who has curned the mountain resort of Les Arcs into a setninar centre so as to keep il running due. ing the six dead' months of spring and aurumn. The economic weekly in which this information was found explains that spting and fall are ideal rimes for executive medita. tion'(L'Expansion, December 1973).'I'he winter low season is e served for 'updaring-reward seminars for successful sales teams', while che high scason receives the prestige seminars of 'top management' and big clients. Gilbert Trigano [president of Club Médirerranée], who can be regarded as an authority on these matters, says thac in twenty years' time, the Club wili be provid ing 50 percent pseudo-conferences and 50 percent real holidays.' Those who inquire into che causes of inflz. tion would do well to take account (among other hidden factors) of the fact thac businessmen, with their 'business tourism', their 'company gifts' and their company cars, are good business for businessinen.

## 1 Have Met Happy Seminarists

If you're looking for the perfecr venue for a successful conference or seminar, the place you want is Mond d'Arbois near Megève (Haute Savoie), in the hearr of the Mont Blanc Massif.

There 1 mer seminarists and conference-goers who were tanned, relaxed and happy to be there. They were there to work, of course, but in a secting thar helped them unwind and relax at the same cime.

On the work side, the Mont d'Arbois Hotel mneers the parricular requirements of each frm. It's fully equipped with conference and committee rooms for groups from 20 to 200, audio-visual faciliries, simulraneous translation booths. ... The arrangement is always 'made to measure.'

There is easy access by air (Mont d'Arbois is 90 minutes from Paris by Air Alpes). by rail (night trains from Paris) and by road (a choice of routcs).

On the entertainment side, it's Paradise. Depending on rhe time of year, you can ski in the heart of the fantastic Mont Blanc Massif or enjoy one of the most beautiful golf courses in France. Ocher facilities include tennis courts, an indoor swim. ming pool with a sauna and a gym.

For the less energetic, there are delightful walks in ourstanding countryside and the charming vil. lage of Megève with its many amen ities. In che horel irself, gala evenings are organized on request, with decorarions, a band and even visiting srars. Ar meal rimes, enjoy the impeccable cuisine and atrentive service offered by this top-class hotel.

And how much does it cost? The prices are actually very competitive, especially in September and December. Mr. Thommen or Mr. Ziegler ar che Mont d'Arbois Horel (rel. 50/ 21 25 03) will be glad to supply derails to help you compare

One final point: it has been proved that altitude stimulates che mental faculries. Mont d'Arb 1,300 metres .. so everyone will b in cop form.

on or public works fms, whereas 13.5 percen of French fms ar in ches category: that there is a relatively high propottion of subscribers in financial Gtablishments, services and distribution, in contrast to commercial firms, hotels, cafés and restauranrs (which represenr a very high proporrion of French firms); that, within their firms, 4.6 percenr of che readers are com. pany heads or directors; that 15 percent of them are sales executives, 12 perceat administrarorsand only 1 percent involved in production." Vl) thar the readers of Entreprise, of L'Fxpansion (who would prcsent similar, but no doubt still more accentuated, characteristics) and of Let Echos differ from the readers of other publications in that they enjoy ralking abour economics and business, rhey make frequent business trips oithin France and abroad, they use credit cards, they read foreign-language journals and they have conremporary furniture—a very equivoeal indicaror, alchough one does observe elsewhere a sysremaric link berween the new boutgeoisie and new urban areas, modern buildings and modern fumiture. Further features of this new bourgeoisie are indicated by the characreristics of che alumui of INSEAD: drawn toa large extent from the traditional pa. tronas (owner employer class), chey have acquired in this Atlancic-oriented institution (the reaching is largely given in English by an international reaching staf often trained in the USA) the capacities needed to achieve a successful reconversion rowards executive posirions (especially in sales and administrarion) in multinational companies. many of them U.S.-based. These 'dynamic young executives' read L'Expansion (63.5 percent), L'Express(3 percent) and Entrepris (33 percent), followed by Le Nouvel Obrvateur (22.5 percent); rhey ski (71.5 percent), play rennis (58 percent), go sailing (37 percent) and riding (23.5 percent). Their wives often work in he new occupations (10 percent of those who work are journal-

The classification struggle which is waged initially within firms, a acy berween production and publicity, between engi-F CC upational interests by imposing a scale of values which sets ing, in which each caregory of managers sœeks to adposed in every aspect of life-style to the 'research and development' man agers, who are generally engineering-school graduates, are more often of working- or middle-class origin and have pastimes very similar ro thoseof the teachers (mountaineering, walking etc.).

This means that changes in posrs (and their occupants) are inevicably accompanied by a whole effort at symbolic resructuring aimed at winning recognition in representations and therefore by a permanent strug. gle between those who seek ro impose the new system of classification and those who defend the old system. Taste is at the heart of these symt bolic struggles, which go on at all times between the fractions of the dominant class and which would be less absolute, less total, if rhey were not based on the primary belief which biuds each agent to his life-style. A materialist reduction of preferences to their economic and social condirions of production and ro the social functions of the seeminglymost disinterested practices must not obscure the fact that, in matters of culture, investments are not only economic but also psychological. Confics over art or the art of living, in which what is really at stake is the impo. sition of the dominant principle of domination within the dominant classor, to put it anocher way, the securing of the best conversion rae for the type of capital with which each group is best providedwould not be so dramatic (as they are, for example, in debates over the school curriculum) if they did not involve the ultimate values of che pe rson, highly sublimated form of interests.

The new bourgeoisie is the initiator of the ethical retooling required by rhe new economy from which it draws its power and profts, whose functionimg depends as much on the production of needs and consumers sumption, based on credit, spending and enjoyment. This economy capacity for production. It finds ardent spokesmen in the new executives of frms in tourism and joumalism, publishing ar

fashion and advertising, decoration and property developmerit. ema, living up to the innumerable duties entailed by the liberated' lifenew form of the sense of moral unworthiness.

Composed of members of the dominant fractions who have recon. and world view ocurring within the bourgeoisie as a whole, which (as table 2 shows) is itself in the vanguard of a general cransformation of lie style which is particularly manifest in the division of labour between the sexes and the method of imposing domination. This is the fraction which impors (from the USA) che new mode of domination, based on 'velvct glove' methods, at school, in church or in industry, and on the 'elaxed' life-style which srarts by euphemizing all the manifestations of soctal distance (especially sartorial ones) and by studiously rejecting the aristocratic stif ness that tends to create distance. After so much historical work on the symbolism of power, it would be naive not to see that fash. ions in clothing and cosmetics are a basic element in the mode of dominaton. And the whole opposition between the vieux jex and the nouveau jex, between the old-style authoritarian industrialisr and the modern managcs, tuned in to the latest techniques of business administration, public relations and group dynamics, can be read in the opposition be tween che pot-bellied, pompous patron and the slim, sun-tanned cadre, who isas 'casual' in his dress as in his manner, as 'relaxed' at cocktail paraesas in his relations wich those he calls his 'social parmers'.

relaxation in tension, case within restraint, a rare and highly improbable   
combination of aritagonistic properties. Everything rakes place as if what

often sking, golf or tennis, but also riding, underwarer fishing, sailing, hunting or yingas indicated by his 'arhileric looks, his sun-tane face and negarively, by his slimness'. As for his use of clothing, which, as I have shown in anorher study,39 is bound up with this relation to the body and rhe ethical dispositions it expresses, I need only quote an article in Le Figaro (1 December 1975) which, after telling us that Antoine Riboud, managing director of BSN, likes relaxed, sporty clorhes and that Gilbert Trigano (Club Méditerranée) rarely wears a tie, conhrms that cloching, like language or any orher property, enters the quasi-conscious strategies of ma nipulation: 'A young French businessman told us: "I have three sryles. When I go to Regional Development Council meerings, where I meet bankers and civil servants, I have to dress very correcrly. For normal business, my clothes are fairly 'way-our', because I work in furnishings, which is close to decoration. To visit factories, I dock in in a leather jacker and a polo-neck"(italics mine).

The life-style of the new ethical avant-garde very directly expresses the asset structure which is rhe basis of irs power and its conditions of exisrence. Executives in major national frms, public or privare (a somewhat arrifcial distinction, at this level), or heads of large, modern, often mul tinarional companies, they are not attached ro a place like the proprietors of small local frms, local notables whose prestige is inseparable from a world of real interactions and personal representation. The new executives look to a 'centre', their headquartets, for directives and promotiol: a large part of their prestige and power derives from academic qualifications which are themselves national or internarional; they are much less dependent on local privilege and prestige, which are increasingly deval ued as the economic and symbolic markers are unified, setting them in the national or international hietarchy. Convinced rhat they owe their position solely to their qualifcations and to the technical and 'human' guarantee, imbued with the economic-political culrure taught in the po litical science institures or business schools and with the modernistic eo nomic and social world view which is bound up with ir and which ther and industrialists (and the whole view of the world, and of France,

gers, the cult of 'lirerarure' (delegared to their wives) and eco  
srplernanag which they read in English. Being both the negation and the   
noITic new nse-they are the ones who transcend, the better to conserve.

power from educational capital acquired in formally pure and perfect academic competition, rarher than direcrly from economic capiral. The new culture of the new masters of the economy, a rationalization of their world view which tends to be ever more widely accepted as management science' is developed within the discipline of economics, provides them with the sense of possessing an authority of intellectual right over the conduct of sociery. Thus the opposition between the 'disinteresred' culture of the inrellecrual and the philistinism' of the bourgeois, preoccupied with the mundane interests of his practice, gives way, and not only among the new bourgeoisie, to the opposition between the gratuitous, unreal, unrealistic culture of the intellectual and the economic or polyrechnical culrure of 'modern managers', which sees itself as actionorienred but irreducible ro the triviality of mere practice'.

If old-style incellectuals continue to preserve an apparent monopoly over legitimare cultural practices, or at least over the defnition of these practices, this is perhaps due to the inertia of the institutions of cultural production and diffusion (in particular, the educational system) and rhe hysteresis of habitus, which are continuously reinforced by the fact thar literary and artistic culrure remains the form par excellence of disinerested culture, and consequently the most legitimate of the marks of distinction from other classes; and also, no doubt, to the division of la. bour bcrween the sexes, which confnes women to the privilege of judgin taste and the tasks of maintaining culrural capital in its tradicional form, reserving the new culture, turned towards action, business and power, for men. T'his only confirms the rendency of the ruling fractions conceive the opposition between the man of action' and the intellec. tual as a variant of the opposition berween male and female.

## Temporal and Spiritual Powers

The diftetent forms of capital, the possession of which defines clss memdistriburion of which determines position in the power fractions.

respond with a sort of paternalistic patronage to the symbolic provoca. tions of che arrists, in the name of a not-so-unrealistic image of what the producers of cultural goods really are, that is, deviant children of the bourgeoisie or poor relations' forced into alrernative trajectories; the pa trons may even find a pretext for their exploitation of the artists in their conspicuous concern to protect them from the consequence of their 'ide alism' and their lack of practical' sense." For their part, intellectuals and especially artists may find in the structural homology berween the rela tionship of the dominated classes to the dominant class and the relationship of the dominated fractions to the dominant fractions the basis of a felt and sometimes real solidarity with the dominated classes. At the same time they are able to play on the symbolic licence which the bourgeois\* are in a sense obliged to grant them, if only because they are obliged to recognize the supreme afhrmation of their spirirual point of honour in the negation of popular marerialism implied in the attistic negation of 'bourgeois\* marerialism.

Those who occupy rhe temporally dominant position within the dominant class are in fact placed in a contradictory situation, which inclines them to maintain an ambivalent relationship with culrural goods and those who produce them. Castigared by the intelectuals and arists for philistine materialism and anti-intellectual machismo, when theyd define themselves in relation to che dominated classes they have t invoke the cificallv defines intellectuals and artists.

other ecessies. The hope of an apocalyptic reversal of the temporal hierarchies which arises from the lived experience of the scandalous disparity berween the hierarchy of 'temporal'grearness and che hierarchy of spiritual' greatness impresses itself as a practical selfevidence on cultural producers, especially those whose position in the field of culrural production is homologous to the position of culrural producers as a whole in the field of the dominant class. Because they are opposed to those pro. ducers who offer produces direcily adjusted to the dominant raste and who are therefore temporally most recognized, just as the whole group of cultural producers is opposed to the dominant fractions, those writers and artists who are temporally—and remporarily—dominared, because theit products must produce their own markets, are the predestined bearers of the eschatological hopes which, irsofar as they support their 'inner-worlldly asceticism' and their sense of 'mission', are the crue opium of the intellectuals. The analogy with religion is not artificial: in each case the most indubitable transcendence with respect to stricrly temporal interest springs from che immanence of struggles of interest.

Cultural Goodwill

The members of the different social classes differ not so much in the ex cent to which they acknowledge culture as in the extent to which chey know it. Declararions of indifference are exceptional, and hostile rejection even rarer—ar least in the legitimacy-imposing situation set up bya cultural questionnaire reminiscent of an examination. One of the surest indications of the recognition of legitimacy is the tendency of the most deprived respondents to disguise their ignorance or indifference and to pay homage to the cultural legitimacy which the interviewer possesses in theit eyes, by selecting from their cultural baggage the items which seem to them closest to the legitimate definition, for example, works ofso caled light music, Viennese waltzes, Ravel's Bolero, or some grear name, more or less timidly pronounced.

Rognio o mat wor o prces wy    the d. at least in the relationship with the interviewer, who, because of the dissymmetry of che surve situation and his social position,s nvested with an aurhority which encourages che imposition of leaitimay.I may take the form of a simple rofession of fai- like idaion of good

74 percent of che respondents with only primary schooling enatre-going, Ihem They give 2n idea of the power to impose which culrural capital and 125 90 many well-mcaning cultural evangelists have done) or to ignore culural institutions can exert, far beyond che specifically culrural sphere. One fi ds, for example, thar lirerary insritutions are most recognized by those furchest from chem, chose who are cherefore least likely really to con. form to the srandards chey impose and guarantce (see cable 23).

While the propensity and capacity to form opinions on book prizes vary with reading and with knowledge of the prizes, a good number of those who do not read books (especially not prize-winning books), and who have no knowledge of lirerary prizes, nonetheless stace an opinion about them, and on the shole a favourable one (e.g., for question 5, 54 percent of those quesnioncd and 67 percent of those answering). This acknowledgement without knowledge is increasingly frequent as one moves down the social hicrarchy (as is shown by the widening gap berween the proporrion who buy neither prize books nor any books and the proportion who express no opinion on prizes or juries). Similarly, the proportion of opinions explicitly afrming the legitimacy of the prizes incteases as one moves down the hier. archy by occupation and education (columns 4b and sb), variations which cannot be alcributed to a direct imposition of legitimacy by the question itxelf (since question 4. which ofers a negative vie', varies in the same way and only receives fewer answers than quesrion S, no doubr because it more clearly apptars as appealing to competence and presupposing specific knowl. tdge of the literary world).

## Knowledge and Recognition

The whole relationship of the perite bourgeoisie co culture can in a sense om che considerable gap berween knowledge and recogni  
tion, the of che cultural goodwill which cakes different forms de  
pending on degree of familiarity with legitimate culrure, rhat is, on   
social che associated mode of cultural acquisition. The rising

## For Today's Career-Woman, Entertaining Means Planning

Because, for her, entercaining means getting home just in rime to ar. range the Aowers, check the table setting or slip into some riew clothes before the first ring of the doorbell. Serring aside all thoughts of her working life, the busy careerwoman turns back into a smiling, attentive hostess.

To bring it off—a welcoming apanment full of Aowers, service ap propriate to the style of the occasion, goed cheer with nothing left Ou, from the frst cigarerte to che frewell glass-the woman who spends her life away from home must make up for her absence by Possessing the talents of a domestic. Science consultant.

all, her home must nor give the impression of being a place she just pases through, but look like a comfortable, rehned retrear, as lively as her own personality. She considers herself the first guest in her home. And as she hasn't much rime to give to it, she wantsit always to be ready to receive people, even when she's out all day long or gocs of on long journeys. Result: a cosy, warm, congenial apartment which restihes to her prescnce and her preferences.

If she has a favourire colour, the décor makes it no secret. 1f she travels a lot, every homecoming brings some improvement. If she loves painting or reading, the walls and shelves proclaim her tastes. Through her home, a woman whose job often requires her to adapt to other people's opinions, rediscovers the very feminine pleasure of saying, 'What I like is ...

How does she enrertain? Thar, of course, depends on the layout of her home, whtther she has a dining room or a fixed or foid-away diningbar. Thar, plus her domesric potencial, will decide wherher she'l give dinncr parties in the traditional style or offer an elegant, humorous 'ready-to-serve' or \*self-service' buffet.

As for the cooking, the work is ofren remote-controlled. norebook in hand, wich evcrything planned in advance. She knows all the simesaving advancages of modern 1echniques: she has a well-rehearsed rep. erroire of dishes thar can be prepared tle night before; she's ex. pert at using rhe services of a carerer wichout depersonalizing her menus.

Thac's how coday's career-woman, a strategist of hospiraliry, combines charm with efficiency to give che il. lusion of being 100 percent devored to her home!

## An Up-to-the-Minute Welcome

Here, the working woman has worked for herself. ... The creator of the décor is none other than Françoise Sée, designer and interior decorator. She lives in a thrte-room apartment overlooking the Seine. and enccrtains in a big living-room divided into two parts: the 'salonsalon' and the 'salon-dimng-room'. lacquer table with a mat chrom support, and folding whire leather chaits byMcGuire. When folded perfecr dining-room piece with its dominared by the big corner sofa in velvet calfskin. In front of it stands a simple green lacquer coffee-table wich steel trimmin'gs.

## Hostess in Paris

Susan Train, a journalist with Vogae and a great travellet, has made her home on the Ieft Bank, in a quiet three-room apartment in a modera block. Her experience of beauty gives her decoration a virtuosity in the play of colours and materials, in successful contrasts of sryle. It'sall done with subtlety and a ciassicalty elegant taste. . . . Although Susan entertains in a relaxed way, shehas set aside a little dining-room, to avoid the discomfort of bufers of coffee-table picnics. So, even with no one to help with the serving, she can shut the door on it afrer the meal and forger the mess. During the meal, she uses a wicker basker to stack the dircy plates and cudlery. The hrst course is served in advanct and che rest of the meal is ready on a trolley with dish-warmers. Simplifed but rehned menus, for, being American and a globe-trotter, she collects delicious recipes and exotic ideas wherever she goes-

This middle-brow culcure (culture moyenne) owes some of irs charm, in the eyes of the middle classes who are its main consumers, to the refer. ences to legirimare culcure it concains and which encourage and justify confusion of rhe two..accessible versions of avant-garde experiments or accessible works which pass for avant-garde experiments, flm adaptatious' of classic drama and literarure, 'popular arrangements' of classica) music or 'orchcstral versions' of popular tunes, vocal inrerpretations of classics in a style evocative of scout choruses or angelic choirs, in short, eveiything that goes to make up 'qualiry'weeklies and quality' shows, which are entirely organized to give the impression of bringing legitimare culture within the reach of all, by combining two normally exclusive characreristics, immediate accessibility and the outward signs of cultural legicimacy.

Unlike legitimate, i.e., scholastic, popularization, which overtly pro. claims its pedagogic objectives and can therefore unashamedly reveal the means it uses to lower the transmission level, ordinary popularization cannor, by definicion, admit to being what it is, and the imposture ir presupposes would necessarily fail if ir could not rely on the complicicy of rtic consumers. This complicity is guaranceed in advance since, in culture as elsewhere. the consumption of imirations is a kind of uncons ious bluff which chiefy deceives che bluffer, who has most interest in taking che copy for che original, like the purchasers of 'seconds', 'rejecs, cul.price or second-hnd goods, who need co convince chemselves that 'i's cheaper and creares che same effct'.

## A 'Very Modest’ Nurse

Mme. B., whose parents had 2 small farm in the departmerır of che Lot, is 48. She has worked at the Saint-Louis Hospital in Paris for almost toenty years. She 'used to love school' and would have liked to be come a reacher, but had to leave a year afrer her CEP because her parents couldn't afford to keep her". Divorced at 28, wirh two children, she had to take a job in a hospital. While working, she srudied to become a nurse. Her son, aged 26, is married; her daughter, aged 20, a biology srudent, lives with her. Mme. B., who 'loves children', is 'horrified by large families' (\*a lot of worry').

## I'm always shocked when l see a mistake'

She very much regrets 'not having a higher level' (of education): 'I make do with what I've got bur l wish I knew more . . . It's very imporrant to be educated.’ Educarion srarrs with knowingthe rules of grammar and spelling. The girls who work wich me don't speak properly, they make words feminine when chey're masculine and vice versa, which proves they've no norion of grammar. Ir just shows you how primitive they are.' 'l'm always shocked when I see a misrake. Yesterday someone had written examens amener with er . . . I'd be really ashamed if I made misrakes as obvious as that. . . . Personally i would have writcen aporsés, but at a pinch ... I'd say amener 3 child, but apporter a test.'

## '1 hate prerentious people'

She likes simple, unpretentious people': '1 hare prerentious people, I

stride in withour so much as a by. your-leave. And why? Because per. haps you're not up to theit level. riors.' (She 'respects the people below her'.) She also detests people who are dirry': 'I chink that even if you're nor rich. at least you can be poor and clean. 1 sce patients com. mg in with dirty feet, it's disgust. ing. Why don't they go to the public baths?'

## 'It's very modest'

Her llar is 'very modest', two rooms plus kicchen, she lives in one room, her daugheer in the ocher. There is no dining room. 'it's very modest There's no washing machine, be cause I don't like washing machines. I do my washing by hand, 1 haven't got many clorhes to wash anyway, and then I wash chings in the boiler, I find that gers rhem very clean ... Anyway, you can't get the same rempcrarure in a washing ma chine ... I've got my fridge, my cooking stove with oven, I paid cash for them, I'm nor keen on hire purchase (insrallment buying). For big things, yes, a dining.room suite or a bedroom, then 1 might ger them on credir, bur fora little cookstove, or a washing machine or a fridge, I don't think it's necessary

shows and especially singers. In fact she 'hasn't really gor time': 'I'd rather have hours of sleep. for example, than ear a lot'(moscly she cacs grilled meat. salads, fruit). She 'lisiens to the radio, mainly France-lnter, for the music', she likes Frédéric François: 'I find thar his songs . .. the words are very meaningful, some of them . and ra critics of ‘quality' newspapers and magazines and all the alists and journalisr-writers) have invented a whole series of acility of cheir style) and in a conspicuous refusal of the heavydidacti while living in the unease of the inherently contradicroty role of a pre. senter' devoid of intrinsic value. The partial revolutions in the hierarchies which the intermediaries' low position in the feld of intellecrual produc. rion and rheir ambivalent relation ro rhe intellecrual or scientifc auchot. ities encourage them to carry out, such as canonization of not-yet-legitimate arts or of minor, marginal forms of legitimate aπ, combine wirh che efects of che allodoxia resulting from their distance from che centre of cuitural values to produce, through rhe mixrure of 'genres', 'styles' and 'levels', those objectified images of petir-bourgeois culture, juxtaposing easy' or 'old-fashioned' (i.e., devalued) legitimate producrs with the most ambitious products of the feld of mass production—anthologies of poetic' songs, wide-circularion ‘intellecrual' weeklies bringing together would-be authorirarive popularizers and self.popularizing authorities, television programmes uniting jazz and symphonic exrracrs, music-hall and chamber music, srring quarrers and gypsy orchestras, violinisrs and fiddlers, bel canto and cantara, prima donnas and songsters, rhe Pas de deux' from Swan Lake and Rossini's 'Cat Duer'. Nothing could be less subversive than these controlled transgressions which are inspired by a concern to rehabilicare and ennoble when they are nor simply the expression of a misplaced recognition of the hierarchies, as anarchic as it is eager. The petit-bourgeois spectarors know they have no need to be alarmed: they can recognize che 'guaran. rees of quality' offered by their moderately revolurionary taste-makers, who surround themselves with all the institutional signs of cultural at thority—Academician conttibutors to painless history magazines, Sor bonne professors debating on TV, Menuhins gracing 'quality' variety shows." Middle-brow culture is resolutcly against vulgarity.

Uncertain of theit classifications, divided berween the tastes they day choices.? In radio programmes, rhey combine a taste for lighr grammes or poetty readings. This is, of course, the categoiy which conains mosr of che keen photographers,jzz and cinema specialists, and its members are (relatively) much better informed abour flm directors than composers. Similarly, in the most legitimate arts, their preferences go with parricular frequency to 'accessible' (moyen) or déclassé' works, Buf. fer ot Vlaminck, Scheherazade, Rhapsody in Blue, La Traviata, L'Arlésienne or the Sabre Dance.

Ir is not difhcult to fnd in these works the properties which, at a given moment, predispose them to the creatment they receive from the new culrural intermediaries and rheir petit-bourgeois audience, when they aie not specially produced for this use. But it would be a mistake to locare in the works which enter into middle-brow culrure at a given moment the properries conferred on them by a particular form of consumption. As is shown by the fact rhat the same object which is today typically middlebrow—'average' (moyen)—may yesterday have fgured in the most 're. lned' constellations of tastes and may be pur back there at any moment by one of those taste-maker's coups which are capable of rehabilitating the most discredited object, the notion of an 'average' culture (culsure moyenne) is as fictitious as that of an 'average', universally acceptable lan. guage. What makes middle-brow culture is the middle-class relarion to culuremisraken identity, misplaced belief, allodoxia. Equalßy, one must avoid treating this objectively and subjectively 'unhappy' relation in substantialist fashion, although it always becrays irself, in rhe eyes of the dominaıt, by rhe most inconresrable and objective indices of a manner and mode of acquisition (such as, nowadays, the typically 'record. shop' air of certain systems of musical preferences). What makes the petit.bourgeois relation ro culture and its capacity to make 'middle-brow' whatever it touches, just as rhe legitimate gaze saves' whrever it lights upon, is nor its 'nature' but the very position of the petit bourgeois in social space, the socil nature of the petit bourgeois, which is constantly impressed on the petit bourgeois himself, determining hisrelation to le.

sung, well or badly, in petir-bourgeois living rooms.

## Education and the Autodidact

an educarional system offering (very unequally) the pofssibility of leam the hierarchy of certificates means, for example, thar possession of che highest educational qualifications is assumed, by implication, to guaran. tee possession of all che knowledge guaranteed by all the lower qualifica. tions. Similarly, two individuals doing the same job and endowed with the same useful comperences (i.e., those direccly necessary for doing the job), but holding different qualifications, are likely to be separated by difference in srarus (and also, of course, in pay), the justification for this being the idea that only the comperence certified by the higher qualifica. tions can guarantee possession of the 'basic' knowledge which underlies all pracrical know-how.

So it presents no paradox to see the autodidact's relation to culrure, and the autodidact himself, as producss of she educasiomal system, che sole agency empowered to transmit the hierarchical body of aptitudes and knowledge which constitutes legitimare culture, and to consecrate arrival at a given le el of initiarion, by means of examinarions and certificates. Because he has nor acquired his culture in the legitimare order estab lished by the educational system, the aurodidact constantly betrays, by his very anxiery about che right classification, the arbitrariness of hisclas sifications and cherefore of his knowledgea collection of unscneng pearls, accumulared in the course of an uncharted exploration, un checked by the institutionalized, standardized stages and obstacles, the cutricula and progressions which make scholastic culture a ranked and ranking ser of inrerdependent levels and forms of knowledge.? The ab sences, lacunae and arbitraly classrifcations of che aurodidacr's culrure only exist in relation to a scholastic culture which has che power to in duce misrecognition of its arbitrariness and recognition of a necessily name of the publisher, the director or the venue to pick our a 'top

always liable to know too much or too lirtle, like che heroes of TV quiz games whose misplaced erudicion makes them ridiculous in 'culricollection, a miniarure culrure.

an art But above all, he autodidact, a victim by default of the efcrs of educational entirlement, is ignorant of the right to be ignorant that is confrred by certificates of knowledge, and it would no doubr be futile to k elsewhere chan in the manner in which it is affrmed the difference beiweea the forced eclecticism of chis culrure, picked up in the course of unguided reading and accidental encounters, and the elective eclecticism of aescheres who use the mixing of genres and the subversion of hierar. chies as an opportunity ro manifest their all-powerful aesthetic disposition. One only has to think of the Camus of The Rebel, chat breviary of edifying philosophy having no other unity than the egoistic melancholy which befirs an intellectual adolescence and infallibly wins a repurarion for beauty of soul; or the Malraux of The Voices ofSilence, which envelops & culrural parchwork with Spenglerian metaphysical bric-à-brac, imperturbably associating the most contradictory intuitions, hasty borrowings from Schlosser or Worringer, rhetorically exalted platitudes, purely incanratory liranies of proper names and insights which are caled brilliant because rhey are nor even false."º° In fact-—bur who will say so, since those who could will nor, if chey even srill know it, because so much of chemstlves is at stake, and those who would have an interest in saying so don't know it?—nothing truly separares char other materialized image of petitbourgcois culrure, Postman Cheval's Ideal Palace—a ramshackle fairyland straight out of the engravings of La Veillée do Chaumière," wich its laby. rnchs and galleries, grottoes and warerfalls, Inize and Velleda the Bruidess, the Saracen tomb and the mediaeval castle, che Virgin Mary's grotto and the Hindu temple, the Swiss chalet, the Whire House and the Algiers mosque--from rhe cawdry pachos of Malraux when he marshals in a single sentence the innumerable laughter of the waves', and the borsemen of the Parthenon, Rubens's Kermeise and Khmer sculpture, Sung paiuting and che Dance of Siva, the Romanesque tympanum and 'Antigone's immorral cry', all in the name of communion with che cosmos.' Noching, except the loftiness of the references and, above all, the arrogance, the complacency, the insolence, in a word, the self-assurance, the cercainty y of having which is grounded in the cercainty of having aly an immemorial gift, and which is the exact opposite of the naivety, "If chere ore stubborn than I, let him ser to work'; 'To a valiant cence, humilicy, seriousness which betray iltegitimacy. heart,tothin impossible'; 'On the field of toil I await my better': Malraux. f pure love of work for work's sake are of course nor by , no doubt, one rouches on the principle of the opposition the insecurity which haunts self-made men, Harpagon as much as Ar nolphe, who are perhaps too aware that property is theft' not to fear the theft of their property.

The stockpiling avidity which is the root of every great accumulation of culture is too visible in the perversion of the jazz-freak or cinema.buf who carries to the exrreme, i.e., to absurdity, what is implied in thelegiti mate definition of cultivated contemplation, and repaces consumption of the work wich consumption of the circumscantial information (cred its, exact composirion of the band, recording dates etc.); or in the acquis. iti ve intensiry of alt collectors of inexhaustible knowledge on socially minuscule subjects. In his symbolic class struggle with the certified holders of cultural comperence, rhe prerentious' challenger--nurse against doctor, technician against engineer, promoted executive against business-school graduate—is likely to see his knowledge and techniques devalued as too narrowly subordinated to practical goals, too 'self interesred', too marked, in their style, by the haste of their acquisition, in favour of more fundamental' and also more 'gratuitous' knowledge Ina whole host of markers, from the major state examinations to editorial boards, from job interviews to garden parties, the cultural producrions of the petit-bourgeois habitus are subcly discredited because rhey recal cheir acquisition in matters in which, more than anywhere else. the important ching is to know without ever having learnt, and because che seriousness with which they are offered reveals rhe ethical dispositions from which they flow, which are the antithesis of the legitimate relation to culrure.

The petit bourgeois do not know how to play the game of cultureais game. They take culture too seriously to go in for bluff or imposture or even for the distance and casuainess which show true familiarlty; to0 seriously to escape permanent fear of ignorance or blunders, or to side edge and refuse to believe him when he professes. in one of those imph ous jests aliowed to a Cardinal, who can take liberties with the

those who are linked to it by birth, that is, by nature and essence.

## Slope and Thrust

The dispositions manifested in the relation to culture, such as the concetn for conformity which induces an anxious quest for auchorities and models of conduct and leads to a choice of sure and certilied products (such as classics and prize winners), or in the relation to language, with thetendency tohypcr-correction, a viligance which overshoots the mark for fear of falling short and pounces os linguistic incorrectnes, in oneself and others (as it does elsewhere with moral incorrcctness), are the very same ones w hich are manifested in relation to ethics, with an almost insatiable thirst for rules of conduct which subjects rhe whole of life to rigorous discipline, or in relation to politics, with the respectful conformism or prudent reformism which are the despair of aesthete revolution. aties. The true nature of the culrural accumulation strategies of upwardly mobile perit bourgeois, or their educational straregies, is most clearly sen in the context of their whole set of strategies, which clearly expresses the nece sity underlying the characteristic dispositions of the petir-bourgeois habitus-asceticism, rigour,legalism,the propensity to accumulation in all its forms. Thus theirfertilitysttategies are those of people who can only achieve their initiaì accumularion of economic and culrural capital by restricting cheir consumption, so as to concentrate al cheir resources on a small number of descendants, whose role is to continue the group's upward trajectory.

tion, 1970-71.

spring, its-dynamically dehned—position in the social structure, that is. achieve the future it expects, by giving its children the means of fulflling the effective ambicions it has for them. The working classes, whose chances of entering the domi nant cless within two generations are virtually nil, have very high fertility cease. As soon as the chances of access to the dominant dlass (or, which and ofice workers, fertility rates fall markedly (rhe fertility rate of chose who only have to mointain their position.

It is a patadoxical characreristic of the perit bourgeois thar their prac. ticeis adjusted to objecrive chances which they would not have if they did not have the pretension of having them and if they did nor thereby add a 'psychological' boost to the force of their economic and cultural capital. Having succeeded in escaping from the proletariat, their past, and aspiring to enter the bourgeoisie, their future, in order to achieve the accumulation necessaty for this rise they must somewhere fnd the re. sources to make up for the absence of capital. This additional force, a thruse inscribed in the slope of the past trajectory which is the precondition for achievement of che fucure implied in chat trajectory, can only be exerted negatively, as a limiting and restricting power, so char its effects can only be measured in the form of 'negative magnitudes', as Kant would have put ir, whecher 'savings'—refused expenditure-—or birth control. If rising petit bourgeois can act as if they had better chances than they have (or,at least, better than they would be if rhey did not believe them to be better) and so actually improve them, this is because their dispositions tend to reproduce not the position of which they are the product, but che slope of their individual or collective social tra. jectory, cransformed into an inclination whereby chis upward trajectory cnds to be continued and completed. A sort of nisus perseverandi, as Leib. aiz pur it, in which past trajectory is conserved in che form of a striving cowards the future which prolongs it, it delimits reasonable' ambicions and therefore the price to be paid to realize this realistic ambition. The tising petite bourgeoisie endlessly remakes che history of the origins of captralism; and to do so, like the Puritans, it can only count on its asceticisn. In secial exchanges, where ocher people can give real guarantees, money, culture or connections, it can only offer moral guarantees; (rela-) p coom, cuual d soca cpital,  can o justify its preensions, and get the chance to realize them, by paying in sacri fche fractions richest in economic capital, i.e., small-and mediumtions riches cultural capital (junior executives and clerical workers)

## A Technician Who 'Tries to Get on'

Jacques C., aged 29, is a draughtsman in an enginecring consulrancy. At secondary school he studied in the rechnical srream and lefr school at 17 after obraining the equivalent of the probationary industrial craining diploma ('It isn't an examina. tion'). He started as a junior draughrsman, ar 450 francs a monch, in the firm where his farher was a senior rechnician. He was nor raken back afrer his milicary service, and he joined another lirm, scill as a draughtsman.

## 'Another fáve years to go at the CNAM'

He has changed firms several cimes: 'I would spend rwo years in a firm to learn all rhere was ro be learnt, and rhen I'd move on. That's how I've worked my way up.'in rhe last three years, he has spccialized in building design. in 1966 he srarted to follow courses at the CNAM (Centre Narional des Arts et Métiers)-'Acolleague gave me the idea.' As he had always wanred to do interior decoration (his farher had been againsr ir when he was younger), he decided to rry and get into archirecture' and 'began to rake courses in archirectural design': 'Thar's how I moved inco the build. ing trade ..'His sister, who is studying to be an archicectural secrecary, cold him about 'lots of architects' offices and the armosphere in chem.'So he has srudied archicec. rure, the lisrory of archicecrure, consrrucrion ('chanks to which 1 got a place in an engincering consultancy') and has 'anorher fve years to go' ar the CNAM.

store sales assistane) is 26 for the last hve years she has been a secre. rary with Renaulr. ## Comfortable, cosy, homey'

They live in an apartment in che western suburbs of Paris; they do nor ofren enrerrain (apart from the family ... we haven't got many friends'). He likes his home to be 'comforrable, that's the main ching' 'cosy'. 'homey' ('l like a nice wam cosy atmosphere'). He would like to have 'a bit more room, all che same', bur there are linics ro whae we can afford.' Their furnirure (a big divan 'from Roche-Bobois, 7,000 francs in che sales', a sideboard bought from an interior decoraror who reduced the price for them. 3,000 francs) was bought on hirepurchase (installment plan) over the course of two years. He likes 'modern things'and would have liked 'oshite furairuie' bur does not like the English style his wife favours (she would like to own a big dresser with a collection of plates').

I haven't seen all his oure forins chey were reproductions in kor... like is Van Gogh, his picrures are temfying. you can feel somerhing boiling up in chem.' (His wife also likes che Impressionisrs. She went to the Picasso exhibition and 'loved the fost part, the whole of che Blue Period.'")

## To (ry to get on, achieve snmething

'What wich the night-classes and the exams', they 'hardly went out ar all last wincer'; a bit more, recently. In some ways he is 'conservarive', wich a taste for 'classical things'; yet he 'has an anri-conformist streak, too': When you're young, you don'r give a damn, you're anti-con. formist .. . you're always a liccle bir, I wouldn'r say rewolurionary, bur you wouldn't mind seeing a thing or rwo changed.'He reads Le Canard Encbaîné because he likes che revclacions abour people in government, che scandals and so on, in policics and the property marker and high hnance', Le Nouve! Well, chey didn't pur any effort into it at atl, it wasn'r even improvisation, they just didn't give a damn!"

e chem the ideal clientele of the bank or the school: cultural good. make gives him the necessary strength co extracr from himself, rhrough every form of self exploitation (in parriculat, ascecicism and Malrhusianism), .ke economic and culrural means he needs in order to rise.

thc It is in the area of sociability and the corresponding sarisfactions thar che petit bourgeois makes the greatest, if nor rhe most obvious, sacrifices. He is convinced rhar he owes his posirion solety ro his own merir, and thar for his salvarion he only has himself to rely on: Every man for himself', 'A man's home is his castle.' To concencrate his efforrs and reduce his costs, he will break the ties, even the family ties, which hinder his individual ascension. The bonds of solidarity which help to chain the (relarively) least deprived to the most deprived can make povercy an erernal vicious citcle. Taking off' always presupposes a break, and the disowning offormer companions in misforrune is only one aspect of this. The solitary renegade has to reverse his whole table of values, convert his whole artitude. Thus, substicucing a small family or a single child for the large family (which the negarive causes, such as inadequate masrery of the iechniques of birth control do nor encirely explain) means renouncing dhe popular conceprion of family relarions and the functions of the domestic unir, abandoning nor only the satisfaccions of the extended family and a whole traditional mode of sociabiliry, with irs exchanges, its fescvitics, its conflicts, bur also che guarantees which it offers, the one almost infalhble prorection, especially for mochers, against the uncerrainties of old age, in a world haunted by domestic instability and social and wnomic usecurity. For the petit bourgeois, kinship and friendship can no longer be an insuance against misforune and dister,a nerwork of suppor and rotcion which will always provide a heiping hand , a loan ded to make the most of economic and cultural capiral. They are forbidden luxuries.

n limiting his family, ofcen to an only son, on whom all hopes and he must limit his expendirure, the number of mourhs he has to feed legitimare fertility, thar is, procrearion subordinared to the imperative social reproducrion. Birth control is one form (no doubt che most who makes himself small co become bourgeois.

If the perit bourgeois is a smali bourgeois in eality and ot just in the so Here as else where, native concepts concentrate che maximum number of so. ciologically pertinenr properties in a particularly evocative form. Further. tnore, an objecrifying reduction, however brural, has nothing in comton wich class contempt, which is Aagrant in so much writing on the perite bourgeoisie, the tradittonal whipping boys of aesthericizing prophecy and the favourite tatger for political anathemas (one only has to think of Marx's artacks on Proudhon): it relares the properries of the habirus, those most often picked on by class racism, such as 'pretension\* or ‘narrowness', to the objective condirions of which they are the product. Those who can afford less surly virtues and present a more prepossessing face forger thar the traits they condemn are the inevitable counterpart of the mechanisms providing for individual mobility, i.e, the selective extraction of apptopriate individuals, and they speak as if the 'vices' and 'virrues' of the petit bour geois (which-need one repeat?-ate only defined as such in relation to the dominant erhic) were, in this case, to be imputed to the individuals and not to the strucrures, on the grounds thar the strucrures have left them free to 'choose' their alienation.

Renouncing the prolificity of the prolerariat, the petit bourgeois 'chooses' restricted, selective reproduction, often limited to a single product, conceived and shaped for the rigorously selective expectations of the importing class. He encloses himself in a cightly knit but narrow and somewhar oppressive nuclear family. It is no accident char che adjective bourgeoy s,  ucha is his strong point: strict and rigorous, its formalism and scruples alw ays largesse.

The survey data collected for the middle classes (n = S83 individuals) were analysed for correspondences, applying rhe same sequence of operations and using the same active and ilustrative variables as for the dominant class (see chaptet S). This is no doubt because the composirion of chis factot includes not only the structure of the capiral possessed but also the overall volume of capiral, the effect of which is not complercly neurtalized by che difficulty and relative arbitratiness of defsning the limits of the class, both on the side of the cultural pole, where the cultural intermediaries, very close to the secondary teachers, mighr have been excluded, and on the side of the economic pole, ohere it is never easy, with the available informarion, to draw the lirie between 'big' and 'small' shop kecpers or crafrsmen. Projection of the illustrarive variables shows thar, as with che dominant class, educational qualifications are distribured in hnear fashion along the first axis (which is nor the case wich incomes). In rerms of individuals, the first facror opposes che craftsmen and small shopkeepers to the members of the new petite bourgeoisie riclhest in culrural capital (cultural intermediaries, medical and social services) and, secondarily, to the primary reachers, with the technicians and junior execurives Occupying intermediare positions.

The second factor sysrematically characrerizes the mosr tradirional or conservarive ethical or aesthetic dispositions: artachment to the old, consecrated valuesin painting, with Raphael (2.6 percent), Ieonardo (2.3 percent), or Warreau (1.6 percent), as in classical music, with La Traviala (2.4 percent), or song, with Mariano, and also in life-sryle, with the rasre for traditional Ftench cooking (2.3 percent), a 'neat' (2.3 percent) and 'harmonious' (1.6 percent) inrerior. Finlly, as in the case of the dominant class where it opposed the Profes sionals, in whom bourgeois taste' s ully eveloped, to the two fractions inrelleczuals on one hand, industrial and commnercial employers on the other), the third factor opposes the fracrions which most, and most compictely, possess he modal characteristics of dhe whole class, the ones which best contrast it with the other classes, in short, the most typically 'middle. range' ones, to the fractions with least culrural capital, i.e., the craftsmen and shopkeepers, and to those who have most, i.e., the culrural interme. diaries and primary teachers (see figure 17). The indices of average cultural competence, such as average knowledge (7 to 11) of musical works (4.0 percent) and average knowledge (3 to 6) of composers (2.9 percent), or the raste for the most typically middle-brow culrural goods, such as Brel (2.9 percent), Buffet (1.7 percent), Van Gogh (1.9 percent), Ieonardo (2.2 percent), Eixe Kleine Nachimusik (1.9 percent), are opposed, on the one hand, to the indices of high knowled e (12 or more) of works (2.2 percent) and composers (4.0 percent) and a caste for more legitimate works like the Ars of Fugue (2.2 percent), and on the other hand to the indices of low knowled e (0 to 2) of works (3.3 percent) and composers (3.3 percent) and a taste for the lcast legirimare works and singeis, such as Guérary (2.4 percent) or Hallyday (1.9 percent). (The indicators strongly explained by chis third factor are all among those which make high absolute contributions and so have already been mentioned.)

Projection of the illustrative variables brings our, as one might expect, an oppositton between the holders. of medium qualifications (BEPC or baccalauréat) and those wich lower (CEP or CA.P) or higher qualifications; this is combined with an opposition berween those originaring predominantly from the middle class and those from the working or upper classes. Aver age culture' is thus most characteristic of the primary teachers, technicians people in the medical and social services, and junior adminisrrative executives.

The whole set of facts which correspondence analysis brings to lighe in systemaric form cannot be adequarely explained simply by observing that, alchough phenomenally very different (since chey are applied to generally less legitimate objects), the choices of the members of the pe tite bour geoisie are organized in a structure similar to the one which or ganizes the rastes of the members: of the dominant class, with the craftsmen and small shopkeepers, whose position is based on possession of a certain economic capital, being opposed to the primary teachers and cultural intermediaries in accordance with principles entirely analogous to those which distinguish the industrial and commercial employers from the teachers and artistic producers. The problem brought to light by the second factor, that of che link berween the sets of aeschetic and erhical dispositions roughly dehnable as conservative' or innovating
