if what goes on in chis cenrral area of social space is generally so little understood, this is because, at best, in order to understand and measure (by using, for example, codes which are, by definition, dehnite), one has to frecze the movements in the same direction or in opposite directions which ransport both the positions and the agents, curning the middle gion of social space, a site of relative uncertainty and indeterminancy beween e wopoles of e socal lases, into a st of mobile crossing Ponts, or rter,aset of shifing crossing-points where agents meet fora to use in order to refer to a reality which is not easily uamed are liable to obscure rhe fact char the agent's represenration of his or herown position, which depends nor only on che objecrive furure of the position but also on the represenration orher agents have of it, helps to dererminethe objecrive future of that position." This link between the future and social ascent or berween che pasr and so. cial decline is strongly emphasized im the domimant world view (an individ ual or a posttion is said to 'have a future', meaning the promise of social ascension, i.e., embourgeoisement). ## The Declining Petite Bourgeoisie

The positions whose numerical decline expresses cheir economic decline are occupied by individuals whose objective properties, pracrices and opinions can be seen as linked to a past age. Situared ar the exrreme point of the first axis, relarively elderly on rhe whole and under-endowed with educational capiral (ar most they have the CEP or CAP), the craftsmen and small shopkeepers manifest in all cheir preferences regressive dispost rions which are no doubt the source of their repressive inclinations. par. icularly visible in their reactions to every si of departure from the old order, not least, of course, rhe behaviour of young people. Thus, reacting against all inclinations towards modernism or comfort, which they can only see s moral decadence, on every question concerned with the every day att of living they make choices which can be called regressive inas

A Baker's Wife Who Gets It 'Just About Right

Madame D. and her husband run a bakery business in Grenoble. She lost both parents by the time she was twelve, and was sent to an orphanage. She went to school until age fourteen bur did not take the CEP. Her husband frst worked at night in a dairy. Then he was employed as a baker for eight years. Twelveyears ago he ser up his own bakery business. They have one mar nicd daughrer, aged 22, who works in a pharmacy. Thcy own a small bakery (employing one other person), arhich they will soon be selling so as toacquireanother small shop, but chis cime abusiness that is less de manding, 'less ofa bind'. They have recently boughc a house in thc suburbs of Grenoble; it has a living-room, dining-room, several bedrooms, kirchen and bathroom and is surrounded by a large, wellkept gatden.

## '] do like things to be clean'

Inside, every'thing is brighrly polished. 'I don'r want to boast, bur I do like things to be cle:an, so I like keeping my house nice and ridy, doing the dusting and sweeping. pcthaps roo much, because if I was here all rhe time, that's all I'd be doing. The concern to be 'reasonable', to be just abour right', never conspicuous, is apparent in ever)- thing she says. She says of their house thar 'in a way it's what we've worked for .. a sort of reward.' 'lt's nice bur of course there are much nicer houses. Iet's say it's nor house. Nor that I'm boasting. If it had been a lirtle bit smaller, I would still have raken it. The fact is, it's just abour righr. Nor super. luxurious, no, bur nor just run-ofthe-mill either.''We were brought up to be very economical. ... Work. ing the way we worked, we didn't have rime to spend money. . . We don't have time to go out, so we're almost forced to save money. Often I say, "There are folks thar have moncy and the time to spend it; I've gor the money but I haven't gor the time." It's a sad fact but it's true.'

## 'Something intermediate berween old and very modern'

Choosing cheir furnirurecven. tually bought from the chain-store Iévitan--was 'a real headache'. 'I went round no end of shops . . . so as not to make a mistake. . . . I wanted somerhing that was right for my age because I can't really see a s0-year-old with ulra-modern furniture. .. . 1 was looking for something intermediate berwecn old and very modern' When buying the house, she preferred to 'take her time'cather chan 'rush into it'. 'Some folks gec a house straighr away, but we wouldn't have been able to enjoy it, it wouldn't have been any use ro us. We waited cuire a few years and that way I gor somerhing more comforrtable.'

## 'Not the most ordinary, but not the most beautiful either'

In the living room—it's nor the most ordinary furniturt in the world, but ic isn't the most beauti. ful either-I went fos somerhing classic.' As for che big grey sertee, with rhas shade, you can sir on it', 'you're not a slave to it' (for fear of marking it) ‘and at che same time it's quite comfortable.' She had to choose all these things on her own; her husband had no time and no inclination to accompany her. 'He couldn't have cared less, he just said "Ger pharever you like." She looked for somerhing chat would go with the sryle of the rooms. I don't claim to be very competent bur you do have to respect a cerrain style in the rooms.'

## 'Those things are worth a bir now they're cleaned up'

On the wall chere is a painting given to her by her brother-in-la and one she bought from a 'picture artisr'. 'I would like to have more paintings but 1 can't aford it' She chinks she might also like to listen to records bur has never bought any and cannot imagine herself doing so. Her sense of economy, the re fusai to waste anyching, has led her to 'tescue' knick-knacks for her house. 'I've gor lors of trinkers and odds and ends that I found in aunrs' and uncles' arrics. They were terribly tarnished and rusry bur I polished them up. Al rhose rhings are worth a bit now they're cleaned up. ... When 1 tescued rhem, no. body would have bothered to pick them up, they were so dirry.'

## '1 don't like showing of'

Her home means a lor to her. When she has money to spare, she would rather 'spend ir on furniture or somerhing for rhe house', buy currains or a carpet char she can 'keep for a long time', rather than a dress rhar will be out of fashion next year or jewellery thar she will hardly ever wear. 'Some people are constantly buying new clorhes. 1 don't feel the nced. Sometimes you end up buying rhings you never

even wear. You liked the look of chem in the shop and chen some how, overnight, you decide you don't like rhem any more. Take shoes; rhey only have to pinch alic rle bir, you wear rhem once for an hous or rwo and then you chink, "No, they hurt roo much, I won't wear them", and chen they stay in the box. I'm sure I'm not che only person that happens to.' Sle likes 'real jewellery', 'gold jeweller', bur does not wear whar she owns. "I don't like showing of, purring all my jewellery on; people would say. "Look ar her, dressed up in all her jewellery!" 1 don't like faunting my wealth, if that's the word for it."

## 'Just a trim to tidy myself up'

She 'never splurges' on clothes: I'ir not one to spend a lor on my ward. robe.' Anyway, 'fashion is consrandy changing... wharever you do, you can never keep up with it.' Insread, she goes more for classic' clorhes. She is not sure wherher to accepr a wedding inviration: 'I'm nor keen because it means buying a lor of ex. pensive new things and chen you only wear them once' She occasion ally goes to the hairdresser's and re gards it as a chore': just a ttim (o tidy myself up.' In che country, where she spent her childhood, 'you didn'r sit for hours in front of a misror putting make-up on.' Normally, Madame D. does nor do much real cooking 'just for the rwo of us'; but when they have guesrs, she 'enjoys cooking traditional things'--quiche lorraine. gralin dav. phinois, all sorts of roasts, scuffed o marocs.

## People who throw theis money away'

She gets on very well with people who have the same raste

wichout being, shall we say, mean' but we really don't like to waste rheir money away.' She cannor understand people who 'haven't two pennies to rub together and yet as goon as they get any money chey spend i. It's not always the bestoff people who stint themselves least; often it's middle-class people who won't deny themselves anything.They go and buy fancy cakes or 2 bottle of good wine whenever they feel like it, and when the money runs out they pur it on their accounr.' She extends the same disapproval to those 'who don't know hoa' to manage cheir budger' and who ask for credit cowards the end of thc monch—meaning, no doubt, although she is nor explicit, work. ing-clax people.

## Cheerful programmes you don't have to think 2bout'

She has nor been to the cinema for ar least ten years', and says she doesn't have time to read either a daily paper or weekly magazines, like Paris March or Jours de France, which customers sometimes leave behind in the shop: 'a lot of pages with not much on chem and a lor of adverrisements.' She warches television a little, but 'nor too much', mainly on Sundays, bur never after ren ar nighr. She is not 'a'IV fanatic' but likes 'cheerful pro grammes' thar ‘you don't have to think about', cspecially light enrer. tainment so long as the producer doesn't 'try to be clever': 'Nowadays Ihnd they'te always trying to be clever with theit variery pro gramules; it was much berrer the way it used to be.' She refuses all fosmal experimentation and special efects, and has nor liked Averty's recerit experimental productions: I don't like those cut-up things ar all, where you sce a head, then a nose, then a icg. tt just sems plain silly to me; I must be old-fashioned... First you see a singer all drawn out, three merres call, then he's got arms wo merres long. Do you find that funny? Oh, I just don't like it, it's stupid; I don't see the point of distorting things.'But she does like to warch a 'tradirional' singcr, 'a singer who just sings. who sings normally, who's normal size and not all dis rorted.

## 'My husband doesn't like hotels'

Every year, they go on holiday in their caravan for two or three weeks. Two or three times they have been to a caravan camp site on the Riviera and last year they srayed beside a lake in the Grenoble region. Unril they got rhe caravan, they did not go on holiday; her husband 'doesn't like horels at all, or resrauranrs.' On holiday, her husband plays bowls and cards and 'makes a lot of friends.' She doesn't like doing nothing, so she relaxes and knirs or makes tapestry: ir's a nice hobby, the rime goes quicker.' They spend a bir of time on the beach and drink pastis with friends. Apart from the summer holidays, their work is so demanding that they have no time to go out. Her husband, who srarts work at nine every Sunday evening, carches up on his sleep Sunday afrcrnoon. Ar most, they go our 'once a yeas, on Easter Monday or Whir Monday. Because ir was a public holiday, we used to shur up shop for two days, Sunday and Monday.'

highest degree of density and intensity. Thus the group (situated above the second axis) of small craftsmen and small shopkeepers, most of whose fathers were also small craftsmen or small shopkeepers, and who, for lack of the economic and especially the cultural capital they would need in order to attempt a reconversion, are condemned to carty on ar ll costs at the head of particularly chreatened small businesses (food shops small tra.dirional crafts erc.) which will not outlive them (rhey are even older than the others), is disringuished by systematically retrograde choices from the rest of the fracrion, which contains a fair proportion of modern craftsmen (elecrricians, mechanics etc.), possessing rhe BEPC or even the baccalauréar, who, especially when young and Parisian, are very close to the technicians in their ethical and aestheric, and no doubr also their political, choices.

Convinced that they owe their position, albeit diminished, to a 'sim. ple', 'serious', 'honest' life, the declining petit bourgeois express in all areas the most austere and traditional values (a 'neat' [signé], classical' interior, a conscientious', 'level-headed' friend, tradirional French cooking, the most canonical painters, Raphael, leonardo, Watteau, and the longest-consecrared singers, Piaf, Mariano, Guétary). Their refusals, expressing resentment against the new morality, its showy prerension, its laxity in matters of money (use of credit), child-rearing or sex, are no les signtficant. Rejecting the most characteristic elements of the life-sryle favoured by manual workers (e.g., the quality 'bon vivant'), they systematically exclude all the virtues cultivared by rhe members of the professiors (arristic', 'amusing', 'stylish', 'refined') and all the 'modernist' tastes the larrer so readily exhibit (they never choose either Picasso, who is one of the whipping-boys of petit-bourgeois resentment against artists, or those exemplary representatives of the new young life-style, Françoise Hardy and johnny Hallyday). Their aesthetic of the well-finished' (soigné) is one dimension of an ethos of conscientiousness' which leads them to ap the old noticed (and also, but with a quite different meaning, among

## The Executant Petite Bourgeoisie

Catraly situated in cerms of capital composition, che mmbers of the ron ofrhe petite bourgeoisie, such as the cult of autodidactic ffort and the taste for all the activities whose common feature is that they chiefly demand time and cultural goodwill (making collections, for example)22 Given an intermediate populatton such as this, it is one and che same thing to show how it difers from the neighbouring fractions and how its members are distribured, in respect of diachronic properties, from the oldest, and especially those of bourgeois or petir-bourgeois origin, who are vety close to the most tegressive fraction of the declining petite bour. geoisie in their ethical and aestheric choices, to the youngest, who, especially when possessing high qualifications, are similar to the m obile elcments of the new petite bourgeoisie. Everything takes place as if the basic disposition which characterizes the fraction as a whole were systematically transformed as a function of age and social origin, from an optimistic progressivism among the rising young to a pessimistic, regressive conservatism among the oldest.

It is among the youngest members of the occupations ofering the most secure future, such as the junior executives and ofhce workers, and more especially among those who originate from the working class and have only moderate qualihcations (BEPC or baccalauréar) that one fnds the most developed form of the asceric disposirions and devorion to culture associated wirh the ambition to pursue by further accumulation of cultural capital a rise made possible by an initial small accumulation. Set on a progressive trajectory by their schooling, they are naturally inclined a progressivist world view based on faith in enlightenment and a temperate reformism aiming to give to each according to his scholastic inerits. As wel as owing all they have to education and expecting from it al they aspire to have, they often stand in a relation of execuston to cnception vis-à-vis the scnior executives, whose instructions they follow, whose plans they implement and whose manuals they use, s that they rend ro identify hierarchies with differences in competence or, more simply, in formal qualications. This is all rhe more so since, very often, those who have risen by promotion come up against limits (e.g, ignoclothes and numerous indices of a cultural goodwill as intense as it is innocenr. Assigned to tasks requiring precision, rigour, seriousness, i short, goodwill and devotion, and richer in culrural goodwill can in cul tural capiral, they direct their preferences' towards typically middle-brow works,23 such as the Sabve Darce or Utrillo, buy cheir furniture from de partment stores, prefer clean and ridy', easy to maintain interiors choose Aznavour, Perula Clark or Johnny Hallyday and are very interesred in photography and the cinema.

This pure and empry cultural goodwill, entirely defined by the require. ments of mobility, has its equivalent in morality. The repressive rigour of the declining fractions, which is based on resentment at social regression, seems to have no other purpose than to provide those who only have a past with the sarisf action of condemning those who have a future, chiefly the young. By contrast the ascetic rigour of the rising fractions, often associared with a prudent reformism m politics, gives rise ro a selfdisciplme and family discipline which is entirely subordinated to social mobility. Proof of this is seen in the fact that the rising petit bourgeois, who are usually much stricter than the ocher classes (parricularlly on everything concerned with their children's upbringing-their work, going our alone, readmng-marter, sexualiry erc.) can, withour conrradic. cion, show themselves much less strict chan the dominant moralicy and the class fracttons most arrached to it (who make it a 'matter of principle'), whenever the practices in question, such as abortion or the avail. ability of contra.ceptives ro young people, can be applied in the interest of mobihity. And ir can be understood in the same terms why rhe rising petit bourgeois tend ro slip from optimistic austerity to repressive pessimism as they grow older and as the furure which made sense of their sacrifices curns sour.

Thus, amoug junior execurives and clerical workers, there are greater difer. ences berween the age groups than in orher fractions whenever the ques rions asked offer an outlet for rhe repressive dispositions. For example. the proportion of this category who rejecr the idea chat teachers are nor severe enough declines from 36.2 percent among the under-35s, to 29 percent among the 35- ro 50-year-olds, and 26 percent among rhe over-50s; the pro pottion who think that teachers are roo policical' rises from 44.6 percent 1970, secondary analysis).

for his son' eats up his present. Because he is committed to strategies exrending over several generations, he is the man of the deferred pleasure, we've paid off che mortgage', 'when the children are older' or 'when weve retired, in other words, very often when it is too lare, when, hav. cut his coat according to his cloth.' There is no compensation for a lost preseat, especially when (with the break-up of identifcation with the children, for example) the disproportion berween rhe sacrifices and the sarisfactions becomes apparent, retrospectively making nonsense of a past entirely defined by rension rowards the furure. In the end these altruistic misers who have squandered everything on the alrer ego they had hoped to be, either in person, by rising in the social hierarchy, or through a subsriture shaped in rheir own image, the son for whom they have done everything', who 'owes them everything', are left with nothing but resentment—the resentment that has always haunted rhem in che form of che fear of being taken for a ride by a social world which asks so much of them.

As they struggle to the peak of their career and the moment of self. assessment, feeling their values and even their conception of their job chreatened by the arrival of new, more highly qualified generations hearing a new ethos, the oldest of rhe junior executives and office workers are inclined to conservative dispositions in aestherics, ethics and politics, as is shown by the analysis of che correspondences, which situares them close to the small shopkeepers and traditional crafrsmen. To have their revenge, they only have to place themselves on their favourite terrain, that of morality, to make a virtue of their necessity, elevate their parricular moraliry into a universal morality. These groups nor only have the moralicy of their interests, as everyone does; rhey have an interest in moralicy. For those scourges of privilege, moraliry is the only rirle which gives a right to every privilege. Their resentment often leads to fundamencally ambiguous polirical positions in which verbal fdelity to past convictions is a mask for present disenchantment, when it does nor simply serve to iustify moral indignation; and the somewhar lachrymose, humanistic anarchism which may ourlive adolescence in some elderly, long-haired bo hemians can easily veer with age into a fascistic nihilism endlessly ruminating on scandals and plots.

The whole existence of the rising perit bourgeois is the anticipation o a furure which he wil, in most cases, only know by proxy, throu

fact, changes in political position and dispositions are apparently related ideologues, has every appearance on its side. Given thar, oll the one hand, adolescents (rhe only ones considered by the ideology) can. for the sake of simplicity, be divided into rwo classes, roughty corresponding to social suc. cess and failure, and on the other hand that these two ciasses of rrajectories borh lead, by differcat roures, to conservative dispositions (which diffet gocatly, of course, in cheir modaliry), it can be seen chat one only has to ig nore the varieries of ideology, and the social principles of variation of the relationship berwcen ideological ageing and social agemg, to rurn a sociolo gically intelligible starisrical relationship into a natural law.

Situated on the opposite side from the previous group as regards age and trajectory, the most educared members of the youngest generarion of junior execurives, rechnicians and especially prima.ry teachers are close to the new petire bourgeoisic," especially to their comperence and prefer. ences in legitimate culrure (rhey equally often choose the Art of Fugue, the Four Seasons, Eine Kleine Nachtmusik and the Well-Tempered Clavier, hut more often the Sabre Dance, the Hungarian Rhapsody and Rhopsosy in Blue, and less often L'Enfant et les sortilèges). They remain apart from them, however, and ail the more so, the less they have been exposed to the new mode of scholastic generation, in everything more directly concerned with the daily arr of living. Thus, rheir ideal friend, who for the new perire bourgeoisie is 'dynamic', 'refined', 'stylish' and 'artistic, is more often 'bon vivant', 'conscientious' and 'sociable', and if they include 'artistic' this is no doubr because it is the one dimension of bourgeois values which the ascric perit bourgeois can ind acceprable by virtue of the value he places on legitimate culrure. The occupation of pnimary teacher tro some exrent shares rhe characterisrics of rhe new occupations, owing to the changes in academic ad social recruitment and in raining These changes, the most visible of which is femir.ization, no doubt linked to a rise in social origin, have led to the coexistence in the same who belong to the upper classes by their origin or by marriage).

# A Nurse Who Lives with Passion'

Eliz2beth F is 2S. She obrained the br (with philosophy) and chen went to nursing college. For four years she has worked as a nurse at the Ciré Universicaire (International Student Residence) in Paris. Her facher was a civil servant and her morher is a Posr Office clerk. She lives alone in a oncroom apartment wih a smalt entrance-hall. It con-(ains no furniture—no table, no chairs, no shelves. Everyching—martress, record-player, books—is on the Aoor On the walls she has a Dario Fo poster about Chile, which she bought at a reciral of pocms and songs about Chile at the Ctté Universitaire, a posrer for a Years play ('very bcauriful'), a srill from the film Aphrodile Child ('very moving'), a phoro of her young nephew, an orange mirror ('very functional') and a big crimson board with a whole ser of necklaces, madc of shels, pearls, enamelware, beans etc., some of which she made herself She does not expect to buy furniture: 'It's not that 1 don't like it. 1 just don't happen to have any-it doesn't seem to be very es. senttal 1 admit it's not very conve nient for people when I invite chem for dinner, but 1 really don'r chink it's important' She bought her Citroën 2CV second-hand, from her godmother ('she let me have it cheap'): she bought her phonograph in her net-olast year at the lyce, Wih e hist money I earned working in a sanarorium in my spare tiene' Ir isn't really 'suitable' for cassical music "wery, very worn out.' c and her records are

## '1 love being creative'

She is cager to express her personal. ity and scizes every opportuniry to 'create' somerhing personal: 1 love drawing ... everyone in the family does drawing: my sisrer draws, my farher used to draw.'Mainly, though only episodically, she does black-and-white portrairs of peopte she likes: '1 enjoy colour but for me it's not the imporcant rhing in a drawing'; che most imporrant things are 'the curves and rhen, well, the expression, capruring someone's expression ... the pleasure comes when I'm doing the drawirig, larer it's of no signihcance.' She learnt how to make enamelware: first from books, which her aunt gave her together with a kiln when she was 15; later she made enamelware with her sisrer and friends at the municipal youth club.

## Peopie who never look outside themselves'

Her only make-up is an invisible foundation cream; her hair is thinned and cuc medium-length. The first quality she looks for in her friends is joy. I like healthy people, who are glad to be alive.' 'i like people who are at ease with themselves, perhaps because I'm nor always ar ease wth myself. 1 think that what makes people carry on liv. ing is always being able to hope for something, never acceprng rhings as chey are. .. . The people  don't like, i can't accept because they seem to me ... empry. I mean they're dull, they have no raste, no passion. Perhaps "no passion"is too strong; 1 mean theydon't want anything, they don't seem to fcel anything, they never look outside themselves. They don't really live.

They're imprisoned by a whole environment. They can't get out of ir and don'r even realize they could get our of it. There are quire a lor of them among my parienrs and rhe people i work with. People who are perhaps coo dependent on material satisfactions, on che material things society can offer them . . . I can't say I don't like chem, buc I'm not interesred in them. They don't interesr me'

## 'Doing something wich my body'

She worships 'nature', everything that is narural in people, in the street', and loves 'nature in Paris, the woods and Aowers.' For a whole year she practised 'physical expression' with the Montreuil theatre group: Doing something wirh my body ... performing movements, gestures, chat aren'c necessarily rheacrical, that express my body—rhat's something I enjoy.' On holiday, she shuns campsites ('they're roo organized nowadays, almost hotels'); she cannor spend more chan a week at the seaside 'doing nothing': 'Going to the beach, sun-barhing, the occa. sional swim ... after two days of thar, I'm bored.' She hicch-hikes (o Greece, Ícaly or the Balearic Islands: 'I love travelling that way partly because it's so diferent from what I do the rest of the year, a different life-style .. . but also in order to live with a bir of insecurity-that's exciting—and to meer diferent people, be able to communicare.' Iast year she rook part in che restoration of an abbey in the Rouergue region, and in her spare cime she has worked with an archaeological ream excavating a site in Eure-et-Loire. Every other Wednesday (she works one Wednesday out of t wo) she arrends a course at rhe Ecole Pratique des Haures Erudes 'roget a bit of

theary' (it helps ro have some na

## 'We used to choose a theme, talk about it and thencrea

For three years she also belonged to an amateur drama group with friends ar Bois d'Arcy, near Paris They put on quite a few cabarer shows based on poety. 'We did one on Boris Vian's work so as ro make it berrer known.' They have also wsirten theirown shows:'We used to choose a cheme, ralk about ir and then creare . . . but che pcople chere were too young and ir broke down. Everyone went off their own way after the bac and we never got together again.' Occasionally with a gir.l-friend she still does a bit of acting, 'just lictle sketches that we work on cogether' ( wouldn't have wanted to make a living our of it; it's just a hobby').

## 1 don't go just because it's cheaper

There are periods when she goes regularly to the theatre. At the the atre in the Cité Universiraire she has recently seen Ali Baba's Ceve 'by 2 troupe from the Théâtre Oblique, [ think; it wasn'r bad', Dario Fo at the Salle Gémier, 'ir was very radical, but it was in the form of stories told to people and it was excelletit. She goes to see shows that are nor expensive: 'I don't go just because it's cheaper, but the fact is, ar the Ciré rhere are very good shows that aren't expensive. . . Of course, ir isn't very comforrable, bur you have 2 good evening our and ir docsn't cost roo much, about ren francs Afrerwards, we normally come back here or visit anorher friend and we discuss rhe play if we feel like it' She went to the Comédie-Française al theatre, but I don't really know boulevard theatre 'isn't theatre at volves people, brings together the performers and the spectators... Boulevard theatre doesn't do anything like it. I'm not even sure if people relax, because after all they see their own lives, what they do every day of the week. Theatre shouldn't be about relaxing, it should be about dreaming, inventing.'

## Boris Vian and lots of things abouc him

She loves classicat music, especially Bach and Beerhoven; she often lis. tens to Félix Leclerc, Léo Ferré, Jacques Brel—her great love' when she was 15 ('the only singer I've Iiked all the nime for years')—and Georges Brassens: 'I don't like Sheila at all—ir's not music, it's commerce, opium, the sort of rhing

I hare.' Although she does not like him, 'perhaps because of my uP bringing' (her family hated him), she thinks thar Johnny Hallyday 'does have somerhing' She likes Pink Floyd—and has one of their records—but 'only superficially'.

She reads Le Monde when she has time (but that is not often), the reviews in Le Nouve! Observateur, and fairly segularly Le Canard Enchaîné. She used to read more when she was at school. She reads novels (recently, a book on Nepal by Han Suyin'), all Françoise Sagan's novels, 'Boris Vian and lots of things about him.'She likes to 'discover an author', 'immetse herself in a book and tell herself she's reading something fascinating.'She likes Van Gogh (she went to the exhibition at the Perir Palais), Goya, Buffet, everyrhing thar's all done in lines' and Impressionism: 'Ir's a vision that's much deeper than a marerial objece .. you really feel you're given the impression of someone who has seen something and repro. duced it.'She recently visited the Rodin Museum, which is fantastic': 'I just love his work, it's wonderful.

generation and therefore differences berwcen generations defned in and by their relationship to the educational system. The best qualified of the younger generation of junior executives or clerical workers (mainly originating from the working and middle classes) share with the members of the new occupations-and especially with rhose of them who do nor originate from the bourgeoisie—a relarion ro culture and, partially ar tablishc positions their original social posirion promised them; and also qualifications and jobs.

promise of the highest profits for non-cerrified cultural capital, which guarantee no particular career prospects (of the type offered by the welk established occupations) but exclude none, not even the most ambirious, are adjusted in advance to the dispositions typical of individuals in de cline endowed with a strong culrural capital imperfectly converted into educational capital, or rising individuals who hav'e not obtained all the educarional capiral which, in the absence of social capiral, is needed o escape the most limited of the middle positions.

In the first place, the willingness or capacity to accepr the (average) risk resulting from che indererminacy of the positions no doube varies, other things being equal, with inherited capiral, partly by an effecr of the dispositions themselves, which are more self-assured when there is mote security, and partly because of the real distance from necessity given by possession of the economic means of persisting in provisionally unprohtable positions.26 Whereas the personal risk entailed by the riskiest positionsdeclines, both subjectively and objectively, as inherited capital increases, the chances of prolir grow as capital increases in all its forms, not only the economic capital which gives the means of waiting for the furure of 'coming' occuparions, or the cultural capital which helps to make that future by the symbolic violence needed to creare and sell new products, bur perhaps especially the social capital which, in these inlformally organized sectors in which recruitment is effected by co-option, enables one to enter the race and stay in it.

Furthermore, these posirions, which are ultimarely less risky and, ar icast in the long run, more proficable, the mor capiral one brings inro them, present a further advantage for people seeking an honourableref uge ro avoid social decline, perhaps the most imporrant advantage in the short term and in the practical shaping of a 'vocation'. One only has consider the opposition between the youth leader or cultural organizer research institute and the bank clerk or post office clerk, to see thar, un like rhe established positions which are situated in a hierarchy and be imposed as public services' offialy recognized and more or less ro dr of erhics and an occupational ideology erc.).

The new petitebourgeoisie comes into its own irı all che occupations involving presentation and representation (sales, marketing, advertising, public relations, fashion, decoration and so forth) and in all the institu. rions providing symbolic goods and services. These mclude the various jobs in medical and social assistance (marriage guidance, sex cherapy, dieterics, vocarional guidance, paediarric advice etc.) and in culrural production and organization (youth leaders, play leaders, turtors and monitors, radioand TV producers and presenter's, magazine journalists), which have expanded considerably in recent ycars; but also some es rablished occupations, such as art crafrsmen or nurses. Thus, the arr craftsmen in the old sense--upholsterers, wrought-iron workers, cabinetmakers, picture-framers, goldsmiths, jewellers, gilders or engravers, wrained in technical schools and very close to small craftsmen and their values—have been joined in the last fifteen years or so by makers of jcarellery, printed fabrics, ceramics or hand-woven clothes, with a highet level of general education, often Parisian and of bourgeois otigin, who are closer in life-style to the cultural intermediaries. Similarly, among the secretaries and nurses, some, originating from the lower or middle classes, are very close to the junior administrative executives, while others, younger, often Parisian and of bourgeois origin, present all the features of the new occupations. In general, the indeterminacy of the new or renovared occupations means that the heterogeneity of the agents' trajectories is patricularlly marked. One can almost always disringuish rwo groups separated by social origin and all the associated dispositions, who disagree more or less overtly over the defnition of che job and the competences or virtues necessaty in order to fll it.

lished perire bourgeoisie (level-headed, classical). These uncertainties r even incoherences no doubr exist in each of the nembers: of che new pro. fessions, who have to invent a. new life-style, particularly in domestic life, and to redefne thei social co-ordinates. If che indererminacy of a Position favours blufing or euphemizing strargies, there ts a price to be paid in terms of che occupant's uncertainty as to his social identity, as we see from the testimony of an industrialist's daughrer, aged 35, proprietor of a bou. tique' in Paris selling 'design cenre' products, contemporary goods' and gifts; she attended a decorative arts school but has no diploma, and conducts her art business as if it were an arr: When people ask me what I do, and I say "I'm a shopkeeper", I always feel it's someone else answering for me because really I don't see myself as a shopkeeper. But I suppose in the end, when it comes down to it, I must be, I don't know .. . All the same, I feel very remore from the preoccupations of my butcher and I feel much closer to someone who works in an advertising agency or to an interior decorator. It's all very complicated. Personally I see myself as rather out on a limb, between two stools, I don't know. For me shopkeeping is like a game; it's always a bit of a gamble, buying and selling.

Those members of rhe new petite bourgeoisie who originate from the upper classes and who, for lack (most often) of educational capital, have had to reconvert into the new occupations such as culrural intermediary or art craftsman have had fewer years of schooling rhan the average for their class of origin but more than rhe average for rhe middle classes; they rherefote possess a very great cultural capital of familiariry and a social capital of 'connections'. They manifest rhe highest competence within the middle classes and incline towards a system of choices very similar to that of the bourgeoisie: the Art of Fugue, the Concerto for the Left Hand, the Firebird Suite, the Four Seasons, Goya, Braque, Breughel, Jacques Douai, the Modern Art Museum, antique shops and the Flea Marker, a 'harmonious', 'discreet', 'studied' interior, 'refned', 'artistic', 'stylish' friends, 'intellectual' films. Salvatore Giuliano, Exterminating Angel, The Trial, or, among the comedies, The Suitor (see figure 18). Their ambivalent relationship wirh the educarional system, inducing a sense of complicity wtth every form of symbolic defance, inclines them to welcome all the forms of culture which are, provisionally at least, on the (lower) boundaries of legitimate culture—jazz, cinema, strip cartoons, science fiction-—and to faunt (for example) American fashions and models jazz, jeans, rock or rhe avant-garde underground, which is their monop oly—as a challenge to legitimate culture; but they often bring into these regions disdained by the educational establishment ar erudite, even aca demic' disposition which is inspired by a clear intention of rehabiliration, the culcural equivalent of the restoration strategies which defne their oc cupational project.

secretaries, who are mainly interesred in actors." Their preference for The Trial, a 'prodigious, harrowing' film, or for Les dimanches de Ville d'Avray by Serge Bourguignon (who uses 'sometimes questionable' but 'never vul. gar' means to retrace with 'unfailing delicacy' the 'very pure and poeric story of a meeting and a friendship berween a twelve-year-old girl and a thirty-year-old man'—Le Monde, 24 November 1962) no doubr reveals their quasi-professional interest in all psychological explorations;\* but it is also a sign, among many others, of the high cultural ambitions (also refected in the frequency with which they say they read works of philosophy) of these transitional, mediating categories, identified in inrention and aspiration with rhe dominant classes whom they serve, often in sPatial proximity (secretary and director, nurse and doctor), yet separated from them by an invisible barrier.

The fact thar a large proportion of rhese new positions are occupied by women no doubr contributes to rhe realization of thetr potentialities, which express themselves precisely in this recruitment. It would clearly be naive to look to the sex-ratio of rhe category, which is one of the prop. erties of the category, for rhe explanation of this or thar other property of the categor y. . The socially inculcated disposirions (in particular, rhe inclination towards che things of raste) which lead especially che women of 'hese fractions towards the adjectives they see as most distinguished ba imposition.

values most clearly associated with rhe established petite bourgeoisic and the working classes. which give to all their practices an air of tension even in relaxation, constraint in the pursuit of a liberated' life-style, af lectation in simplicity, are indeed rhe most signihcant manifestations of this new variant of the petit-bourgeois ethos.

This well-armed pretension, based on a familiarity with culrure combined with high social origin, is quite distinct in its means and its modal. ity from che arixious pretension of rhe promoted petite bourgeoisic. lr functions as a sotr of social 'flair', allowing its owner to steer through difhcult situations when the usual landmarks are missing. Thus, al. though the members of the new petite bourgeoisie are no more inclined than the others to concede (especially in practice) that photography can transhgure objects such as a pregnant woman, a scrap-yard, a burcher's stall or a car crash, they are more skilful at ide ntif yingthe 'soppy' obiects of popular taste or petir-bourgeois aestheticism-the sunset, the land. scape,the little girlplaying with a cat.the folk dance (all of which chey much more rarely say would make a beauriful photograph). In general, the cultural 'bluff"which relies on chis Aair—measured by the gap be tween the tendency to select the rarest painters, composers or works (L'Enfant et lessortilèges. the Firebird Suite. Kandinsky, Dali. Braque) and the frequency of visits to muse:ums, especially the Modern Art Museum—varies with the proportion of inheritors and parvenus in each category. It is particuladyfrequent among the cultural intermediaries and commercial executives, very rare among the promoted petit boutgeois (as it is among the public-sector execurives and secondary teachers), and axis).

The dispositions of which the new perite bourgeoisie is the bearer find effcets ro doubt one of the factors conducive to appropriation of the advantages associated wich proximity to the centre of cultural values, such as a more intense supply of culrural goods, che sense of belonging and the incentives given by contact wirh groups who are also culturally favour d.3 Consequently there is no ocher category in which the systematic differences between Parisians and provincials are more marked: differ. ences in the intensity of the legitimate practices (museum visits etc.) and the range of competence (in music, for example); differences in the rela. tionship to legitimate culture, with the sense of being an outsider to the world ofpainting or music ('not my strong point', 'I don't know much about it') always being more marked among provincials, other things being equal; differences, above all, in the abiliry ro recognize ofren wirhout knowing chem—smarr opinions. the Parisiaris always being more inclined, at all levels of competence, to opt for the most legitimate judgements (All music of quality interests me') as soon as they are formulated, whereas the provincials more often concur with the judgemenrs expressing a recognition of legitimacy combined with a confession of ignorance ('l don't know much about it') or incompetence ('It's complicated'); differences in the indices of cultural pretension, with, for example, the choice of an 'imaginative' or 'srudied' interior, clothes thar are 'chic' and 'stylish' (rare), two expressions typical of the new art of living promulgated by the mass-circulation womeri's magazines, and above all the declared preference for the rarest works of music, the Fire. bird Suite, the Art of Fugue, the Well-Tempered Clavier (instead of the provincials' Rhapsody in Blue), through which the new petite bourgeoisie distinguishes itself from the promoted petite bourgeoisie much more de. cisively in Paris than in the provinces; and differences in all the indices of life-style, in the choice of clothes, in tastes in cooking or ethical prefer. enes in which che provincials are always more prudent, less audaciousl 'liberated'.

## From Duty to the Fun Ethic

One can see how inherited dispositions predispose individuals to occupy the posirions towards which they orient them. With these 'need merchants', sellers of symbolic goods and services who always sell themselves as models and as guarantors of the value of their products, and who sell so well because they believe in what they sell, the symbolic authority of the honest, trustworthy vendor takes the form of an imposition that is both more violent and more gentle, since the vendor deceives the customer only insofar as he deceives himself and is sincerely 'sold' on the value of what he sells. Because the new 'subsritution' industry, which sells fine words instead of things to those who cannot aftord rhe things but are willing to setcle for words, fnds its ideal clientele in the new pe. tite bourgeoisie, this group is predisposed to collaborate with toral conviction in imposing the life-sryle handed down by the new bourgeoisie, the probable destinarion of irs trajectory and the teal goal of its aspirarions.32 In short, this petite bourgeoisie of consumers, which means to acquire on credir, i.e., before its due time, rhe atcributes of the legitimate life.styleresidences' with 'olde-worlde' names and holiday flars at Merlin.Plage, mock luxury cars and mock luxuiy holidays-is perfectly adapted to act as a transmission belr and pull into the race for consump. tion and competition those from whom it means to distinguish itself. In fact, one of its distinguishing features is precisely its sense of legitimacy in teaching others the jegitimate life-style by a symbolic action which not only produces the need for its own product, and therefore, in the long run, legitimates itself and those who exercise it, but also legitimates the life-style put forward as a model, that is, that of the dominant class, or, more precisely, of the fractions which constitute its ethical avant-garde.

erarchy of the institurions of culrural producrion and circulation and ex. tcliecrual solidariry with the dominated classes, the new cultural ing the cultural order and rhe hierarchies which the culrural 'hierarchy' of the (rechnocraric) prerension to the monopoly of competence, hostility to hicrarchies and rhe' hierarchy, the ideology of universl creativity. But in fact chese occuparions condemn their occupants to the essential ambigu. ity resulting from the discrepancy berween the (symbolically) subversive dispositions linked to their position in the division of labour and the manipulative or conservative functions attached ro the position, berween the subjective image of the occupational project and rhe objective function of the occupation. The very performance of the function may presuppose this discrepancy, a principle of dissimulation and misrecognition, as in rhe case of some veteran revolutionaries of May 1968 who have become industrial psychologists: to accept their ambiguous position and to accept themselves doing so, they are forced to invent the ski lfully ambiguous discourses and practices that were, so to speak, inscnbed in advance in che very definition oftheposition.3Obligedto live out the contradiction berween their mes. sianic aspirations and the reality of their practice, to cultivate unceriainty as to theirsocial identityin orderto be able to accept it, and therefore con demned to a questioning of the world which masks an anxious self. questioning, these 'inrellectual lackeys' are predisposed to experience wich particular intensity the existential mood of a whole intellectual generation, which, weary of desperately hoping for a collective hope, seeks in a narcissistic self-absorption rhe substitute for the hope of changing the social world Or even of understanding ir.

Thus, in the struggle it wages, within the dominant fractions of che dominant class, to replace old-style conservatism based on an overtly authoritarian image of the hieraichical relations berween the classes, generations or sexes with a reconverted conseivatism corresponding to the enlightened self-interest of rhose for whom a rational use of che educa. tional system has provided the means of reconverting themselves in accordance with the new logic of che cconomy, the new or renovared bourgeoisie finds a natural ally, both economically and politically, in the new petire bourgeoisie. The latter recognizes in the new bourgeoisie the embodiment of irs human ideal (rhe 'dynamic' executive) and, having abandoned the somewhat morose asceticism of the rising petite bour geoisie, collaborates enthusiastically in imposing the new ethical norms (especi ally as regards consumption) and the corresponding needs.

Šeeking its occupational and personal salvation in the imposicion posed to play a vanguard role in the srruggles over everything conce with the art of living, in particular, domestic life and consumption, gela am rions berwecı the sexes and the generations, the reproducrion of the

conservatism often centes on moral indignation at moral disordcr,and predominantly petit-bourgeois right-wing thought, from Proudhon to Pareto. But it is equally opposed, by the aristocratic prerension of its fundamental choices and by its subversive representation of the relations betoeen the sexes, to the asceticism of the promoted petite bourgeoisic, whose austere optimism, rigorous but not withour a sort of heroism. is opposed to the repressive pessimism of the declining petite bourgeoisie. Thus, whereas the old morality of dury, based on the opposition between pleasure and good, incluces a generalized suspicion of the 'charming and arrractive', a fear of pleasure and a relation to the body made up of'reserve', 'modesty' and 'restraint', and associates every satisfaction of the forbidden impulses with guilt, the new erhical avant-garde urges a morality of pleasure as a duty.3s This doctrine makesit a failure, a threat to self-esteem, rtot to 'have fun'," or, as Parisians like to say with a litte shudder of audacity, jouir," pleasure is not only permitred but demanded, on erhical as much as on scienrific grounds." The fear of not getting enough pleasure, the logical outcome of the effort to overcome the fear of pleasure, is combined with the search for self-exptession and 'bodily expression' and for communication with ochers (relaring éhange), even immcrsion in orhers (considered not as a group but as subjectivities in search of their identity); and the old personal ethic is thus rejected for a cult of personal health and psychological therapy. At the opposite pole from the 'politicization' which depersonalizes personal experiences by presenting them as particular cases of generic experiences common to a class, 'moralization' and \*psychologizarion' personahze experiences, and are thus perfectly consistent with che more or less secularized forms of rhe search for religious salvation.39 As is shown by the use it makes of psychoanalytic jargon, che modernist morality is a psychological vulgate which moralizes under che guise of analysis; and as is shown by the emphasis it places on Erikson's 'utopia of full orgasmic reciprocity', it transmutes a spuriously positive dehnition of the 'normal' into an imperative of normality and bases the orgasm-dury of its theoretical moraliry on the fndings of a bogus science of mores à la Kinsey, thus introducing rhe deadly, rational accountancy of equivalences into the area of sexual exchanges, which fot most societies is one of the last refuges of collective misrecognition.º Invoking the prestige of a false science of sexual behaviour to naruralize a conception and an experience of sexualtty'—a very recent historical invention which depends on social conditions of posibilizy that are very unequally distributedir consigns to the pathology of sexual poverty', i.e., to the attentions of the Psychoanalyst or e seologist,olc bitersoflegima sua comp.

with the sexual revolurion' are once again the victims of a univ tion of the conditions of acquisition.

character-building' and may even find a form of pleasure in the experi. ence of rension; in its place comes the new gymnastics', a self-styled anti-gymnasrics'. with a system of equally imperative and exacdy oppo site preceprs. Aiming to substirute relaxarion for tension, pl asure for ef. fort, 'creativity' and 'freedom' for discipline, communication for solirude, it treats the body as the psychoanalyst trears the soul, bendingits ear to 'lisren 'to a body whichhas to be 'unkr.orted', liberated or, more simply, rediscovered and accepted ('feeling ar home'). This psychologization of the relarion to the body is inseparable from an exalration of the s lf. but a sellf which truly ful6ls itself (growth', 'awareness', responsivene') only when 'relating' to others ('sharing experiences') through the interme diary of the body treared as a sign and not as an instrumcnt (which opens the door to a whole politics ofthe 'alienated body'). Such are the inten tions which lie behind exercises of 'bodily expression', a sort of painless delivery of one's own body.

The same oppositions are found in the area of child-rearing. The puritantcal view of the chil d's nature as a sire of dangerous, powerful (essenrially auro-croric) desires leads upbringing to be seen as a training', a straighrening, and pedagogy as a set of techniques. for controlling the child while caming his bad insrincts; ir gives adults che power to define needs, i.e., legirimare desires, to disringuish, for example, between legin. mare cries (of hunger or pain) and illegitimate ones, or berween 'good' and bad' habizs. By contrast, the therapeutic ethic, with its psychobabble of liberationist commonplaces ("farher figure', 'Perer Pan complex' erc.), credits the child with a good nature which must be acccpted as such, with its legitimate pleasure needs (for artention, affection, marernal care). Child-rearing, which is also a source of legitimare pleasures (so that procreation, which produces this consumer object bringing joy, youth and unron to borh parents, is alsoa psychorherapeutic dury), trears the child as a sort of apprentice who must discover his body and rhe world through exploration; and, blurring the boundaries between work and play, duy and pleasure, it dene play as muscular and menalear dispensable, thus making pleasure a duty for children and parenrs

The Choice of the Necessary

The fundamenral proposirion thar che habirus is a virrue made of neces. sity is never more clearly illustrated than in the case of the working classes, since necessiry includes for them all thar is usually meant by the word, thar is, an inescapable deprivarion of necessaty goods. Necessity imposes a raste for necessity which implies a form of adapration to and consequently acceptance of the necessary, a resignation to the inevitable, a deep-seared disposition which is in no way incompatible wirh a revolurionary intention, although it confers on it a modality which is not that of inrellecrual or artistic revolts. Social class is nor dehned solely by a po sition in the relations of production, bur by the class habirus which is 'normally' (i.e., with a high statistical probability) associated with that position. The narodniki of all rimes and all lands, by identifying with their object to the point of confusing their relarion to the working-class condition with the working-class relation to thar condition, by speaking and wriring as if it were suffhcient to occupy the worker's position in the relations of production for a brief while, as observer or even as partict pant, in order to understand rhe worker's experience of rhat posirion, present an account of the working class condition rhar is starisrically mprobable, since it is not the product of the relarion to thar condition which is ordinarily associated with the condition, precisely because ofthe condicionings which it exerrs.

It is not a question of the trurh or falsity of rhe insupportable image rhe working-class worild that rhe intellectual produces when, himself in rhe place of a worker wizhout having rhe habitus of a worker he apprehends the working-class condition through schemes of ercep tion and appreciation which are not those that the members of the workg class themselves use to apprehend it. It is truly the experience that an intellectual can obtain of the working-class world by putting himself provisionally and deliberately into the working-class condition, and it may become less and less improbable if, as is beginning to happen, an increasing number of individuals are thrown into the working-class condiinspires have all the appearances of legitimacy to support them.

## The Taste for Necessit y and the Principle of Con formit y

The specific effect of the tasre for necessity, which never ceases to act, though unseen--because its acrion combines with that of necessity-is most clearlly seen when it is, in a sense, operating out of phase, having survived the disappearance of the condirions which produced ir. One sees examples im the behaviour of some small ctaf tsmen or busincssmen whe, as they themselves say, 'don't know how to spend the money they've earned', or of junior clerical workers, still arrached to their peasant or working-class roots, who get as much satisfaction from calculating how much they have 'saved' by doing without a commodity or service (or 'doing it themselves') as they would have got from the thing itself, but who, equally, cannot ever purchase it without a painful sense of wasrit g money. Having a million does not in itself make one able to live li ke a millionaire; and parvenus generally rake a long time to learn thar what they see as culpable prodigality is, in their new condirion, expenditure of basic necessity.

It rends to be forgotten that to appreciare the 'true value' of the purely symbolic services which in many areas (horels, hairdressing etc.) make the essential difference between luxury establishments and ordinary businesses, one has to feel oneself the legitimare recipient of this burcaucraticaly personalized care and attention and to display vis-à-vis rhose who are paid to fer it the mixture of disrance (including generous graru ries) and freedom which the bourgeois have towards their servants. Anyone who doubrs that knowing how to be served' is one component of the bourgeois art of living, need onl think of the workers orsmall lerks who, entering a smart restaurant for some grand occasion, immediately marginal value of chose two million francs varies with the number of are inno way a squandering and, as well as being obligatory elements in a crtain style of life, chey are very ofren-like engagement parties-an excellent investment in social capital.

For a host of reasons—in particular the physical and social separation of the universes of life--these experiences (of rhe watch and the engagement parry) are very improbable—although both actually happened. In fact, as Marxobservedwith some brutality: 'Whar and how much he can see depends nor onlyon the existing state of affairs in the world, a state of affairs by no means created by him, but also on his purse and on the position in life which falls to his lot owing to the division of labour, which perhaps shues very much away from him, although he may have very acquisirive cyes and cars." In general the working classes 'have no idea' of whar the sysrem of needs of the privileged classes might be. Their knowiedge of upper class resources is equally abstcact and unreal. (Thus, when asked 'the average price of a good mcal around the Champs Elysées', 13 percent of the manual workets said they did not know, 35 percent put it ar betwseen 15 and 24 francs, 22 percent berween 25 and 29, 13 percent between 30 and 39,and 13 percent over 50 francs, as against 2 percent, 11 percent, 20 percent, 33 percent and 14 percent respectively for the executives, industrialists and professionals; the junior execurives gave inrermediare evaluations.)

What sratistics records in the form of systems of needs is nothing othec than the coherence of che choices of a habirus. And the inability to 'spend more', or differently, chat is, to rise to the system of needs implied in a higher level of resources, is the best illustration of che impossibiliry of reducing (rheoretically) che propensity to consume to the capacity to appropriate or of seducing che habitus to the economic conditions prevailing at a given moment (as represented, for example, by a given level of income). Ifeverything encourages a belief in the existence of a direct relarionship betweeu income and consumption, this is because casce is almost always the product of economic conditions identicał to rhose in which it fun tions, so that income tends to be credited wich a causal ef-Produced. of skilled workets (34,581 francs as against 25,716 francs) devote a very similar proportion of their income to food (35.4 percent, as against 38.3 percent for skilled workers and 30 percent for junior execuzives), so thae their actual spending on food is equal to that of senior execurives (12.503 francs as against 12,904 francs). Thirdly, spending on products which are expensive but characreristic of the bourgeois life-style increases much less or not at all This is true of veal, lamb, mutron, fish, shellish, citrus fruit etc.; freslı veg erables increase much more than fresh fruit, and both increase less than charcuterie.

Another example: as one moves from the 30,000-50,000 francs income bracket into the higher bracket, the food purchases of senior execurives do not change in at all rhe same way as rhose of manual workers. Though expenditure on food increases in borh cases (relatively more so among the executives), the irems which expand are, by order of importance, for senior execurive's (iucluding teachers and engineers): aperitifs, restaurant meals, non-alcoholic drinks, lamb, cakes and pastries, beef, fresh fruir, fsh and shellhsh, cheese; and for manual workers: pork, aperirifs, rabbir, fresh fruit, dried vegetables, bread, fresh vegerables.

The principle of the most important differences in the order of life. style and, even more, of the stylization of life' lies in the variacions in objective and subjective distance from the world, with itsmaterial constraints and temporal urgencies. They thus rep resent the zeto degree of make-up, of which the complexiey (lipstick alonc. jal cost in money, and especially in rime, increase as one moves up the sac hierarchy (in rhe same order as before), at least up to the level of che cter cal workers and junior execurives.

money, time and effort that would in any case be of low proftability, they stem from a choice of the necessaly ('That's not for us'), both in the sense of what is rechnically necessary, practical' (or,as others would say, unctional), i.e., needed in order to ger by', to do 'the proper thing and no , more', and of what is imposed by an economic and social necessiry condemning simple', 'modest' people to simple', 'modest' rastes. The adjustment to the objecrive chances which is inscribed in the dispositions constituting the habitus is the source of all the realistic choices which. based on the renunciarion of symbolic profirs that are in any case inaccessible, reduce practices or objects to their rechnical function, a short back-and-sides' or quick trim-up' at the barber's, 'a simple little dress', 'solid' furniture ecc. Thus noching is more alien to working·class women than rhe typically bourgeois idea of making each object in the home rhe occasion for an aesthetic choice, of extending the intention of harmony or beauty even into rhe bathroom or kicchen, places stricrly defined by their function, or of involving specifically aesthetic criteria in the choice of a saucepan or cupboard. Festive meals and 'Sunday best' clorhes are opposed to everyday meals and clorhes by the arbitrariness of a conventional division—'doing chings properly'—just as thc rooms socially designated for 'decorarion', the sitting room, the dining room or living room, are opposed to everyday places, that is, by an attichesis which is more or less that of the 'decorative' and the practical', and they are deco. ared in accordance wirh established conventions, wich knick-knacks on the mantelpicce, a foresr scene over the sideboard, ftowers on the table, without any of these obligatory choices implying decisions or a search for effecr.

This conventionalism, which is also chat of popular photography, concerned ro fix conventional poses in the conventional compositions,’ is the opposite of bourgeois formalism and of all the forms of art for art's sake recommended by manuals of graceful living and women's magazines, the art of encertaining, the art of the cable, che art of morherhood. In addirion to providing a form of basic security in a world in which there can be hardly any assurance, the choice of 'doing the proper thing' Or 'the done ching' (che vendors of domestic goods understand the power of 'Ir's the done rhing' over working-class insecurity) has a natural place in an economy of practices based on the search for the pracrical' and the refusal of frills' and fancy nonsense.

Even the choices which, from rhe scandpoint of the dominant norms.   
appear as the most 'irracional' are grounded in the tasre of necessity—   
plus, of course, the entirely negarive effect of che absence of information ompetence which resules from rhe lack of culrural capical.   
for examp he taste for the rrinkets and knick-knacks which adorn   
manrel hallways is inspired by an intention unknown to ecortonary aestheres, that of obtaining maximum 'effect' ('Ir'll fic effect') at minimum cost, a for mula which for bourgeois s the very definition of vulgarity (one of the intentions of distinc-

tion being to suggesr with the fewest 'effects' possible the greatesr ex. pendirure of time, money and ingenuicy). What is che gaudy' and the were almost given away'? treet hawkers and sales-promorion specialis know that chey must release che brakes and censorships which forbid ex. travagances' by presenting the forbidden goods as 'bargains'-the un fashionable settee which, if you can forger the colour and just rhink of the price, is exactly the one you had always wanted 'ro go in front of che TV', or the unwearable nylon dress you ended up buying because it was reduced in rhe sale, though you had 'sworn you would never again wear nylon.'

And if it still needed to be proved that resignarion to necessity is the basis of che raste of necessity, one only has to consider the waste of time and enetgy resulting from the refusal to subject the daily manage. ment of domestic life to the constraints of rational calculation and formal life-principles ('a place for everything', 'everything in its ame' etc.), which only apparently contradicts the refusal to devore time and care to health ('molly-coddling yourself') or beauty (getting dolled up'). In fact, in these two fearures of their life-style, working-class women, doubly dominated, show that they do not set suficient value on their trouble and their time, the only things they can spend (and give) withour counting, to be concerned abour sparing and saving chem, or, to put it another way, that they do not value themselves suff. ciently (and they do indeed have a low value on the labour market, un. like bourgeois women with their skilled labour-power and cultivated bodies) to grant themselves a care and arrention which always imply a certain indulgence and to devote to their bodies the incessant care, cortcern and attention that are needed to achieve and maintain health, slimness and beaury.'

A whole ser of convergent indices rends to show that working-class women set less value on and have less interest in rheir bodies chan women in other classes. For example, 40.2 percent of agricultural workers' wives and 36.0 percent of manual workers' wives consider chemselves below average in beauty as against 24.2 percent of uppet-class women and 33.2 percent of middle-class women; 13.0 percent and 14.0 percent respecrively, as againsı 10.1 percent and 7.6 perctnt, say they look old for their age; aııd working class women almost always award themselves lower marks (except for skin nose and hands) chan women in other classes. They set less store by beaugy and systematicaly devot less time, mone andinterest to al esonal care (see table 20 in chapter 3).

The calls to order ('Who des she hink she is? That's not for the likes of us') which reafhrm che principle of conformity—che norm of popular taste—and aim to encourage the 'reasonable

warning against che ambition to distinguish oneself by identifying with orher groups, thar is, they are a reminder of the need for class solidarity. The gaps between the cultural practices and preferences of the different classes are to a large extent due to the fact that the chances of finding in courses to which chey give rise can receive a value vary in much che same way as the chances of having such experiences, and no doubt play a part in determining these chances. The low interest which working-class peo. ple show in the works of legitimate culture to which they could have ac. cessspecially through television—is not solely the effect of a lack of competence and familiarity: just as supposedly vulgar subjects, such as relevision, are banished from boutgeois conversation (C.S. VI), so the fvourite subjects of bourgeois conversation, exhibitions, theatre. con. certs or even cinema, are exciuded, de facto and de jure, from workingclass conversation, in which they could only express the pretension to distinguish oneself. Perhaps the most ruthless call to order, which in it. xelf no doubt explains the extraordinary realism of the working classes, stems from the closure effect of the homogeneity of the directly experi. enced social world. There is no other possible language, no other lifestyle, no other form of kinship relation; the universe of possibles is closed. Orhet people's expecrations are so many reinforcements of dispositions imposed by the objective conditions.

The ritualization of pracrices and urrerances, which can go as far as stereoryping, is to some extent an effect of the very rigorous applicarion of the principle of conformity. A full-grown man who visirs another household is expected to have a drink; a middle-aged woman who dresses too young for herage'is severely, even cruelly, sanctioned (jibes, remarks behind her back etc.). Whereas great class differences may pass unnoticed and are in any ce very well rolerared (He's a real character' 'He's not like us') because chey arc steen as based on differences of narure (rhe docror's wife wifll be de scribed as 'made for fine clothes'), not che slightest deviation is permitted those who belong to the same class (or originare from it), because in this case difference could only arise from che desite to distinguish oneself, that is. from refusal or repudiation of the group (rhus the son of a bourgeois who breaks with his family is favourably regarded. whereas a worker's son who does the same ching is condemned). What is objecrionable is not been sharpened by political or trade-union activity, those of gentleman' etc.)

tion of virility, a rwofold submission which ordinaly language, narurally is predisposed to express.

Ir is nor only in thoughts that the opposition berween the working classes and the dominant class (especially its dorinared fracrions) is organized by analogy with the opposition berween the male and the female, thar is, through the categories of the strong and the weak, the fat (and coarse) and the thin (or fne) and so forth. Thus, as regards food, the working claes (and no doubt the men rather than the women) are opposed to the orher classes as men are opposed to women. Ir is known, for example, that a higher proporcion of senior exeeurives than of any orher class (except fam workers) drink milk for breakfast every day (59 percent, manual workers 42 percenr); the same is true of chocolare (executives 12 percent, manual workers 5 percent, farm workers S percenr, with no difterence berween the sexes in this case) and tea, in France a rypically bourgeois and feminine drink (executives 27 percent, manual workers 3 percent, farm workers 0 percent). But the working class is chiefly distinguished by the inctusion of salry, substantial, clearly masculine foods, such as soup (earen almost exclusively by farm workers), meat (46 percent of the farm workers, 17 percent of the manual workers, 6 percent of rhe senior executives say they at lease occasionally eat meat for breakfast) and cheese, whereas che senior executives and professionals rake frst place for sweer foods such as jam or honey (40 percent say they eat them every day, as against 29 percent of the farm workers and l1 percent of the manual workers), which are also clearly marked as feminine (only 38 percent of men ear them occasionally, 63 per cent of vomen; CS. XLVII).The same opposition emerges from an older survey, in which the proporrion who ear something savouy for breakfasr workers to the manual workers, to the clerical workers and senior execu in the opposite way.'

## Jaws

labour power which the laws of culrural reproduction and of rhe labour market teduce, more chan for any orher class, to sheer muscle power:1s and it should nor be forgotten that a class which, like che working lss, is only rich in its labour power can only oppose to rhe other classesapart from the withdrawal of its labour—its fighting strength, which de. pends on the physicalstrength and courage of its members, and also cheir number, i.e., their consciousness and solidariry or, to pur it another way. their consciousness of their solidarity.

Thus it would be a misrake to ignore the specifcally political effect of moralization (or 'de-moralizarion') which is exerred through ihe vehicles of the new therapeutic moraliry (women's magazines, the glossy week. lies, ra.dio programmes etc. ). As is shown hy the limiting case of tlie peas ancrywho, through the imposirion of the dominant life-style and the legitimare image of the body, have suff ered a blow to their specific conditions of reproduction (with the celibacy of peasant farmers) and to rheir very existence as a class capable of defining the principles of irs own identity-it is perhaps one of rhe last refuges of the auronomy of the dominared classes, of their capacity to produce their own representation of rhe accomplished man and the social world, thar is being threarened by all the challenges to working-class identifcation with the values of virility, which are one of rhe most auronomous forms of their self. afirmation as a class. The most fundamental principles of class identicy and uniry, those which lie in the unconscious, would be affected if, on the decisive poiut of relation ro the body, the dominared class came to see itself only through the eyes of the dominant clas, that is, in rerms of the dominant definition of the body and its uses. This having been said, in this area as in so many other and equally important areas which are not politically constituted, there is no realistic chance of any collective resistance ro rhe effect of imposicion thar would lead either to the valorzation of properties stigmarized by the dominant raxonomy (the 'black is beautiful' strategy) or to the creation of new, positively evaluated properties. Thus the dominared have only two oprions: loyalty ro self and the group (always liable to relapse into shame), or the individual effort to assimilace the dominant ideal which is the antithesis of the very ambition ofcollectively regaining contol over social identity (of the pe pursued by the collective revolr of the Amrican feminists when it advocares the 'natura.l took').
