## The Effects of Domination

enumerate the features of the life-style of che dominated classes which. Gramsci who said somewhere that the worker tends o bring his execurant dispositions with him into every arca of life. As much as by rhe ab sence of luxury goods, whisky or paintings, champagne or concers, cruises or art exhibitions, caviar or anticques, the working-class life-style is characrerized by the presence of numerous cheap subsritures for these rare goods, sparkling white wine' for champagne, imitation leather for real leather, reproductions for paintings, indices of a dispossession at the sec. ond power, which accepts the dehnirion of the goods worthy ofbeing possessed. With 'mass market' culrural products--music whose simple, repetitive structures invite a passive, absent participation, prefabricated entertainments which the new engineers of culrural mass production design for television viewers, and especially sporting events which establish a recognized division berween rhe specrators and the professionals, virtuosos of an esoteric technique or 'supermen' of exceptional ability-dis. possession of the very intention of determining one's own ends is combined with a more insidious form of recognition of dispossession.

The critique of culrural mass production, of which T. W. Adorno long ago provided the formula by esrablishing a direct, naive analogy berween the very form and uses of 'popular' music and the world of alienated labour, which, like a certain critique of sport, no doubr owes much of irs credibility to the fact thar it enables the nostalgia and revulsion of an amateur to be expressed with populist impeccability, has in fact masked what is essential i It is not only in music or sport that ordinary people are reduced to the role of the fan', the milirant 'supporter' locked in a passionare, even chauvinistic, bur passive and spurious participarion which is merely an illusory compensation for dispossession by experts. What the relation to 'mass' (and, a fortiori, 'elite') cultural producrs Produces, I activates and reinforces is not the monorony of the production line or office but rhe social relation which underlies working-class experience of the world, whereby his labour and the product of his labour, opus proprium, prsent rhemselves to the worker as opus alienm 'alienared' labour.

Dispossession is never more totally misrecognized, and therefore tacitly recognized, than when, with the progress ofautomation, possession is combined with the cultural dispossession which provi hierarchized individuals and activities, workers encounter legitimate cul. tical uility in order to be justifed. The experiences which che culsurally most deptived may have of works of legitimate culture (or even of many of the prefabricated entertainments offered by 'show business') is only one form of a more fundamental and mote ordinary experience, that of the division berween practical, partial, cacit know-bow and theoretical, sysrematic, explicit knowledge (a division which tends to be reproduced even in politics), berween science and techniques, theory and practice, 'conception" and 'execution', the 'intellectual' or the 'crearor' (who gives his own name co an 'original', personal' work and so claims ownership) and themanual' worker (che mere servant of an intention grearer rhan himself, an execurant dispossesseu elf an execurant dispossessed of the idea of his own practice)." h is irself ar

The educational system, an institutionalized classifier which is itself an objectified system of classification reproducing the hierarchies of the social world in a transformed form, with its cleavages by 'level' corresponding to social strara and its divisions inro specialities and disciplines which rfect social divisions ad infinitum, such as the opposition berween theony and pracrice, conception and execution, transforms social classificacons into academic classihcations, with every appearance of neutrality, and establishes hierarchies which are not experienced as purely technical, and therefore partial and one-sided, but as total hierarchies, grounded in narurc, so that social value comes to be identified with 'personal' value, scholastic dignities wich human dignity. The culture' which an educational qualification is presumed to guarantee is one of the basic compolents in the dominant definition of the accomplished man, so thar privation is perceived as an intrinsic handicap, diminishing a person's identity and human dignity, condemning him to silence in all offcial sit uations, when he has to appear in public', present himself before others 18 with his bodv. his manners and his language.

Misrecognition of the social dererminants of the educational career and therefore of the social trajectory it helps to derermine-gives the l system one of the fundamental agencies of the maintenance of the social order. It is no doubr in the area of educarion and culture shat the members of the dominated classes have least chance of discovering their ignorance.

Awareness of the effects of social background is particularly weak among craf tsmen and small shopkeepers, farm workers and manual workers. Among rhe senior execurives, recognition of these effecis does nor exclude belief in the virtues of intelligence (see rables 26 and 27). Everyching seems to indicare that in order for the working classes to recognize their educarional interest, they at least have to be involved in the educational system: discovering'schooling as a conservative force' presupposes some expetience of 'schooling as a liberating force. Thus it is students from the middle and working classes who are most sensitive to the problematic of 'democratization', alihough tliey often accepr the charismaric values which dominare the educarional sysrem. But it is above all the experience of relegation to the most devalued couises and sections, as well as the discovery of the devaluation of qualilications already held, that is likely to induce the beginnings of collective awareness.

The most visible principles of the offcial differences (i.e., those off. cially recorded in statuses and wages) found within the working class are senioriry and educarion (rechnical or general); ir may be wondered whether these are valued, especially in fotemen, as guarantees of compe tence or as cerrificates of 'morality', that is, of conformiry and even docij ity. The proportion of individuals who have no educarional qualifcariorı ar ali (or whose farher had none) declines sharply as one moves from the unskilled laboures, chrough the semi-skilled and skilled workers, to the foremen. Various indicarors of an ascetic disposition often linked to che ambition of upward mobility, such as fertilicy rate or the practice of sports such as gymnastics and swimming, vary in the same way, and so do indices of culrural good intentions, such as visits to cháreaux and public library. # A Foreman Who Has Always Worked for Other People'

Mr. L, aged 61, started work as an appren tice on the French Railways at the age of fourteen and a half. He became a skilled worker and is now a foreman. He 'deals with passenger rolling-stock.' His wife, aged 52, has never worked. She completed secondary school and would like to find a job. They have had four children; the eldest is a computer programmer, the second a Do. minican monk, the chird used to be a personaì assisrant bur sropped work when she married, and the youngest is studying for his baccalauréar They live in a municipatstyle Aar in Grenoble.

## 'You have to know what you're doing'

Mr. L. has done a lot of work on the apartment: 'You can sce if you look; there used to be a room there and a room here. Because chere were so many of us it was really cramped, so 1 rook the wall down, which means we've gor more space, we can enterrain more pcople. especially with the whole family.... We need a lot of room, especially when my son has his friends round, all sing. ing and dancing.'Most of their furnitute was boughr in Tunisia, where they lived for a number of years. Second-hand dealers and antiquedealers are al twisters ... con-men', and his pife adds: 'You need to know what you're doing . bur we've no idea whar things like rhat ane worth; char's why my husband says chey're all cwisters, our to chear you. They can seil us fakes and

rell us they're rcal, and as for the prices

## ·I koew how to make good use of it'

Their home is 'no museum'; the ornaments and vases are 'nor just dust traps' The various objects adorning the aparrment are mostly presenrs from the children or friends, or have been 'rescued', and they all have their 'use'. Madame L. will nor buy anything unril she 'has alrcady found a place for ir.' Thar vase now, I needed a vase. I wanted a vase because I wanted fowers, and when I was asked whar 1 wanted, 1 said "a vase" and they boughr it for me because I kncw how to make good use of ir.' His children 'know perfecrly well rhere's no point in buying things thar I won't use or that I'll pur away in a sideboard. Everything has to have a place for it otherwise, I don't like to have things just to put them away.' They make most of their purchasts in supermarkers (Carrefour, Record) or department srores (Nouvelles Ga. leries): I don't like window-shopping, I go straight to a big supermarker and you find everything there; you can choose wirhour going round a dozen shops,'Mr. L. explains. On holiday he always srarrs by 'checking out the marker': 'I'm interested in the price 1 pay and the quality of whar I ger. 1f you buy with your eyes closed, they'll fob you off with anything.

## 'Now, I'll be glad to work'

Recently they became the owners of a lirrle house in che mounrains ourside Grenoble. Some friends-he was an engineer'--invired them to rake it over several years ago. 1n the lasc three years, they have done a lor of work on it: 'Everything was rot. ren. I was walking around upsrairs and l fell through the foor There were no ciles left on the roof. the rain came in on all sides.'Mr. L. has done all che repair work himself: You know, I srarted as an apprentice on the railways, and an apprentice had to learn everything. You got a chrce-month course on sani ta. tion, chree months on electro.weld. ing and so on. And you always remember your trade, to some ex. rent.' They intend to make this house cheir mam home. 'Now, I'll be glad to work, now thar it's my house; l'm nor working for orher people I'm fed up with working and being exploired.'

## 'Learning to ski at 42'

He is 'very fond of cold wearher, snow, but nor skiing': l'm afraid of hurting myself. I go robogganing with my grandchildren. You see, I had an accident in my brother-inlaw's car in Tunisia ... and I injured my wrist. You only have to look at it to see the srate it's in. Thar's why I prefer tobogganing. I can sit up and there's no danger of falling.’(His wife 'srarted learning to ski ar 42, because of che children. .. We used ro go our wirh chem, we didn't want to spend the whole day sitring in a café or freezing in he car.') Whhen he was young, he 'did a lor of sport, espe. cially foorball.'

## You just have to like them'

He sometimes does the cooking: 'When my wife's away or with her sisters and l'm here ol my own, I'd rarher do my own cooking than go and car ar my mother's or one of my sisters-in-law's.'When she

'wants to give someone a treat', his wife looks up a menu in her recipe books.' 'I don't alrogether agree with my children when chey say. "You should cook somerhing you like" I say. "You ofren do chings in life that you don't like to start wirh, bur you just have ro like chem." buy rable wine ar Care. four; it's cheaper rhere. It's eleven proof and it costs one franc forry: by the bortle, it's tw francs chirty. five, I think.'

## ·Always something to keep me busy'

They go camping for their holidays. 'Ar the seaside, we go swimming .. I like looking for shell. fsh ... In the mounrains, I go looking for mushrooms, or snails There's always somerhing to keep me busy.'And his wife adds that last sumıner 'he was a bit disap. pointed because there was norhing to go looking for . . . He doesn't like being idle, he always has to find something to do. In fact rhat's why we go camping. He couldn't really see himself sitting around in hotels and resraurants, whereas when we go cainping, he can keep himself busy with the shopping. and a bit of swimming, of course, and alt clic chings thar have to be done. And then we generally have a look round the surrounding area.. . When we did Italy, we went all over rhe place lookmng at all the old sires, all the archaeological things. My husband doesn't enjoy that as much as I do Personally, I belong to an adult-edu cation group and so chat sort of thing interests me.

## \*Circuses, games, quizzes\`

On celevision. he watches foorball marches, cyclmg, all sorts of

When the World Cup was on, two or chree years back, I used to ger up at one or two in the morning to watch the matches.' Otherwise he doesnot watch much: 'I do like a good Westem, Or a swashbuckling adventure. But if ir's a play or some thing like thar, I jusr drop of.' His wife. who enjoys warching plays on TV. adds thar she also likes 'circuses, games—the Guy Lux pro. gramme--quizzes ... He doesn't go to football marches, because he is 'nervous in crowds', but he 'reads the sports resulrs in the papers.' He docs not buy a newspaper but somecimes picks up those left by the pas. sengers. The previous day. he 'brought home Le Figaro, L'Aurore, Le Canard Enchainé and Le Nourvel Observaleur.'The onc he most reads is L'Aurore, says his wife, 'road accidencs, fighcs, things like thar, whercas Le Monde is full of politics.' He reads 'detective stories, Maurice l.eblanc, Michel 7evaco ... historical advencures. When they curn up, I read thein. Otherwise, frankly, I don't pay much atrention. I go to bed and pick up a newspaper straight away so as to go to sleep. His wife, who borrows books from the local library, has recently read Solzhenitsyn's Cancer Ward, Michel de Saint-Pierre, Pierre-Henr Simon and cle lerrers of Françoise Parturier. She buys books when she 'thinks them worchwhile.'

## 'Don't ask me for the names of classical coınposers'

When he was young, he played the clariner a bit, then I rook up foorball and there wasn't time for borh.. .. I've always encouraged my children's interest in music becaust I enjoy it.' Vivaldi is nice. Lovely music, really catchy. Beethoven is good because it's soothing.. .. Don't ask ine for the names of clas sical composers, aparr from Beetho. ven, Chopin, Bach, people like thar I can't remember the ochers.' They have a few records, mainly dance music. His favourire singer is Sheila. 'There are two I can't stand, Sylvie Vartan and that Guy Béart.' (His wife's favourires are Marie Laforêt, Les Compagnons de la Chan. son and Mousraki.)

## 'You can see whar they represent'

They rarely go to the cinema: 'Why borher, wljen you've gor TV?' When they visited Paris. chey saw The White Horse and The Golden Flece at the 'Théîrre du Châreler and Vienna Waltzes at the Théâtre du Mogador. Since they were married they have not been co the opera: when he was young, he saw and enjoyed Tasca and La Traviata. On their first trip to Paris, ihey did all the muscums': 'even the Musée Grévin, thc Louvre, Versailles, the Panthéon; we've been to them all once.' 'I like art bur I don't know artists' naines. ... I've heard of Goya, and Pirandello, and Michelangelo, people like that.' His wife is somewhat cmbarrassed by his admission of lack of interest and reminds him: Ah, bur Michelangelo now, you liked him. You did like the Sistine Chape).' Then he adds: 'I liked those picrures because you can see what they represent. Bur when you see half a dozen squiggles and people spending a fortune on it, well, personally, I'd throw thar sort of ching in the dustbin if I found it. . . Anyway, l'd be afraid of being swindled.'

do nor manifest the concern for appearance which characterizes che no manual occuparions, strtring with the clerical workers. In short, every thing suggests that between the skilled workeis and foremen, who main subject ro the principle of conformity, and che office workers, wh life-style and in polirical positions.

The formcr are much less concerned chan the latter to mark their disrance from che most typically popular amusements and pasrimes, such as funfairs or sporting events: 60.4 percent of them (and 58.2 percent of the semiskilled and unskilled) say they have been to a funfair at least once in the previous year, compared to 49.5 percent of he clerical workers and 49.6 per. cenr of the junior execurives. Manual workers in general rather more often watch sporr and circuses on TV, whereas junior execurives and clerical workers much more ofren warch scientific, historical or lirerary program,nes (C.S. VII). Our of very similar incones, the manual workers spend more on food and less on everyrhing concerned with personal appearance and care (clothing, hygiene, hairdressing, pharmaceutical producrs) (C.S. HI); rhe men's spending on clorhing is 85.6 percent, and the women's 83.7 per. cent, of the corresponding amounts among clerical workers. They huy the same clotlies more cheaply (for example, thcy spend only 83 percent of the amount spent by clerical workers on Overcoats, 68.7 percent on jackets, 83.s percent on shocs a difference which is much more marked among women) and, above all, differenr clothes: on the one hand, learher or imitation-leather jackets and lumber jackers (for cold, early-morning travel by scoorer), boiler-suirs. dungarees and overalls, on the othet hand, overçoars (symbols of perir-bourgeois respecrabiliry), blouses, aprons. sports jackets and biazers. The skilled workers, the only category isolared in the available statistics, differ from the clerical workers, alhough they have similar incomes, almost as much as manual workers in general (excepr on one point, expendirure on films and records).

It is nor, however, in the arca of culrure thar one should look for a distance, or a selfdistancing, from the dominant class and irs values, other than a purely negarive one, by defaulr. There is, of course, every. thing which belongs ro the art of living, a wisdom raught by necessity, suffering and humiliation and deposited in an inherited language, dense even in its stereorypes, a sense of revelry and festivity, of self expression and practical solidarity with orhers (evoked by the adiecive bon vivant' the existence of a 'popular culrure',a paradoxical notion which imposes, willynlly, the dominant defnition of culrure, must expect to fndif hey were to go and look-only the scartered fragments of an old erudite culrure (such as folk medicine), selecred and reinterpreted in rerms of the fundamental principles of the class habitus and integrared into the unitaly world view it engenders, and not the counter-culrure they call for, a culrure truly raised in opposition to rhe dominant culture and consciously claimed as a symbol of starus or a declararion of separare exis. Ecnce.

if ihere is no popular art in the sense of an art of the urban working class, it is perhaps because this class knows no orher hierarchies than the purely negative ones which are mcasured by disrance from the absolute povery and insecurity of the sub-prolerariat, and remains fundamentally defined by the relation of dispossessed to possessor which links ir to the bourgeoisie, in culture as in orher areas.2' Whar is generally meant by popular arr, i.c., the art of the peasant classes of capitalist and pre·capitalist societies, is the product of a srylizing intention which is associated with the exisrence of a hierarchy: locally based, relarively auronomous milieux also have their hierarchies of luxury and necessiry, which the symbolic marks, clorhing, furniture, jewellery, express and reinforce. Here roo, art marks diff erences w'hich it presupposes. It is no accident that the only area of working-class praciice in which style in itself achieves srylizarion is thar of language, with argot, the language of leaders, 'godfarhers', which implicirly afhirms a counter-legitimacy with, for example, che intenrion of deriding and desacralizing the 'values' of the dominant morality and aesthetic.

it rends to be forgorren that the specific logic of cultural domination qeans that the fullest recognition of culrural legitimacy can and often des coexist with rhe most radical challenging of polirical legitimacy. Furthermore, the awakening of polirical consciousness is ofren bound up Rith a whole process of rehabilirating and rebuilding self-estcem, which, bccause it involves a reaflirmation of culrural dignity tha is experienced as (and indeed always is) libcrarory, implies a submission to the domi-

CGT) among skilled workers, falling back to 18 percent among foremen and rechnicians, the relationship between educational level and rate of unionization being weakened by the fact thar the foremen, while more edu. cated, are also less unionized It is also clear that, like educational level, knowledge of legitimate culture rises with position in the occupational hier. archy. The skilled workers and foremen, older and having had a slighely longer schooling than che semi-skilled and unskilled workers, manifest slightly greater culcural competence: only 17.5 percent know (by name) fewer than two of the works of music, as against 48.5 percent of the semiskilled and unskilled, of whom a very high proportion decline to answer the quesrions on pai nting and music. Skilled workers and fotemen more often cite the canonical artists, Iconardo (38 percent as against 20 percent) Watteau, Raphael, whereas the semi-skilled and unskilled pick out vaguely familiar names more or less ar random, Picasso, Braque, Rousseau (no doubt confusing the 'douanier' with }ean-Jacques)." And, above all, whereas the semi-skilled and unskiled readily admit thar painting do not interest them or that classical music is comphcared', the skilled workeis more under the sway of cuitural legitimacy, morc: often identify wich a token of recognition combined with a confession of ignorance ('I love clas. sical music but I don'r know much about it' or 'Paintings are nice but dif. fcult').

Thus, everything leads one to believe that the most politically conscious fracrion of che working class remains profoundly subject, in culture and language, to che dominant norms and values, and cherefore deeply sensitive to the efects of aurhority imposition which every holder of culrural authority can exert, even in polirics, on chose in whom the educational system—this is one of the social effects of primary educarion—has inculcated a recognition withour knowledge.

## Culture and Politics

Perhaps the most radical approach to the problem of polirics is to ask of it the question char Marx and Engels raise in relarion to art. Having analysed the concentrarion of the capacity for artistic producrion in the hands of a few individuals and the correlative (or even consequent) dis. possession of the masses, they imagine a (communist) sociery in which 'there are no painters bur at most people who engage in painting arong ocher things'.' and in which, thanks to the development of the productive forces, the general reducrion of working rime (chrough an overall decrease and an equal distribution) allows ‘everyone sufhicient free time to take part in the general affairs of sociery—cheorelical as well as psacical.' 'There are no politicians bur at most people who engage in politics among orher activities': here as elsewhere, a uropia is scientifically (and, no doubt, politically) justifed by the way it demolishes self-evident appearances and forces one to bring to light the presuppositions of the usual order. Indeed, although irs apparent generosicy makes it the diametric opposite of the élirist denunciarions of universal suftrage which che intellectuals and arrists of anorher age went in for, the indulgent Populism which credits the common people with innate knowledge of polirics equally helps to disguise and so consecrate che 'concentrarion in a few individuals' of rhe capacity to produce discourse about the social world, and, through this, the capacity for consciously changing that world. The uropian paradox breaks the doxa: by imagining a social world in which anyone in whom chere is a potential Raphael' of painting or politics could develop without hindrance, it forces one to see thar the concentration of che embodied or objectifed instruments of production potential Raphaels whom the mechanisms responsible for this monopoly keep excluded much more effcrively than any deological state appar euses'.

Even if one only credits the idealized people' with a wholly pracical knowledge of che social world as such, or at least of its position and inter. ests in chat world, one srill has to examine whether, and how, chis poliri. cal sense can be expressed in a discourse corresponding to the truth ir contains in pracrical form, and thus become the moror of a conscious and, through the mobilizing power of explicit srarement, truly collective action.² Sraying closer to reality, it has to be asked whether this political sense is really the infallible Rair it is sometimes claimed to be, which could at least identify che most appropriate products on the market of discourses produced and supplied by the owners of the means of produc. tion of legitimare problems and opinions.

Polirical science long ago began to register the fact that a large pro. portion of the persons surveyed absrained' from answering questions on politics and that these 'non-responses' varied significantly by sex, age, educational level, occupation, place of residence and political rendency. Bur no conclusions have been drawn, and che psephologists merely de plore chis culpable absrention'. As soon as one sees thar the inert 'don't know'caregory is largely recruited from what orhers call 'the masses' or 'the people', one begins to suspecr the funcrion it performs in the operarion of liberal democracy' and the contribution it makes to maincaining che esrablished order. Absrentionism is perhaps not so much a hiccup in the system as one of the condirions of irs functioning as a misrecog nized-—and cherefore recognized—restriction on political participation.

Whar needs to be questioned is the very notion' of personal opinion'. The opinion poll, by urging all irs respondenrs, wichout distinction, to produce a personal opinion—an incention underllined by ail che according-to-yous', in-your-views'and whar-do-you.personally-thinks' in the quesrionnaires-or to choose, by their own means, unaided, between several pre-formulared opinions, implicitly accepts a political philosophy which makes political choice a specifically political judgement, applying political principles to answer a problem that is presented as political, and which credirs everyone with not only the right but also the power to pro duce such a judgement. A social history of the norion of personal opin ion'would no doubr show thar chis eighreenth-century invention :s coored in the rationalist belief thar the faculry of judging well', as Ds cartes called it, thar is, of discerning good from bad, truth from falsehood, by a sponraneous and immediate inner feeling, is a universal aptitude of universal application (like the faculty of aesthetic judgement according to Kant)even if it had to be conceded, especially from the nineteenth cenrury on, char universal education is indispensable in order to give this apritude its ful development and to secure a real basis for the opinion perhaps owes part of its slf-evident chatacter to the fact chat being constructed against the Church's claim o a monopol of che legitimare producrion of judgements, the means of production of judgements and che producers of judgements, and being inseparable from che idea of tolerance, that is, the challenging of all aurhority in the name of che conviction that in such matters ll opinions, whoever their ptoducer, are equally valid-it expressed from the very beginning the interests of the intellectuals, small, self.employed opinion producers whose tole developed paralel to the constitution of a specialized field of production and a marker for culrural producs, chen of a sub.feld specializing in che producrion of political opinions (wich che pres, che parties and all the representarive bodics).

The act of producing a response to a questionnaire on politics, like voring or, ar another level of participation, reading a party newspaper (journal d'opinion) or joining a party, is a particular case of a supply meeting a demand. On one side is the field of ideological production, a relatively autonomous universe in which amidsr comperition and con-Alicr, che instruments for chinking the social world are creared and where, chrough chis process, che field of the politically thinkable, or, to puc it anocher way, the legitimare problemaric, is defined. On the ocher side are social agetts, occupying different positions in the field of class rela. tions and dehned by a grearer or lesser specific political competence---a grcater or lesser capacity to recognize a political question as polirical and to trear it as such by respondmg to it politically, i.e., on the basis of specihically political principles (cacher than ethical ones, for example). This capacity is inseparable from a more or less strong feeling of being competent, in the full sense of che word, chat is, socially recognized as encirled to deal with polirical affairs, to express an opinion abour them or even modify cheir course. In facr, chere is reason to suppose thar comperence in the sense of rechnical capacity (political culture) is correlared with competence in the sense of a socially recognized capacity, ascribed by and ascribing srarusthe opposite of which is boch imporence and objective and subjective exclusion ('That is none of my business'; That doesn't interest me').

## Selective Democracy

As soon as one rakes seriously the don't knows' and their variations, which are the most important information supplied by opinion polls, it is clear that the probabilicy of having a particular opinion thar is associated with a given category (which is indicated by the frequency with Which members of that caregory choose one or another of che alternatives offered) is only a condisional probability—the probability that an eventuality will occur on condicion that anorher eventuality occurs, in probability of producing a particular opinion) according to the proper. ties of che respondents and also the properties of the question, or, more precisel,according to the relationships between these two sets of proper. ties. This probability is greater for men than for women, greater for the young than the old; greater in large rowns (especialiy Pais); and rises with educational capital (measured by qualifications) and economic capital (measured by income) and with social posirion. The variations linked to these variables are that much more marked when che questions are more remote from experience, more abstract and detached from ordinaty realities in their content and phrasing (and also, but secondarily, when chey have only recently appeared in the field of ideological production) and when they require more insistently a response produced on the basis of specifically political principles (a demand thar is perceived in the very syntax and vocabulary of che question).

Everything takes place as if the most 'legitimare' agents, that is, those most comperent in both senses, wete and felt that much more legitimated—i.e, inclined and called upon to express an opinion—the more 'legitimare' the problem posed. Thus, one fnds that those who cannot reply to the question of their political allegiance or preference (indicat. ing the party to which they feel closest) are those who are also most in. clined to leave the other questions unansweredespecially when the question posed is clearly located in the register of professional politics. The interviewees who fall into the non-aligned ‘grey area' in the SOFRES survey do nor reply much less often (81 percent) than those who idencify with the extreme left (91 percent), the left (90 percent), the centre (86 percent), the right (93 percent) or the extreme right (92 percent), when asked whether France should help poor countries'. But when asked whether France should favour countries wich a 'democratic regime', they reply significantly less ofren (57 percent) than those idenrifying with the extreme left (76 percent), the left (67 percent), the centre (75 percent). the right (70 percent). or the extieme right (74 percent) (ŠOFRES, 1971).

whch are somrimes the practical cquivalent of these. In pratice, the best 'don'i knows' varies from one institute to another (orher things being equal, SOFRES always seems to fnd fewer than IFOP) and, independently of che question, from one survey to another, i.e, according to the inter-. ¿icwers' instructions and their inclination to follow them. For a given sub. iect, it also varies over time.

Furthermore, the absence of a reply is nor always the product of a negative deiermination and, alongside the non-responses for lack of comperence which are analysed here, one also has to count the elective non-responses, a genuine abscention expressing the efect of a discordance wich the legitimare reply, which is censored because it dare not be expressedan ethical or polirical conflict wirh no ourcome but silence. In 3 particularly clear case of censorship, a considerable proportion of the farmers and small employers (17.1 percent and 15.8 percent, as against 4.1 percent of the office workers and junior execurives, 5.1 percent of che senior execurives and professionals, 8 percent of the manuat workers) refrain from answering a question on tax evasion (IFOP, February 1969). In a typical case of conflict accompanied by censorship, senior executives abstain relatively frequently (22 percent—like manual workers—compared with 19 percent for clerical workers and junior executives, 27 percent for the employers and 41 percent for the farmers) from answering a question on the role of che unions (IFOP, April 1969); and the ambiguity of the peasants'relation to the workers and cheir organizations can surely be read in their high rare of abstention, which cannot be imputed solely to incompetence since, in the same survey, they reply more ofren (72 percent compared to 59 percent) to a question on the scudent movements, which they know no better.

A similar unease may express itself in the typical distribution of the mmbers of a group into three fairlly equal classes: those who escape by abstention, those who approve and those who disapprove. When asked by IFOP in 1968 about Czecho Soviet relations ('Do you think that the Moscow agreements signed on August 26 are satisfactory or unsatisfacrory for the Czechs?'), 37 percent of the Communist Party (PCF) supporters did tot answer, 19 percent said chey were satisfactory and 44 percent said they were nor. Those who would vote for the orher left- or right-wing parties answered more often: only 18 percent of the PSU voters were don't-knows' 22 percent of che Centrises, 26 percent of the Socialises and Radicals, 27 percent of the Gaullists (UDR), 32 percent of the Giscardians (RI): and they much more often said the agreements were unsatisfactory (8 percent of the PSU voters, 73 percent of che Centrists, 70 percent of the Socialisrs and Radicals, 69 percent of the Gautlists, 64 percent of the Giscardians). Similarly, asked whecher chey approved or disapproved of the introduction of sex educarion into the school curriculum' (IFOP, 1966), 19 percent of the agriculrural workers gave no answer (as against 11 percent of the employ.

and 7 percent of che senior executives and professionals), 33 percenr of rhem disapproved and 48 percent approved (whereas 74 percent of the se nior execurives and professionals, 72 percent of che manual workers, clenica workers and junior execurives and 60 percent of rhe employers were favour. able).

It is nontheless pssible to ger a fairly precise de of the specih efece of the relarionship berween the respondent's competence and the subiecr and form of the question by examining how the gaps berween the rates of non-responsee.g, for men and women—vary in a single suivey (a SOFRES survey of February 1971 on France, Algeria and the Third World), that is, in a case where all other things may be considered equal (see table 28). One first hnds that though women reply almost as often as men when asked if France is or is not doing enough to help immigrant workers find accommodarion' (85 percent in each case), 'to give rhem training' (70 percent as against 75 percent), 'to make them welcome' (80 percent as against 83 percent), 'ro give them decent wages' (77 percent as against 83 percent), all of which are problems that can be considered ethicaily and thar, according to the traditional morality, women are comperent to deal with, rhey are much less inclined to reply than men when confronted with a specifically political problem. Only 75 percent of them, compared with 92 percent of the men, answer a ques. tion on continuing the policy of co-operation with Algeria', a problem, as the question itself indicates, of pure politics, foreign policy being more rcmote from concrete experience than internal politics, especially when approached, as here, without any ethical rcference (As regards Franico-Algerian relations, do you think it desirable that France should continue a policy of co-operation with Algeria?). As soon as the abstract question of co-operation is put back into rhe realm of erhics, or even the charity. which the traditional division of labour assigns to women, specialists of the heart and feelings (Among the different groups of under-developed countries, in your view should France rake a particular interest in the poores! countries?'), women reply as ofren as men (88 percent each). Bur when the survey ierurns ro specifically political or political-scientific questioning—and an abstract vocabulary which means different rhings to different groups—by asking if France should be interested in countries which have a democraic regime', the proportion of women who rcply again falls sharply, to 59 percent compared with 74 percent of the men

In fact, analysis by fractions would probably show that, in polirics as in aesthetics, the diff erences berween the sexes tend to diminish both when one moves from the dominated classes to the dominant classes and when, within the dominant class and no doubt also the petite bourgeoisie, one moves from the economically dominant fraction to the dominated fractions. Everything suggests that the refusal of sexual starus, in political or other matters, tends to increase with educational level. Thus when the sexes have similar secondary or higher education, more women than men consider that membership in a family planning movement is a political act or that sex education is a political problem (the relationship betwen the sexes on che same questions is inverted at lower levels of education).

More generally, the more the questions deal with probiems of daily ex istence, private life or domestic morality—housing, food, child rearing between the least educated and the most educared, and sometimes it disappears completely. For example, to a question on girls' upbringing, vourite dishes)—excepr for questions on wines, knowledge of which is a ized (Would you say thar, in general, the French eat to much, about the light amount, or not enough?'men 98 percent, women 96 percent; SOFRES, December 1971; C.S. XXXIV).

The same logic explains why manual workers, who normally have a particularlly high rate of non-response, are the most eager to answer ques. ions on the government's role in industrial disputes (the level of non. response runs from 13 percent among manual workers to 18 percent among the executives and professions, 19 percent among clerical workers and junior executives, 25 percent among craftsmen and shopkeepers and 31 percent among farmers and farm workers) or on the parties and unions which best defend the interests of wage-carners (manual workers 36.4 percent, employers 37.6 percent, clerical workers and junior executives 38.9 percent, senior executives and the professions 40.1 percent, farmers 9 percent).1º By contrast, the more specifically political or political-scientific the question, i.e., the more it is constitured as such in both subject and language without direct reference to the experience or interests of the group concerned, the greater the gap between men and women or between the least- and most-educated. Thus, in an extreme case, in an IFOP question on the connection between the 1967 Middle-East 'conflict' and the Vietnam ‘war'," the level of don't knows'rose to 40 percent for women, 21.8 percent for men, 40.6 percent for those with primary educarion, 8.5 percent for those with higher education. In anαher question, previously referred to, on Czecho.Soviet relations the corresponding figures are 44.6 percent, 21.1 percent, 39.4 percent and 11 percent 12 In a sort of experimental reproduction of electoral democracy, the survey shows that the antinomy between democratic spontaneism, which grants everyone the right and duty to have an opinion, regardless of sex and class, and technocratic aristocracy, which restricts opinion to experts' elected for their intelligence' and competence', finds a practical solutton in the mechanisms which induce the free’ self exclusion of those whom technocratic selection would exclude in any case.

## Class Consciousness

'To srart with, when I gor married, I had no definire opinions, I was from Brirtany, see, 1 certainly felt there were problems and, well, 1 fele it, but 1 couldn't define them. I married a Communist, wirhout realizing it. At first, you know, when 1 had just arrived in che house, when I saw L'Humanité Dimanche, I was furious. You see, 1 didn't know che paper, for months and years I didn't know abour ic. And then, little by lirrle, you see, I was led to recognize, you see, thar maybe its atritudes were a little bir hard, but the fact was, they were righr. My husband was a trade-union man, so I was dealing wirh a rrade unionist, and chen I had one, cwo, three children, then, you sce, I wenr through che difhculries of life like everyone, little by little. There were some very bad parches, because I had to look after my mother as well as every. thing else. And my husband's a worker, right, so 1 knew about the workers' problems. And then 1 really made up my mind ar the rime of the Algerian war, you see, I real. ized char monstrous things were being done thar we had always beer against. We'd always thought that chat war was wrong and char was really when 1 understood what the Party stood for. Especially when there was the massacre ar Charonne, remember? Well, that completely revolted me, because you see, I'd made my husband stay at home that evening, otherwise he might have been chere.'

Seamsiress, turner's wife, age 42, Communist

the volume of educational ca.piral (64 percent of such 'office holders' have higher education, 31 percent of the rest), or, in parries in which the grear majority of Depuries have higher educarion, on the rype of educational cap iral (law and the humanities are over-represented).

So it wrould be naive ro see the very close relationship between educational capital and the propensiry to answer the most specifically political questions as a simple and direct eftect of the unequal distribution of specific political comperence, defned, in the restricted sense, by possession of the theoretical and practical knowledge needed to produce polirical acrions and judgements and perhaps especially by command of poliri cal language, capacities which, one may assume, vary with educarional capital.

When the respondents were invited ro classify a set of movements, group ings or political parties as they saw ft, in general the higher their social po sition or the greater thcir educarional capiral, the more classes they produced. Most of those educated below the baccalauréat made a maxilnum hose with a degrce above che licence distinguisheclar least nine groups (a performance rarely achieved by those with the CEP or CAP and never by the unqualihed). The ability to make refined classificatioas, the readiness to comment on them and, especially, to give names or qualihers to the categores vares even more strongly by social position, educational capital and also social origin.

Such a reading—overemphasizing the unequal distribution of political capital—which would be supported by all the appearances, would do no more than record rwo contrasting and complementary representa. gions of the division of political labour: the rechnocratic represenrarion, which makes technical comperence (as defined by technocrars) che precondition for access to polirical responsibilitics' or 'responsible' polirical choices, and the complementary representation, based on the sense ofincomperence and imporence, which condemns the economically and cul. turally deprived to reliance on 'experts' or a belief in 'cryptocracy', another way of overestimating the orher classes. Like every recording of 'dara' ar face value, this endorsement of appearances would have the effect of preventing incuiry inro the objective bases of these representarions, and, more precisely, would prevent one from looking into the reality of the division of political labour for the trurh contained in these representarions of che division of labour.

In facr, here as elsewhere, the relationship berween reality and represen. tations is esrablished through the dispositions which are the internalized form of the probabilities associated with a given position in the division of labour. 'Technical' competence depends fundamentally on social comperence and on the corresponding sense of being entitled and required by starus to exercise this specific capaciry, and cherefore to possess it. In other words, to undersrand rhe relarionship berween educational capiral and the propensiry to answer political questions, it is nor sufcient to consider the capaciry to undersrand, reproduce, and even produce polirical discourse, which is guaranteed by educational qualifications; one also has to consider che (socially authorized and encouraged) sense of being entirled to be concerned wich politics, authorized to ralk politics, by ap. Plying a specific political culture, i.e., explicitly political principles of classihcation and analysis, instead of replying ad hoc on the basis of ethical principles.17

The effect of educational qualihcations is nor so dif erent as it seems fom the effect of sexual sratus. In cach case, what is at srake is as much a starus-linked right to politics as a simple political culture, the prercquisite to che exercise of this right which those who fecl entitled to exercise it Provide for themselves. Technical competence is to social competence whar che capacity to speak is to the right to speak, simultaneously a pre-Condirion and an effect. The marking produced by the imposition of both on the marked individual, who is called upon ro live up' to his or her social definition, and on orhers, who expect him or her to actual his or her essence. (The psychological translation of this relationship parricularly visible in the relations within couples.) Thar is why compe tence in the sense of specifc culrure is to competence in the sense of st tus property as exisrence is to essence: only those who oughr to have can really acquire it and only those who are aurhorized to have it fel called upon to acquire ir.

The beginnings of a proof of this argument are seen in the fact thar, other things being equal (in particular, educational capital), women difer from men nor so much in strict technical comperence as in their manner of affirming ir. Thus, at equal educacional levels, women dene almost the same number of caregories within the universe of polirical movements and par. ries, and almost as ofren give them names. Ir is true that, partly because polirics is their business and they invest more in it, the men know more politicians' names, more ofren know which party they belong ro, can more ofren give the name and party of their local Depury. Buc the difterence is greatest when it is a marter of socially assercing chis competence. Ar equal educational levels, more women say they do nor know wherher the various problems mentioned to them are political or nor;'\* they more ofren confess they do nor know who 'Philippon' is (see note (6). whereas the men are more ofren inclined to disguise this ignorance; they are more often conteti to indicate a general zone in the political scale whereas the men rend ro situare themselves precisely. Above all, they are more often prepared to acknowledge chat politics is a matter for specialists. Finally, they are much more inclined to contess that at election rimes they cannot decide whom to vore for. Not only do they tend more to delegate their capacity for polirical choice to others (in most cases, no doubr, to their husbands), but they seem to have a more local, and more moral and emotional, view of polincs. Thus, at all levels, rarher more of them say thar help for the handicapped is a political problem; and rarher fewer of them say this of voting in parliamentary or local elections or ofa collection for Viernam.

Thus, the variations in the rare of non-responses by sex, educational capital or social class, that is, roughly according to the probabiliry of pos sessing power in any held wharsoever, show thar political competence in the sense of a socially recognized capacity is one of the aptitudes a person has insofar as he has a right or dury to have them. And che variarions which are also found, orher things being equal, according to the satura tion of the quesrion with indices of contormity to the norms of depolici limirs of comperence when, by besrowing the appearances of s the 'incompetent'.

## The Right to Speak

The propensity to speak politically, even in the most rudimentary way, thar is, by producing a yes' or a 'no', or purting a cross beside a prefabricared answer, is strictly proportionare to the sense of having the right to speak. Norhing shows this more clearly than the composition of the so called sponraneous sample of people who responded to a nationał consulration' on the educarional syscem, organized, with the aid of the press, afrer the events of 1968.9 In so doing they asserted themseives as parties to the debate, entirled to express an aurhorized, aurhoritative opinion, to voice the performarive utterance of a legitimare pressure group.2o The opinion mobilizeds if by a peritionabour education closeìy coincides with the population of users of higher educarion. The probabiliey that an isolared agent, in che absence of any delegation, will form an explicit, coherent opinion on the educarional system depends on the exrent to which he depends on it for his reproduction and is objectively and subjecrively inzerested in irs funcrioning.

As if the propensiry to infuence che destiny of the institution rose with one's importance wirhin it, the rate of response rises with the sense of legitimacy in speaking about it and with the immediacy of self-interest in its funcrioning. Thus the probabiliry of responding, which is much grearer for men chan for women (che la.rrer rending to write in as parents racher than as authorized spokespersons of a group or the general inrerest) and for Parisians than for provincials, is, for a given social class, very close to its objective chances of getting its children into one of the grandes écoles (i.e., very close to nil for pcasants and manual workers of the order of 0.09 and 0.05 in 10.0000.7 per 10,000 for crafrsmen and shopkepers, 0.9 for clerical workers, 3 for iunior executives. 19 for primary teachers, 5 for industrial and commercial employers, 11 for senior executives, 22 for engineers, 26 for members of the professions, 110 for secondary' and higher-education reachers-rhese fgures are obrained by comparing the male population of each clas of respondents with the corresponding fraction of the working populawon). For pupils and studenes, who respond more often when at higher levels of study and when enrolted in more prestigious establishments (a lycée rather than a comprehensive or technical school, a grande école rather than a faculty) or when living in Paris than in the provinces, the probabilor srudents, ana six times greater mn rne case or pupils.)

## Quantity and Quality

Thus che self-selecring, sponraneous sample m the 'narional consulrarion' is che limis rowards which che population of responderts in a represencarive survey rends; or, to pur it anocher way, the answers obtained from a represenrative sample consrirure a spontaneous sample which, al though ir is nor perceived as such, is produced by law's which are fundamenrally the same as those described here.

Wirhin the sponraneous sample, the less a caregory is represented, the less irs 'representatives' are typical of the caregory as a whole, and the prin ciple of the bias is almost always their relarionship to the educational sys. tem. (T'hus, 90.7 percent of the mdustrial and commercial employers have at least the baccalauréat, compared with only 11.3 percent in the caregory in the working popularion, and, for the craftsmen and shopkeepers, 28.7 percent compared with only 2.8 percent.) Similarly, the more remote a fracrion is from the educational sysicm, the more chose who respond belong to char part of the fracrion most inceresred in che educarional system, i.e.. those who are of an age ro have children in secondary or higher educarion Thus 49.7 percent of che reachers in che sponraneous sample are aged 35-54 (38.9 percent in the working popularion), 697 percent of the professionals (53.5 percent in the working population), 77.1 percent of the employers (50.7 percent in the working popularion).

It follows from this over-selection that the dif erences between che classes and the fractions (who, within the middle and dominant classes, are repre sented in accordance with their cultural capital) arc reduced so a minimum. so thar the degree of agreement over the problems mentioned is no doube greater chan in the popularion as a whole? If reachers are so strongly over represented, this is because they are interesred and legitimared on several feci of legitimacy predominates over the effect of interest due to simple membership The representation of the different categories of teachers in creases as one moves up the hierarchy of institurions (19 per l0.000 for pr. maty teachers, 34 for CET reachers. 60 for CEG reachers,23 199 for lycee teachers, 224 for highereducation teachers) and also by rank within cach rspond more readily chan any other category. This is no doubr because, while being more legitimate chan, for example, primary ceachers, who are university teachers, who can place rheir investments ourside che institution, and not only in research (except for those in the most traditional disci. plines, French, Iatin, Greek, or history, who are much more inclincd ro respond than those in other disciplines and ocher faculties). It is perhaps also because, more than all orher categories, agreges felt themselves personaly caled inro question by the crisis.

If teachers of the disciplines previously dominant in the educarional sysrem\_Larin, Greek, French, history—were parricularly prone to reply (they wrore many books and arricles afrcr May 1968), this is also because the redelinition of the legitimare curriculum and legitimare pedagogy, especially in secondary schools, threatened their very existence as producers (or repro duceis} of products which had no other marker or raison d'être than secondary education and the compesirions for recntitment to it.

But che essential lesson of this analysis is thar in forcing on everyone, uniformly, problems which only arise for a few, by a procedure as irreproachable as the administration of a questionnaire wirh pre-formed answers to a representative sample, there is every likelihood of creating a pure arrifact out of thin air. Opinions are made to exist which did not pre-exise the quesrions, and which otherwise would nor have been expressed; or which, if rhey had been orherwise expressed, i.e., through authorized spokesmen, would have been quire difterenr; opinions which, in any case, had litrle chance of being formulated spontaneously, of being demonstrareda demonsrrarion being one way of giving srrength ro opinions by demonsrraring che group which chey mobilize. Polirical opinion is nor a purely informative judgement which 'catches on' by che intrinsic force of its cruch, but an idée-forte, containing a prerension to become a reality; and the more numerous and powerful the group it mo. bilizes by its symbolic efficacy, the greater its power of potential enact. ment. In orher words, because it necessarily conrains a power to mobilize and a pretension ro exist, political opinion is defined, nor only by irs informarive content but also by che social force whereby it exists as a policical force, although it is polirical opinion which helps to make that social force exist by mobilizing the group which conrains it in potential form.

The authorized specch of status-generared comperence, a powerful speech which helps ro create whar it says, is answered by the silence of an equally starus-linked incomperence, which is experienced as rechnical incapacity and lcaves no choice bur delegarion-a misrecognized disposnopoly of che competenr. These rwo opposed but complementary repredivision of political powers' berween che classes and che sexes in disposi tions, practices and discourses, and so help to reproduce the division it. self.21 And so it is that, by one of the paradoxical reversals that are common in such matteis, education, which the nincteenth-cenrury reformers expected above all to ensure the proper functioning of universal suffrage by producing citizens capable of voting ('We must educare our masters') now tends to function as a principle of selection, the more ef. fective for nor being officially or even tacitly imposed, which supports and legitimates unequal participation ia electoral democracy and, ren. dentially, the whole division of political labour.

## Personal Opinion

Nierzsche somewhere derides the academic cult of the personal rum of phrase', and it would be no small undertaking to describe the whole range of institutional mechanisms, especially the intellectual and educa. tional ones, which help to encourage the culr and culture of the 'person', that set of personal properties, exclusive, unique and original, which includes personal ideas', 'personal style' and, above all, 'personal opinion'. It could be shown thar che opposition berween the rare, the distinguished, the chosen, the unique, the exclusive, che diff erent, the irreplace able, the original, and the common, the vulgar, the banal, che indifferent, the ordinary, the average, the usual, the trivial, with all the associated oppositions between the brilliant and rhe dull, the fine and the coarse. the refined and che crude, rhe high (or heighcened) and the low is one of the fundamenral oppositions (the other being organized around the op position berween ease and poverty) in the language of bourgeois erhics and aesthecics. Those who aim to identify the contribution the eduçacional syscem may make to the inculcarion of a view of the social world, and who, as in so mny studies of che content of history manuals, look for it in the most direct and most visible ideological interventions, or even, like the work of the Frankfurt School on the basic elements of the which permeares che reaching of that discipline, are probably missing the essential point." In fact, the educational institurion as  whole, from irs stricly individualist organization of work to the taxonomies it applies in its cassifying operations, which always favour the original over the common. not to mention the contents it teaches and the way they are taught, rends to reinforce the propensity to individualism or egoism which che children of the petite bourgeoisie and grande bourgeoisie bring into the Sysrem. Literature, in which, as Gide said in his fournal, 'only the personal has any value', and the celebration which surrounds it in the lirer. ary held and in the educational system, are clearly central to this cułt of the self, in which philosophy, often reduced to a lofry assertion of che chinker's distinccion, also has a patt to play. And there is every reason to predict thar psychoanalysis, which, although it describes generic mechanisms, encourages immersion in the uniqueness of formative experiences (unlike sociology, which would nor be so resisted if it did nor reduce the personal' to the generic, the common), will have a place in che modernist variant of this cułt.

To explain the petir-bourgeois prerension to 'personal opinion', one has to consider not only the reinforcement by the educational system and the media but also the specific social conditions which produce the 'opinionated' habitus. it can be seen that the claim to the right to 'personal opinion' and disrrust of all forms of delegarion, especially in politics, have their logical place in the disposition system of individuals whose whole past and whole projecred future are oriented towards indi. vidual salvation, based on personal 'gifts' and 'merits', on the break-up of oppressive solidarities and even the refusal of onerous obligations, on the

Senior execurives are the category who mosr often say that, for political inforination, they rely on daily papers {27 percent of chem, and 24 pescenr of the junior executives and clerical workers, as againsr 14 percent of che farmers, 11 percent of manual workers, 8 percent of craftsmen and shopkeepers) or on weekly magazines (19 percent of rhem, as against 7 percent of junior executives and clerical workers, 6 percent of manual workers, 5 percent of craftsmen and shopkeepers and 4 percent of farmers). This may be seen as a manifestarion of che concern (which rises with educariona) level) to form an opinion' by resorting to the most specific and most legitimate instrument, the 'journal of opinion', which can be chosen in relation to one's own opinion, as opposed to television or radio--mass media' offering ho. mogenized products (SOFRES, Télévision et politique, May 1976). And one is rempted to see a similarly strucrured opposirion in the fact that, to pursue their demands, senior executives believe in personal representations ro a public service, whereas manual and office workers rely on strikes more often than other categories, while artisans, shopkeepers and junior executives prefer a demonstration, a sudden mobihization which does nor pre-exist or outlive the event.

But even a rapid reflection on the social conditions of the creation of demand for a 'personal opinion' and of the achievement of this ambition is su fhcient to show that, contrary to the naive belief in formal equality before politics, the working-class vicw is realistic in seeing no choice, for the most deprived, other than simple abdication, a resigned recognirion of status-linked incompetence, or total delegation, an unteserved remis. sion of self, a tacit confdence which chooses irs speech in choosing irs spokesmen.

## The Modes of Production of Opinion

In fact not all answers are opinions, and the probabiliry that a given group's tesponses are only disguised non-responses, polire concessions to the imposed problemaric or ethical discourses naively teceived as 'personal opinions', no doubr varies in the same way as the probability of non-response which characrerizes that group. The propensicy and abilicy to raise incerests and experiences ro rhe order of political discourse, to seek coherence in opinions and to inregrate one's whole set of accirudes around explicit policical principles, in fact depends very closely on educawith the relative weight of cultural capital as against ecor a political question is the product of an act of political judgement. fact, an answer to a question which, by che dominant dehnirion of poli. rics, would be classified as political (e.g., a question on abortio n or stu. dent demonstrations) can be produced by three very different modes of production. The principle of production may be, first, a class ethos, generative formula not constiruted as such which enables objectively co herent responses, compatible with the practical premises of a practical re. lation to the world, to be generated for all the problems of everyday existence. Secondly, it may be a systematic political slanr' (Parti), a sys. tem of explicit, specifically political principles, amenable to logical control and reflexive scrutiny, in short, a sott of political 'axiomarics' (n ordinaiy language, a line' or programme') which enables the inhnityof political acts and judgemenrs implied in the algorithm, and no others, to begenerated or forescen. Thirdly, it may be a two-srage choice, i.e., the identification, in the mode of knowledge, of the answers consistent wich the 'line' of a political parry, this time in the sense of an organization pro viding a political line' on a set of problems which it constitutes as political. The adherence implied in this tacit or explicit delegation may itself be based on practical recognition by the ethos or on an explicit choice enrailed by a 'slant'.30

There is every difference berween che intentional coherence of the practices and discourses generated from an explicit political' principle and the objecrive systemaricity of the practices produced from an implicit principle, below the level of 'political' discourse, that is, from objectively systematic schemes of thought and action, acquired by simple familiarizarion, without explicit inculcation, and applied in the pre-reflexive mode Without being mechanically arrached to class condition, these two forms of political disposition are closely linked to it, chiefly through the material conditions of existence, whose vital demands are of unequal urgency and therefore unequally easy to 'neutralize' symbolically, and through the educational training which can give the instruments of symbolic mastery of practice, i.e., of verbalization and conceptualization of political exper! ence. The populist inclination to credit the working classes with a politics' (as, elsewhere, an aesthetic'), spontaneously and naturally endowed with the properties included in the dominant definition of politics, 'g nores the fact that rhe practical masrery expressed in everyday choices (which may or may not be capable of being constitured as political in cerms of the dominant definition) is based nor on the explicir principles

1 was a kid-know what 1 m Municipal clerk, Communist

ifone must use the simplistic formulae of political discussion on a class unconscious rather rhan a class consciousness.

Tus. when confronted with a cluestion of domestic morality such as sex educarion, which tends to be made a political question through scholastic institutionalizarion, all social caregones except the senior executives and professionals produce responses governed by class ethos and largely indepen. dent of dectared polirical opinions. Farmers, for example, are the group which most often say that one should not talk about sex to children, or thar no sex education should be given before the age of fifteen; whereas clerical workers and junior executives are once again led by their cultural good intentions to recognize the dominant norm (which is recalled in the question itself) and most often say this education should be given before age eleven; for each category, the variations by polirical tendency are lictle marked. By contrast, everything suggests thar the responses of the senior exccutives and professionals express a combination of a class ethos (which inclines them to a certain pedagogic laxity) with explicit political principles. Eighry percent of those who say they are on the left think sex educa. tion should be given before age eleven, 50 percent of those who say they are centrist, and 33 percent of those who say they are on the right. 1t can be seen that, as has becn shown for the 'pure\* aesthetic disposition, which makes every aesthetic choice the manifestation of an acschetic 'siant'. the propensity to make everyday choices on the basis of polirical principles, i.e., wuhin che logic of che political slant' racher than ethical intuition, is irself a dimension of an ethos which is aìso expressed in the relation to language, the body, to others and to the world in general.

In fact, the second and third modes of opinion production differ ftom the first mode in thar, in them, the explicitly political principles of the production of political judgement are made explicit and formulated as Princples. This may be done either by the institution that is enrrusted with che production and management of these principles or by the isolared political agent, who possesses his own means of production of politkal questions and answers and can provide systematic political responses to problems as diftferent in appearance as a factoiy occupation, ex educaonor polluion In cicher case e relationship betwen soial cl and politically or not.

discourse (scientific or otherwise) on the social world, and in the definition of a line of political action on the world, it is clas erhos that is called upon to make up for the lacunae in axiomarics or method (or for insufficient mastery of these means of thought and action). The 'wotk. erism' of revolutionzry parties no doubr arises from an intuition into the duality of the principles of producrion of political opinions and actions and from a justified scepticism as to the possibiliry of answering all the practical questions and challenges of ordinary existence solely on the basis of the principles of a political axiomatics.

At all events, there is every difference in the world between the conscious, quasi-forced systematicity of a political 'line' and che sysremaricity 'in-itself' of the practices and judgements engendered by the unconscious principles of the erhos; or even between the minimal and yer fundamen. ral consciousness which is necessary to delegare the producrion of the principles of political-opinion production to a party and the sysreruaric consciousness which can see every situation as political and can bring to it a political solution generared from political principles. If political consciousness without the appropriate dispositions is unreal and uncertain, disposirions without consciousness are self-opaque and always exposed to seduction by false recognitions.32

The members of the inellectual occuparions (reachers, researchers, arises) declare themselves, more ofren than al ocher categories, supporteis of cev Iutionary action', opposed to 'aurhorirarianism' and in favour of 'inremarional class solidarity'. They more often think that the crisis of May 198 was benehcial to the general interest of the popuilarion', and say as often as manual workers that 'strike pickers are juscified', chat the Popular Front was a good thing', that they prefer 'socialism'to liberalism' and thar things would be better if the Stare owned al the major industries' But inantclass.

The dispositions which underlie the production of opitions are chiefly re. ifcant nuances which are almost inevitably lost in the ordinary recording of responses (which is generally simpified to the utmost for the sake of spid, srandardized surveying). Ihe resuit is that opinions which are identj. al lf aken at face value, bur which express very different dispostions and imply very diferent or even opposingactions, are lumped together in the seme class Ordinaty inruirion, which uses the imponderables of posture and manner. the nuances of argument and hexis, as indices of the different xays of being right-wing' or "left-wing', 'revolutionary' or 'conservarive'. the principle of every double entendre and political ambiguity, reminds us that thesame habirus can espouse apparently differentopinions, whereas different habirus can be expressed in superficialy (i.e., electorally) simidar opinions which nonetheless differ in their modaliry

Thus, when Lipset concludes that, in a student population, there is no correlarion bctween the parents' occupation and the children's political posi. tion, and attributes all the differences he fnds to factors such as typt of universiry and discipline, he nor only forgets thar differences in university position at a given moment are the scholastic retranslation of differences in social origin, even at the level of aspirarions, since the choice of a discipline expresses the ambitions available to individuals of a given social origin with a given level of academic success. Also, and espec.ally, he forgers that, having failed to use adequate questioning to obrain indicarors of the modality ofpolitical practice and judgements, he has been forced to lump together political stances which, even if identical in theit political content, may express opposing dispositions, and which, as the subsequent polirical realignments show, are less useful predicrors of practice, especially in the long tun, chan rhe appartntly insigni ficant details of bearing and diction which indicare the modality of 'commirments, not to menrion che eloquent silences which may cxpsess approval, scepricism, hostility, contempt, resignaron etc.

The opposition between frst-peison production and producrion by when, in the case of a strike, for example, they contrast thie 'democratic' logic of a vote or an opinion poll with the centralist' logic of expression y a trade nion, in order to try to break the organic relationship of delearion and to reduce rhe individual to his own resources by sending him other cals for the 'self-management' of polirical opinion, unconsciously their own means of opinion production who have no reason to delegate to others the power ro produce opinions for them. The usual image of the relationship berween the party apparatuses and their ctientelesin particular, the ideology of imperfect represenration, to the eftect chat the political elite docs not respond to the demands of its social base' or 'cre ates the political demand which keeps it in power'—ignores the quite dif ferent forms which this relationship may take, depending on the parry and on the categories of clientele within a single party.

These variations in the relationship between delegates and their man. dators depend, inter alia, on the modes of recruitment, training and promotion of party ofhcers (with, on one side, for example, the Communist Party, which has in a sense to create its politicians from scratch, by a total training almost entirely provided by the party,3 and, on the other, rhe conservative parties which can simply incorporate norables already furnished with a general training and occupying an established position elsewhere); on the social characteristics of the 'base' (in particular, its level of general educarion and the modes of political thought it tends to apply); and on the modes of construction of political discourse, or, which amounts ro the same thing, the modes of organization of the groups in which this discourse is construcred and circulates.

![](images/e66b5bd65f4d5e5321490c2ea59a55aeb2ea87bfa88acc5f43a7b0fc783cb6d3.jpg)

than those of Communist electors (see higure 19).

All political judgements, including the would-be most enlightened logic of political choice, which is a choice of sokesmen and representa tives and also a choice ofideas, projects, programmes, plans, embodied in 'personalities and depending for their reality and credibility on the real. ity and credibility of rhese personalities'. Uncertainty even as to che ob. ject of judgement, a person or a ser of ideas, is inscribed in the logic of politics, which, wharever the regime, entailsenrusting to individuals the task of formulatir:g and imposing political problems or solutions. These individuals may always be chosen either for their (objectified) programme, in the sense of a catalogue of already formulated judgements and prederermined, publicly announced measures (a manifesto), or for their personality', i.e., their habitus, an internalized program' (in the computing sense) or algorithm, the generative principle of a set of judgements and actions ('political measures') which are not explicitly formulared ar the moment of choice' by either the candidate or the elector and which have to be inruited through the subtle indices of dispositions re vealed only in bodily hexis, diction, bearing, manners. Every political 'choice' takes into account, simulraneously, the guarantor's personaliry and what he guarantces. The delegate is both a representative who ex. presses the already expressed opinions of his mandarors (he is 'bound' by a programme, a sort of explicit contract of delegation) and an agent who, following his internal programme—or the specifc interests associated with his position in the feld of ideological production-more than his declared programme, expresses as yet unformulated, implicit or potential opinions which he thereby brings into existence. He may even use the monopoly of speech implied in his spokesman status to credit his mandators, by an unverifable usurpation, with expectations, intentions and demands which they do not acknowledge (and which, depending on the case, are those of a vanguard or a rear-guard of the group as a whole). In shott, the fact tht the delegate is the guarantor of the programme, not only as an opus operatum but also as a modus operandi, no doubt explains why,to dapt a phrase of Durkheim's, nor everything in the conrac of political delegation is contractual.

The advocates of change are forced to spell out their LOP ce
