## Dispossession and Misappropriation

This essential ambiguity of political choice is only one of the reasons why no agent, not even a vittuoso of policical consciousness, can in practice avoid having recourse to different modes of production—and the more so, the less the situation to which he has to respond is polirically conscituted. Other things being equal, the more clearly the problem posed is recognized as political, the more frequent is the use of the tw specifcally political modes of production, proxy and autonomous production. They are also more frequent, in respect of explicitly political problems, when political consciousness is higher. In other words, they depend on the set of factors (sex, education, social class etc.) which govern the pro pensity to answer the most specifically political questions (as opposed to the propensity to abstain).

Our survey shows thar the proporrion who recognize all or virtualy atl the problems mentioned as political is very low among the educationally unqualifed, grows steadily with level of education and rises to two-thirds among those with the baccalauréat or a licence. (No doubtbecause ir was a qucstion of 'problems', che variations by education are greater here chan for the aptitude to sce various acsions as policical.) As might be expecred, the variations by educational capital are greater in relation to those problems which have appcared relatively recently in political debates, such as women's libecration, the protection of che environment or sex education, or throse which are rarely posed ourside the field of ideological production, such as che dectine of latin in secondary educarion. But there are also nificant variarions in the capacity to sœ political problems in unemployment, workers' participation, wage raises or the price of meat.

![](images/bf726cc76db7c362d2aa11e5cd046cdf70e05981661d36e67f7ce33e5444d79f.jpg)

This means that che relationship berween social class and political opinions varies by social class, i.e., according to the mode of opinion pro. duction most frequent in that class. The probability of producing a polit. ical response to a politically constituted question rises as one moves up the social hierarchy (and the hierarchy of incomes and qualifcations). Thus, apropos of a typically political problem, such as foreign policy, on which chere can only be policical opinions, there is a very strong correlarion with social class (and also, of couise, with sex and education) in the capacity to have an opinion, indicated by the rares of non-response (namely, farmers 37.7 percerit, manual workeis 38.6 percent, small employers 30.9 percent, office workers and junior executives 25.0 percenie, senior executives and professions 16.1 percent). There is a very close correlarion with declared political allegiance in the specifcally political conrent of the opinions expressed (proportion of opinions approving the governmenr's foreign policy: Communist Partry {PCF} 48.7 percett, So. Galsts 47.7 percent, Radicals 41.2 percent, Centrists 52.3 percent, Giscat ured along rhe axis of non-response, whereas party preferences are distrib uted along the axis of sarisfaction. It can be seen that, in general, the fact of ignoring 'don't knows', as usually happens in the analysis of surveys or elections, and of calculating frequencies solely in relation to the popul. rion of respondents, in accordance with electcoral logic, has che effect of cancelling out, or at least weakening, the relationship with social class. The greater che proportion of each category which eliminaces irself, the less cypical the survivors' are of the category, precisely in respecr of their degree of politicization.

Furthermore, che gap berween the capacity to produce a minimal re. sponse, a 'yes' or 'no', and the capacity to produce the corresponding question or, ar least, to grasp the specifically political signifcance the question has for those who produce it and ask it (i.e., in the feld of pro. duction of specifically political problematics) leads to the imposition of a problematic, as a resule of which the meaning of the answer given is mis. appropriared. T'he respondents are dispossessed of the meaning of their response whenever chey choose one of the answers ofered ro a question which they do not have the means of asking, and so allow an opinion which is cocally alien to them (even if it happens to express' them) to be reared as their own opinion; or whencver they respond, not to the ques. cion asked, but to the one chey chemselves have had to produce to try to appropriare the initial question. (T'his work of re-appropriation, which is almost always manifested by a retranslation into anorher language, so thar 'rationalizarion of budgerary options' becomes not wasting money', for example, is almost invariably ignoted by political scientists, either at the level of observation or at the time of coding.)

One example (see table 30) will sufice to show the imposition effect.-and the allodoxia which resulrs. This is a question on the political infuence of 'the business world' (les affaires), in which we incidenrally see che use of two very common rhetorical devices: the imposition of presuppositions herc, 'in cvcry councry', an anodyne phrase which insinuares the whole con. servarive philosophy of history ("always and cvcrywhere the same')—and the false symmetry which alìows a highly improbable, almost absurd answer to exist, under the guise of giving equal chances to all replies. To this should no doubt be added the effect of neurralizarion and euphemizarion, tending ro discourage any 'indecent' expression of indignation or revolt. The question itself was only devised in order to legitimate (by another false symmetry) the asking of another qucstion, this time definitely part of thc dominant problematic: And in your view, is che infuence of the trade unions too great, about right, or too small?" It can be seen that the propor tion who consider the inAuetice of 'the business world' too great rises as one moves up the social hierarchy (and, we may assume, alchough there are no dara on this, che hierarchy of educational levels), rhar is, in precisely in verse ratio to the proportion who fail ro reply. And so we see only 34 percent of manual workers judging' the infuence of big business excessive

while 52 percent rerreat inro abstention or neurrality, with a considerable proporrion acrually 'judging'that inAuence too small. The retranslarion which rhe least competent respondents have to perform in order to begin to appropriare a long and complex question on a 'political-scientific' issue, often resulting in misundersrandings, is seen in anorher case (table 31). where the leasr equipped have no choice but to absrain or to answer in rerms of rheir image of Mitterrand. Here too, a neurralization eff'ect is achieved through stylistic effects as subcle as the 'Mr.' before Mitterrand, or theuse of an academic verb such as préconiser (advocate); the expression 'socialism of the possible' itself belongs to the register of high policics.

It seems to be proved that, in the survey sicuation, the least comperent agents are almost entirely at rhe mercy of imposition effects which ail re sult, in che last analysis, from the fact that rhey have to choose between answers which only cake on chcir meaning in relation to a political problematic. However, this problematic is nothing orher than the ideological feld irself, i.e., the universe of objective relations, automatically converted into political stances (prises de position) defined in their content by the site from which they emanate, among the positions held and de. fended in this feld by the agents and institurions competing for che monopely of production and imposition of che legitimate representation of the social world and of legitimare action upon this world. The only way fully to appropriate chis problematic is to participate in its production, to occupy a position which counts, that is, which orhers have to take actons, forcing cheir occupants to rerhink their own "positions'. (One only has ro consider the scructural effects resulting from the emergence of ultra-leftism as an idée-force in the field.) Political 'problems' (like all those in philosophy, religion erc.) always exisr in and through rhe relationship berween rwo or moie antagonistic groups. This means that outsiders, being unable to rake an active parr in the political game which is de facro reserved for fuli-rime professionals (politicians, permanent offcials of the parry apparateses-the right word, for once--polirical journalises, prof essional ideologues), would only have some chance of picking our the opinions which 'suit' rhem, from wichm che universe of edy-made opinions, if the products on offer aiways boie a trademark,

## Reading the Labels

ordinary or exrra.Ordinaty, suberranean or overt, individual or colece spontaneous or organized) in which he can sicuare and orient himself pracrically and the field of ideological production which repro duces the structure of the political feld in accordance with its own logic

Misappropriarion almosr inevirably occurs whenever the question in. vires rwo different readings, when the uninitiared cannor answer it with. out bringing it down from the specifcally political plane, where it is siruated by the professionals who produce it and who will analyse or commcnt on the resulrs, to the plane of ordinary experience, to which the unconscious schemes of the ethos can be directly applied. Thus the politically 'incomperent' have every likelihood of placing themselves in the camp of the champions of the moral order and the social order, and cven of appearing more conservative in this area than the conscious de. fendters of the social order, whenever they are led to apply the categories of their class erhos to problems already constituted polirically at rhe level of the field of ideological production. It is known that, so long as one remains in the order of domestic moraliry, the propcnsity to liberalism or rolerance tends to increase as one moves up the social hierarchy (which seems to jusrify the well-known theses about working-class aurhoritarianism'), and it no doubt does so through the mediarion of educational level But this relarionship tends to be inverred as soon as the social onder, and not solely the moral order, is in question.

'So if you want to develop communism and achieve socialism, you've gor to seize power. Right now, we're too democratic, giving way on too many things. 'There isn't the violence there was a few years ago. Violence, I don't mean smashing everyrhing, but they're too soft when they go on strike, they don't take a decision. And I rell you, I think it's because the working class isn't miserable enough.'

Carpenter, age 67, Communiss

they expect an improvement in their condirions to come only through sud. den, dramatic changes. The evolutionist millenarianism' which is the natural culmination of this polirical theology makes the raising of living standards and the educarion of the working classes the motor of a universal movement towards American democracy, that is, the abolition of authoritarianism and of the classes which are its bearers.

It should scarcely be necessary to point out that if che members of the dominant class are more innovating' in domestic morality but more conservative' in rhe area more widely regarded as ‘pdlitical', i.e., in everything concerned with the economic and political order and with class relations (as shown by their answers on strikes, unions etc.), this is because their propensity to adopt 'innovating' or 'revolurionary' positions varies in inverse ratio with the degree to which the changes in question affect the hasis of their privilege. Thus, to take just one example, senior execurives may ap pear more liberal' in their view of immigrant workers than manual workers, who are more directly concerned by their competition: these executives much more often say that 'France is not doing enough to help foreign workers find accominodarion' (67 percent compared with 48 percent), 'to give them training' (52 percent and 36 percent) or 'make them welcome' (45 percent and 33 percent). Bur their liberalism is less marked when measures which might have consequences for themselves,are mentioned: 41 percent of the senior executives and professionals consider that France is not doing enough to give decent wages'to immigrant workers, as against 43 percent of the manual workers and S1 percent of the office workers and junior executives; 48 percent consider thar 'France should reduce or stop its aid to under-developed countries' as against 3ì percent of manual workers and 35 percent of ofhice workers and junior execurives (SOFRES, La France, l'Algérie et le T'iers-Monde, February 1971).

## Moral Order and Political Order

The duality of the principles of discourse production is constanrly re vealed, in the very discourse of activists or the most politicized workers, through the changes of tone and style from one area to another, and even through chepermanent rension, within rhe same discourse, berween che two modes of expression. On one side, there are the ready-made

## An Assumed Style

Gentlemen:

The present form of contact is an innovarion which is advantageous for all, every effort should be made develop che latter, and tcam spirit should be vigorously pursued. It is time for theory to give way to technology, audio-visual merhods should be optimized, industrial experience is desirable, and also politicizarion so long as it is geared to a sufhciently broad overview for sta. bility to be decisively prioritized.

Adulr education and updating are now crucially indispensable; basic training is no longer adequate at this point in rime; continuous assessment and know-how should maximize social advancement.

Serting aside all sectarian interests, the goal is progress, a major retooling of vocational guidance is called for, education is not synonymous with intelligence, a worthy caprain of industry may be found very young, leadership potential is easily spotted ..

Typesetser, extract from a letter to the AEERS in response to the questionnaire published in the press

lae. indivisible units of prefabricated thought and speech, which giv'e a discourse its colour of conceprual universality but also its air of having been learnt by heart, and the unreality so characteristic of dissertations (a railwayman talks about the 'underprivileged strara' as a schoolboy might write an essay on 'contemporary youth'), the most academic terms of political vocabulary ('mandarors', 'revaluation of wages'), which serve to show, ar the risk of malapropisms ('c'est contraire à l'intérêt de la masse salariale'),' that the speaker is able to stand up to a quasi-scholastic test and defend both the status attribute of a man and the honour of a class, in shorr, that he is not to be 'soft-soaped', that he has 'got what it takes todeal with those smart-talking politicians.'On the other side, separated by abrupt breaks in rone, are the most concrete references to immediate experience, unique and specifc, which give a discourse its reality, its plenirude, its authenticiry, while at the same time tending to prevent universalization, the pre-condition of mobilization.'

discussion of rhe educational system, most often reduced to key-words or slogans (\*pseudo-reform', 'fraud' 'reabsorprion of unemployment'-meral worker; integrating the dynamic elements of che nation', underprivileged strata?-railwayman), the responses are extremely particularized. It is as if the respondents seized this opporrunity to formulate a personal grievance nor immediately relevant to the quesrions asked. Often, too, the submission is a way of lerting people at the top know whar is going on', particularly about the tcache s' lack of conscientiousness. A farmer writes exactly the same sentence in reply to each question, "The reachers don't do their job properly, all they think about is their holidays':; another respondent rerurns in each of his comments, to the waste of working rime; and another (a shorrhand-typist married to a coach-builder) leaves half the questions unan. swered and replies to each of the orhers: 'They've gor no sense of profes. sional dury, all they ralk about is leisure.

No doubt partly because it is consrantly confronced with these interfer. ence effecrs and with the cendency to self-exclusion which Alows from dis possession and che sense of imporence, the educarional effort of the political or trade-union organizations to inculcare the will to have an opinion and ro provide the means of producing it musr irself constantly oscillate berween the formalism of general principles on the economic and social world and direct reference to immediate experience. For all this, it cannot ever perform rhe analysis which would truly relate the par. ticular case to its ulrimare foundarions in political economy, an operation which is no doubr as impossible for the teacher as for the pupil. While arrention ro the concrere siruarion is indispensable in order to win cre dence, it is no less essenrial ro go beyond isolaring the parricular case in order to achieve collective mobilization around common problems. This dialeccic of the general and the particular is at the heart of all politics, bur especially of the process of poliricizarion, with rhe need, for some, whose interests are bound up wich the established order, to universalize their parricular interests, and, for others, to grasp the universality of their particular conditions.

In political practice, of which opinion surveys indicare the diversiry by the variery of areas of existence they deal with. there is an imperceptible gradarion from particular, privare questions, including those which con cern domestic morality (child-rearing, sexuality, parental authoriry, the sexual division of labour etc.), which may already be political stakes for some vanguard groups, to questions which, chough they still concern upbringing or sexuality, are siruared at a more general and abstract level, since chey involve educational instirurions, bur also a level more remote from practical experience, such as reaching merhods, the recruitmenr.

dent demonstrations erc., and finally to questions constirured as polirical greatesr when, in ambiguous political siruarions such as all crisis situakions, which challenge established guidelines and schemes of thought (in the case in point, just after May 1968), the politically most deprived who are also, to a large extent, the economically and culrurally most deprivedapprehend polirical problems which for them are stillill-defned, those, for example, posed by student demonstrations, through cheir everyday schemes of perception and appreciation (in this case, their ethical aversion to 'long-haired students' and the spoilt children of the idle rich). They thus seem to prove their ‘authoritarianism' and reinforce the narural defenders of the established order.

Questions on school disorders and srudent demonstrations or on polirics in educarion are predisposed to funcrion as traps, because they only take on cheir futl meaning in cheir rea) conext, clie dominant problematic, i.e., che set of questions generaced from the fundameitral quesrion of che mamrenance of the esrablished order. As is shown by the inventoiy of che questions posed by opinion research institutes (sce cable 32), this problematic marshals the questions which present themselves ro those, exclusively, who have to take account of the distriburion of opinions abour their policy in derermining their policy. It only recognizes the questions which the dominant class ask themselves about the groups which are problems for them, and ignores the quesrions thest groups ask (and the problems they have).

The most deprived do not, strictly speaking, know what question they are answering, and do nor possess che interests and dispositions that would enable chem to reactivare the quesrion by secing ir as a particular form of che gucstion of che conservation or subversion of che established order. They cherefore respond nor to the question thar is acrually asked, but to a quescion rhey produce from their own resources. i.e, from the practical principles of their class echos. When the questions posed are in the intermediare area between morality and politics, the contamination of politics by morality and the slippage from mora.l indignation to political reaction is most clearly secnespecially in chose whose position in social space predisposes them to a moral perception of the social world, such as the petite bourgeoisie and especially its declining fractions or individuals. Resentment is clearly the basis of the reactionary or conservarive-revolurionary stances of the declining perit bourgeois who are anxious to maintain order on all fronts, in domestic moraliry and in sociccy, and who invest their revolt against the worsening of their social Posizion in moral indignation against che worsening of morals. But it ofren also pervades the Jjacobin sigour and meritocratic revolt of rhe ascendant petit bourgeois, who are convinced of their right to settle scores with a society which has not sufhciently rewarded their merits. This simple reversal of pretension, the disposition of groups condemned to occupy subjectively a position which is not objectively theirs, is characteristic of all those who condemn the established order only because it recognizes them less than they recognize it, in their very revolt, and cannot recognize in them the values it recognizes ofhcially. This is why enlightened conservatism, which is always ready to accept or even initiate changes in every area which does touch the foundations of the social order,4 finds, on both right and left, the foils it needs in order to present itself as a vanguard.

## Class Habitus and Political Opinions

Here as elsewhere, it would be a mistake to seek the explanatory principles of the responses in one factor or in a set of factors combined by addition. The habitus integrates into the biographically synthesizing unity f a generative principle the set of effects of the determinations imposed by the material conditions of existence (whose effcacy is more or less subordinated to the effects of the training previously undergone as one advances in time). It is embodied class (including biological properties that are socially shaped, such as sex or age) and, in all cases of inter- or intra.generational mobility, it is distinguished (in its effects) from class as objectified at a given moment (in rhe form of property, titles etc.), in. asmuch as it perperuates a different state of the material conditions of existence-those which produced it and which in this case differ to some extent from the conditions of its operation. The determinations which agents undergo shroughot their existence constiture a system in which a predominant weight belongs to factors such as the capital possessed (it overall volume and also its composition) and rhe correlarive position i the relarions of production (identifed chrough occupation, with all associated dispositions, such as the inAuence of working conditions and occupational milicu).

This means rhat the intrinsic efficacy of a factor taken in isolation can never really be measured by the correlarion betwcen this factot and the opinion or practice in question. The same factcor may be associated with dif. ferent effecrs, depending on the system of factors in which it is inserted. Thus the baccalauréar qualification may be a principle of revolt when possessed by the son of a junior executive or skiled worker who has fallen back into the ranks of rhe semi-skilled (rhe regular increase in the number of bacheliers among unskilled worke rs is probably not unconnected with the growth of ultra-leftism m che working class), or a source of integralion when possessed by a junior executive whose farher was a factory worker or peasant smallholder. Ir could be shown in the sarme way rhat the value of an educational qualification and the associaced relarion to the social world vary considerably according to rhe bearer's age (inasmuch as the chances of possessing it a revery unequał for the dif'erent generarions), social origin (inasmuch as mherited social capital-name, family conneccions etc.-gov. erns its real profitability) and also, no doubr, geographical origin (through embodied properties such as accent and also through the characterisrics of the labour market) and sex.

So it is understandable thar, through the mediation of the habirus, which defnes the relation to the position synchronically occupied and consequently the practical or explicit positions' taken vis-à-vis rhe sociał worlld, the discribution of political opinions between right and lefe should correspond fairly closely to the distribution of the classes and class fractions in the space whose first dimension is defined by overall volume of capital and the second by the composition of chis capiral: the propensity to vore on the right increases wich the overall volume of the capical possessed and also with the relative weight of economic capital in the capiral composition, and the propensiry to vore on the left increases in the opposite direction in borh cases. The homology berween the opposirions esrablished in these two respects-the fundamental opposition between rhe dominant and rhe dominated, and the secondary opposition between the dominant fractions and the dominated fractions of the dominant class-tends to favour encounters and alliances berween the occupants of homologous posirions in the dif erent spaces. The most visible of these paradoxical coincidences occurs berween the dominated fractions of the dominant class, intellecruals, artists or teacheis, and the dominared classes, who each express their (objectively veay different) relarion to the same dominant fractions in a particular propensicy to vote on che lef.

f. on the basis of the distributions of voting intentions by socic-occupational category established by G. Michelat and M. Simon, each class fraction is characterized by she algebraic deviation between the percentages of lefrwing votes and the percenrages of right-wing votes (leaving aside the don't knows', which vary relativel lite), it can be secn that everything takes place as if the effecrs of volume and the cffecrs of composition compounded each other. The political space thus appears as a systemaric skewing of che social space, with primary teachers {(43) found next to the miners (-44), the secondary and tertiary reachers (-21) next to the skilled workers (-19), the artists (-15) close to the unskilled workers (-15), and che clerical workers (-9) alongside the semi-skilled workers (-10); the orher extremity of the political space is occupied by the industrial employers (+61), followed by the professions (+47), the senior adminisrrative executives (+34) and, very close ro them, the commercial employers (+32); the technicians (+2) and the foremen (+1) are situated on the frontier b rween righr and left. Everything suggests thar a secondary opposition is esrablished between fractions of which a large proportion of the members move towards the most classifyring choices of their especrive regions of che political space: the industrialists and professionals, on one side, who give a high proporcion of votes to the liberal right', and the unskilled, semiskilled and skilled workers, on the ocler side, who give a high proportion of vores to the Communist Party, thus secm to be opposed to the fractions which combine a high proportion of abstentions and relatively less-classify. ing choices (non-Communist left or Gaullist), i.e., the artists, teachers and foremen, who perhaps express in this way the contr:adictions and ambiguities of their social positions.

The classihcations used by Michelar and Simon both for the sociooccupational caregories and for political opinions (reduced ro very broad categories, with no indicacion of modaliry) do not make ir possible ro establish a clear opposition berween rhe established petite bourgeoisie, more attaced to the tradirional politica) organizations (alrhough here too, effecrs of downward mobiliry have induced new forms of demands and struggle), and rhe new petite bourgeoisie, which recognizes its image in all che new political forms-from ulira-lefrism, chrough the PSU and che ncw rendencies in che Socialisr Parry, to ccology—and in al the participationist and self-management catch-words likely to satisfy dhe aspirations to autonomy and personal sovercignty of petit bourgeois vith intellectual pretensions. However, it may be noted thar among all rhe caregories situaced in the centre of the political space, the medical and social services (+28) are situated towards che pole marked by the indusrrialists, whereas the rechnicians incline more towards the miners' pole (+2); the junior adminiscrative exec. utives (+14) and commercial employees (+16) occupy an inrermediate posirion, with the craftsmen (+13), che engincers (+19) and the farmers (+20).

Politicał practices or opinions, like orher praccices, cannor, of course, be filly accounted for if one leaves our everyching that is apprehended through the ordinary indicators of social origin. Here it is necessary to distinguish, ar least, the ffect of the trajctory leading from the orginal position to the present position, the ffect of the conditionings inscribed in a particular socio economic condition and especially important wh one is trying to understand political positions' as explicit positions' taken on the social world-the effect of inculcation, since political edu. cation, like religious education (which is a disguised form of it) isalways partly received from the family, from the earliest days of life.

Before expressing surprise at the intensity of the correlarion betwcen religious pracrice and polirical opinion, one has to consider wherher this is nor largely due to the fact that these are simply two different manifestations of che same disposition. This is not only because, both in irs content and in the disciplines of inculcation, religious training is a disguised form of pohiticalsocialization; bur also because the imposition of a pracrice and of a declared belief implies axignment to a class, and cherefore the attribution of a social identity which, whatever the content of the correspondingincukation, is defined relationally by tts opposition to the complemenrary classof 'non-believers' and is thus charged with all the properties excluded from the latter at a given moment (such as the conservarive potitical disposition in. plied in opposition to the reds'). Fidelicy to chis identity and to those who share it (l am a Chriiscian') confers a great autonomy on the professed faith with respect to the pres nt condirions of existence. As for the specific cfect of che conrent of che religious message, it may be assumed chat it reinforces che propensity to conceive the social world in rerms of the personalist' logic of 'personal salvation', to see poverty or oppression as personal misfortunes, like illness or death. (By contrast, the political mode of thoughr cends ro exclude from polirics everyrhing that personalist thinking and teligion constitute as ethics, in particular everything concerned with the domestic economy; it is therefore ill-prepared to politicize domesric mattets, such as consumption or the condirion of women. The difficulry is ag gr vated by he fact that the vanguard in politicizing the dorestic is often made up of individuals or movements of Christian origin, and it is not casy to derermine whether they are politicizing the domesric or domesticating and depoliticizing the political.)

## Supply and Demand

To endeavour to specify the relationship between the social classes and the political opinions socially constitured at a given moment, one may be tempred to examine, on the basis of rhe statistics available, how the different claxes and class fractions distribute theit choices among the differ ent politically marked newspapers and magazines. However, the inherert fallacies of such a procedure cannot be avoided unless one starts by considering what 'reading a newspaper' means for the different caregories of readers, sitce it may have nothing in common with the functions com monly attribuced to it or with those assigned to it by the producers or heir backers. Only an echnocentric faith in the myth of personal opinn', an opinion 'formed' by a permanent effort to keep informed, can mask the facr that a newspaper (for those who actually read one) is a ‘viewspaper' (journal d'opinion) for only a minority.

Thus, the readeis of narional newspapets such as France-Soir and Le Parisien Libéré (circulations 510,000 and 360,000 respectively in 1977) are distrib. uted fairly equally between righr and lefr: 41 percent to 36 percent for the former, 33 percent to 33 percent for the larter (and, cven more surprisingly, given itrs more distinctly right-wing content, 27 percent of the readers of L'Axrore declare themselves to be left-wing vorers), 60 percent and 64 per. cent of the respective readerships of the first two papets say they would nor stop reading them if the papers took political positions contrary to their own, and a high proportion decline to pronounce on rheir objectiviry or their stances in the election campaign. Bur above all, only a small proporrion of their readers (24 percent for Le Parisien Libéré and 29 percent for Firance-Soir—as against 37 percent for Le Figaro and 42 percent for L'Aurore) say they are in complere agreement with the opinions expressed by these papers, whereas a large proporrion (40 percent and s0 percent respectively) consider them more righe-wing than chemselves Ir can be seen chat the specifically political effect of a newspaper is not measured by the polirical orientation of its specihcally political discourse as evaluated in the feld of ideological producrion, stil less by the number of column-inches directly devored to polirics, but by the relationship the readers have to the paper, hose political message rhey may ignore and whose most important polirical acrion may be to give no imporcance to politics.

It would seem that the lower the readers are sicuared in the social hierarchy, the more independent their political opinion is of the opinion explicitly professed by their newspaper. Buying a paper like Le Parisien Libéré or France-Soir (or even L'Aurore) is, for manual workers or clerical workers who vore Socialist or Communist (and who no doubt make up the major. ity of the Communist or Socialist vorers among those papers' readerships, ie, 10 percent and 20 percent of the readership of the former and 9 percent and 29 percene of rhe readership of the latter), an act which, although it is objectively policical, presupposing and no doubt producing 'depoliticizauon', implies no affliation, no political delegation. Le Parisien Libéré, which devotes 16.3 percent of irs space to sport (as against 2.6 percent in Le Monde), and to the most popular sports, is regarded by many of its readers as a sports paper which, unlike L'Eguipe, provides general and anccdotal news as well for the same price.

The telative independence of che re ders' political opinions from their newspapers' political rendency thus scems from the fact thar, unlike a political party, a newspaper offers informarion char is not exclusively political (in the narrow sense normally given to this word). It is a multipuipose product, of ering, in very vatiable proportions, international and national politics, miscellaneous news and sport, so char it can be che obnouncements (just as they are excluded, for che same reasons, from casual conversation berween strangers, in favour of safe topics, such as the weather), except for those which can be perceived as least political, i.e. offcal declarations (chis is what gives 'omnibus' newspapers their ait of being semi-offcial or 'governmental" organs)."9 This imperative, which becomes stronger as the clientele expands, necessarily bringing in consumers with increasingly different tastes and opinions, is sufhcient to explain che invariant features of all omnibus culrural goods-family-audience' feature films, best-sellers, the carefully depoliticized political mes. sages of catch-all' political parties, the homogenized attractions of Hollywood srars or the professionals of bureaucratic charm, rhe perfectly polite and polished profile of the model managers who, in che cause of upward mobility, have smoothed away all cheir social 'rough edges'. The ideal ‘wide-audience' product is rhus perfectly non-classifying, insipid', but, by virrue of chis, acceptable ro all castes (this law also applies to macerial products: one only has to think of the new mild' cheeses, such as Bonbel, or baby foods).

There is thus every dif erence berween the major omnibus daily and weekly papers, which maximize their clientele by neutralizing their products, and the vanguard grouplets or avant-garde reviews, which testify to their fidelicy to their initial progtamme eicher by rapid disappearance or by living a precarious existence (dependent on subscriptions, extraordinary eftorts by cheir offcials, the devotion of their a.ctivists and so on). Their alrernarive course is ro overcome or manage che conficrs arising both in the production unit and in cheir audience from rhe pursuit of a wider clientele, the pre-condirion for access to power, by making conces sions, compromises or arrenuations which conrradict che initial pro gramme and weaken cheir links with the oldest and most 'signifcant' fraction of their audience. Thus, for some major parties (such as the present-day Socialist Pacty) and some major publicacions, a rational management of comperition wichin rhe production unit, which chen func cions as a field, can be the means of supplying the different fractions of readers or electors (for example, in the case of Le Monde, the different fractions of the dominant class) with diversified products adjusted to their different or even opposing expectations, without any conscious iultention of doing so.

But in addition, manual and clerical workers—except the most politicized of chem, who read L'Humanité or another farleft paper--practical never regard the daily newspaper either as that sort of political guide or moral and cultural mentor which it perhaps ceally is only for a section of the readers of Le Figaro, or as the instrument of inf ormation, documentamon and analysis which it no doub is only for the pupils of Sciences Po or ENA, for senior civiß servants and some teachers, in other words, the rarget audience of Le Monde. As well as the weekend sports results and commentaries, che newspaper is expected to provide 'news, i.e., informarion on the whole range of events with which one feels concerned be cause chey concern acquaintances (the deaths, marriages, accidents or scholastic successes reported in local papers) or people like oneself whose misforrunes are experienced vicariously (e-g., the catastrophe in a popular campsire in Spain in 1978). The inrerest in such 'news', which che socalled 'serious' papers disdainfully relegare ro che back page, is perhaps no different in narure from the interest which chose members of che dominant class closest to the centres of political decision-making have in socalled 'general news', the appointment of civil servants to ministerial cabinets or of technocrars to 'think-tanks', elections to the Académie française or receptions at the Elysée Palace, the power struggles within the political parties or the wars of succession wichin a major newspaper or firm, not to mention che diary of social events or the list of admissions to the grandes écoles. Only in bourgeois conditions of existence, around bourgeois dinner tables, do proper names of general interest—the name of rhe Minister of Finance or his chief adviser, the director of Schlumberger or the chairman of a prize jury and so forth-refer to familiar individuals, known and frequented, belonging, like neighbours and cousins in the space of a village, to the world of mutual acqquaintance (which gocs a long way to explain why reading Le Monde is a sine qua non for entry to che beau monde). One forgets char the dominant class is defned precisely by the facz that it has a particular interest in af airs 'of general incerest' because che parricular interests of its members are particularly bound up with chose af airs.

But this is only one of che many reasons for questioning the semiscientific opposition berween 'news' and ‘views', or between the so-called sensacional newspaper and the 'journal of reflecrion'. Behind che reading of these two cacegories of papers lie in fact two quire dift erent relations to politics. Reading a national newspaper, and especially one of the major, legitimate papers, such as Le Figaro or Le Monde, is one way among orhers (like wricing in che papers, or to the papers, signing pericions or answering questionnaires published by them) of showing one's sense of belonging to the 'legal nation', of being a full citizen, entitled and dury bound to participate in policics and exercise a cirizen's rights.

virrue ofan effecr of assignment by status. Educarional qualifications strongly contribute to rhe sense of full membership in the universe of legitimate polirics and culture, which includes che sense of the right and ducy ro read a legirimare newspaper (sce rables 33, 34, and 35).

While the probability of reading a national newspaper rises strongly wirh educational capiral (with rhe probabiliry of reading a regional newspaper varying in the opposite way), the variations are particularly marked in rhe case of Le Monde and Le Figaro, the readers of which ate to a very large ex rent dra.wn from among graduares of higher-cducarion instirurions. Through the link with educarional capiral, rhe probability of reading a narional newspaper is linked to social class (though less closely): it is very low in rhe working classes, in which reading is almost exclusively limired to the sports papers and omnibus papers (Frane-Soir and Le Panssen Libéré), and incrcases steadily as one moves up the social hierarchy. There is every reason to think that, orher things being equal, women are socially more inclined than men to be interested in rhe 'non.political' conrent of newspapers (local 'news', anecdoral items, the diary of social events etc.). in politics, unlike cultural practices, the effecr of assignnent by srarus exerred by scholzsric capital cannor make itself felt in full until rhe efecrs of the tradirional division of labour berween the sexes are weakened or nullifed, as is the case in the dominared fractions of the dominant class: thus we find rhat, except in the dominant class, the rare of readership of a narional newspaper is higher among men than women (the relarionship is reversed for readership of local rewspapers). Wherczs the probabiliry of reading ar least one daily paper rises steadily wirh age, the probabiliry of reading a national paper is virrually independent of age—although slighrly higher for the 25-49 age group than for older groups, as if ageing were accompanied by a weakening of the sense of needing to keep up to date with politics. (This rendency does nor appear in the case of L'Axrore and Le Figaro, a high pro portion of whose scaders are industrial and commercial empłoyers who ofren concinue their acrivity to an advanced age.)

But the difference between rhe 'sensarional' press and the 'informative" press ultimately reproduces the opposition berween those who make politics and policy, in deeds, in words or in chought, and those who undergo it, between active opinion and opinion that is acred upon. And it is no accident that the antithesis berween understanding and sen sibility, reflection and sensation, which is at the heart of the dominant tepresentation of the relarionship berween the dominanr and the dominated, evokes the opposirion between two relations to the social world, between the sovereign viewpoint of those who dominare the social world in pracrice or in thought ('General ideas', said Virginia Woolf, 'are always generals' ideas') and the blind, narrow, partial vision (that of the ordinary soldier lost in the battle) of those who are dominated by this world. Political analysis presupposes distance, height, the overview of the obseiver who places himself above the hurly-burly. or the 'objecrivity' of the histonan, who takes and gives the time for refection by performing a sort of political ‘alienation', which, like acschetic 'alienation', can neutralize the immediate presence, the urgency and functions of the object, substitute the euphemistic translations of the indirect style for the direct utterance of words or ra.ll ying-cries,3º and subsume under the unif ying concepts of po litical analysis the immediacy, actuality, factuality of ephemeral events, of everything which satishes the curiosity of the ordinary reader, immersed ir events and short-term, 'facile' sensations. Like 'difhcult' art as opposed to 'facile' art, or eroricism as opposed to pornography, the so.called quality newspapers call for a relarion to the object implying the afrmation of a distance from the object which is the aformation of a power over the object and also of the dignity of che subject. They give the teader much more than the'personal'opinions he needs; they acknowledge his dignity as a political subject capable of being, if not a subject of history, then at least the subject of a discourse on history.

Having chus specified the meaning of the relationship of the diferent social classes to their newspapers, through which we no doube can grasp one dimension of their objective and subjective relation to 'policics' (which is also seen in the rates of participation in the leaderships of che different parties, and in electoral curn-out rates), we can try to derive some indications as to political positions from the variations in readership of the politically most clearly marked national newspapers (see table 36). First, it is possible to draw fairly precisely the simultaneously pol cal and cultural dividing-line berween the working classes—who, apart from local papers, read almost exclusively omnibus papers—and the middle classes. Technicians, whose level of newspaper readership is very close ro that of foremen, are opposed to clerical workers, who read more papers but also more right-wing papers (i.e., more ofren La Croix, Le Figaro and Le Monde, less often L'Humanité or L'Equipe). One no doubt sces here che combined effects of very different work milieux, the workshop and the ofce, and also of types of schooling which tend to reinforce pre. exiscing differences—cechnical craining, which encourages practices and inrerests close to those of ocher manual workers, and secondary schooling, which, by ar least slighcly initiat ing its pupils into legicimate culture and its values, introduces a break wich the popular world view.

To verify this, one only has to obxrve that foremen, airhough they have distinctly higler incornes and a geographical distribution very similar to char of th.e clerical workers (aho are only a little more concentrated in Paris), have much lower readership rates for the national dailies and the weeklies (18.5 percent and 28.4 percent respectively, as against che clerical workers' 41.4 percent and 43.2 percent); foremen more often read rhe om. nibus papers and, among the weeklies, L'Humanité Dimanche, whereas the clerical workers slightly more often read Le Figaro, Le Monde and La Croix, and. among the weeklies, La Vie and especially Le Nouvel Observateur. There is every rcason to think that those whom chis dividing-line separares are op posed in other respecrs, particularly as regards their attitudes to religion, the trade unions and the political patties, with, on the clerical workers' side, a higher proportion of practising Catholics, Force Ouvrière unionists and Socialists (or pSU, or extreme-left) voters, but also Gaullists, and on the manual workers' side, a higher proportion of agnostics or non-practising Catholics, CGT unionists and Communist voters" These opposirions are thcmselves an integral part of life-styles linked to different social trajectories and differçnt working conditions (for example: differences according to how long the lincage has been urbanized and proletarianixed, according to the size of thc firm etc.).

The boundary between the industrial working class and rhe peasantry is also marked by the probability—much higher among industrial workers—of reading a national rewspaper, especially L'Humarité, and by the fact that agricultural workers almost always ccad only Catholic weeklies (Le Pèlerin, La Vie) whereas industrial workers rcad a greazer variery of papers. As one moves up che occupational hierarchy (which is to somne cxtent linked with seniority in the working class) the Catholic pres is increasingly supplaated by rhe Communist press, which is not surps'ising when ore knows that the rendency to vote on the left varies in the same way.

Excepr for L'Hamanisé, the newspapers and weekly magazines only really fulhl their role as political markers starting at the level of the middle classes. The space mapped out by the quantiry and quality of reading reproduces fairly exactly the ordinary oppositions by volume and compo-
