sition of capiral, both at the level of the middle classes and at the level of the dominant class. On one side, we ind the economically (relarively) rich fractions, craftsmen and small shopkeepers, or industrial and com. mercial employers, who read lirrle, and mainly omnibus papers; on the other, the culturally (relatively) wich fractions, clerical workers, junior executives and primary teachers ar the first level and, at the second level, pro fessionals, engineers, senior executives, secondary and higher education reachers, who read a lot of national dailies, especilly the most legitimare' ones, and weekly magazines. In the middle classes as in the domi nant lass, the proportion of readers of national papers and left-aing papers declines, and the proportion of rcadcrs of regional papers and nght-wing papers increases, as one moves from the primary or secondary reachers to the smal or big commercial employers

nly for the dominant class docs the newspaper fully play irs role as an attitude-gentrating principle defined by a certain distinctive position in a field of such insritutionalized principles: and che adequacy with which it 'expresses ts readers no doubr depends on the degree of homology berween its position in che field of the press and its teaders' position in the field of the classes (or class fraccions), the basis of their opinion generating principle. 'T'hus at one pole we find the industrial and commercial employers, ers (and, still further away, the intellectuals), reading a lot, mainly Le Monde or L'Humanité or Le Nouvel Observateur. The space of the weckly the daily course of political life, because chey devore more space to cultural matters and also because, in order to achieve the high circulation needed o attract advertisers, they must avoid all principles of division and exchusion and seek the most neutral areas, objects and styless less clearly marked out, although Le Noertel Observaseur is still fairly clearly opposed ro L'Expres and Le Point. We should perhaps not read too much into the dif. ferences berween che fractions defining incermediate positions (especially the most hererogeneous of them, such as the executives or engineers), since surveys in successive years produce divergences attributable to the smallness of the corresponding popularions in the representative sample. However, we know from the survey on press readership in the dominantclass alone (C.S. V}, which separares them our, thar privare-secror execurives much more often read L'Aurore and Le Figaro (and the economic and financial press: Les Ecbos, Entreprise), much less often Le Monde and Le Nouvel Obser. vaseur, than public-sector executives, and also that the members of the liter. ary and scientifc occupations read Le Nouvet Observateur more often and Le Figaro less often than the teachers. So it would seem that the mode of the distributions of the daily and weekly papers ranked by polirical conrentomnibus papers (with L'Aurore), Le Figaro, Le Monde, Le Nouvel Observateur, L'Humanité—tends to shift steadily as one moves through the fractions in the following orier: commercial and industrial employers, privacesector executives, professions, public-sector executives, teachers, intellectuals (rhe central categories, especially the profcssions and engineers, being characterized by a particularly strong dispersion of their choice of reading-matter).

The opposition berween the fractions in terms of rhe composirion of their capital is blurred by the effects of the opposition, within cach frac. tion, berween 'old' and 'young' or, more precisely, predecessors and suc. cessors, the 'old gang' and the 'new wave'. The dominared fractions, who, because of their position in the space of the dominant class, incline towards partial, symbolic subversion, contain their own (temporally) dominant groups who may be pushed towards conservation (partly in response to che subversive dispositions of rheir own challengers). Simi lariy, within the dominant fractions, who have an interest in ail forms o conservation, the successors (and, to a lesser extent, women}, who are remporarily kept away from power, may, up to a point and for a cercain time, share the world view put forward by the dominated fractions. Thus the opposition berween Le Figaro or L'Express and Le Nouvel Observateur expresses not only che opposition between the dominant and the dominated fractions, berween che private and the public, and, more precisely. berween, on the one hand, the employers closest to the private pole of the economic feld, least rich in educational capital and no doubr most hreatened with decline, the oldest private-sector executives, those most linked to the employers, and, on the orher hand, the public-sector execurives and the teachers; but also the opposition berween the predecessors and the successors, the old and the young."

Playing unwittingly on the confusion between class struggles and class-fraction struggles, or, mote simply, between right and righr-bank or lcft and left-bank, and also on the classihcatoly' and conceprual fuzzincss sesulting from the partial overlaps between the different incernal divi. sjons of the dominant class, che Nouvel Observateur enables all domilnantclas members who are dominard in one or anorher of the possible re spects-incellectuals, the young, women—to experience the sum of their necessarily partial challenges as the most radical assault on the established onder. Consigning the class struggle to history, it ofers its readers as a bonus the means and the grarifications of a simulraneously echical, aesthetic and polirical snobbery which can combine, in a sort of antibourgeois pessimism, the appearances of intelleccual vanguardisrn, which leads to elirism, and polirical vanguardism, which leads to populism. If it aPpears thar most of the judgements it delivers on the social world have no other basis than the quarrel berween the old gang' and the new wave', and if its contesrarion of the esrablished order boils down to a contesting of forms—those of established etiquette, politics or art—this is because the straregies of symbolic subversion which offer themselves in the competitive struggles for succession reach their limits in recognition of the game and of the stakes which they presuppose and produce. More precisely, it is because the internal order of the dominant class depends very directly on every'thing which regulates the structure of social rime, i.e, the order of successions, whether it be the representations which assign to each age its passions and powers, its liberties and duries, or the respect for forms and forms of respect which, berrer than any rules, guarancee social distances by maintaining temporal discances, the gaps, pauses, delays and expectarions which propriety imposes on the impatience of the inheritors.

## The Political Space

At chis point, it is possible ro represent and sysrematize the set of rela. tions established, in a provisional diagram of che political space (see fgure 21). This seeks to show how the diff erent class fractions are distribured in relation to one another (relative positions clearly being easier to verify than distances) and also in relation to the set of political products' or 'marques' which function as landmarks or insignia. This space can immediarely be seen to be a systematic distottion of the space of che classes and class fractions distribured by the volume and composition of their capical. The set of fraccions situated on the left in the polirical space (and in the figure) are pulled downwards and those on the right are pulled 'markers'.

![](images/f3dffc122403061f75b6e09f68efdecfa142000cb0fc932af603968b7ed3e1a6.jpg)

upwards, Which is understandable since the effects of the opposition by overall capital volume and the opposition by capital composition (and the associated trajectories) are combined. The positions given to the class fractions or 'marques' only indicate, of course, the central point around which the group or readership in question is more or less widely distribted. The 'social surface'corresponding to each of these points in fact varies considerably with the volume of the population, which is generally linked to its social scatter (although the increased volume of a readership may result as much from more intense penetration in a restricted space as front extension of the space covered).

## The Specific Effect of Trajectory

Political choices are much {css independent of social class than is gen. erally supposed, even when the larter is synchronically dehned by posxession of a capitał of a given volume and composition. To demonstrate this, one only has to construct the categories adcquately—and one could go much further in che direction opened up by Michelat and Simon by taking account of the diachronic properties of each social position and, perhaps above all, by securing the means of describing and understanding what the different political trade-marks'and the corresponding products mean for each class or class fraction. lr is regretrable that the surveys available (in France) do not make it possible to identify and isolate the effects of trajectory and inculcation (through the parents' occupation and political opinions); even more so, perhaps, that they are equipped with no means of grasping directly-in the way in which they are urtered or justifed—the differences which make nominally identical opinions really incommensurable. The fact that electoral logic ignores the differences berwecn che Communist voce of an artist or a universiey teacher and that of a primary teacher, or a fortiori a clerk, a factory worker or a miner is not an excuse for sociology co do the same thing. If it is co have any chance of producing a scientifc explanation, it must uncover the really different ways of being or declaring oneself Communist and the dif. ferent meanings of a vote for the Communisc Party that are hidden under the nominal identity of votes, while at the same time allowing for the Politically important fact that electoral logic treats opinions which differ in both cheir intentions and their reasons as identical.

The fact remains that one cannot truly understand the sometimes immense differences between categories which are nonetheless close in social space, such as craftsmen and small farmers, or foremen and cechnicians, unless one rakes into account not only capiral volume and compo. sition bur also the historical evolution of these properties, i.e., the trajectory of the group as a whole and of the individual in question and his lineage, which is the basis of the subjective image of the position ob iectively occupied. One of the most determining characteristics of politi-

![](images/a9435848e88afd04763396dd497cee9cafb6d9f96f5e3a96f734aec72df8b3e3.jpg)

cal choices lies in the fact that, more than all other choices, much more than the obscure, deep-roored choices of the habitus, they involve the more or less explicir and sysrematic representation an agent has of the social world, of his position within it and of the position he 'ought'to occupy. Political discourse, when ir exists as such, is ofren no more than the more or less euphemized and universalized expression (never perceived as such by those who utter it) of thar represencacion. In orhet words, between the position seally occupied and the political 'positions' adopted, there intervenes a representation of the position which, although decermined by the position (so long as the posirion is defined completely, thar is, aljo diachronically), may be at odds with the political 'positions'the position seems to entail for an external observer (this ts what is sometimes called 'false consciousness'). The slope of the individ ual and, above all, the collective trajecrory governs, through temporal dispositions, the perception of the posicion occupied in the social world and the enchanted or disenchanted relarion to that position which is one of the principal mediattons through which the relationship between s0 cial position and political position' is established. The degree to which individuals and groups are turned towards the futire, novelty, movement, innovation, progress dispositions which are particularly manifested in liberalism towards 'rhe young', the bringers and beneficiaries of the new order--and, more generally, inclined towards social oprimism, or, on the other hand, orienred towards the past and inclined to social resenrment and conservatism depends on rheir past and potential collective trajectory, i.e., on the extent to which they have succeeded in rpro ducing the properties of their ascendants and are (or feel) able to repro duce their properties in their descendancs.

A class or class fraction is in decline, and therefore turned towards the past, when it is no longer able to reproduce itself with all its properties of condicion and position and when a large proportion of its youngest members, in order to reproduce their overall capital and maintain their position in social space, must ar the least perform a reconversion of their capital which is accompanied by a change in condition, marked by a horizontal movement in social space; in orher words, when the reproduc. rion of class position becomes impossible (décassement) or requires a change of class fraction (reconversion). In this case, the change in the mode of social generation of she agents leads to the appearance of different generations whose conficts cannot be reduced to what is usually put under the heading of generation conflicts since they arise from the opposition berween values and life-stytes axociated with the predominance of eirler economic or culrural capital in the asset scrucrure. The liberal conservarism of those fractions of the dominant class whose reproduccion can be taken for granced thus contrasts with the reactionary dispositions of the fractions whose colective furure is threarened and who can only maintain rheir value by binding themselves to the past, referring ro systems of values, i.e., a logic of the derermination of value, corresponding to a superseded stare of the structure of the field of class relations.

The repressive dispositions of the small independent crafrsmen and shopkecpers and, more espccially, of the oldest fraciions of these relatively old groups, appear in a whole set of convergent indices. Thus they manifest a suspicion verging on hoscility towards moder art and artists, who, in all theis practices, and especially in the liberties rhey rake with the linguistic, sartorial, cosmetic or cthical norms, no doubc symbolize al rhat these groups deplore or derest in 'young people.' Twenty percent of them declare that 'the modern artist cakes his audience for a ride', as againsr 13 percent of both the senior execurives and che manual workers, 9 percent of the junior executives and 6 percent of the farm workers; 28 percent of chem concur with the view that art is just a marter of business, compared to 20 per cent of rhe junior executives, manual and farm workers and 15 percent of che senior executives (C.S. Ll). They are the caregory most inclined to say that teachers do not know how to win respect (62 percent of them, as against 55 percent of che junior execurives and clerical workers, 4 percent of the manual workers, 48 percent of the farm workers, 45 percent of the senior executives), to attribute children's failure ar school to their 'nor working hard enough' (57 percent, as against 47 percent of junior executives and clerical workers, 46 percent of manual and farm workers, 40 percent of senior executives) or to consider that school discipline is too lax (45 percent, as against 38 percent of manual workers, 36 petcent of junior execurives and clerical workers, 31 percent of farm workers and 30 percent of senior executives) (SOFRES, 1973). They are more often in favour of stricter film censorship chan all other groups—in contrast to the manual workers and junior executives (C.S. L). It is not surprising rhar, like the big commercial employers and the foremen, they devote a significant pro portion of their (generally limited) reading to Minute, a magazine wh specializes in al the rhemes and social fantasies (such as xenophobia) perit-bourgeois resentment. There is every reason to supposc that the extreme right recruits strongly in these categories.

Although i iscerrain that individuals occupying similar positions ray have different opinions according to their social origins and their trajectories, everything sems to indicate that the efects of individual trajec. tory, which are particularly visible in the case of groups occupying less determinare positions in social space and therefore more dispersed in al respects, are exertedwithin the limits of the inherent effects of class; so that the crhico-polirical dispositions of the members of the same class ap pear as transformed forms of the disposition which furdamentallycharacterizes the whole class." Thus obsequium, the deep-rooted respect for the established order which sets limits to petit-bourgeois revolr, is also rhe basis of rhe social virtues of the new pecite bourgeoisie. When it is a matter of sellimg goods and services which, like culrural goods or mate. rial comfort' goods—household equipment, buildings or furniture, clothing or leisure goods—are more or less successful materializations of the dominant life-style, the acquisition of which implies a recognition of rhe dominanr ethical or aesthetic values, norhing succeeds berter than the disposirion to sell one's own virtues, one's own certainties, one's own values, in a word, the certainty of one's own value, in a sort of ethical sπobbery, an assertion of exemplary singularity which implies condemnation of all other ways of being and doing. This disposirion is particularly called for when endeavouring to convert the working classes, to bring them into the race by foisring on them the latest bourgeois 'must', rhe latest mode or the larest morality, and when applying rhe same indignant conviction to repressing the 'repressive' dispositions of rhe working classes which ochers applied in the past to repressing their irrepressible laxity and imcemperance.

In the political arena, erhical submission to rhe dominant class and to the 'values' it embodics is revealed in a limired challenging of the established order which, being based on the sense of not occupying one's proper place in that order, conforms to the proprieties of the carger class even when challenging that class. Thus, in applying rheir favourite straregy, which consists in denouncing rhe established order in the name of rhe very principles it proclaims. the perit bourgeois bear witness to cheir recognition of those principles and appeal for recognition of their rireless sttuggle against scandals and, above all, hypocrisy, the source of alì scandals. Their desire for social recognicion and their prolepcic identification with the dominant class are revealed in rhe nature of their demands, which give prioriry to the symbolic aspects of exisrence, nor only because afronrs ro the dignity of the person' are felt more acurely by those who are freed from the most brural forms of oppression and exploitation, but also because rheir very concern for dignity inclines them to demands which testify to the dignity of rhe demander. Thus the fear of losing all they have won, by tiying to ger al they have been promised (parricularly through educarion and qualifications), does not fully explain the form raken by petir-bour geois bargainmg strategies. The ordinary means of the workers' struggle, strikes or demonstrations, are for the m a last resort, which they will consider only when driven to extremities by excessive injustice ('If need be, we'll rake to the streers'). They prefer symbotic weapons, starring wirh campaigns for 'education', which sers up a rela. tion of moral domination, or 'informarion\`, in which they have inordi. nate faith, and rhat particular form of collective action which is achieved chrough the 'association', a strictly serial grouping of individuals assem. bled solely by the same 'cause', the same desire to deliver a sort of ethical summons. Voluntary acrion, the conspicuous expenditure of goodwill, a pure, disincerested erhical intervention which recognizes no end ourside itself, confers, among orher privileges, that of nghreous indignation, in the name of the impeccability of those who have 'done their bit, done their whole duty and, above all, creared a fait accompli which calls for recognition." Stricrly 'disinreresred', "clean' activity, free of all 'com. promises' with politics, is in fact the pre-condition for the success of the institutionalization, the most perfect form of social recoguition, that is more or less secrerly pursued by all associations, petit-bourgeois rriovemenrs par excellence, which, unlike parties, secure the profirs of dignity and tespectability for undertakings 'of general interest' while promising to satisfy particular interests.

There is every reason to suppose thar the perir bourgeois a:re particularly loath to compromise themselves with the Communist Parry—an unseemly party, unappreciarive of their specific interests, and above all, unreceptive to the moralizing, edif,ing. vaguely humanisric phraseology wich which ihey radily identify ('values', 'taking responsibility', 'personal development', 'growth', 'parrnership', concem' etc.)—when their sense of personal worrh and their individualisric reservarions have been encouraged by more com. fortable condirions of existence and an early upbrin ging impregnated with teligious values conducive to personalism. The higher their social origin, che more probable are chese effccts of condirioning or inculcatiort, so thar they are very likely to be reinforced by the effects of a dechnirig trajectory One may also suppose thar the perir bourgeois would move towards the PSU and its combination of proter and propriery, if it were less Overtly rev. olurionary or if it 'grew up' to be a 'responsible parry of government, as has now happtned with the technocratic-modernisr wing of the Socialist Party. But they respond equally well to a reformiug, intelligent' conservatism. In short, they waver, and at times of crisis they can swing abruptly.

is also, and especially, because the chances rhat political 'choice' will be no more than a politically blind response of class ethos increase as one moves towards the highest age groups, towards rhe smaller towns and villages, or down the hierarchy of levels of education or of social positions, and are distinctly greater among women than men. The contami. nation of politics by ethos, although it is less marked among manual workers, who are more 'politicized", than among small fariners and smal employers, does nor spare the membets of che working classes. For those of chem who, because of theirsex (women), their age (che old), their place of residence (country dwellers), and, associated with this, their occupational milieu (workers in small frms) are more exposed to the threat of social decline, of falling or relapsing into the sub-prolerariat, and at the same time are politically the least educared and organized, and therefore less inclined and equipped to grasp problems and sicuations through political categories of perceprion and appreciarion, thereis nothing to counterbalance the tendency to pessimism and even resentment which inclines them to a generalized rejecrion of politics' and 'pd. iticians' of every sort and so to absrention or conservatism.

To understand the dispositions which may, according to the degree of poliricization, be expressed in either progressive' or conservative' political positions, one would have to analyse the deep insecurity engendered by the experience of a whole lifecime lived under rhe chrear of accident, illness, unemployment and the negative carter leading to che sub.proletariar; and the corresponding arcachment to acquired advantages and to the individual or collective straregies responsible for their acquisition, which has norhing in common with attachment to the esrablished order. One would have to ex. amine how working condirions and, specifically, the disciplines, especiafly as regards time, imposed by the employer rend, in themselves, quite aparr from any ideological inculcation, conrinuously to reinforce the dispositions inculcated by an early upbringing which (both necessarily and deliberarely) reproduces rhe nccessiries. harshness and rigidity of the existence for which it prepares. (Ir goes wichout saying that political education' cannot be reducedasthose who interestthemselves in che subject almost always sup. posero the conscious transmission of the representations most directly linked to the sphere of the 'political' in theordinarysense of the word. le would be at least as absurd to reduce the conditions of production of the dispositions which are the basis of political judgements and practices to spe cifically political socialization--or, worse, to its institutionalized aspect, 'civic instruction' in schoolsas it would be ro reduce rhe social conditions of the production of rastewhich is also a political disposition-to specifcaly artistic training.) One would have to analyse more precisely the universe of pracrical mediations through which rhe attachment to an ordered world, starting with an ordered domestic world, i.e., one subordinated to the order imposcd by the universe of labour, impresses itself on industrial workers; how the order of the factory-temporal, moral and social-extends even inco the sphere of so-called privare' life, into the schemes of thought and expression which enable it ro be thought and expressed and which are often applied outside thar sphere. One would have to consider whether the island osecurity, autonomy and stabiliry which the family represents is not both the last bastion, with, for instance, the memory of pasr struggles and evcrything which consritures the class sense of honour (including, fot example, a cerrain cult of viri liry, the basis of a whole conservative' vision of the division of labour between che sexes), and also the point of least resisrance (symbolized in and embodied by women, who are 'depoliticized' by their siarus and put in charge of consumprion) chrough which the forces of domination penetrate to the heart of practice and che deepest level of the unconscious. unconscious. scenrible ro rhe 'screening'

One may assume rhat individuals are more susceprible to the screening or 'false context' effecrs of locally based groups (and also fields), that is, more likely to understand their position in social space in terms of a geographically based sub-space (village, neighbourhood etc.), the more chey jack che properties which decermine the chances of access co opinion or to the mcans of expressing it (such as reading a nacional newspaper). Those who are dominant in a space chat is dominared overalł (che owners of fifay hectares in a region of small holdings, local πotables, foremen etc.) may thus make political choices which are in harmony with the choices of those who dominate the whole society." The same logic no doubt partly explains why office workers, who are ar the bortom of the hierarchy of the civil service and of middle-class wageearncts, more often vote on the left rhan foremen, who are at the top of the working class. More generally, one may assume chat, other things being equal, and whatever the position of rhese helds in the social space, chose dominant in a relarively autonomous feld are more inclined co vote on che right than those dominated in che corresponding frelds, and that the dominared of all helds are more inclined to vore on the left chan the corresponding dominant agents.

## Political Language

Thus, che elemeit of discrepancy which remains unexplained so long as the question is looked ar in cerms of the determinants of ethical dispositions is explained b y the relationship that is set up, in each opinion poll ot election, berween a supply of ready-made opinions and a determinare capacity to choose. The consumers of opinions may make mistakes over the object (allodoxia) and, indeed, the more they rely on the politically uncertain, or even indetertninate, schemes of cheir class echos, for lack of specifically political principles of perception and appreciation, the more likely they are to endorse opinions thar are not their own. Bur they may also choose one constituted opinion when, whether they know it or nor, they would choose another, more or less different one if ir were con ruted as such and, above al,i, being professed by a dererminate groupof professionals, it had some claim to respecrability.

The political feld purs forward a universe of political possibles which, as such, thus exerts a twofold effect. First, it favours che effect of spurious identification resulting from the fact that the same implicit sense may recognize its political expression in different forms of the already-made. explicit'. Secondly, it tends to produce an effect of closure by cacitly Presenting the universe of realized possibles as the universe of possible pos. sibles, thus delimiting the universe of the politically thinkable. These (wo eff ects are exerted here wich particular force because demand' hard)y ever (at least in the dominated classes) pre-exists a supply of political discourse: unformulated or partially formulated, it only knows what it means' when it 'sees what it recognizes', rightly or vrongly, in an ofered opinion.

That is why it is necessary ro return to the analysis of the logocentrism of the politico-scientifc' survey which, in its methodological innocence, performs a sort of in vitro replication of the most fundamental effect of the division of political labour. By offering a choice among several utterances and asking for a position to be caken on the already-urrered, the survey—like political consulrarions—proceeds as if it had already resolved the essential problem of politics, namely, the question of the transmura. tion of experience into discourse, of the unformulated ethos into a con. sticured, constituting logos, of a class sense, which may imply a form of adaptation and resignation to rhe self evidences of the social order, into a conscious, i.e., explicitly formulared, apprehension of that order. Eliminating the labour of formulation, the survey tacitly presupposes that the person questioned would have been capable of producing or even repro ducing the proposition which constitutes che sratement of che question, or even of spontaneously adopting the relation to language and to politics which underlies the production of such an inquiry (although the 'yes' or 'no' it can always produce cannot be regarded as an index of that aptitude). At the same time, by unconsciously begging the question, it denies itself the possibility of gathering the information which governs the meaning that can be given to all rhe information directly gathered. Recogn izing only the electoral imperative of formal equality before the questionnaire, which combines with the rechnical imperative of standardized information-gathering, the pre-condition for the formal comparability of che data and the material and mental automation of rhe analysis, the survey attributes the operation of cœonstitution to the respon dent, even when it is in fact a product of the questioning. The effect of questioning is all the stronger, rhe more completely the 'respondents' are deprived of rhe means of seeing rhe questions as political' and of giving them an answer—and a polirical' answer—and rhe further they are from

fulfling che condirions of produ.iction of a corpus of coherent, homoge. neous opinions, generatedfrom an explicitly consrituted principle. Between echos and logos, practical mastery and verbal ma tery, there is a radical discontinuity.® There is no necessary hnk berwcen the practical mastery which can guide everyday practice, including all its (objectively) political aspects, without ever being made explicit or, still less, systemacically conceprualized, and the symbolic mastery of experience which is expressed in discourse socially recognized as political and which presup. poscs the bracketing of all direct, exclusive reference to the concrete par. ticularicy of a situation. Because this is so. and because the relationship of experience to expression, that is, to consciousness, isrelacively underermined, the same ex eriences may recognize their images in very ditt erent aiscourscs. very diff erent discourses. 59 ro credir polir-

This tlexibility is not unlimited, and it would be wrong to credit political languagewith the power of arbitrarily bringing into being what it designates. Manipulation tends to be containedwithin certain limits because one maybe ableto tesist an atgument without being capable of atguing resistance, still less of explicitly formulating its ptinciples; and also becausepopular language has its own resources which are nor those of anajysisbut which sometimesfnd an equivalent in a parable or meraphor (such as this one, expressingscepticismabout profit-sharing':You lend me your warch and l'll give you the time'). The fact remains that, even when recognizing itselfn a speech or a spokesman, class habitus is not infallible. lf the members of che dominated classes frequently produce a discourse that is in contradiction with itself, with the meaning of their pracrice and with cheir objective condition, this is because chey have to calk politics without possessing the instruments of production of cheir discourse, without having control over their olitical rongue'. aiscourse, witnout is arhere ihe

This crossing-point between experience and expression is where the professional producers of discourse come in; it is he re that the relations are st up berween the experts and the laymen, the signifiers and che sig. nified. The dominated,whose interests are bound up with the raising of consciousness, i.e., with language, are at the mercy of the discourses thar are presented to chem; whenever chey emerge from doxa they are liable to fall inro allodoxia, i:nro all che false recognitions encouraged by che domi.

nant discourse. Ar best, rhey are art rhe mercy of cheir own spokesmen, whose role is to provide them with the means of repossessing their own expenence. The essential indererminacy of the relarionship between expe rience and expression is compounded by the efect of legitimacy imposition and censorship exerted by rhe dommant use of language, racirly recognized, even by rhe spokesmen of the dommated, as the legitiniate mode of expression of polirical opinion. The dominant language discred. its and destroys the spontaneous political discourse of the dominared. Ir leaves them only silence or a borrowed language, whose logic depares from that of popular usage but without becoming that of erudite usage, a deranged language, in which the 'fine words'are only there to mark che dignity of the expsessive inrenrion, and which, unable to express anyshing rue, real or 'felt', dispossses the speaker of the very experience it is supposed to express. lt forces recourse to spokesmen, who are cheinselves condemned to use the dommant language (which is sufficient to introduce a distance from the mandarors and, worse, from tlheir problems and their experience of their problems), or at least a routine, routinizing language which, in addition to its funcrions as a mnemonic and a safery net, consrirutes the only system of defence for those who can neither play the game nor spoil' it, a language which never engages with reality bur churns out its canonical formulae and slogans, and which dispossesses the mandators of their experience a xcon dime

Through the language and relation to language, bound up with a whole life-style, which foist the msdves on anyone who seeks to participare in political life', a whole relation tothe world is imposed, a relation of denral which (like art) distances and neurralizes, en ablingspeakers to speak without thinking what they speak. This intrinsically euphemistic language, which presents itself in the guise of universality, de-realizes everything it names (for example, withmillions out of work, a minister speaks of a cerrain erosion of full employment'), imposing a toral but totally invisible censorship on rhe expression of the specific intereses of the dominared, who can only choose berween the sanirized words of of. ficial discourse and inarticulate grumblings. Questions which, at best,   
seem co demand a simple 'yes' or no' are in fact addressed, by a tacit priv  
ilege, to individuals or groups defined nor so much by a particular care  
gory of political opinion as by the capacity to sustain the neurralized,

'of general interest', i.e., rhe capacity not only to decipher ancl manipulate

question, to identify it as such and feel called upon to answer ir, and to

lireness. In short, a politico-scientific' question calls for the expatiating dispOsition which enables one to manipulare language withour reference to any practical situation, tarheras one might with an essay topic or ex. ainarion question, to grant it the conventional seriousness that is applied ro scholastic exercises or parlour games.

One only need look at the definition which the'referee'of the television programme Face à face' gives ofpolicical debare as he understands it: 'It is perhaps nor superfuous to remind you that this is a polirical debare. 1 think thar, if you will agree, I will start hy asking each of you, starting with Mr Habib Deloncle, since we have agreed that he is to speak frst, to defne your general position, your overall interpretation of the problem, and to give examples from two arcas, first educarion, in the broad sense, and then informarion, again in the broad sense, and then, of course, you may sum up.' Like the televised debare, the survey is very close to the scholastic siruarion in the quesrions it asks, which are ofren, to within a word or cwo, those which furnish ENA dissertation topics, political science lecture themes or the titles of articles in Le Monde. Above all, it is very close co the classroom by virtue of the form of the social relarion in which the questioning takes place. Only scholastic ncutralization and disrancing can enable one to respond adequately ro a situation in which a perfect stranger comes up and asks polirical questions (normally excluded from such encounters) withour even thinking of invoking the auchoriry of a third party. lt has been estab. lished empirically thar polirical discussions most ofren occur berween people of identical opinions. 1f this is so, it is becauxe a whole spontaneous semiology is implemented so as to avoid 'burning issues', the foremost of which are political issues, and to esrablish the provisional consensus which, in the accidental encounters of everyday life, can only be creaced by recourse to commonplaces and by constant vigilance.

One is reminded of a remark by Pierre Greco, who pointed out that the question, Are your friends' friends your friends? invites answers which, even if identical, may difer radically as regards their principle, being produced either by a simple logical calculation based on a syrtactic rewriting of the utcerance itself. or by mental reference to rhe real world of friends. Like all the discourse of the 'doxosopher', who never teaches anyrhing other than the difficulty of raking sides, the questioning he proposes produces disrance: it disrances the receiver by reminding him of the complexity and depth of the questions chrough the very complexiry of the formulation; it disrances reality by imposing the empty intentionality demanded by the obligatory subjects of scholastic discourse.

The unreality of the most specifically policical quesrions also, and espe. cially, arises from the fact that they only take on their fulì meaning in relarion to the semancic held which is none orher than the feld of difterent political positions corresponding to dif erent positions in the field of ideological production. For a complete undersranding of the meaning objecrively implied in che most summary rsponse (\*yes' or 'no' in a refer. endum) to a question such as, Are you in favour of or opposed ro a re. gional reform designed ro creare regions endowed with extensive powers»"one would have to be able ro mobilize che entirely specific competence possessed by political commentarors and analysts, which en. ables chem to construct the system of possibles, itemize che ser of rele. vant positions on che question, define che meaning each of these has in the incernal and external strategies of che polirical groups concerned and uncover what each group has at stake. In short, the most genuinely polit. ical questions, those which most imperatively demand specific political comperence, are chose which arise for che professionals who argue abour them (such as problems of conscirucional law or, in ocher circles, the quesrion of che dicrarorship of che proletariat) or those which, though very directly affecting the most concrere conditions of exisrence of their addressees, appear in the form chey have to take in order to become rec. ognized stakes in che struggles which rake place in che feld of ideological producrion.

Norhing more clearly reveals the truth of this self-interested questioning than the ruling fracrion's obsession wich the economic informarion of the cirizens', inspired by cheir dream of a dominared class possessing just enough economic comperence ro recognize che economic competence of rhe dominant class. The informarion which governments so readily and abundantly supply is of the sort which tends to disqualify (as a doctor does his patient's knowledge) the information the dominared derive in practical form from their ordinary experience (for example, their knowledge of the rise in che cost of living or of inequalicy in raxation). This leads to a policical discourse which, racher chan giving underscanding. diccares a standpoint, and, far from providing the means of rclaring particular, practical information to general information, is content to sh unt individual experiences into the appropriare general frameworks. Opinion surveys often reproduce the logic of those surveys on the economic information'of the popularion' which seck ro measure the respondents knowledge and acknowledgement of ofcial economics and economic informarion, the simple validaring srare. ment of a political economy, and which, with a sovcreign erhnocentrism, reduce the pracrical economics and polirics of the agents to a clumsy srarement of official economics and political science (in parricular through a priori and a posreriori coding operarions calculared to oblirerate whar is es sential, namely, the mode of expression).

Everything combines to reinforce the deep distrust—not incompatible with an equally deep form of recognition—which the dominared feel towards polirical language, broadly identifed, like cverything symbolic, wich che dominant, the masrers of the art of packagmg and of fobbig off with words. This suspicion of the political 'srage', a 'thearre' whose rules are nort understood and which leaves ordinary caste with a sense of helplessness, is ofren the source of 'apathy' and of a generalized discrust of all forms of speech and spokesmen. And often the only escape from ambivalence or indererminacy towards language is to fall back on what one can appreciare, che body rather than words, substance rarher than form, an honest face rather chan a smooch tongue.

# Conclusion: Classes andClassifications

If I have to choose the lesset of two evils, I choose neither

Karl Kraus

Taste is an acquired disposition to 'differentiate' and 'appreciate',' as Kant says—in orher words, to establish and mark differences by a process of distinction which is not (or not necessarily) a distinct knowledge, in Leibniz's sense, since it ensures recognition (in the ordinary sense) of the object withour implying knowledge of the distinctive features which define it.2 The schemes of the habitus, the primary forms of classification, owe their specific efhcacy to che fact thar they function below rhe level of consciousness and language, beyond the teach of introspective scrutiny or control by the will. Orienting practices practically, they embed what some would mistakenly call values in the most automatic gestures or the apparently most msignificant techniques of the bodyways of walking or blowing one's nose, ways of eating or calking—and engage the most fundamental principles of construction and evaluation of the social world, those which most directly express the division of labour (berween the classes, the age groups and the sexes) or the division of the work of dominarion, in divisions berwcen bodies and between relarions to rhe body which borrow more fearures than one, as if to give rhem the appearances of naturalness, from the sexual division of labour and the divisioll of sexual labour. Taste is a practical mastery ofdistributions which makes it possible to sense or intuit what is likely (or unlikely) to befall\_and therefore to befit-an individual occupying a given position in social space. Ir functions as a sort of social orientation, a 'sense of one's place', guiding the occupants of a given place in social space towards che social positions adjusred to their properties, and towards the pracrices or goods which befit the occupants of that position. It implies a practical anticiparion of what the social meaning and value of che chosen practice or thing will probably be, given cheir distribution in social space and the practical knowledge the other agenrs have of the correspondence between goods and groups.

Thus, the social agents whom the sociologist classifies are producers ot only of classifiable acts but also of acts of classification which are themselves classifed. Knowledge of che social world has co cake into account a practical knowledge of this worlld which pre-exists it and which ir musr not fail to include in irs object, alchough, as a firsr stage, this knowledge has to be constituced against the partial and interested repre. sentations provided by practical knowledge. To speak of habitus is to include in che object the knowledge which the agents, who are part of the object, have of the object, and the contribution this knowledge makes to the reality of the object. But it is not only a marter of putting back inco che real world that one is endeavouring to know, a knowledge of the real world that contributes to its reality (and also to the force it exetts). It means conferring on chis knowłedge a genuinely constitutive power, che very power it is denied when, in the name of an objectivisr conception of objectivity, one makes common knowledge or theorerical knowledge a mere reflection of che real worlld.

Those who suppose they are producing a materialist theory of knowledge when they make knowledge a passive recording and abandon che 'active aspect' of knowledge to idealism, as Marx complains in che Theses on Feuerbach, forget thar all knowledge, and in particular all knowledge of the social world, is an act of construction implementing schemes of thought and expression, and chat berwcen condicions of existence and pracctices or represencations there incervenes the struccuring activicy of the agents, who, far from reacting mechanically to mechanical stimulations, respond to the invitarions or chreats of a worlld whose meaning they have helped ro produce. However, the principle of this structuring activity is not, as an intellectualist and anti-generic idealism would have it.a sysrem of universal forms and categories but a system of internalized, embodied schemes which, having been constitured in che course of collective history, are acquired in rhe course of individual history and function in cheir praclical scate, for practice (and not for the sake of pure knowledge).

## Embodied Social Structures

'This means, in rhe frst place, chat social science, in constructing the social worlld, cakes note of the fact char agents are, in their ordinary practice, the subjects of acts of construction of the social world; but also thar it aims, among other chings, to describe che social genesis of the principles of construction and seeks the basis of these principles in che social world. Breaking with the anri-genetic prejudice which often accomrions of che use ma e of these principles according to the position occu. pied in the distributions (questions which all require the use of statistics).

The cognitive structures which social agents implement in rheir practicai knowledge of the social world are intemalized, 'embodied' social structures. The practical knowiedge of the social world that is presupposed by reasonable' behaviour prithin it implements classificatory schemes (or 'forms of classihcation', 'mental structures' or 'symbolic forms'--apart from their connorations, these expressions are virtually interchangeable), hisrorical schemes of perception and appreciation which are the product of the objective division into classes (age groups, genders, social classes) and which function below the level of conscious. ness and discourse. Being the product of the incorporation of the fundamencal structures of a society, these principles of division ate common to all the agents of the sociery and make possible the production ofa common, meaningful worlld, a common-sense world.

All the agents in a given social formation share a set of basic perceptual schemes, vhich reccive the beginnings of objectification in the pairs of antagonistic adjectives commonly used to classify and qualify persons or objects in the most varied areas of practice. The network of oppositions between high (sublime, elevated, pure) and low (vulgar, low, modest), spirirual and material, fne (refined, elegant) and coaise (heavy, fat, crude, brural), light (subtle, lively, shatp, adroit) and heavy (slow, chick, blunt, laborious, clumsy), free and forced, broad and narrow, or, in as). other dimension, berween unique (rare, different, distinguished, exclusive, exceptional, smgular, novel) and common (ordinary, banal, commonplace, crivial, routine), briliant (intelligent) and dull (obscute, grey', mediocre), is the matrix of all the commonplaces which find such ready acceptance because behind them lies the whole social order. The network has its ultimate source in the opposition berween the élite' of rhe dominant and che mass' of che dominated, a contingent, dis organized mulriplicity, interchangeable and innumerable, existing only statistically. These mythic roots only have to be alowed to take their oisexclusiveness.

The seemingly most forınal oppositions within chis social mythology social order. the opposition berween the dominant and the dominated, which is inscribe in the division oflabour, and che opposition, rooted in the division of the labour of domination, between two principles of domination, two powers, dominant and dominated, temporal and spiritual, material and intellectual etc. It follows that the map of social space pre. vioudy Put forward can also be read as a strict cable of the historically constituted and acquired categories which organize the idea of thesocial world in the minds of all che subjects belonging to that world and shape:d by it. The same classificatory schemes (and the oppositions in which they are expressed) can function, by being specified, in fields orga. nized around polar positions, whether in the field of the dominant class, organized around an opposition homologous to the opposirion constituting the field of the socia) classes, or in the field of cultural production, which is itself organized around oppositions which reproduce the structure of the dominant class and are homologous to it (e.g., the opposition berween bourgeois and avant-garde theatre). So the fundamenral opposition constantly supports second, third or n' rank oppositions (those which underlie the 'purest' ethical or aestheric judgements, with their high or low sentiments, their facile or difficult notions of beauty, their light or heavy scyles etc.), while euphemizing irself to the point of misrecognizabilicy.

Thus, the opposirion berween the heavy and the light, which, in a number of its uses, especially scholastic ones, ser es to distinguish popular or petir-bourgeois tastes from bourgeois castes, can be used by theatre ctiticis aimed at the dominant fraction of the dominant class to express the relarionship berween 'incellecrual' theatre, which is condemned for its laborjious' prerensions and 'oppressive' didacticism, and 'bourgeois' thearre, which is praised for its tact and its artof skimmmg over surfaces. By contrast, 'intellectual' criticism, by a simple inversion of values, ex-Presses che rclationship in a scarcely modifed form of the same opposition, with lighoness, identified with frivolicy, being opposed to profun. dicy. Similarly, it can be shown that the opposition berween right and left, which, in its basic form, concerns the relationship berween the dominant and che dominated, can also, by means of a frst transformation, tions within the dominant class; the words right and left then meaning close to the meaning they have in expressions like theatre or 'lefr-bank' cheatre. With a further degree of 'de-realizar artistic or titetary group, and so on.

able. Each particular use of one of chese pairs only rakes on its full mean. usually implicitsince it is question of the system of self-evidences and presuppositions thar are taken for granted in the feld in relation to which the speakers' straregies are dehned. But each of the couples speci. fed by usage has for undertones all the orher uses it might have because ofche homologies berween the felds which allow transfers from one field to another- -and also all the other couples which are interchangeabe with iz, within a nuance or two (e.g., hne/crude for light/heavy), that is, in slightly different contexes.

The fact char the semi-codifed oppositions contained in ordinary language reappear, with very similar values, as the basis of the dominant vision of the social world, in all class-divided social formarions (consider the tendency to see the 'people' as the site of totally uncontrolled appe. tites and sexualiry) can be understood once one knows that, reduced to their formal structure, the same fundamental relarionships, precisely those which express the major relarions of order (high/low, strong,/weak etc.) reappear in all class-divided societies. And the recurrence of the mi adic structure srudied by Georges Dumézil, which Georges Duby shows in the case of feudal society to be roored in che social strucrures it leginmates, may wel be, like the invariant oppositions in which the relation ship of domination is expressed, simply a necessary outcome of the intersection of the two principles of division which are at work in all class-divided socieries—the division berween the dominant and the dom inared, and che division berween the different fractions compering for dominance in the name of different principles, belfatores (warriors) and oratores (scholars) in feudal society, businessmen and intellectuals now.

## Knowledge without Concepts

Thus, chrough che diferentiared and differentiating conditionings asso ciared with the different conditions of existence, chrough the exchu and inclusions, unions (marriages, affairs, alliances etc.) and divisions (mcomparibilities, separarions, struggles etc.) which govemn the soci strucrure and the structuring force it exerts, through all the hierarchies linits, a practical anticipation of objective limits acquired by experience of objective limits, a 'sense of one's place' which leads one to exclude oneself from the goods, persons, places and so forth from which one is excluded. FXC

The sense of limits imphes forgetting the limits. One of the most important effects of the correspondence berween real divisions and practical principles of division, berween social structures and menral structures, is undoubredly the fact thar primary experience of the social world is that of doxa, an adherence to relarions of order which, because they strucrure inseparably both the real world and the thought world, are accepted as self evident. Primary perception of the social world, far from being a simple mechanical reftection, is always an act of cognirion irivolving princi. ples of construction that are exrernal to the constructed object grasped in its immediacy; but at the same time it is an act of miscognition, implyirig the most absolute form of recognition of the social order. Domir ated agents, who assess the value of their position and theit characteristics by applying a system of schemes of perception and appreciation which is the embodiment of the objecrive laws whereby their value is objectively constituted, tend to attribure to themselves what the distribution atrcribures to chem, refusing whar they are refused (That's nor for the likes of us'), adjusting cheir expectacions to cheir chances, defning themselves as the sta.blished order defines chem, reproducing in their verdict on themselves the verdict the economy pronounces on them, in a word, condemning chemselves to what is in any case their tot, ta heausou, as Plaro put it, consenting to be whar they have to be, 'modesr', 'humble' and 'obscure' Thus the conservation of the social order is decisively reir.- forced by what Durkheim called 'logical conformiry',6 i.e., the orchesrration of caregories of perceprion of the social world, which, being adjusted to the divisions of the esrablished order (and thereby to the in. rerestsof those who dominate it) and common to all minds strucrured in accordance with chose structures, present every appearance of objective necessity.

The sysrem of classificarory schemes is opposed to a raxonomy based on explicit and explicitly concerted principles in the same way char the dispositions consticuring raste or erhos (which are dimensions of it) are opposed to aesthetics or ethics. The sense of social realities thar is acquired in the confrontarion with a particular form of social necessicy is what makes it possible to act as if one knew the stucture of the social world, one's place within it and the distances t

preme form of materialistic vulgarity, the reduction of che unique to the class, che explananon of the higher by the lower, the application to the would-beunclassifiable of explanatory models ft only for the 'bourgeois', the petit-bourgeois, the limited and common, scarcely nclines intellectuals to conceptualize the sense of social position, still less their own position and the perverse relation to the social worlld it forces on them. (The perfect example is Sartre, whose whole work and whole existence revolve around this afrmation of the intellectual's subversive point of honour. This is seen particularlly clearly in the passage in Being and Nothingness on the psychology of Flaubert, which can be read as a desperate effort to save the person, in the person of the intellectual, an uncreated creator, begotten by his own works, haunted by 'the project of being God', from every sort of reduction to the gencral, the rype, the class, and to affirm the transcendence of the ego against 'what Comte called morerialism, that is, explaining the higher by the lower')

The practical mastety of classification has nothing in common with the reflexive mastery that is required in order to construct a taxonomy that is simultaneously coherent and adequate to social reality. The practical 'science' of positions in social space is the competence presupposed by the art of behaving comme il faut wirh persons and things that have and give 'class' ('smart' or 'unsmart'), finding the right distance, by a sort of practical calculation, neither too close ('getting familiar') nor too far ('being distant'), playing with objective distance by emphasizing it (being ‘aloof, 'stand-ofsh') or symbolically denying it (being ap proachable,''hobnobbing'). lt in no way implies the capacity to situate oncself explicitly in the classihcation (as so many surveys on social class ask people to do), still less to descriibe this classification in any sysrematic way and state its principles.

There is no better opportuniry to observe the functioning of this sense of the place one occupies than in condescension strategies, which presuppose both in the author of the strategy and in the victims a practical knowledge of the gap between the place really occupied and the place fictitiously indicated by the behaviour adopted (e.g.. in French, use of the familiar ¿u). When the person naturally' identifed with a Rolls Royce, a top hat or gols (sce appendix 4) takes the metro, sports a ßat cap (or a polo neck) or plays football, his practices take on their meaning in relation ro chis attribution by status, which continues to colour che real practices, as if by superimposi tion. But one could also point to the variations chat Charlles Baly obserucd in the style of speech according to zhe social gap between rhe interlocurors or the variarions in pronunciation according to the addresscc: rhe speaker accent.

the practical 'artributive judgement' whereby one puts someone in a class by speaking to him in a cerain way (chereby putting oneself in a class at the same time) has nothing to do with an intellectual operation implying conscious reference to explicit indices and the implementation of casses produced by and for the concept. The same classificatory oppo sition (rich/poor, young/old etc.) can be applied at any point in the dis. riburion and reproduce its whole range within any of its segments (common sense tells us that one is always richer or poorer than someone, superior or infcrior to someone, more right-wing or left-wing than someone--but this does not entail an elementary relarivism).

In a scries of incerviews (n = 30) on the social classes, based on a test which involved classifying thirty occuparions (wrirren on cards), the re. spondents often first asked how many classes the set should be divided into, and chen severa) times modifed the number of classes and the criceria of claxsification, so as to take account of the different dimensions of each occuparion and therefore the different respects in which it could be evaluated; or they spontaneously suggested that they could carry on sub.dividing indefinitely. (They thereby exposed the artificiality of the situarion created by a theoretical inquiry which called for the adoption of a theoretical attirude to which, as their initia) uncertainty indicated, the tespondents were quite unaccustomed.) And yet they almost always agreed on the ranks of the different occuparions when taken rwo by two. (Lenski made similar obscrvations in an experimenc in which the respondents were asked to rank the families in a small town in New England.)

It is not suiprising that it is possible to faul the practical sense of so cial space which lies behind class-attributive judgement; the sociologists who use their respondents'self-conrradictions as an argument for deny. ing the existence of classes simply reveal that they understand noching of how this 'sense' works or of the artificial situation in which they are making it work. In fact, whether it is used to situate oneself in social space or to place others, the sense of social space, like every practical sense, always refers to the particular situation in which it has to orient practices. This explains, for example, the divergences betwcen surveys of the represenration of the classes in a small town ('community studies') and surveys of class on a nation-wide scale." But if, as has often been ob. served, sespondents do not agree either on the number of divisions they make within the group in question, or on the limits of the 'strara' and the criteria used to defne them, this is not simply due to the fuzziness in ercnt in all practical logics. 1t is also because people's image of the classificarion is a function of their position within ir.

So noching is further removed from an act of cognition, as conccived by the mtellecualist tradition, than this sense of the social serucure, perceiving favouis' and the capacity to discern aestheric values'-is so bodily automarisms. Everything takes place as if the social conditionings linked to a social condition tended to inscribe the relation to the social world in a lasting, generalized relation to one's own body, a way of bearing one's body, present ing it to others, moving it, making space for it, which gives the body its social physiognomy. Bodily hexis, a basic di. mension of the sense of social orientation, is a practical way of experienc. ing and expressing one's own sense of social value. One's relationship to the social world and to one's proper place in it is never mote clearly expressed than in the space and rime one feels enticled o take from ochers; more precisely, in the space one claims with one's body in physical space, through a bearing and gesrutes that are self -assured or reserved, expansive or constricted (presence' or 'msignifcance') and with one's speech in rime, through the interaction time one appropriates and the self-assured or aggressive, careiess or unconscious way one appropriates it.

There is no berter image of the logic of socialization, whichtrears the body as a 'memory-jogger", than those complexes of gestures, postures and words--simple interjections or favourite clichéswhich only have to be slipped into, like a theatrical costume, to awaken, by the evocative power of bodily mimesis, a universe of ready-made feelings and experiences. The elementary actions of bodily gymnastics, especially the specifcally sexual, biologically pre-constructed aspect of it, charged with social meanings and values, function as the most basic of metaphors, capable of evoking a whole relationship to the worlld, 'lofty' or 'submissive', 'expan. sive' or 'narrow', and through it a whole world. The practical choices' of the sense of social orientation no more presuppose a representation of the range of possibilities than does the choice of phonemes; these enacred choices imply no acts of choosing. The logocentrism and intellectualism of intellectuals, combined with the prejudice inherent in the science which rakes as its object the psyche, the soul, the mind, consciousness. represenrations, nor to mention the petir-bourgeois prerension to the searus of person', have prevented us from seeing thar, as Ieibniz put it, we are auromatons in three-quarters of whar we do', and thar the ulrimare values, as they are caled, are never anything ocher than the primary, primitive disposirions of the body, 'visceral' rastes and distastes, in which the group's most vital interests are embedded. the things on which one is prepared to stake one's own and other people's bodies. The sense of discinction, che discretio (discrimination) which demands that certain things be brought together and others kept apart, which excludes all misalliances and all unnatural unions--i.e., all unions contrary to the common classificarion, to the diacrisis (separation) which is the basis of collective and individual identity-responds with visceral, murderous horror, absolute disgust, metaphysical fury, to everything which lies in Plato's 'hybrid zone', everything which passes understanding, that is, the embodied axonomy, which, by challenging the principles of the incarnate social order, especially the socially constituted principles of the sexual division of labour and the division of sexual labour, violares the mental order, scandalously Aouting common sense.

It can be shown thar socializstion tends to constitute the body as an analogical operaror establishing alt sorts of practical equivalences berween che diferent divisions of the social world--divisions between the sexes, between the age groups and between the social classcs or, more precisely, berween the meanings and values associated with che individuals occupying practically equivalent positions in the spaces defined by these divisions. And it can be shown thar it does so by integrating the symbolism of social domination and submission and the symbolism of sexual domination and sub. mission into the same body language—as is seen in eriquerte, which uses che opposition berween rhe straight and the curved or, which amounts to the same thing, berween raising (oneself) and lowering (oneself), as one of che generative principles of the marks (of respect, contempt etc.) used to symbolize hierarchical relations.

## Advantageous Attributions

The basis of the pertinence principle which is implemented in perceiving the social world and which defines all the characteristics of persons or things which can be perceived, and perceived as positively or negatively interesting, by all chose who apply these schemes (a'nother definition of common sense), is based on nothing other than the interest the individuals or groups in question have in recognizing a fearure and in identifying the individual in question as a member of the set defined by thar feature; interest in che aspect observed is never completely independent of the advantage of observing it. This can be clearly seen in all the classihcations built around a srigmatized fearure which, like the everyday opposition between homosexuals and heterosexuals, isolare the interesting trait from all the rest (i.e, all ocher forms of sexuality), which remain indiferent and undifferentiared. It is even clearer in all 'labelling judgements', hich are in fact accusations, calegoremet in the original Aristorelian sense, and which, like insuirs, only wish to know one of the properties consrituting the social identity of an individual or group (You're just a '), regarding, for example, the married homosexual or converted Jew as a closer gucen' or covert Jew, and thereby in a sense doubly Jewish or homosexual. The logic of the stigma reminds us that social identiry is che stake in a struggle in which the stigmatized individual or group, and, more generally, any individual or group insofar as he or it is a potential objcct of categorization, can onl retaliate against che paria perception which limits it to one of its characteristics by highlighting, in its self gling to impose the taxonomy most favourable to its charactcristics, least to give to the dominant taxonomy the content most Aattering to what it has and what it is

Those who are surprised by the paradoxes that ordinary logic and language engender when they apply their divisions to continuous magntrudes forget the paradoxes inherent in treating language as a putely logical instrument and also forget the social situation in which such a re lationship to language is possible. The contradictions or paradoxes to which ordinary language classifications lead do not derive, as all forms of positivism suppose, from some essential inadequacy of ordinary language, but from the fact that these socio-logical acts are not directed towards the pursuit of logical coherence and that, unlike philological, logical or lin guistic uses of language-which ought really to be called scholastic, sinice they all presuppose schole, i.e., leisure, distance from urgency and neces sity, the absence of vital stakes, and the scholastic institution which in most social universes is the only institution capable of providing all these—they obey the logic of the parti pris, which, as im a court-room, juxtaposes not logical judgements, subject to the sole criterion of coherence, but charges and defences. Quite apart from all that is implied in the oppositions, which logicians and even linguists manage to forget, be. tween the art of convincing and the art of persuading, it is clear chat scholastic usage of language is to the orator's, advocate's or politician's usage what the classificatory systems devised by the logician or statistician concerned with coherence and empirical adequacy are to the care gorizations and categoremes of daily life. As the etymology suggests, the latter belong to the logic of the trial.'3 Every real inquiry into the divisions of the social world has to analyse the interests associated with membership or non-membership. As is shown by the attention devoted to strategic, 'frontier' groups such as the 'labour aristocracy', which hesitates between class struggle and class collaboration, or the 'cadres', a category of bureaucratic statistics, whose nominal, doubly negative unity conceals its real dispersion both from the interested parties' and from their opponents and most observers, the laying down of boundaries between the classes is inspired by the strategic aim of counting in' or 'being counted in', 'cataloguing' or ‘annexing', when it is not the simple recording of a legally guaranteed state of the power relation between the classified groups.

Leaving aside all cases in which the statutory imposition of an arbitrary boundary (such as a 30-kilo limit on baggage or the rule that a vehicle over two tons is a van) suffices to eliminate the dificulties chat arise from the sophism of the heap of grain,4 boundaries even the most formallooking ones, such as those between age-groups--do indeed freeze a par ticular state of social struggles, i.e., a given stare of the distribution of advantages and obligations, such as the right to pensions or cheap fares, compulsory schooling or military service. And if we are amused by Al. phonse Allais's stoty of the father who pulls the communication cord to srop the train at the very moment his child becomes three years old (and so needs a ticket to travel), it is because we immediarely see the sociological absurdity of an imaginary variation which is as impeccably logical as those on which logicians base their beloved paradoxes. Here the limits are frontiers to be attacked or defended with all one's strength, and the classifcatory systems which fx them are not so much means of know)- edge as means of power, harnessed to social functions and overtly or covertly aimed at satisfying the interests of a group.

A number of ethical, aesthetic, psychiatric or forensic classifications that are produced by the institutional sciences', not to mention those produced and inculcated by the educational sysrem, are similarly subordinated to social functions, although they derive their specific efhicacy from their apparent neutrality. They are produced in accordance with the specific logic, and in the spccific language, of relatively autonomous fields, and they combine a real dependence on the classificatory schemes of the dominant habitus (and ultimately on the social structures of which these are the product) with an apparent independence. The latter enahles them to help to legitimate a particular state of the classification struggle and the class struggle. Perhaps the most typical example of these semi-autonomous systems of classifcation is the system of adjectives which underpins scholastic ‘appreciations'.1

Commonplaces and classificatory systems are thus the stake of struggles between the groups they characrerize and counterpose, who fight over them while striving to turn them to their own advantage. Georges Duby shows how the model of the three orders, which fixed a state of the social structure and aimed to make it permanent by codifying it, was able to be used simultaneously and successively by antagonistic groups: first by the bishops, who had devised it, againsr the heretics, the monks and the knights; then by the aristocracy, against the bishops and the king; and finally by the king, who, by setting himself up as the absolute subject of the classifying operarion, as a principle external and superior to the classes it generated (unlike the three orders, who were subjects but also bjects, judges but als parties), assigned each group its place in the s cial order, and established himself as an unassailable vantage-point. in the same way it can be shown that the schemes and commonplaces which ptovide images of the different forms of domination, the opposition be. tween the sexes and age-groups as well as the opposition berween the generations, are similarly manipulated. The young' can accept the dehnition that their elders offer them, take advantage of the temporary licence they are allowed in many societies ('Youth must have its Aing'), do whar is assigned to them, revel in the 'specific virtues' of youth, virtù, virility, enthusiasm, and get on with their own business-knight-errantry for dlie scions of the mediaeval aristocracy." love and violence for che yourh of Renaissance Florence, and every form of regulared, ludic wildness (spore rock erc.) for contemporary adolescentsin short. allow themselves be kept in the state ofyouth', that is, irresponsibiliry, enjoying the fre. dom of irresponsible behav iourin return for renouncing responsibiliry.'º In situations of specific crisis, when che order of successions is threatened, 'young people', refusing to remain consigned ro 'youth', tend to consign the 'old' to 'old age'. Wanting to cake the responsibilities which dehne adults (in che sense of socialy complere persons), rhey must push rhe holders of responsibiliriesinto that form of irresponsibility which defines old age, or rather retirement. The wisdom and prudence claimed by the elders then collapse into conservarism, archaism or, quire simply, senile irresponsibiliry. The newcomers, who are likely to be also the biologically youngest, bur who bring with them many other distinctive properries, stemming from changes in the social conditions of production of the producers (i.e., principally' the family and the educational system), escape the more rapidly from youth' (irresponsibility) the readier they are ro break with the irresponsible behaviour assigned to them and, freeing themselves from the internalized limits (those which may make a 50. year-old feel 'too young reasonably to aspire' to a position or an honour), d o nothesitare to push forward, 'leap frog' and 'take the escalator'to precipirate cheir predecessors' fall inro the past, the outdared, in short, social dearh. But they have no chance of winning the struggles over the limits which break our berween the age-groups when the sense of the limits is lost, unless they manage ro impose a new definition of the socially complete person, including in it characteristics normally (i.e., in terms of the prevailing classificatory principle) associared with youth (enthusiasm, energy and so on) or chara.cteristics that can supplant the virtues normally associared with adulthood.

In short, what individuals and groups invesr in the particular meaning they give ro common classificatory systems by the use they make of them is infinitely more than their 'inrerest' in the usual sensc of the term; it is their whole social being, everything which defines their own idea of rhemselves, the primordial, tacit contract whereby they define us' as op posed to 'them', 'other people', and which is rhe basis of the exclusions ('not for the likes of us) and inclusions they perform among the charac teristics produced by the common classificatory system.

arhereby agents constsuct cheir perception of realicy, in particular the process of accentuating dif crences vis-à-vis oursiders' (dissimilarion) and reinforcing similarities wirh insiders (assimilarion). Similarly, studies of racism have shown that whenever diff erent groups are juxraposed, a defnition of che approved, valorized behaviour rends to be contrasted with the despised, rejected behaviour of the other group. Social identity lies in dif. ference, and difference is asserted against whar is closest, which tepresenes the grearest threac.
