Analysis of stereoryping, the propensiry to assume a cortcspondence between membership of a caregory (e.g., Nordic or Mediterra. nean, Western or Oriental) and possession of a particular properry, so thar knowledge of a person's category strongly inftuences judgements of him, is in line ith analysis of rhar sort of social stereotyping in which alì the members of a social formation cend to concur in atcribucing cerrain properries to membets of the different social classes (see appendix 4).

The fact that, in their relationship to the dominant classcs, the dominated classes attribute to themselves strength in the sense of labour power and fighting strength—physical strength and also sttength of characrer, courage, manlinessdoes not prevent the dominant groups from similarly conceiving the relationship in terms of rhe scheme strong/weak; but they reduce the strength which the dominated (or the young, or women) ascribe to themselves to brure strength, passion and instincr, a blind, unpredicrable force of nature, the unrcasoning violence of desire, and they attribure to themselves spiritual and intellectual strength, a self-control that ptedisposes them to control others, a strength of soul or spirit which allows them to conceive their relationship to the dominated—the 'masses', women, the young—as thar of the soul to rhe body, understanding to sensibility, culture to nature.

## The Classification Struggle

Principles of division, inextricably logical and sociological, function within and for the purposes of the seruggle berwcen social groups; in ptoducing concepts, they produce groups, the very groups which pro. duce rhe principles and the groups against which they are produced. Whar is at stake in the struggles about rhe meaning of the social world is power over the classificatory schemes and systems which are the basis of the representations of the groups and rherefore of their mobilization and demobilizarion: the evocative power of an urterance which puts things in a different light (as happens, for example, when a single word, such as 'paternalism, changes the whole experience of a social relationship) or which modifes rhe schemes of perception, shows something else, ocher properties, previously unnoticed or relegared ro the background (such as common interests hitherto masked by ethnic or national differences); a separative power, a disrinction, diacrisis, discretio, drawing discrete units out of indivisible continuity, difference out of che undifferentiared.

Only in and through rhe srruggle do the internalized limits become boundaries, barriers thar have to be moved. And indeed, the system f classificatory schemes is constituted as an objectified, institutionalized system of classifcation only when it has ceased to function as a sense of limits so that che guardians of the established order must enunciate, sys rematize and codify the principles of production of chat order, both real and represented, so as to defend them against heresy; in short, they must constiture rhe doxa as orthodoxy. Official systems of classification, such as the theory of the three orders, do explicitly and systematically what the classificarory schemes did racirly and practically. Attribures, in the sense of predicares. thereby become altributions, powers, capaciries, privileges, prerogatives, attributed to the holder of a post, so that war is no longer what the warrior does, but the officium, the specific hinction, the raison d'êrre, of the bellator. Classificatory disretio, like law, freezes a certain srate of the power relations which ir aims to fix forever by enunciaring andcodifying it. The classificatory system as a principle of logicat and political division only exists and functions because it reproduces, in a transhgured form, in the symbolic logic of differential gaps, i.e., of discontinuity, the generally gradual and continuous differences which strucrure the established order; but it makes its own, that is, specifically symbolic, contribution to the maintenance of thar order only because it has the specifically symbolic power to make people see and believe which is given by the imposition of mental strucrutes.

Systems of classihc ation would not be such a decisive object of struggle if they did not contribute to the existence of classes by enhancing the ef. ficacy of the objective mechanisms with the reinforcement supplied by representations structured in accordance with the classification. The imposirion of a recognized name is an act of recognition of full social existence which transmutes the thing named. It no longer exists merely de facto, as a tolerated, iltegal or illegitimate practice, but becomes a social funcrion, i.e., a mandate, a mission (Beruf ), a task, a role—all words which express rhe difference berween authorizcd activity, which is as signed to an individual or group by racit or explicit delegarion, and mere usurpation, which creates a 'srate of affairs' awaiting instirutionalization. But the specific effect of collective represenrations', which, contrary to what the Durkheimian connorations might suggest, may be the product of rhe applicarion of the same scheme of perception or a common system of classification white still being subject to antagonistic social uses, is most clearly scen when the word precedes the thing, as with vołuatary associations thar rurn into recognized ptofessions or corporate defence groups (such as the trade union of te cadres'), which progressively impose the representation of their existence and their unicy, both on their own members and on other groups.

A group's presence or absence in the ofácial classification depends on its capacity to ger itself recognized, co get itself nociced and admitted, and so to win a place in the social order. It thus escapes from the shad. owy existence of the 'nameless crafts' of which Emile Benveniste speaks: business in antiquity and the Middle Ages, or illegitimate activities, such as those of the modern healer (formerly called an empiric'), bone-serrer or prosticure. The face of groups is bound up with the words that designare them: the power to impose recognition depends on the capacity to mobilixe around a name, 'proletariat', 'working class', 'cadres' etc., to appropriate a common name and to commune in a proper name, and so to mobilize che union that makes them strong, around the unifying power ofaword.22

In fact, the order of words never exactly reproduces the order of things. It is the relative independence of rhe structure of che system of classifying, classifcd words (within which the distinct value of cach par. cicular label is defined) in relarion to the strucrure of the distribution of capital, and more precisely, it is the time-lag(partly resulting from the inertia inherent in classifcation systems as quasi-legalinstitutions sanctioning a state of a power relation) berween changes in jobs, linked to changes in the productive appararus, and changes in titles, which creates the space for symbolic strategies aimed at exploiring the discrepancies between the nominal and the real, appropriating words so as to ger the things they designate, or appropriating things while waiting to get the words that sanction chem; exercising responsibilities without having entitlement to do so, in order to acquire the right to claim the legitimate titles, or, conversely, declining the material advaatages associared with devalued titles so as to avoid losing the symbolic advantages bestowed by more prestigious labels or, at least, vaguer and more manipulable ones; donning the most flarrering of the available insignia, verging on impos. cure if need be-like the porters who call themselves art craftsmen', or technicians who claim to be engineers—or inventing new labels, like physiotherapists (kinésithérapeutes) who count on this new citle to separate them from mere masseurs and bring them closer to doctors. All these strategies, like all processes of competition, a paper-chase aimed at ensuring constant distinctive gaps, tend to produce a sready inflarion of rirlesrestrained by the inertia of the institutionalized taxonomies (col. lective agreements, salary scales etc.)—to which legal guarantees are attached. The negotiarions berween antagonistic interest groups, which arise from the establishment of cotlective agreemenrs and which concern, inseparably, the tasks entailed by a given job, the propercies required of irs occupanrs (e.g., diplomas) and the corresponding advantages, boch material and symbolic (the name), are an instirucionalized, thearrical version of the incessant struggles over the classifcations which help to produce che classes, although these classifcations are the product of the struggles berween the classes and depend on the power relations berween them

## The Reality of Representation and the Representation of Reality

The classifying subjects who classify the propetties and practices of orhers, or cheir own, are also classifable objects which classify themselves (in che eyes of others) by appropriating practices and properries that are already classified (as vulgar or disringuished, high or low, heavy or light erc.—in orher words, in the lasr analysis, as popular or bourgeois) according ro cheir probable distriburion berween groups char are chem. selves classihed. The most classifying and besr classified of these properries are, of course, chose which are overtly designared to function as signs of distincrion or marks of infamy, stigmara, especially the names and cicles expressing class membership whose intersection defines social identity at any given rime—che name of a nation, a region, an erhnic group, a family name, the name of an occuparion, an educarional qualifcarion, honorific citles and so on. Those who classify chemselves or orhers, by appropriating or classifying practices or properties that are classified and classifying, cannor be unaware thar, chrough disrinctive ob jects or pracrices in which rheir 'powers' are expressed and which, being appropriared by and appropriare to classes, classify rhose who appropriare chem, they classify chemselves in the eyes of other classifying (but also classifiable) subjects, endowed with classificatoty schemes analogous co those which enable chem more or less adequately to anticipate their own classification.

Social subjects comprehend the social worlld which comprehends chem. This means that rhey cannor be characterized simply in terms of marerial properties, srarting with the body, which can be counted and measured like any other object in the physical worlld. In fact, each of rhese properties, be it the height or volume of cthe body or the exrent of landed properry, when perceived and appreciared in relarion to other properties of rhe same class by agents equipped with socially constirured schemes of perceprion and appreciation, functions as a symbolic properry. It is therefore necessary to move beyond che opposirion between a 'social physics'—which uses staristics in objectivist fashion to esrabłish distriburions (in borh che sratistical and economic senses), quantifed expres. sions of rhe diff erential appropriarion of a finite quantity of social energy by a large number of compering individuals, idenrified chrough 'objecrive indicators'—and a ‘social semiology'which seeks ro decipher meanings and bring to light che cognitive operations whereby agenrs produce and decipher them. We have to refuse the dichoromy berween, on the one hand, the aim of arriving ar an objective 'realiry', independent ofindividual consciousnesses and wills', by breaking wich common represenrations of the social world (Durkheim's 'pre-notions'), and of uncovering 'laws'--thar is, significant (in the sense of non-random) relacionships berween distriburions—and, on the other hand, the aim of grasping, nor reality', bur agents'representations of it, which are the whole 'realiry of a social world conceived 'as will and representation'.

In short, social science does not have to choose berween char form of social physics, represenred by Durkheim—who agrees with social semiology in acknowledging rhat one can only know 'realiry' by applying logical instruments of classification-and the idealist semiology which, underraking to construct 'an account of accounrs', as Harold Garhnkel purs it, can do no more than record che recordings of a social world which is ultimarely no more than the product of mental, i.e., linguistic, srructures. Whar we have to do is to bring into the science of scarcity, and of comperition for scarce goods, the practical knowledge which che agenrs obrain for themselves by producing—on che basis of cheir experience of che discributions, irselfdependenton cheir position in the distributionsdivisionsand classificarions which are no lessobjectivethan chose of the balance-sheers of social physics. In other words, we have to move beyond theoppositionbetween objecrivist theories which identify the social classes (bur also rhe sex or age classes) with discrete groups, simple countabie popularions separated by boundaries objectively drawn in realiry, and subjectivist (or marginalist) cheories which reduce the so. cial order' to a sort of collective classification obrained by aggregaring the individual classifications or, more precisely, rhe individual strategies, classified and classif ying, through which agents class rhemselves and orhers.24

One only has to bear in mind thar goods are converted into disrinctive signs, which may be signs of disrinction but also of vulgarity, as soon as chey are perceived relationally, to see thar the representation which indi. viduals and groups inevirably project through their pracrices and proper. ties is an integral parr of social realiry. A class is defined as much by irs being- perceived as by irs being, by its consumption—which need nor be conspicuous in order to be symbolic—as much as by its position in the rela. tions of producrion (even if it is crue chat rhe latrer governs the former). The Berkeleian--i.e., perit-bourgeois--vision which reduces social being to perceived being, ro seeming, and which, forgerting char there is no need to give rhearrical performances (représenlalions) in order to be rhe object of mencal represencarions, reduces rhe social world to che sum of che (mencal) represenrations which the various groups have of the theatrical performances pur on by the orher groups, has che vitrue of msisring on che relarive auronomy of che logic of symbolic reprsentarions wich respect to rhe marerial determinants of socio-economic condirion. The individual or cotlective classifcarion struggles aimed ar cransformitig the caregories of perception and appreciarion of che social world and, chrough chis, rhe social world irself, are indeed a forgorcen dimension of the class struggle. But one only has ro realize rhar che classificatory schemes which underlie agents' practical relarionship to their condition and the represencarion they have ofit are themselves the product of thar condition, in order to see the limits of chis auronomy. Posirion in rhe classification struggle depends on position in the class structure; and so cial subjects—inciuding intellecruals, who are not those best placed to grasp that which defnes the limits of their chought of the social world that is, che illusion of rhe absence of limirs—are perhaps never lesslikely to ttanscend 'rhe limits of their minds' than in che representation they have and give of their posicion, which defines rhose limits.

# Postscript: Towards a 'Vulgar'Critique of 'Pure' Critiques

T'he reader may have wondered why, in a text devoted to taste and art, no appeal is made to the tradition of philosophical or licerary aesthetics; and he or she will no doubt have realized that this is a deliberare refusal

It is certain that the 'high' aeschetic, borh that which is engaged in a practical form in legitimate works and that which is expressed in writings incended to make it explicit and present it formally, is fundamencally constiruted, whatever the variants, against all chat chis research may have established-namely, the indivisibilicy of caste, the unity of the most 'pure' and most purihied, che most sublime and most sublimared casres, and the most 'impure' and 'coarse', ordinary and primirive castes. This means, conversely, that chis project has required, above all, a sort of deliberate amnesia, a readiness to renounce the whole corpus of cultivated discourse on culture, which implies renouncing not only the profits secured by exhibiting signs of recognition but also che more intimare profies of erudite gratification, rhose Proust refers to when he indicates how much his lucid vision of the pleasures of reading has cost him: I have had to struggle here with my dearest aescheric impressions, endeavouring to push intellectuaì honesty to its ulimate, cruellest limits'- without being able to ignore that the pleasures of lucid vision' may rep resent the purest' and most refined, albeit often somewhat morose, form of enjoyment.'

And if we must now allow the 'retum of rhe repressed', having produced che cruch of che taste against which, by an immense repression, the whole of legitimare aesthetics has been constructed, rhis is not only in order to subject che truths won to a final cest (though it is not a question of comparing and contrasting' rival theories), but also in order to pre. vent the absence of direct confrontation from allowing the two dis. courses to coexist peacefully as parallel aiternatives, in two carefully separated universes of thought and discourse.

## Disgust at the 'Facile'

'Pure' taste and the acsthetics which provides its theory are founded on a refusal of 'impure' taste and of aistbesis (sensation), the simple, Primitive form of pleasure reduced toa pleasure of the senses, as in what Kant calls 'che caste of the tongue, the palate and the throat', a surrender to imme. diare sensation which in another order looks like imprudence. Ar the risk of seeming to indulge in the 'facile effects' which pure raste' stigmatizes. it could be shown that the whole language ofacsthecics is contained in a fundamental sefusal of che facile, in all the meanings which bourgeois erhics and aesthierics give to the word;² thar pu re taste', purely negarive in its essence, is based on the disgust thar is ofren called 'visccral' (it 'makes one sick' or 'makes one vomit') for everything that is 'facile'—fac. ile music, or a facile stylistic effect, but also'easy virtue' oran easy lay'. The refusal of w hat is casy in che sense of simple, and chcrefore shallow, and 'cheap', beca use it is casily decoded and culturally 'undemanding', naturally leads to the refusal of what is facile in the et hical or aest hetic sense, of everything which offers pleasures that are too immediately accessible and so discredited as 'childish' or primitive' s opposed to the deferred pleasures of legitimare art). Thus people speak of facile cfects' to characterize the obtrusive elegance of a certain style of journalistic writ. ing or rhe too insisrenr, too predicta ble charm of what is called 'lighe' music (a word whose connorations virrually correspond to those of'facile'—consider 'easy listening') or cerrain performances of classical music; thus a critic denounces the 'vulgar sensuality'or 'Casbah orientalism' which reduces one interpreration of the 'I)ance of the Seven Veils' in Strauss'sSalome tocabaret music'. 'Vulgar'works, as the words used to describethem indicate-facile' or 'light', of course, bur also frivolous', 'futile', 'shallow', 'superhcial', 'showy', 'flashy', 'mercrricious', or, in the iegister of oral satisfactions, 'syrupy', 'sugary', 'tosc-water', 'schmaltzy', 'cloying'are not only a sort of insult to refnement, a slap in the face to a demanding' (difficile) audience which will nor stand for facile' offerings (it is a compliment to an artist, especiaily a conductor, to say he 're spects his audience'); they arouse disraste and disgust by the methods of scduction, usually denounced as 'low', 'degrading', 'demeaning', which they ry to use, giving the spectaror the sense of being treated like any Tom, Dickor Harry who can be seduced by tawdry charms which invite him to regress to the most primitive and elementary forms of pleasure, whether chey be the passive satisfactions of the infantile taste for sweet liquids ('syrupy') or the quasi-animal gratifications of sexual desire. One might evoke the Platonic prejudice, endlessly reaffrmed, in favour of the 'noble senses', vision and hearing, or the primacy Kant gives to form, which is more 'pure', over colour and its quasi-catnal seduction.

But it will suffice to quote a quite exemplary text in which Schopenhauer establishes an opposition between the 'sublime' and the 'charming' identical to the one Kant makes in the Critique of fudgemens berween 'pleasure' and 'enjoyment', the 'beaurifıl' and the 'agreeable', 'thar which pleases' and 'that which gratifes'. Schopenhauer dehnes the 'charming or attracrive' as that which 'excites the wil by presenting to it directly its fulfilment, its sarisfaction', thar which 'draws che beholder away from the pure contemplarion which is demanded by all apprehension of the beautiful, because it necessarily excites this witl, by objects which directly ap. peal to it'; and, significantly, he condemns simulcaneously the two forms of satisfaction, oral and sexual, against which the satisfacrion recognized as specifically acsthecic is to be constiruted: The one species [of the charming], a very low one, is found in Durch paintings of still life, when they err by representing articles of food, which by their deceptive likeness necessarily excite the appetite for the things they represent, and chis is just an excitement of the will, which puts an end to all acstheric con. templarion of the object. Painted fruit is yer admissible, because we may regard it as the further development of the fower, and as a beautiful product of narure in form and colour, without being obliged ro think of it as earable; but unfortunarely we often find, represented with deceptive naturalness, prepared and served dishes, oysters, herrings, crabs, bread and burrer, beer, wine, and so forth, which is altogether to be condemned. In historical painting and in sculpture the charming consists in naked fig. ures, whose position, drapery, and general treatment are calculared to ex. cite the passions of the beholder, and thus pure aestherical contemplation is at once annihilared, and the aim of art is defeated.'

Schopenhauer is here very close to Kant, and to all aesrhetics in which, in a rationalized form, the erhos of the dominared fraction of the dominant class is expressed; as he so well purs it, the 'charming', which reduces the 'pure knowing subject', 'freed from subjectivity and its impure desires' to a 'willing subject, subject to every desire, every servirude', exerts real violence on the beholder. Indccent and exhibitionist, it cap. rures the body by its rhychm, which is arruned to bodily rhychms, and captivares the mind by the deceprions of its plots, suspense and surprises, forcing on it a real participation which is quite opposed to the 'distance' and disinteresredness' of pure rasre, and bound to appear as out of place as on Quixore when, carried away by real anger at a fictitious scandal, he assaults Master Pedro's puppers.

rent, distant, highly rirualized, with obligatory applause, and even shouts of enthusiasm, at the end, or even perfectly silent (concerts in churches). Jazz, a bourgcois entertainment which mimics popular entcrtainmerıt, is only an apparent exception: the signs of participation (hand-clapping or foorcapping) are limired to a silent skerch of rhe gesture (a: leasr in free jazz).

## The Taste of Reflection' and the 'Taste of Sense'

Whar pure taste refuses is indeed che violence to which rhe popular specrator consents (one thinks of Adomo's description of popular music and its effects); it demands respect, the disrance which allows it to keep its distance. It expects the work of art, a finality with no other end rhan itself, to treat the spectaror in accordance with the Kantian imperative, that is, as an end, nor a means. Thus, Kant's principle of pure casre is nothing other than a refusal,\* a disgust--a disgust for objects which im. pose enjoyment and a disgust for the crude, vulgar taste which revels in this imposed enjoyment: 'One kind of ugliness alone is incapable of being represented conformably to narure withour destroying all aestheric delight, and consequently arristic beauty, namely, that which excites dis. guss. For, as in chis strange sensation, which depends purely on the imaginarion, che object is represented as insisring, as it were, on our enjoying it, while we still set our face against it, the artificial represencation of the object is no longer distinguishable from the narure of the object itself in our sensation, and so it cannot possibly be regarded as beauriful' (pp. 174-175).9

Disgust is che paradoxical experience of enjoyment extorted by vio. lence, an enjoyment which arouses horror. This horror, unknown to those who surrender to sensation, results fundamentally from removal of the distance, in which freedom is asserted, between- rhe represencarion and che ching represenced, in short, from alienation, the loss of che sub. jecr in the object, immediare submission to che immediare present under the enslaving violence of the 'agreeable'. Thus, in contrast to the inclina. tion aroused by the ‘agreeable', which, unlike beauty, is common to humans and animals (p. 49), is capable of seducing those who are always intent only on enjoyment' (p. 45; also 47, 117) and 'immediately satisfies rhe senses'—whereas it is 'mediarely displeasing'co reason (p. 47)‘º 'pure taste', che 'rasre of reflection'(p. S4) which is opposed ro che 'rasre of sense' as charms' are opposed to 'form' (pp. 65, 67), must exclude inrerest and must nor 'be in the least prepossessed in favour of the real existence of the object' (p. 43).

which allows the author to rcmain silent as to che condirions of realizarion of what is in face a performarive urterance. Some cypical examples: 'We say of a man who remains unatTccted in che presence of whac we consider sublime. chat he has no fecling. We demand both taste and feeling of every man, and, granted some degree of culture, we give him credit for both' (p. ll6); 'Every judgement which is to show the tasre of the individual is required to be an independent judgement of the individual himself' (p. 137); 'I take my stand on the ground chat my judgement is to be one of taiste, and not one of understanding or reason'(p. 140); 'finc art must be frec art in a double sense.' (p. 185).

The objecr which ‘insists on being enjoyed', as an image and in reality, infleshand blood, neutra.lizes both ethical resistance and aescheric neucralizarion; it annihilares che distanciaring power of representation, che essentially human power of suspending immediate, animal artachment to the sensible and refusing submission to che pure affecr, to simple aischesis. In the face of this twofold challenge to human freedom and to cul. cure (the anti-narure), disgust is rhe ambivalent experience of the horrible seduction of the disgusring and of enjoyment, which performs a sort of reduction to animality, corporcality, the belly and sex, char is, to whar is common and therefore vulgar, removing any difference between chosc who resist wich all cheir might and chose who wallow in pleasure, who enjoy enjoyment: Common human understanding ... has rhe doubtful honour of having che name of common sense ... bestowed upon it; and bestowed, too, in an acceptarion of the word common (not merely in our language, where it acrually has a double meanimg, bur also in many ochers) which makes it amount to what is vulgar (das Vulgare)—whar is everywhere to be met wich-a qualiry which by no means confers credit or distincrion upon its possessor'(p. 151). Nature under. stood as sense cqualizes, but at the lowest level (an carly version of the 'levelling-down' abhorred by the Heideggerians). Arisrorle taught thar different chings differentiate themselves by whar makes them similar, i.e., a common character; in Kant's cext, disgust discovers with horror the common animalicy on which and against which motal distinction is construcred: 'We regard as coarse and low the habits of thought of those who have no feeling for beautiful narure . . . and who devore themselves to che mere enjoymenrs of sense found in earing and drmking' (p. 162).

Elsewhere Kant quite directly srates the social basis of che opposition berween che 'tasce of reflcction' and rhe 'caste of sense': 'In che beginning. the novice must have been guided by instinct alone, thar voice of God which is obeyed by all animals. This permitted some things to be used for nourishment, while forbidding others. Here it is not neccssary to assume a special instinct which is now lost. It could simply have been the sense of smell, plus its afinity with the organ of taste and the well-known relation of the latcer to the organs of digestion: in short an ability, perceiv. able even now, to sense, prior to che consumplion of a cerrain foodsruff, whether or not it is fit for consumption. It is not even necessary to as. sume chat chis sensitivity was keener in the first pair than it is now. For ic is a familiar enough fact that men wholly absorbed by their senses have much greater perceptive powers than those who. occupied with thoughts as well as with the senses, are to a degree turned away from the sen. suous." We recognisxe here the ideological mechanism which works by describing the terms of the opposition one establishes berween the social classes as stages in an evolution (here, the progress from nature to cul. rure).

Thus, although it consistently refuses anything resembling an empirical psychological or sociological genesis of taste (e.g, pPp. 89, 116), each time invoking the magical division between che transcendental and the empirical,2 the theory of pure taste is grounded in an empirical social re lation, as is shown by the opposition it makes berween the agrecable (which 'does nor cultivate' and is only an enjoyment—p. 165) and cul. ture,' or irs allusions to the teaching and educability of raste.' The antithesis between culture and bodily pleasure (or narure) is roored in the opposition berween the cultivated bourgeoisie and the people,'" the imaginary sire of uncultivated narure, barbarously wallowing in pure en. joyment: 'Taste that requires an added element of charm and emotion fot its delight, nor to speak of adopting this as the measure of its approval, has not yet emerged from barbarism'(p. 6s).

If one follows through all the implications of an aesthetic which, in accord. ance with the logic of Kant's 'Essay on Negative Magnitudes', has to mea. sure virtue by the magnitude of the vices overcome and pure taste by the intensity of the impulse denied and the vulgarity refused, then the most accomplished art has to be recognized in those works which carry the anti. chesis of civilized barbarism, conrained impulse, subtimared coarseness, to the highest degree of tension. This criterion would point to Mahler, who went further than any orher composer in the dangerous game of facilicy and every form of high-cultural recuperarion of the 'popular ares'or even 'schmaltz'; and, eatlier, Becthoven, whose recognizedl greainess is measured by the negarive magnitude of the violences, extravagances and excesses, ofren celebrated by hagiography, which artistic rescraint, a kind of 'mourning', has had to overcome. T'he inhibition of too immediately accessible pleasure, initially the pre-condition for the experience of ‘pure' pleasure, can even become a source of pleasure in icself: refinement can lead to a culriva. tion, for its own sake, of Freud's 'preliminary pleasure', an ever increasing defermenr of the resolution of tension, with, for exa mple, a growing distance berween the dissonant chord and its full or conventional resolurion Thus the 'purest' form of the acsthete's pleasure, aisthesis purified, subli. mated and denied, may, paradoxically, consist in an asctticism. aseesis, a trained, sustained rension, which is the very opposite of primary, primitive aisthesis.

Pure pleasureascetic, empty pleasure which implies the renunciation of pleasure, pleasure purified of pleasureis predisposed to become a symbolof moral excelience, and the work of art a test of echical su perioriry, an indispurable measure of the capacity for sublimarion which defines the truly human man.16 What is at Srake in aesthetic discourse, and in the attempted imposition of a defnition of the genuinely human, is nothing less than themonopoly of humanity.1 Art is called upon to mark the difference berween humans and non-humans: artistic experience, a free imitation of natural creations, nalura naturans, wherebythe artist (and through him, the beholder) affirms his transcendence of natura nasurata by ptoducing 'a second nature' (p. 176) subject only to the laws of creative genius (p. 171), is the closest approach to the divine experience of iniuitus originarius, the creative perception which freely engendeis its own object without recognizing any rules or constraints other than its own (p. 168). The world produced by artistic 'creation' is not only 'another nature' but a counter-nature', a world produced in the manner of nature but against the ordinary laws of narure—those of gravity in dance, those of desire and pleasure in painting and sculpture etc.--by an act of artistic sublimation which is predisposed to fulfiì a function of social legitimation. The negation of enjoyment—inferior, coarse, vulgar, mercenary, venaì, servile, in a word, natural--implies affirmation of the sublimity of those who can be satisfied with sublimated, refned, distinguished, disintetested, gratuitous, free pleasures. The opposition between the tastes of nature and the castes of freedom introduces a relationship which is that of the body to the soul, between those who are only natu ral' and those whose capacity to dominate their own biological narute affirms their legitimare claim to dominate social nature. No wonder, then, that, as Mikhail Bakhtin has pointed out apropos of Rabelais, the popu. lar inagination can only invert the relationship which is the basis of the aesthetic sociodicy: responding to sublimarion by a sirategy of reducrion or degradarion, as in slang, parody, burlesque or caricature, using obscenity or scatology to turn arsy-versy, head over heels, all the 'values’ in which the dominant groups project and recognize their sublimity, it rides roughshod over difference, fouts distincrion, and, like the Carnival games, reduces the distinctive pleasures of the soul to the common satisfactions of food and sex.18

## A Denied Social Relationship

The theory of beauty as the absolute creation of artifex deus, enabling every man (worthy of the name) to mimic the divine act of creation, is no doubt the 'narural' expression of the occupational ideology of those who like to call themselves 'creators', which explaios why, even without any direct infuence, it has constantly been reinvented by artists, ftom leonardo da Vinci, who made the artist 'the master of all things', to Pau)

Klee, who aimed to creat as nature does.'9 And—quite apart from its clear relationship with the antithesis berween the two forms of aesthetic pleasure, and through rhis, with the opposition between the cultured élite' and the barbarous masses—the opposition Kant establishes between 'free art', 'which is agrccable on its own account' and whose prod. uct is freedom-since it is agreeable on its own account and in no way constrains the beholder2o-and 'mercenary art', a servile activiry 'only accractive by means of what it results in (e.g-, the pay), which is consequently capable of bemg a compulsoty imposition' (p. 164), whose product forces itself on the behotder with the enslaving violence of its sensible charms, very directly expresses Kant's-conception of che position of pure' or ‘autonomous inrelectuals in the division of labour, and more precisely in the division of intellectual labour. These 'pure' intellectuals are, according to The Conflict of the Faculties, none other than philosophy professors,2 but attists and writers would no doubt be the purest of all. 'The Critique of /ud gement is less remote than it seems from the 'Idea for a Universal History', which has rightly been seen as the expression of the sublirnated interests of the bourgeois intelligentsia; this intellectual bourgeoisie, 'whose legitimation', as Norbert Elias puts it, 'consists primarily in its intellectual, scientifc or artistic accomplishments', occupies an uncomfortable position in social space, entirely homologous to that of the modern intelligentsia: 'an elite in the eyes of the people, it has a lower rank in the eyes of the courtly aristocracy."2

A number of the oddities of Kant's text are explained once it is seen thar the second term of the fundamental opposition between pleasure and enjoyment is twofold; the ethical purity of the pleasure of culture is defined not only against the barbarism of enjoyment but also against the heteronomous enjoyment of Civilization: To a high degree we are, chrough art and science, cultured We are civilized--perhaps too much for our own goodin all sorts of social grace and decorum. But to consider ourselves as having reached morality—for that, much is lacking. The ideał of morality belongs to culture; its use for some simulacrum of moralicy in the love of honour and outward decorum constitutes mere civilizacion.'²3

Kant casts into the darkness of the 'empirical' 'the interest indirectly actached to the beauciful by the inclinarion towards society' that is produced by the process of Civilization, although this 'rehned inclination' giving no satisfaction of enjoyment is as close as possible to pure pleasure. The negation of nature lcads as much to che perversion of'unneces sary inclinations' as to the pure morality of aesthetic pleasure: 'Reason has this peculiarity that, aided by the imagination, it can create artifcial desires which are not only unsupported by natural instinct but actually contrary to it. These desires, in the beginning called concupiscence, gradually generate a whole host of unnecessary and indeed unnatural inclinations called luxuriousness.'

Tbe counter-nature’ proves ambiguous: civilization is bad, culrure good. The difference between heteronomous, exlernal, caivilized pleasure and culrivated pleasure, which presupposes 'slow efforts to improve the mind' (langsame Bemihung der inneren Bildung der Denkungsart),2 can only be resolved, for Kant, on the terrain of ethics, i.e., of the dererminants of aesthetic pleasure, external and pathological on one side, purely incernal on che ocher.

This pure acschetic is indeed the rationalization of an ethos: pure pleasure, plesure cotally purifed of all sensuous or sensible interest, perfectly free of all social or fashionable interest, as remote from concupiscence as it is from conspicuous consumption, is opposed as much to che refned, alcruistic enjoyment of the courtier as it is to the crude, animaì enjoyment of the people. Nothing in the content of this typically professorial aesthetic could stand in the way of its being recognized as universal by its sole ordinary readers, the professors of philosophy,2 who were too concerned with hunting down historicism and sociologism to see the hisrorical and social coincidence which, here as in so many cases, is the basis of their illusion of universality.28 And the formalization which is required in order for social impulses and interests to be expressed within the limits of the censorship of a particular form of social propriety can only help to encourage this illusion, so that a discourse which makes art a criterion of an ethical and aesthetic distinction which is a misrecognized form of social difference can be read as a universal expression of the universality of art and aesthetic experience.

Totally ahistorical, like all philosophical thought that is worthy of the name (every philoso phia worth its salt is perennis)--perfectly ethnocentric, since it takes for its sole datum the lived experience of a bomo aestheticus who is none other than the subject of aesthetic discourse constitured as the universal subject of aesthetic experience—Kant's analysis of the judgement of raste fnds its real basis in a set of aesthetic principles which are the universalization of the dispositions associated with a particular social and economic condition. But it would be a mistake ro see only a formal mask in all the features which the formalized discourse owes to the effort to resolve the problems raised by the theoretical division and conceptual distinctions worked out in the other Critiquer and to express 'the chinking of Immanuel Kant’ in accordance wich the discursive schemes constituting what is called 'Kantian rhought'.9 Since we know that the very principle of the symbolic effcacy of phitosophical discourse lies in che play berween rwo structures of discourse which rhe work of formalization seeks to integrate without enrirely succeeding, it would be naive to reduce the truth of this double discourse to rhe subterranean discourse in which the Kantian ideology of the beautiful is expressed and which analysis reconstitutes by reconnecting the web of notarions blured by the interferences of che structures. The secial categories of aesthetic judgement can only function, for Kant himself and for his readers, in the form of highly sublimated categories, such as the oppositions zation, euphemisms which, wirhour any conscious intention of dissi rm. laring, enable social opposirions to be expressed and experienced in form conforming to the norms of expression of a specic held. Whar is hidden, thar is, the double social relarionship to the court (the site of civilization as opposed to culture) and to the people (the sire of nature and sense)—is borh present and absent; it presents itself in the text in such a guise tht one can in all good faith not see it there and chat the naively reductive rcading, which would reduce Kant's text to the social relarionship rhat is disguised and transfigured within it, wouuld be no less false than the ordinary reading which would reduce it ro the phenomenal truth in which it appears only in disguise.

## Parerga and Paralipomena

There is perhaps no more decisive way of manif esting the social mecha. nisms which lead ro the denial of the real principles of the judgement of taste (and their re-denial in all faithful readings) than to see them at work in a commentary intended (apparently, at least) to manifest them, i.e., in the reading of the Critique of Judgement offered by Jacques Derrida.3º Although this reading brings to lighr some of the hidden presup. positions of Kant's approach to raste by transgressing the most binding rules of orthodox commentary, ir remains subject ro the censorships of the pure reading.

Derrida does indeed sce that what is involved is the opposirion between legitimare 'plcasure' and 'enjoyment' or, in terms of objects, between the agreeable arts which seduce by the charm' of their sensuous content and the Fine Arrs which offer pleasure without enjoyment. He also sees, without explicitly connecting it with the previous opposition, the antithesis between the gross tastes of those who are content to enjoy the simple sensations of the senses, at table or over a borrle'consumprive orality' seen as interested taste'—and pure taste. He indicates that disgust is perhaps the true origin of pure taste, inasmuch as it ‘abolishes representative distance' and, driving one irresistibly towards consumption, annihilates the freedom that is asserred in suspending immediate attachment ro the sensuous and in neurralizing the affect, thar is, 'disinterestedness', a lack of interest as to the existence or non-existence of the thing represented. And one can, no doubr, though Derrida avoids doing so explicitly, relate all the foregoing oppositions, which concern the consumer's relation to the work of art, to rhe last of the oppositions picked up, the one which Kant establishes, at rhe level of production, between 'free art', involving free will, and 'mercenary art', which exchanges the value of its labour for a wage.

It goes without saying thar such a transcription of Derrida's text, which condenses and tightens, thus making connections excluded from the original, which, by instituting an 'order of reasons', produces links that are only suggested and which, above all, gives to che whole enterprise the air of a demonstration directed rowards establishing a trurh, nstitutes a ransformation or distortion. To summarize a discourse which, as is shown by the attention Derrida devotes to the writing and typography, is the product of the intention of putring content inro form, and which rejects in advance any summary aiming to separare content from form, ro reduce the text to its simplest expression, is in fact to deny che most fundamental intention of the work and, by a sort of transcendental reduction which no critique has any thought of carrying out, to perform the epochē of everything by which the philosophical text affirms its existence as a philosophical text, i.e, irs 'disinterestedness', its free dom, and hence its elevation, its distinction, irs distance ftom all 'vulgar' discourses.

But Derrida's supremely intellectual game presupposes lucidity in commitment to the game: 'It is a question of pleasure. Of thinking pure pleasure, the being-pleasure of pleasure. Starting out from pleasure, the third Critique was written for it, and must be read for it. A somewhar arid pleasure—without concepts and without enjoyment—a somewhat strict pleasure, bur we learn here, once again, that there is no pleasure without stricture. Letting myself be led by pleasure, I recognize and, at the same time, 1 pervert an injunction. I follow it: the enigma of pleasure sets the whole book in motion. I seduce it: in treating the third Critique as a work of art or a beautiful object, which it was not meant simply to be, I act as if the existence of the book were indiff erent to me (which, Kant explains, is required by every aesthetic experience) and could be considered with imperturbable detachment'.

Thus, Derrida rells us the truth of his text and his reading (a particular case of the experience of pure pleasure), thar is, that it implies the epoche of any thesis of existence or, more simply, indifference to the existence of the objecr in «question, but he does so in a text which irself implies thar epoche and that indiff erence. It is an exemplary form of denegation—you tell (yourself) the truth but in such a way that you don't tell it—which defines the objective rruth of the philosophical texr in its social use; which confers on the philosophical rext a social acceptability proportionate to its unreality, its graruitousness, its sovereign indifference.3 Because he never withdraws from the philosophical game, whose conventions he tespects, even in the ritual rransgressions ar which only traditionalists could be shocked, he can only philosophically tell the truth about the philosophical text and its philosophical reading, which (apart from the silence of orthodoxy) is the best way of not telling it, and he cannot truly tell the truih about the Kantian philosophy of art and, more gen. erally, about philosophy itself, which his own discourse has helped to Produce. Just as the pictorial rhetoric which continues to foist itself on every artist produces an inevitable aestheticization, so the philosophical way of talking abour philosophy de-realizes everyrhing thar can be said about philosophy.

The radical quesrionings announced by philosophy are in fact circum. scribed by the interests linked ro membership in the philosophical field, that is, to the very ex'istence of this field and the cortesponding censor. ships. The fetd is the historical producet of the labour of rhe successive philosophers who have defned certain topics as philosophical by forcing them on commentary, discussion, critique and polemic; but the prob. lems, rheories, themes or concep ts which are deposited in writings con. sidered at a given moment as philosophical (books, articles, essay topics etc.) and which consriture objectified philosophy impose themselves as a sort of auronomous world on would-be philosophers, who must nor only know them, as items of culrure, but recognize them, as objects of (pre reflexive) belief, failing which chey disqualify themselves as philosophers. All those who profess to be philosophers have a hfe-or-dearh interest, qua philosophers, in the existence of this repository of consecrared rexrs, a mastery of which constitutes the core of their specific capital. Thus, shorr of jeopardizing their own existence as philosopheis and rhe symbolic powers ensuing from this title, they can never cariy rhrough the breal which imply a pracrical epoche of the rhesis of the existence of philosophy, thar is, a denouncement of the cacit conrract defining the conditions of membership in the field, a repudiation of che fundamental belief in the conventions of the game and the value of the stakes, a refusal to grant the mndisputable signs of recognition—references and reverence, obse quium, respect for convention even in their outrages—in shorr, everything which secures recognition of membership.3

Failing to be, at rhe same time, social breaks which truly renounce the gratifications associared wirh membership, rhe mosr audacious intellec. tual breaks of pure reading srill help to preserve rhe stock of consecrated texts from becoming dead lerters, mere archive marerial, fit at best for the history of ideas or the sociology of knowledge, and to perperuate irs exisrence and its specifically philosophical powers by using it as an emblem or as a marrix for discourses which, whatever their stared incention, are always, also, symbolic srrategies deriving their power essencially from the consecrated rexts. Like rhe religious nihilism of some mystic heresies," philosophical nihilism too can fnd an ultimare parh of salvation in the riruals of liberatory rransgression. Just as, by a mira.culous dialectical renewal, the countless acts of derision and desacralization which modern art has perperrared against art have always rurned, insofar as these are still attistic acts, to the glory of art and rhe artist, so the philosophical 'de construction' of philosophy is indeed, when the very hope of radical reconstruction has evaporared, the only philosophical answer to the destruction of philosophy.

, common practice among arcists, since Duchamp-has the eff ect of turning commentary, a typically scholastic genre, both in its conditions of production (lectures, especially in agrégation classes) and in the docile yet rigorous dispositions it demands, into a personal work suitable for publicarion in avant-garde reviews, by a further cransgression, scandalizing che orthodox, of the sacred frontier berwcen the academic field and the licerary field,i.e., berween the 'serious' and the 'frivolous'. This entails a dramatiza. cion (mise en scèπe), particularly visible in 'paralel-column' produccion, which aims to draw arrenrion to the philosophical 'gesrure, making the vesy utterance of discourse an 'act' is the sense which avant-garde painters give to the word and placing the person of the philosopher at the centre of the philosophical stage.

Philosophical objectifcation of the truth of philosophical discourse encounters irs limits in the objective conditions of its own existence as an activity aspiring to philosophical legitimacy, that is, in the existence of a philosophical field demanding recognition of the principles which are the very basis of irs existence. By means of this semi-objectification one can situate oneself simulraneously inside and outsi de, in the game and on the touchline, i.e., on the margin, at the fronrier, in regions which, like rhe 'frame', parergon, are so many limirs, the beginning of rhe end, the end of the beginning, points from which one can be as distant as possible from the interior without falling into the exterior, into outer darkness, that is, into the vulgariry of the non-philosophical, the coarseness of 'empirical', 'ontic', 'positivist' discourse, and where one can combine the profits of transgression with the profts of membership by producing the discourse that is simultaneously closest to an exemplary performance of philosophical discourse and to an exposure of the objective truth of this discourse.3

Whereas the orthodox reading takes lirerally the overt logic of the sacred text—the 'order of reasons' it puts forward, che plan which it announces and through which it continues to impose its order on its own decodingthe heretical reading takes liberties wich che norms and forms imposed by the guardians of rhe text. On one side, chere is the right'rcading, the one which Kant has designared in advance, by manifesting the apparent architectonics and logic of his discourse, with a whole apparatus of skilfully articulated citles and sub-titles and a permanent display of the external signs of deductive rigour:, by basing on his previous writings (with a neat effect of circular self-legitimarion) a problematic which is, to à large extent, the artificial product of the divisions and oppositions (between understanding and reason, theory and practice erc.) produced precisely by his own writings, and which have to be known and recognized by anyone (after Hegel and so many others) who seeks to be known and recognized as a philosopher. On ctie othier side, there is a deliberately skewed approach, decentred, liberated and even subversive, which ignotes the signposrs and refuses the imposed order, fastens on che details neglecred by ordinary commenrators, nores, examples, parentheses, and thus finds irself obliged if only to justify the liberty it takesto denounce the arbitrariness of the orthodox reading and even of the overt logic of the discourse analysed, to raise diffculties and even to bring to light some of the social slips which despite al the effort at rationalizetion and euphemization, betray the denied incentions which ordinary commentary, by dehnition, overlooks."

## The Pleasure of the Text

Alchough it marks a sharp break wich the ordinary rirual of idolacrous reading, this pure reading still concedes the essential point to the philo. sophical work.3 Asking to be treated as it treats its object, i.e., as a work of art, making Kant's object its own objective, i.e, cultivated pleasure, cultivating cultivated pleasure, artificially exalting this artificial pleasure by a roué's ultimate refinement which implies a lucid view on this plea. sure, it offers above all an exemplary specimen of the pleasure of art, che pleasure of the love of art, of which, like all pleasure, it is not easy to spcak. It is a pure pleasure, in the sense that it is irreducibie to the pursuit of the prohts of discinccion and is felt as the simple pleasure of play, of płaying the culrural game well, of playing on one's skill at playing, of culrivaring a pleasure which 'cultivates' and of thus producing, like a kind of endless fire, its ever renewed sustenance of subcle allusions, def. erent or irreverent references, expected or unusual associations.

Proust, who never ceased to cultivate and also analyse cultivated plea. sure, is as lucid as ever in describing this. Endeavouring to understand and commuuicate the idolarrous pleasure he takes in reading a famous page (a passage from Ruskin's Stones of Venice), he has to evoke not only the properties of che work itself, but the whole nerwork of criss-crossing references woven around it—reference of the work to the personal experiences it has accompanied, facilicated or produced in the reader, reference of personal experience to che works it has insidiously coloured wich its connorations, and references of experience of the work to a previous experience of the same work or experience of other works, each of them enriched with all the associations and resonances it carries wirh ic: 'It is itself mysterious, full of images both of beauty and religion like that same church of St. Mark where all the figures of the Old and New Testaments appear against the background of a sort of splendid obscurity and changing brilliance. I remember having read it for the first time in St. Mark's itself. during an hour of storm and darkness when the mosaics shone only with their own marerial light and with an inner gold, earthy and ancient, to which the Venetian sun, which sets ablaze even the angels on the campaniles, added nothing of itself; the emotion I felt in reading that page there, among all the angels which drew their light from che surrounding shadows, was very grear and perhaps not very pure.

As the joy of seeing those beautiful, mysterious figures increased, but was gltered by the pleasure, so to speak, of erudirion which I felt in understand. ing the texts inscribed in Byzantine characters beside their haloed foreheads, so the beauty of Ruskin's images was intensihed and corrupred by che pride of referring to the sacred text. A sort of egoistic self-regard is inevicable in ehere mingled joys of art and erudition in which aesthetic plea. sure may become more acute but not remain so pure."

Cultivated pleasure feeds on these intertwined references, which reinforce and legitimare each ocher, producing, inseparably, belief in the value of works of art, the idolatry'which is the very basis of cultivated pleasure, and the inimitable charm they objectively exert on all who are qualified to enter the game, possessed by their possession. Even in its purest form, when it seems most free of 'worldly' interest, this game is always a 'society' game, based, as Proust again says, on a 'freemasonry of customs and a heritage of tradirions': 'True distinction, besides, always affecrs to address only distinguished persons who know the same customs, and it do not "explain". A book of Anatole France implies a host of lcarned knowledge, includes unending allusions that the vulgar do not perceive chere, and these, its other beauties apart, make up its incomparable nobilicy. Those whom Proust calls the aristocracy of intellect' know how to mark their distinction in the most peremptory fashion by addressing to the 'élite', made up of those who can decipher them, the discreet but irrefutable signs of cheir membership of the élite' (like the loftiness of emblematic references, which designate not so much sources or authorities as the very exclusive, very select circle of recognized interlocutors) and of the discretion with which they are able to affrm their membership.

'Empirical' inrerest enters into the composition of the most disinceiested pleasures of pure taste, because the principle of the pleasure derived from these rehned games for rened players lies, in the last analysis, in the denied experience of a social relarionship of membership and exclusion. The sense of distinccion, an acquired disposition which funccions with the obscure necessicy of inscinct, is affirmed not so much in the manif estos and positive manifescarions of self-conhdence as in che innumerable stylistic or thematic choices which, being based on the concern to underline diffierence, exclude all che forms of intellecrual (or artistic) activity regarded at a given moment as inferior—vulgar objects, unworthy references, simple didactic exposition, naive' problems (naive essentially because they lack philosophical pedigree), 'trivial' questions (Does the Critigue of fudgement get it right? Is the aim of a reading of the Critique to give a true account of what Kant says?), posirions stigmatized as empiricism' or 'histroricism' (no doubt because they threaten the very existence of philosophical activity) and so on. In short, the philosophical sense of distinction is anocher form of che visceral disgust at vulgaricy

<table><tr><td>which defines pure caste as an intematized social relationship, a social re lationship made Resh; and a philosophically distinguished reading of the Critique of Judgement cannor be expected to uncover the social relation. ship of distinction at the heart of a work that is rightly regarded as the very symbol of philosophical distinction.</td><td></td></tr></table>

Appendix 1

Some Reflections on the Method

For an earlier version of this text, it was decided to adopt, for once, an order of presentation as close as possible to the actual process of research, and to present progressively, in the order in which they were performed, the operations which led to a systematic explanation of the data collected in various statistical and ethnographic surveys. It was hoped that in this way there might be more ready acceptance of a body of theoretical hypotheses which, if presented at the outset, might have appeared arbitrary or forced; but which could never have been extracted from the material analysed if they had not been present, in the form of heuristic schemes, from the very beginning of the research. Although the present order of exposition, which starts out from the point of arrival of the research, is less favourable to the smug display of data and procedures which is usually regarded as the best guarantee of scientificity, and although the additional rigour it implies is offset by a whole series of ellipses and shortcuts tending to reinforce the suspicions of those who remain attached to a naively empiricist conception of scientific work, it eventually presented irself as the only sequence which allows each fact to be replaced in the system of relations from which it derives its truth-value. For this reason, it is necessary to outline the main operations of the research, without seeking to disguise the artifciality of this retrospective reconstruction.

The survey on which the work was based was carried out in 1963, after a preliminaty survey by extended interview and ethnographic observation, on a sample of 692 subjects (both sexes) in Paris, Lille and a small provincial town. To obtain a sample large enough to make it possible to analyse variations in practices and opinions in relation to sufficiently homogeneous social units, a complementary survey was carried out in 1967–68, bringing the total number of subjects to 1,217 (see table A.1). Because the survey measured relatively stable dispositions, this time-lag does not seem to have affected the responses (except perhaps for the question on singers, an area of culture where fashions change more rapidly).'

An initial analysis showed that certain categories were extremely heterogeneous, as regards both their objective characteristics and their preferences, in particular the independent craftsmen and shopkeepers (artisans et petits commerçants ), the junior executives (cadres moyens), the senior executives (cadres supérieurs) and the teachers (professeurs). It was decided to isolate, despite their small number, the artistic producers (classed among ‘intellectual occupations' by INSEE and grouped with the professeurs), the cultural intermediaries (grouped with primary teachers by INSEE) and the art craftsmen and small art-dealers (artisans et petits commerçants d'art ); and to distinguish, among the cadres moyens, between junior administrative executives, junior commercial executives and secretaries; among the cadres supérieurs, between private-sector and publicsector senior executives; and, among the professeurs, between secondaryschool teachers and higher-education teachers (although there were very few of the latter).

working classs</td><td></td><td>62.0</td><td>38.0 36.7</td><td>34.9</td><td>27.1 88.2 112</td><td></td><td></td><td>06</td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td>0</td><td>83.3</td></tr></table>

The sample contained approximately the same number of Parisians and provincials,² and was devised so as to allow analysis of practices and choices by class fraction. The upper and middle classes were therefore over-represented so as to give an adequate sample of each of their fractions without distorting the composition of the class. In particular, this made it possible to study the taste of socio-occupational groups which were very small in number at the time of the survey, but which, like the new petite bourgeoisie, were found to occupy strategic positions and which have in fact grown steadily in number and importance.

In the case of the classes populaires, there was under-representation of the most disadvantaged category, that of the semi-skilled workers³ and unskilled labourers, who are very uniform with regard to the object of the survey, i.e., vety uniformly excluded from legitimate culture. And the working class as a whole does not have the weight it would have in a representative sample. This of course implied that not all the data concerning the whole sample would be published (such information is, strictly speaking, always meaningless). Furthermore, the farmers and farm workers were excluded from the analysis, after a preliminary survey which showed that the questionnaire was completely inappropriate and that quite other methods were required to identify the dispositions of a population totally excluded from legitimate culture and even, to a large extent, from 'middle-brow' culture (la culture moyenne). This experiment did, however, bring to light the only and most fundamental piece of information which can be derived from putting questions on legitimate culture to a category that is excluded from it, namely, the almost universal recognition of the dominant culture. It also made it possible to observe, at maximum intensity, the effect of imposition of a problematic which any questioning of this sort produces when it forgets to question itself and forces itself authoritatively on agents for whom it would not exist outside of that situation (an effect which, as was subsequently shown by secondary analysis of numerous opinion polls', leads to the production of pure artifacts).

hererogeneous, as regards both their objective characteristics and their preferences, in particular the independent crafrsmen and shopkeepers (artisans es petits commerçants), the junior executives (cadres moyens), the scnior executives (cadres supérieurs) and the teachers (professeurs). It was decided to isolate, despire their small number, the artistic produceis (classed among intellecrual occupations' by INSEE and grouped with the professeurs), the culrural intermediaries (grouped with primary teachers by INSEE) and the art craftsmen and small art-dealers (arlisans et perits commerçants d'art ); and to distinguish, among che cadres moyens, be rween junior administrative executives, junior commercial executives and secreraries; among the cadres supérieurs, between private-sector and publicsector senior executives; and, among the prosesseurs, berween secondaryschoo) teachers and higher-education teachers (although there were very few of the latter).

The sample contained approximarely the same number of Parisians and provincials, and was devised so as to allow analysis of pracrices and choices by class fraction. The upper and middle classes were therefore over-represented so as to give an adequate sample of each of their fractions without d'istorting the composition of the class. In patticular, this made it possible to srudy the taste of socio-occupational groups which were very small in number ar the time of the survey, but which, like the new petite bourgeoisie, were found to occupy stracegic positions and which have in fact grown steadily in number and importance.

In the case of the casses populairer, there was under-representation of the most disadvantaged caregory, thar of the semi-skilled workers and unskilled labourers, who are very uniform with regard to the object of the survey, i.e., very uniformly excluded from legitimare culrure. And the working class as a whole does nor have the weight it would have in a representative sample. This of course implied that not all the dara conceming the whole sample would be published (such informarion is, strictly speaking, always meaningless). Furthermore, the farmers and farm workers were excluded from the analysis, after a preliminary survey which showed thar the questionnaire was complerely inappropriare and that quite other methods were required to identify the dispositions of a Population totally excluded from legitimare culture and even, to a large extent, from 'middle-brow' culture (la cullure moyenne)." This experiment did, however, bring to light the only and most fundamental piece of informarion which can be derived from putting questions on legitimate culture to a category that is excluded from it, namely, the almost universa) recognition of the dominant culrure. It also made it possihle to observe, at maximum intensity, the eflect of imposition of a problematic which any questioning of this sort produces when it forgets to question itself and forces itself authoritatively on agents for whom it would nor exist ourside of thar situation (an effect which, as was subsequently shown by secondary analysis of numerous opinion polls', leads to the production of pure artifacts).

For each socio-occupational category, the distribution by sex, age and qualifications was checked against the results of the 1968 census for the whole of France. This verification was not possible in the case of the cate gories constituting the new petite bourgeoisie, which the censuses d not break down by age and qualification.

The questionnaire was based on the hypothesis of the unity of taste and included, in addition to a set of questions on personal photography and attitudes to photography which have been analysed in anothe study, twenty-five questions on tastes in interior decoration, clothing singers, cooking, reading, cinema, painting, music, photography, radio pastimes etc. (see the questionnaire, reproduced at the end of this appen dix). When endeavouring to grasp systems of tastes, a survey by closed questionnaire is never more than a second best, imposed by the need to obtain a large amount of comparable data on a sample large enough to be treated statistically. It leaves out almost everything to do with the modal ity of practices; but in an area which is that of art, in the sense of a per sonal way of being and doing, as in ‘art of living', the way things are done and the way they are talked about, blasé or off-hand, serious or fer vent, often makes all the difference (at least when dealing with common practices, such as viewing TV or cinema). That is the first reason why everything that is said here about differences between the classes or class fractions in fact applies a fortiori.

Furthermore, in order to give the systems of dispositions constituting taste as much scope as possible within the limits of a questionnaire, a series of gambles was made, in which the exploration of à whole area (e.g.. music, cinema, cooking, clothing) was entrusted to two or three ques tions (sometimes only one) which often had to stand in for a whole battery of tests and observations. For example, the subjects were asked to choose, from a list of adjectives established a posteriori on the basis of non-directive interviews and tests (showing photographs of socially marked faces, building a composite ['identikit'] picture of the ideal' male or female face by adding socially marked features, hairstyle, moustache, whiskers, beard etc.), those which seemed to them best suited to their friends. This could not be expected to yield more than an attenuated, blurred image of the deep dispositions which guide the choice of spouses, friends or colleagues (partly because the list of adjectives, however carefully prepared, was imperfect and forced a number of respondents into the negative choice of the least unacceptable option).

Howevet, the loss of precision and detail in the analysis of particular areas, each of which would require a whole set of surveys, observations and tests, is offset by a gain in systematicity. Just as in a single field, painting for example, the particular configuration of preferences (Renoir does not mean the same thing when combined with Leonardo and Picasso as when linked with Utrillo and Buffet) is a substitute for the indications of manner which would be yielded by direct observation and questioning; so too, the meaning of each particular application of the single system of dispositions emetges in its relationship with all the others. Nothing more clearly manifests the systematicity of the habitus than the systematic redundancy within unlimited invention which defines all its products, judgements or practices.

It is, however, by mobilizing around a systematic survey all the statistical data available on each of the areas directly explored, and also on areas excluded from the initial definition of the object of inquiry, such as knowledge of economics, theatre-going, attitudes to child-rearing or sexuality etc., that one is best able to check and fill out the data provided by the main survey. This makes it possible to compensate for any patchiness or superficiality (depending on the strength of the indicators used) of information gathered directly from such a wide range of helds; and to do so without falling into the abstract unreality of ‘secondary analysis' of the disparate data mechanically accumulated by 'data banks' devoid of theoretic capital, positivistic institutions which are the pride and joy of research bureaucracies.8

The fact remains that some of the inherent limitations of secondary analysis cannot be overcome. Thus, like the various surveys by INSEE (Institut national de la statistique et des études économiques) the survey conducted by SOFRES (Société française d'enquêtes par sondages) in 1966 at the request of a number of firms, for the strictly limited purpose of discovering consumer intentions (C.S. V), gives little information as to the frequency and occasion of consumption or on the quality of the goods consumed, which often make all the diffetence (e.g., theatre-going covers both avant-garde and boulevard theatre); it does not distinguish bctween museum visits and visits to exhibitions, which (as we know from the INSEE survey on leisure, C.S. IV), do not always vary in the same way—museum-going, a more ascetic activity, being more common among teachers, whereas exhibitions attract more members of the professions and a fraction of the old business bourgeoisie—and it gives no indication of how often they are visited. It lumps concerts, opera and ballet together in one question, although one would probably find an opposition between concerts and opera analogous to that between museums and exhibitions.

Similarly, the reading of works of philosophy does not mean much until one knows which philosophy'. There is reason to think, for example, that each fraction of the dominant class has its' philosophers or even its idea of the philosopher and philosophy, and that one gtoup will think of Teilhard de Chardin, or even Saint-Exupéry or Louis Leprince-Ringuet, whereas another will think of Sartre or Michel Foucault. Thus, in 1967 when the arts students of the Ecole Normale Supérieure (ENS), to a large extent drawn from the dominated fractions of the dominant class, were asked whom they would like to see invited to speak in their college, they thought first of Sartre, Claude Lévi-Strauss, Paul Ricoeur and Fou-

For each socio-occupational category, the distribution by sex, age and qualifications was checked against the results of the 1968 census for the whole of France. This verification was not possible in the case of the cate gories constituting the new petite bourgeoisie, which the censuses do not break down by age and qualification.

The questionnaire was based on the hypothesis of the unity of tastes and included, in addition to a set of questions on personal photography and attitudes to photography which have been analysed in anothet study, twenty-five questions on tastes in interior decoration, clothing singers, cooking, reading, cinema, painting, music, photography, radiog pastimes etc. (see the questionnaire, reproduced at the end of this appen dix). It leaves out almost everything to do with the modal ity of practices; but in an area which is that of art, in the sense of a per sonal way of being and doing, as in ‘art of living', the way things are done and the way they are talked about, blasé or off-hand, serious or fer vent, often makes all the difference (at least when dealing with common practices, such as viewing TV or cinema). That is the first reason why everything that is said here about differences between the classes or class fractions in fact applies a fortiori.
